commit_message:
vm.c: create break object before clearing GC arena.

Otherwise it possibly cause use-after-free.
cve_desc: Use-After-Free in str_escape in mruby/mruby in GitHub repository mruby/mruby prior to 3.2. Possible arbitrary code execution if being exploited.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Use of uninitialized variable 'blk' in OP_SEND/OP_SENDB handler
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the L_SENDB_SYM block, the local variable 'blk' is declared but never initialized before being passed to prepare_missing() and later checked with mrb_proc_p(blk). The variable is declared as 'mrb_value recv, blk;' but no value is assigned to 'blk' before its use. This means 'blk' contains whatever was on the stack at that point. If mrb_proc_p() happens to return true for the garbage value, the code will dereference it as a proc pointer via mrb_proc_ptr(blk), potentially leading to memory corruption, information disclosure, or code execution. The same issue exists in the OP_SUPER handler where 'blk' is similarly uninitialized before being passed to prepare_missing().

vulnerabilities.short_description: Use of uninitialized variable 'blk' in OP_SUPER handler
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the OP_SUPER handler, the local variable 'blk' is declared but never assigned a value before being passed to prepare_missing(). This passes stack garbage as a block argument, which could lead to incorrect method dispatch behavior or memory safety issues if the garbage value is later interpreted as a valid object pointer.

commit_message:
i2c: fix stack buffer overflow vulnerability in i2c md command

When running "i2c md 0 0 80000100", the function do_i2c_md parses the
length into an unsigned int variable named length. The value is then
moved to a signed variable:

    int nbytes = length;
    #define DISP_LINE_LEN 16
    int linebytes = (nbytes > DISP_LINE_LEN) ? DISP_LINE_LEN : nbytes;
    ret = dm_i2c_read(dev, addr, linebuf, linebytes);

On systems where integers are 32 bits wide, 0x80000100 is a negative
value to "nbytes > DISP_LINE_LEN" is false and linebytes gets assigned
0x80000100 instead of 16.

The consequence is that the function which reads from the i2c device
(dm_i2c_read or i2c_read) is called with a 16-byte stack buffer to fill
but with a size parameter which is too large. In some cases, this could
trigger a crash. But with some i2c drivers, such as drivers/i2c/nx_i2c.c
(used with "nexell,s5pxx18-i2c" bus), the size is actually truncated to
a 16-bit integer. This is because function i2c_transfer expects an
unsigned short length. In such a case, an attacker who can control the
response of an i2c device can overwrite the return address of a function
and execute arbitrary code through Return-Oriented Programming.

Fix this issue by using unsigned integers types in do_i2c_md. While at
it, make also alen unsigned, as signed sizes can cause vulnerabilities
when people forgot to check that they can be negative.

Signed-off-by: Nicolas Iooss <nicolas.iooss+uboot@ledger.fr>
Reviewed-by: Heiko Schocher <hs@denx.de>
cve_desc: In Das U-Boot through 2022.07-rc5, an integer signedness error and resultant stack-based buffer overflow in the "i2c md" command enables the corruption of the return address pointer of the do_i2c_md function.

commit_message:
i2c: fix stack buffer overflow vulnerability in i2c md command

When running "i2c md 0 0 80000100", the function do_i2c_md parses the
length into an unsigned int variable named length. The value is then
moved to a signed variable:

    int nbytes = length;
    #define DISP_LINE_LEN 16
    int linebytes = (nbytes > DISP_LINE_LEN) ? DISP_LINE_LEN : nbytes;
    ret = dm_i2c_read(dev, addr, linebuf, linebytes);

On systems where integers are 32 bits wide, 0x80000100 is a negative
value to "nbytes > DISP_LINE_LEN" is false and linebytes gets assigned
0x80000100 instead of 16.

The consequence is that the function which reads from the i2c device
(dm_i2c_read or i2c_read) is called with a 16-byte stack buffer to fill
but with a size parameter which is too large. In some cases, this could
trigger a crash. But with some i2c drivers, such as drivers/i2c/nx_i2c.c
(used with "nexell,s5pxx18-i2c" bus), the size is actually truncated to
a 16-bit integer. This is because function i2c_transfer expects an
unsigned short length. In such a case, an attacker who can control the
response of an i2c device can overwrite the return address of a function
and execute arbitrary code through Return-Oriented Programming.

Fix this issue by using unsigned integers types in do_i2c_md. While at
it, make also alen unsigned, as signed sizes can cause vulnerabilities
when people forgot to check that they can be negative.

Signed-off-by: Nicolas Iooss <nicolas.iooss+uboot@ledger.fr>
Reviewed-by: Heiko Schocher <hs@denx.de>
cve_desc: In Das U-Boot through 2022.07-rc5, an integer signedness error and resultant stack-based buffer overflow in the "i2c md" command enables the corruption of the return address pointer of the do_i2c_md function.

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/issues/131
cve_desc: Heap-based buffer overflow in the CalcMinMax function in coders/mat.c in ImageMagick before 6.9.4-0 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (out-of-bounds read and application crash) via a crafted mat file.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in ldblk calculation leads to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: MATLAB_HDR.SizeX is an unsigned 32-bit value read from the file. Multiplying it by 2, 4, or 8 can overflow the unsigned int before the cast to ssize_t. For example, if SizeX is 0x40000001, then 4*SizeX wraps to 4. The buffer BImgBuff is then allocated based on the small overflowed ldblk value, but image->columns is set to the original large SizeX. When ImportQuantumPixels processes image->columns pixels from the undersized BImgBuff, it reads far past the allocated buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow. An attacker can craft a MAT file with a large SizeX to trigger this and potentially achieve code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Undefined behavior / integer overflow when shifting by image->depth of 32 or 64
vulnerabilities.long_description: When CellType is miINT32/miUINT32, image->depth is set to 32; for miINT64/miUINT64 or miDOUBLE, depth is 64. The expression `one << image->depth` where `one` is size_t performs a left shift by 32 or 64 bits. Shifting by an amount equal to or greater than the width of the type (64 bits on a 64-bit system, 32 bits on a 32-bit system) is undefined behavior in C. On a 32-bit system, shifting by 32 is UB; on a 64-bit system shifting by 64 is UB. This can lead to unexpected values for image->colors and unpredictable behavior.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of quantum_info when jumping to ExitLoop from inside the loop
vulnerabilities.long_description: If execution jumps to the ExitLoop label (via goto ExitLoop from within the do-while loop due to ReadBlob failure or SyncAuthenticPixels failure), the `quantum_info=DestroyQuantumInfo(quantum_info)` statement that precedes the label is skipped. This causes quantum_info to never be freed, resulting in a memory leak. Repeated processing of malformed MAT files could exhaust available memory.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in filepos calculation can cause infinite loop or incorrect seeking
vulnerabilities.long_description: MATLAB_HDR.ObjectSize is read from the file as an unsigned 32-bit value, and filepos is MagickOffsetType (signed 64-bit). While the addition itself won't overflow a 64-bit type, a maliciously large ObjectSize could cause filepos to point to arbitrary positions. Combined with the while(!EOFBlob(image)) loop, a crafted ObjectSize could cause the parser to re-read previously processed data or loop indefinitely, leading to denial of service.

commit_message:
Fix integer overflow in string search causing oobread ##crash

* Reported by @greatergoodest via huntrdev
* BountyID: 8a3dc5cb-08b3-4807-82b2-77f08c137a04
* Reproducer bfileovf
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.7.0.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in outlen calculation can lead to heap buffer overflow during charset encoding
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `outlen` is declared as `int` but is assigned `len * 4` where `len` is `st64` and can be up to `ST32_MAX` (2147483647). Multiplying by 4 overflows a 32-bit signed integer, resulting in undefined behavior that could produce a negative or small positive value. This incorrect `outlen` is then passed to `r_charset_encode_str` as the output buffer size limit. While `calloc(len, 4)` correctly allocates the larger buffer (calloc handles the multiplication internally with overflow checks), the `outlen` parameter may not correctly represent the buffer size, potentially allowing `r_charset_encode_str` to write beyond what the caller intended or behave unexpectedly with a negative size, leading to a heap buffer overflow.

code_snippets:
if (needle + 4 < to) {
    ut32 n1 = r_read_le32 (buf + needle - from);
rc = r_utf8_decode (buf + needle - from, to - needle, NULL);
rc = r_utf32le_decode (buf + needle - from, to - needle, &r);
rc = r_utf16le_decode (buf + needle - from, to - needle, &r);
vulnerabilities.short_description: Heap out-of-bounds read when charset encoding produces shorter output but loop bounds remain unchanged
vulnerabilities.long_description: After charset encoding, `buf` is replaced with the encoded output buffer of size `original_len * 4`, and `len` is updated to `res` (the encoded length). However, the loop condition `needle < to` still uses the original `to` value where `to - from = original_len`. While the allocated buffer (`calloc(len, 4)`) is large enough, the `to - needle` parameter passed as the remaining-length argument to decode functions like `r_utf8_decode`, `r_utf32le_decode`, and `r_utf16le_decode` may exceed the actual meaningful data in the buffer. If charset encoding produced a result shorter than the original, the decode functions will be fed `to - needle` as the available bytes which is larger than the actual encoded content, potentially reading uninitialized heap memory (the zero-filled calloc tail) and producing incorrect results. More critically, if charset encoding is not used but a future code path changes buffer sizes, the disconnect between `to` and actual buffer size is a latent out-of-bounds read risk.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read on the string literal lookup table when escape character value is unexpected
vulnerabilities.long_description: The escape code handling indexes into a string literal using the raw rune value `r` as an index. While the code checks `r < 93`, the string literal used for the lookup appears to be exactly 93 characters (indices 0-92) including the final backslash. The check `r < 93` allows `r` values from 0 to 92. However, the `strchr` filter above only allows specific characters (`\b\v\f\n\r\t\a\033\\`), whose values are 8,11,12,10,13,9,7,27,92 respectively—all less than 93. While the specific allowed values do map correctly, if the strchr check were ever modified or bypassed, arbitrary indexing into the string literal could occur. The current code is fragile but technically safe given the strchr guard.

commit_message:
Prevent use after free.

A very old version of the code used `result` as a simple pointer to a resource. Two years later, the pointer got changed to a `unique_ptr` but author forgot to remove the call to `Unref`. Three years after that, we finally uncover the UAF.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387924872
Change-Id: I70fb6f199164de49fac20c168132a07b84903f9b
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation for `tf.raw_ops.BoostedTreesCreateEnsemble` can result in a use after free error if an attacker supplies specially crafted arguments. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/f24faa153ad31a4b51578f8181d3aaab77a1ddeb/tensorflow/core/kernels/boosted_trees/resource_ops.cc#L55) uses a reference counted resource and decrements the refcount if the initialization fails, as it should. However, when the code was written, the resource was represented as a naked pointer but later refactoring has changed it to be a smart pointer. Thus, when the pointer leaves the scope, a subsequent `free`-ing of the resource occurs, but this fails to take into account that the refcount has already reached 0, thus the resource has been already freed. During this double-free process, members of the resource object are accessed for cleanup but they are invalid as the entire resource has been freed. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 5ecec9c6fbdbc6be03295685190a45e7eee726ab. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Double-free of BoostedTreesEnsembleResource when InitFromSerialized fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: When InitFromSerialized fails, `result->Unref()` is called on the resource, which (assuming ref count was 1 from creation) will decrement to 0 and delete the object. However, `result` is managed by a `std::unique_ptr`, so when OP_REQUIRES causes an early return, the unique_ptr destructor will attempt to delete the same object again, causing a double-free. This can lead to heap corruption and potentially arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of BoostedTreesEnsembleResource when CreateResource returns ALREADY_EXISTS
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `CreateResource` returns ALREADY_EXISTS, the raw pointer obtained via `result.release()` is never reclaimed. `release()` relinquishes unique_ptr ownership, and since CreateResource did not take ownership (it failed), the resource is leaked. An attacker could repeatedly trigger this code path to cause unbounded memory consumption, leading to denial of service.

commit_message: fiber.c: should pack 15+ arguments in an array.
cve_desc: NULL Pointer Dereference in GitHub repository mruby/mruby prior to 3.2.

code_snippets: mrb_stack_extend(mrb, len+2); /* for receiver and (optional) block */
vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in stack size calculation (len+2) may lead to undersized allocation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expression `len+2` can overflow if `len` (an `mrb_int`) is close to its maximum value. If `len+2` wraps around to a small or negative number, `mrb_stack_extend` will allocate an insufficient buffer. The subsequent copy loop `while (b<e) { *b++ = *a++; }` would then write `len` elements past the end of the allocated stack, causing a heap buffer overflow. An attacker who can control the number of arguments passed to a fiber resume could exploit this to achieve arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Truncation of mrb_int len into the narrow callinfo `n` field
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `n` field in `mrb_callinfo` is typically a small bitfield (e.g., 4 or 15 bits) in mruby. Assigning a full `mrb_int` value to it silently truncates the value. This means the recorded argument count `n` can be much smaller than the actual number of arguments placed on the stack. Later code that relies on `n` to determine argument count will read incorrect values, leading to inconsistent state, potential out-of-bounds reads/writes, or logic errors that could be exploited.

commit_message:
xfrm: policy: check policy direction value

The 'dir' parameter in xfrm_migrate() is a user-controlled byte which is used
as an array index. This can lead to an out-of-bound access, kernel lockup and
DoS. Add a check for the 'dir' value.

This fixes CVE-2017-11600.

References: https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=1474928
Fixes: 80c9abaabf42 ("[XFRM]: Extension for dynamic update of endpoint address(es)")
Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org> # v2.6.21-rc1
Reported-by: "bo Zhang" <zhangbo5891001@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Vladis Dronov <vdronov@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: Steffen Klassert <steffen.klassert@secunet.com>
cve_desc: net/xfrm/xfrm_policy.c in the Linux kernel through 4.12.3, when CONFIG_XFRM_MIGRATE is enabled, does not ensure that the dir value of xfrm_userpolicy_id is XFRM_POLICY_MAX or less, which allows local users to cause a denial of service (out-of-bounds access) or possibly have unspecified other impact via an XFRM_MSG_MIGRATE xfrm Netlink message.

commit_message:
Prevent use after free.

A very old version of the code used `result` as a simple pointer to a resource. Two years later, the pointer got changed to a `unique_ptr` but author forgot to remove the call to `Unref`. Three years after that, we finally uncover the UAF.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387924872
Change-Id: I70fb6f199164de49fac20c168132a07b84903f9b
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation for `tf.raw_ops.BoostedTreesCreateEnsemble` can result in a use after free error if an attacker supplies specially crafted arguments. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/f24faa153ad31a4b51578f8181d3aaab77a1ddeb/tensorflow/core/kernels/boosted_trees/resource_ops.cc#L55) uses a reference counted resource and decrements the refcount if the initialization fails, as it should. However, when the code was written, the resource was represented as a naked pointer but later refactoring has changed it to be a smart pointer. Thus, when the pointer leaves the scope, a subsequent `free`-ing of the resource occurs, but this fails to take into account that the refcount has already reached 0, thus the resource has been already freed. During this double-free process, members of the resource object are accessed for cleanup but they are invalid as the entire resource has been freed. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 5ecec9c6fbdbc6be03295685190a45e7eee726ab. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak when CreateResource returns ALREADY_EXISTS
vulnerabilities.long_description: When CreateResource returns ALREADY_EXISTS, it means the resource was not adopted by the resource manager. However, result.release() has already relinquished ownership from the unique_ptr. Since the ALREADY_EXISTS case is silently ignored (no error raised, no cleanup), the newly allocated BoostedTreesEnsembleResource is leaked — neither freed nor Unref'd. An attacker who can repeatedly invoke this op when a resource already exists can cause unbounded memory growth, leading to denial of service.

commit_message:
Fix security vulnerability with LSTMBlockCellOp

PiperOrigin-RevId: 446028341
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.LSTMBlockCell` does not fully validate the input arguments. This results in a `CHECK`-failure which can be used to trigger a denial of service attack. The code does not validate the ranks of any of the arguments to this API call. This results in `CHECK`-failures when the elements of the tensor are accessed. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Rank checks performed after dimension values are already used, allowing out-of-bounds access on scalar/1-D tensors.
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code calls dim_size(0) and dim_size(1) on x_tensor, cs_prev_tensor, h_prev_tensor, w_tensor, and b_tensor before verifying that these tensors actually have the expected number of dimensions (the `dims() == 2` / `dims() == 1` checks occur much later in the function). If a caller provides a scalar (rank-0) or rank-1 tensor for x_tensor, then `x_tensor->dim_size(1)` is an out-of-bounds read. Similarly for cs_prev_tensor, h_prev_tensor, w_tensor, and b_tensor. An attacker who controls the input tensors could trigger undefined behavior, potentially reading out-of-bounds memory or causing a crash. The shape validation checks and the rank checks should be reordered so that rank is validated first.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in arithmetic on untrusted dimension values before use in allocation.
vulnerabilities.long_description: Values like `input_size + cell_size` and `cell_size * 4` are computed as int64 arithmetic on user-controlled tensor dimensions without overflow checks. While int64 overflow is unlikely in practice, extremely large dimension values could cause `cell_size * 4` to wrap around, passing the equality check and leading to incorrectly-sized tensor allocations or buffer overflows in the subsequent LSTM computation. The dimension arithmetic should be checked for overflow before being used in comparisons and TensorShape construction.

commit_message:
xfrm: policy: check policy direction value

The 'dir' parameter in xfrm_migrate() is a user-controlled byte which is used
as an array index. This can lead to an out-of-bound access, kernel lockup and
DoS. Add a check for the 'dir' value.

This fixes CVE-2017-11600.

References: https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=1474928
Fixes: 80c9abaabf42 ("[XFRM]: Extension for dynamic update of endpoint address(es)")
Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org> # v2.6.21-rc1
Reported-by: "bo Zhang" <zhangbo5891001@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Vladis Dronov <vdronov@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: Steffen Klassert <steffen.klassert@secunet.com>
cve_desc: net/xfrm/xfrm_policy.c in the Linux kernel through 4.12.3, when CONFIG_XFRM_MIGRATE is enabled, does not ensure that the dir value of xfrm_userpolicy_id is XFRM_POLICY_MAX or less, which allows local users to cause a denial of service (out-of-bounds access) or possibly have unspecified other impact via an XFRM_MSG_MIGRATE xfrm Netlink message.

commit_message:
vm.c: create break object before clearing GC arena.

Otherwise it possibly cause use-after-free.
cve_desc: Use-After-Free in str_escape in mruby/mruby in GitHub repository mruby/mruby prior to 3.2. Possible arbitrary code execution if being exploited.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Use of uninitialized variable 'blk' in OP_SEND/OP_SENDB handler
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the OP_SEND/OP_SENDB handler, the local variable 'blk' is declared but never initialized before being passed to prepare_missing() and later checked with mrb_proc_p(blk). Since 'blk' is an mrb_value on the stack, it will contain whatever garbage data was previously there. This uninitialized value is passed to prepare_missing() and later used in a mrb_proc_p() type check, which could interpret the garbage data as a valid proc pointer, leading to memory corruption or arbitrary code execution when p->flags is subsequently modified. An attacker who can control stack contents (e.g., through prior operations) could potentially exploit this to gain control flow.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Use of uninitialized variable 'blk' in OP_SUPER handler
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the OP_SUPER handler, the local variable 'blk' is declared but never initialized before being passed to prepare_missing(). This uninitialized mrb_value will contain indeterminate stack data. When the method being called is undefined and prepare_missing is invoked, the garbage value in 'blk' could be misinterpreted, potentially leading to memory safety violations. An attacker crafting mruby bytecode that triggers a super call to an undefined method could exploit this uninitialized variable.

commit_message: Fix handling of APNG with 0 delay_den (#313)
cve_desc: libjxl v0.3.7 is affected by a Divide By Zero in issue in lib/extras/codec_apng.cc jxl::DecodeImageAPNG(). When encoding a malicous APNG file using cjxl, an attacker can trigger a denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in image buffer size calculation leads to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variables rowbytes and imagesize are unsigned int (32-bit). The multiplication `w * 4` can overflow if w is large (but still under cMaxPNGSize), and `h * rowbytes` can also overflow. This results in a much smaller buffer being allocated than expected. Subsequent writes into frameRaw.p via frameRaw.rows (e.g., by libpng's decompression or the pixel reading loop) will write far beyond the allocated buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow. An attacker can craft a PNG with large but valid dimensions to trigger this.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in dec_pixels accumulation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The multiplication `w0 * h0` is performed on unsigned int (32-bit) values which can overflow before being added to dec_pixels. This could bypass any pixel budget checks that rely on dec_pixels, allowing decompression bombs or excessive resource consumption.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer underflow when processing fdAT chunks with small size
vulnerabilities.long_description: When handling fdAT chunks, the code computes `chunk.size - 16` and `chunk.size - 4` without verifying that chunk.size is large enough. If chunk.size is less than 16, the subtraction underflows (since these are unsigned values), resulting in a very large value being passed to png_save_uint_32 or processing_data. This can lead to out-of-bounds reads or other memory corruption when libpng processes the fabricated IDAT data.

code_snippets: for (j = 0; j < h; j++) frameRaw.rows[j] = frameRaw.p + j * rowbytes;
vulnerabilities.short_description: Incorrect allocation size for row pointers array (over-allocation, potential overflow)
vulnerabilities.long_description: The allocation `new png_bytep[h * sizeof(png_bytep)]` allocates h*sizeof(png_bytep) pointers instead of just h pointers. While this over-allocates (wasting memory), the expression `h * sizeof(png_bytep)` can itself overflow on 32-bit systems or with large h values, potentially resulting in a smaller-than-expected allocation followed by out-of-bounds writes in the initialization loop.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read when parsing fcTL chunk without size validation
vulnerabilities.long_description: When a fcTL chunk is encountered, the code reads fields at offsets up to 33 from chunk.p without first verifying that the chunk's actual size is large enough to contain all these fields. A maliciously crafted PNG with a truncated fcTL chunk would cause out-of-bounds heap reads, potentially leaking sensitive memory contents or causing a crash.

commit_message:
rsi: release skb if rsi_prepare_beacon fails

In rsi_send_beacon, if rsi_prepare_beacon fails the allocated skb should
be released.

Signed-off-by: Navid Emamdoost <navid.emamdoost@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Kalle Valo <kvalo@codeaurora.org>
cve_desc: A memory leak in the rsi_send_beacon() function in drivers/net/wireless/rsi/rsi_91x_mgmt.c in the Linux kernel through 5.3.11 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (memory consumption) by triggering rsi_prepare_beacon() failures, aka CID-d563131ef23c.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak when rsi_prepare_beacon fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: When rsi_prepare_beacon() fails, the function returns -EINVAL without freeing the allocated skb. This leads to a memory leak each time beacon preparation fails. An attacker or adverse conditions causing repeated failures could exhaust kernel memory (denial of service). The fix is to call dev_kfree_skb(skb) before returning.

commit_message: fiber.c: should pack 15+ arguments in an array.
cve_desc: NULL Pointer Dereference in GitHub repository mruby/mruby prior to 3.2.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in stack size calculation leads to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `len` is a large positive `mrb_int` value (close to MRB_INT_MAX) but less than 15, the expression `len+2` can overflow, wrapping to a small or negative value. If `mrb_stack_extend` receives this overflowed value, it may allocate a very small stack buffer. The subsequent while loop then copies `len` elements (the original, large value) into the undersized buffer starting at `c->stbase+1`, resulting in a massive heap buffer overflow. An attacker who can control the number of arguments passed to a fiber resume/transfer could exploit this to corrupt heap memory, potentially achieving arbitrary code execution.

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/issues/131
cve_desc: Heap-based buffer overflow in the CalcMinMax function in coders/mat.c in ImageMagick before 6.9.4-0 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (out-of-bounds read and application crash) via a crafted mat file.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in ldblk calculation leads to undersized heap buffer allocation and subsequent heap buffer overflow.
vulnerabilities.long_description: MATLAB_HDR.SizeX is read from the file as a 32-bit unsigned integer. The multiplication (e.g., 8 * MATLAB_HDR.SizeX) is performed in 32-bit unsigned arithmetic before being cast to ssize_t. If SizeX is large enough, the multiplication wraps around, producing a small ldblk value. BImgBuff is then allocated based on this small ldblk. However, image->columns is set to the original (large) SizeX. When ImportQuantumPixels processes a full scanline of image->columns pixels, it reads far beyond the undersized BImgBuff buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow. An attacker can craft a MAT file with a large SizeX to trigger this, potentially achieving code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Undefined behavior from shifting by the bit-width of the type when image->depth is 64.
vulnerabilities.long_description: When CellType is miINT64/miUINT64 or miDOUBLE, image->depth is set to 64. The expression 'one << image->depth' where 'one' is a size_t (typically 64-bit) results in undefined behavior because shifting by the exact width of the type is undefined in C/C++. This can lead to unpredictable values for image->colors, potentially causing issues in downstream processing.

vulnerabilities.short_description: QuantumInfo is allocated each loop iteration but never freed, causing a memory leak.
vulnerabilities.long_description: In each iteration of the main while loop, quantum_info is allocated via AcquireQuantumInfo but is never released with DestroyQuantumInfo before the next iteration or before exiting the function. Over multiple frames or objects in a MAT file, this causes progressive memory leaks. A specially crafted MAT file with many objects could exhaust memory.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential out-of-bounds array access on z2qtype when z has an unexpected value.
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable z is derived from a value read from the file (ReadBlobXXXLong) in the DimFlag==12 and DimFlag==16 cases. While there are checks for z!=3 and z!=1, z is used as an index into the z2qtype array after being modified (e.g., 'if(z==1) z=0'). If the z2qtype array is not large enough to handle all possible values of z (0, 1, 2, 3), an out-of-bounds read could occur, leading to undefined behavior or use of an incorrect quantum type.

commit_message: Fix wrong boundary checks in inflections parser resulting in stack buffer over-read with corrupt input
cve_desc: Buffer Over-read in GitHub repository bfabiszewski/libmobi prior to 0.11. This vulnerability is capable of arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds access when pos is decremented below zero in the delete branch
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the delete ('d') branch, when dir == '<', pos is decremented before any bounds check against zero. If pos is 0 at that point, it becomes -1. The subsequent bounds check (`l < 0 || d + l > decoded + INDX_INFLBUF_SIZEMAX`) does not catch a negative pos: `l = *decoded_size - (-1)` is positive, and `d = decoded + (-1)` wraps to before the buffer, so `d + l` may still be within INDX_INFLBUF_SIZEMAX. This leads to an out-of-bounds read at `decoded[pos]` (i.e., `decoded[-1]`) and an out-of-bounds memmove, potentially allowing information disclosure or memory corruption.

code_snippets:
const int l = *decoded_size - pos;
if (l < 0 || d + l > decoded + INDX_INFLBUF_SIZEMAX) {
vulnerabilities.short_description: Insert branch may increment pos and decoded_size without adequate re-validation across iterations
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the insert ('i') branch, after a successful insertion, `(*decoded_size)++` and optionally `pos++` are performed. There is no check that the new `pos` value remains within [0, *decoded_size] before the next loop iteration processes a new rule byte. A crafted rule sequence could manipulate pos (e.g., via direction changes that set pos to *decoded_size followed by further inserts) to write beyond the intended buffer bounds. The existing overflow check only validates against INDX_INFLBUF_SIZEMAX but does not re-validate pos against decoded_size, potentially allowing writes past the logical end of the data.

commit_message:
Fix integer overflow in string search causing oobread ##crash

* Reported by @greatergoodest via huntrdev
* BountyID: 8a3dc5cb-08b3-4807-82b2-77f08c137a04
* Reproducer bfileovf
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.7.0.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow when computing outlen for charset encoding buffer
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `len` is of type `st64` and can be up to `ST32_MAX` (~2^31 - 1). When `int outlen = len * 4` is computed, the multiplication overflows the 32-bit `int` type for `len` values greater than `INT_MAX / 4` (~536 million). This overflowed `outlen` value (which could be small, zero, or negative) is then passed to `r_charset_encode_str` as the output buffer size limit. Meanwhile, `calloc(len, 4)` may allocate a large buffer successfully. If the encode function trusts the small/negative `outlen` as its bounds, it could either write out of bounds or behave incorrectly, potentially leading to a heap buffer overflow.

code_snippets: rc = r_utf8_decode (buf + (needle - from), to - needle, NULL);
rc = r_utf32le_decode (buf + needle - from, to - needle, &r);
rc = r_utf16le_decode (buf + needle - from, to - needle, &r);
vulnerabilities.short_description: Heap out-of-bounds read after charset re-encoding changes effective buffer content length
vulnerabilities.long_description: After charset encoding, `len` is reassigned to `res` (the number of encoded bytes), which can be significantly smaller than the original `to - from`. However, the scanning loop still iterates `needle` from `from` to `to` using the original range bounds. All buffer accesses use `buf + (needle - from)` and `to - needle` as the remaining length. While the allocated buffer (`calloc(len, 4)` with original `len`) is large enough to avoid a crash in most cases, the `to - needle` parameter passed to decode functions like `r_utf8_decode`, `r_utf32le_decode`, and `r_utf16le_decode` can be larger than the actual meaningful data in the buffer, causing reads of uninitialized (zeroed) memory beyond the encoded content. The decode functions' length parameter `to - needle` does not reflect the true buffer content boundary, which can lead to incorrect behavior and potentially exploitable information processing issues.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Stack buffer over-read when indexing escape character lookup string with rune value
vulnerabilities.long_description: The escape code handling indexes a fixed-length string literal (93 characters including the concatenated parts) using the rune value `r` directly. The condition checks `r < 93`, but `r` is a `RRune` (likely unsigned) that was already verified to be `< 0x100` and matched by `strchr`. While the `strchr` limits which values reach this code, the string literal lookup `[r]` uses `r` as a direct index. If `r` is 0x1b (escape, 27) it maps to 'e', and `r` = 0x5c (92) maps to '\\'. The string literal is exactly 93 characters (indices 0-92), so `r < 93` is correct for bounds. However, the outer loop condition `i < sizeof(tmp) - 4` combined with `rc = 2` incrementing `i` by 2 means `i` could reach `sizeof(tmp) - 5`, and writing at `tmp[i+1]` = `tmp[sizeof(tmp) - 4]` is within bounds. This is borderline safe but fragile.

commit_message:
ALSA: pcm: oss: Fix race at SNDCTL_DSP_SYNC

There is a small race window at snd_pcm_oss_sync() that is called from
OSS PCM SNDCTL_DSP_SYNC ioctl; namely the function calls
snd_pcm_oss_make_ready() at first, then takes the params_lock mutex
for the rest.  When the stream is set up again by another thread
between them, it leads to inconsistency, and may result in unexpected
results such as NULL dereference of OSS buffer as a fuzzer spotted
recently.

The fix is simply to cover snd_pcm_oss_make_ready() call into the same
params_lock mutex with snd_pcm_oss_make_ready_locked() variant.

Reported-and-tested-by: butt3rflyh4ck <butterflyhuangxx@gmail.com>
Reviewed-by: Jaroslav Kysela <perex@perex.cz>
Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/CAFcO6XN7JDM4xSXGhtusQfS2mSBcx50VJKwQpCq=WeLt57aaZA@mail.gmail.com
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20220905060714.22549-1-tiwai@suse.de
Signed-off-by: Takashi Iwai <tiwai@suse.de>
cve_desc: A race condition flaw was found in the Linux kernel sound subsystem due to improper locking. It could lead to a NULL pointer dereference while handling the SNDCTL_DSP_SYNC ioctl. A privileged local user (root or member of the audio group) could use this flaw to crash the system, resulting in a denial of service condition

commit_message:
rsi: release skb if rsi_prepare_beacon fails

In rsi_send_beacon, if rsi_prepare_beacon fails the allocated skb should
be released.

Signed-off-by: Navid Emamdoost <navid.emamdoost@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Kalle Valo <kvalo@codeaurora.org>
cve_desc: A memory leak in the rsi_send_beacon() function in drivers/net/wireless/rsi/rsi_91x_mgmt.c in the Linux kernel through 5.3.11 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (memory consumption) by triggering rsi_prepare_beacon() failures, aka CID-d563131ef23c.

vulnerabilities.short_description: memset zeroes MAX_MGMT_PKT_SIZE bytes but skb_pull alignment may leave less usable space, and memset itself may write beyond allocated tailroom
vulnerabilities.long_description: The skb is allocated with dev_alloc_skb(MAX_MGMT_PKT_SIZE), which reserves some headroom (typically NET_SKB_PAD + NET_IP_ALIGN). The memset then writes MAX_MGMT_PKT_SIZE bytes starting at skb->data, but the actual linear data buffer available from skb->data may be less than MAX_MGMT_PKT_SIZE due to the headroom reservation. This can cause an out-of-bounds write past the end of the skb's allocated data region. Additionally, after the alignment adjustment via skb_pull (up to 63 bytes), the remaining buffer available is further reduced, so subsequent operations in rsi_prepare_beacon could also write out of bounds if they assume MAX_MGMT_PKT_SIZE bytes are available.

commit_message:
RDMA/rtrs-clt: Fix possible double free in error case

Callback function rtrs_clt_dev_release() for put_device() calls kfree(clt)
to free memory. We shouldn't call kfree(clt) again, and we can't use the
clt after kfree too.

Replace device_register() with device_initialize() and device_add() so that
dev_set_name can() be used appropriately.

Move mutex_destroy() to the release function so it can be called in
the alloc_clt err path.

Fixes: eab098246625 ("RDMA/rtrs-clt: Refactor the failure cases in alloc_clt")
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20220217030929.323849-1-haris.iqbal@ionos.com
Reported-by: Miaoqian Lin <linmq006@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Md Haris Iqbal <haris.iqbal@ionos.com>
Reviewed-by: Jack Wang <jinpu.wang@ionos.com>
Signed-off-by: Jason Gunthorpe <jgg@nvidia.com>
cve_desc: drivers/infiniband/ulp/rtrs/rtrs-clt.c in the Linux kernel before 5.16.12 has a double free related to rtrs_clt_dev_release.

commit_message:
RDMA/rtrs-clt: Fix possible double free in error case

Callback function rtrs_clt_dev_release() for put_device() calls kfree(clt)
to free memory. We shouldn't call kfree(clt) again, and we can't use the
clt after kfree too.

Replace device_register() with device_initialize() and device_add() so that
dev_set_name can() be used appropriately.

Move mutex_destroy() to the release function so it can be called in
the alloc_clt err path.

Fixes: eab098246625 ("RDMA/rtrs-clt: Refactor the failure cases in alloc_clt")

Signed-off-by: Md Haris Iqbal <haris.iqbal@ionos.com>

cve_desc: drivers/infiniband/ulp/rtrs/rtrs-clt.c in the Linux kernel before 5.16.12 has a double free related to rtrs_clt_dev_release.

commit_message:
swtpm: Check header size indicator against expected size (CID 375869)

This fix addresses Coverity issue CID 375869.

Check the header size indicated in the header of the state against the
expected size and return an error code in case the header size indicator
is different. There was only one header size so far since blobheader was
introduced, so we don't need to deal with different sizes.

Without this fix a specially craft header could have cause out-of-bounds
accesses on the byte array containing the swtpm's state.

Signed-off-by: Stefan Berger <stefanb@linux.ibm.com>
cve_desc: swtpm is a libtpms-based TPM emulator with socket, character device, and Linux CUSE interface. Versions prior to 0.5.3, 0.6.2, and 0.7.1 are vulnerable to out-of-bounds read. A specially crafted header of swtpm's state, where the blobheader's hdrsize indicator has an invalid value, may cause an out-of-bounds access when the byte array representing the state of the TPM is accessed. This will likely crash swtpm or prevent it from starting since the state cannot be understood. Users should upgrade to swtpm v0.5.3, v0.6.2, or v0.7.1 to receive a patch. There are currently no known workarounds.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Incorrect size check uses sizeof(pointer) instead of sizeof(struct), leading to potential out-of-bounds read
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `bh` is declared as `blobheader *bh`, so `sizeof(bh)` evaluates to the size of a pointer (4 or 8 bytes depending on architecture), not the size of the `blobheader` structure. The correct check should be `sizeof(*bh)` or `sizeof(blobheader)`. Because the check is too small, the function may proceed to access fields of the `blobheader` structure (totlen, min_version, version, hdrsize, flags) on a buffer that is shorter than the structure, resulting in an out-of-bounds read. An attacker supplying a crafted small blob could trigger information disclosure or a crash.

commit_message:
swtpm: Check header size indicator against expected size (CID 375869)

This fix addresses Coverity issue CID 375869.

Check the header size indicated in the header of the state against the
expected size and return an error code in case the header size indicator
is different. There was only one header size so far since blobheader was
introduced, so we don't need to deal with different sizes.

Without this fix a specially craft header could have cause out-of-bounds
accesses on the byte array containing the swtpm's state.

Signed-off-by: Stefan Berger <stefanb@linux.ibm.com>
cve_desc: swtpm is a libtpms-based TPM emulator with socket, character device, and Linux CUSE interface. Versions prior to 0.5.3, 0.6.2, and 0.7.1 are vulnerable to out-of-bounds read. A specially crafted header of swtpm's state, where the blobheader's hdrsize indicator has an invalid value, may cause an out-of-bounds access when the byte array representing the state of the TPM is accessed. This will likely crash swtpm or prevent it from starting since the state cannot be understood. Users should upgrade to swtpm v0.5.3, v0.6.2, or v0.7.1 to receive a patch. There are currently no known workarounds.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Insufficient length check uses sizeof(pointer) instead of sizeof(struct), enabling out-of-bounds read
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `bh` is declared as `blobheader *bh`, so `sizeof(bh)` evaluates to the size of a pointer (4 or 8 bytes depending on architecture), not the size of the `blobheader` structure. If the `blobheader` struct is larger than a pointer, the guard check passes for buffers that are too small, and subsequent accesses to fields of `bh` (e.g., `bh->totlen`, `bh->min_version`, `bh->hdrsize`, `bh->version`, `bh->flags`) will read beyond the allocated buffer. An attacker supplying a crafted short blob can trigger out-of-bounds reads, potentially leaking sensitive memory contents or causing a crash. The correct check should be `sizeof(*bh)` or `sizeof(blobheader)`.

commit_message: Clean up handling CAP LS
cve_desc: Directory traversal vulnerability in the client in HexChat 2.11.0 allows remote IRC servers to read or modify arbitrary files via a .. (dot dot) in the server name.

commit_message: Clean up handling CAP LS
cve_desc: Directory traversal vulnerability in the client in HexChat 2.11.0 allows remote IRC servers to read or modify arbitrary files via a .. (dot dot) in the server name.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Stack buffer overflow via repeated strcat into fixed-size buffer
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function uses a 256-byte stack buffer initialized with "CAP REQ :" and then repeatedly appends capability strings using strcat without any bounds checking. If the server advertises all recognized extensions, the total concatenated length could approach or exceed 256 bytes (the combined known strings plus separators total roughly 200+ bytes). More importantly, the extensions are parsed from server-controlled input, so a malicious server could send duplicated or crafted capability names that, while matching the strcmp checks, cause repeated concatenation. Since strcat performs no bounds checking, this can overflow the stack buffer, potentially allowing code execution.

commit_message: Fix #184: Heap overflow in S3M loader
cve_desc: XMFile::read in XMFile.cpp in milkyplay in MilkyTracker 1.02.00 has a heap-based buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of 'orders' array on early return paths
vulnerabilities.long_description: The 'orders' array is allocated before the checks for header->insnum and header->patnum. If either check fails and the function returns MP_LOADER_FAILED, the 'orders' array is never freed, causing a memory leak. A malformed S3M file could trigger this repeatedly, leading to resource exhaustion.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Heap buffer overflow when writing to header->ord[] without bounds checking
vulnerabilities.long_description: The loop copies order entries from the 'orders' array into 'header->ord[]' without checking whether 'j' exceeds the fixed size of the 'header->ord' array. Since 'header->ordnum' is read from the file as a 16-bit word (up to 65535), and the only termination condition is encountering a value of 255, a crafted S3M file with a large ordnum and no 0xFF sentinel values can cause an out-of-bounds write past the end of the 'header->ord' buffer, leading to heap corruption and potential code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential out-of-bounds write to pattern buffer when row overflows
vulnerabilities.long_description: Though there is a check for 'row >= 64', the check happens only when pi == 0 (end of row marker). If the packed data contains many non-zero bytes without a zero row separator, 'row' increments aren't happening but the code keeps processing. More critically, if the packed stream has corrupted data where row terminators appear exactly at row 63, but processing continues for one more iteration before hitting the break, the slot pointer calculation 'row*32*5 + chn*5' could reference memory beyond the 64*32*5 byte pattern buffer if a row terminator is missed or data is malformed.

commit_message: 0.16.1: fix for dcraw ljpeg_start() vulnerability
cve_desc: Integer overflow in the ljpeg_start function in dcraw 7.00 and earlier allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via a crafted image, which triggers a buffer overflow, related to the len variable.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Stack buffer overflow via negative or excessively large `len` value passed to fread
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `len` variable is computed as `(data[2] << 8 | data[3]) - 2`. If the two-byte length field in the JPEG marker is 0 or 1, `len` becomes -2 or -1. Since `fread`'s size parameter is `size_t` (unsigned), a negative `len` is implicitly converted to a very large value (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFE), causing a massive read attempt into the stack-allocated `data[0x10000]` buffer, resulting in a stack buffer overflow. An attacker can craft a malicious JPEG file to trigger this, potentially achieving arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read in 0xffda marker parsing when data[0] is large
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the `0xffda` case, `data[0]` (number of scan components) is attacker-controlled. The indices `1+data[0]*2` and `3+data[0]*2` can reach up to 513 (when data[0]=255). While this is within the 0x10000-byte `data` buffer, only `len` bytes were actually read from the file. If `len` is small (e.g., a few bytes), these accesses read uninitialized stack memory. This can leak sensitive stack data or cause unpredictable behavior.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in calloc size computation
vulnerabilities.long_description: Both `jh->wide` (up to 65535) and `jh->clrs` (derived from attacker-controlled data plus `jh->sraw`) are attacker-influenced. The multiplication `jh->wide*jh->clrs` is performed as an `int` multiplication and could overflow or produce a smaller-than-expected value before being passed to `calloc`. This could result in a heap buffer that is too small, leading to subsequent heap buffer overflows when the row data is written.

commit_message:
ALSA: pcm: oss: Fix race at SNDCTL_DSP_SYNC

There is a small race window at snd_pcm_oss_sync() that is called from
OSS PCM SNDCTL_DSP_SYNC ioctl; namely the function calls
snd_pcm_oss_make_ready() at first, then takes the params_lock mutex
for the rest.  When the stream is set up again by another thread
between them, it leads to inconsistency, and may result in unexpected
results such as NULL dereference of OSS buffer as a fuzzer spotted
recently.

The fix is simply to cover snd_pcm_oss_make_ready() call into the same
params_lock mutex with snd_pcm_oss_make_ready_locked() variant.

Reported-and-tested-by: butt3rflyh4ck <butterflyhuangxx@gmail.com>
Reviewed-by: Jaroslav Kysela <perex@perex.cz>
Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/CAFcO6XN7JDM4xSXGhtusQfS2mSBcx50VJKwQpCq=WeLt57aaZA@mail.gmail.com
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20220905060714.22549-1-tiwai@suse.de
Signed-off-by: Takashi Iwai <tiwai@suse.de>
cve_desc: A race condition flaw was found in the Linux kernel sound subsystem due to improper locking. It could lead to a NULL pointer dereference while handling the SNDCTL_DSP_SYNC ioctl. A privileged local user (root or member of the audio group) could use this flaw to crash the system, resulting in a denial of service condition

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential division by zero when computing silence padding size from format width
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `width` is obtained from `snd_pcm_format_physical_width(format)`. If `format` is invalid or unrecognized (e.g., `snd_pcm_oss_format_from` returns an unexpected value), `snd_pcm_format_physical_width` can return 0 or a negative value. Since `width` is declared as `unsigned long`, a negative return value wraps to a large number, but a zero return causes a division-by-zero crash (kernel oops/panic). This is used in two places: `(8 * (...) + 7) / width` and `size * 8 / width`. An attacker who can influence the OSS format parameter (e.g., via ioctl from userspace) could trigger this to cause a denial of service.

commit_message:
Merge pull request from GHSA-48w2-rm65-62xx

* Fix HTTP request smuggling vulnerability

See GHSA-48w2-rm65-62xx or CVE-2021-41136 for more info.

* 4.3.9 release note

* 5.5.1 release note

* 5.5.1
cve_desc: Puma is a HTTP 1.1 server for Ruby/Rack applications. Prior to versions 5.5.1 and 4.3.9, using `puma` with a proxy which forwards HTTP header values which contain the LF character could allow HTTP request smugggling. A client could smuggle a request through a proxy, causing the proxy to send a response back to another unknown client. The only proxy which has this behavior, as far as the Puma team is aware of, is Apache Traffic Server. If the proxy uses persistent connections and the client adds another request in via HTTP pipelining, the proxy may mistake it as the first request's body. Puma, however, would see it as two requests, and when processing the second request, send back a response that the proxy does not expect. If the proxy has reused the persistent connection to Puma to send another request for a different client, the second response from the first client will be sent to the second client. This vulnerability was patched in Puma 5.5.1 and 4.3.9. As a workaround, do not use Apache Traffic Server with `puma`.

code_snippets: size_t puma_parser_execute(puma_parser *parser, const char *buffer, size_t len, size_t off)
vulnerabilities.short_description: Const-qualified input buffer is modified in-place by casting away const
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function signature declares `buffer` as `const char *`, establishing a contract that the buffer will not be modified. However, in the header field parsing states (tr21 and tr23), the code calls `snake_upcase_char((char *)p)` which casts away the const qualifier and writes to the buffer. If the caller passes a read-only memory region (e.g., a string literal, mmap'd read-only page, or a buffer from a language runtime that assumes immutability), this will cause undefined behavior, a segfault/crash, or silent corruption of shared data. An attacker who can send HTTP requests with header fields could trigger this write to read-only memory, causing a denial of service.

commit_message: Merge pull request from GHSA-7fw8-54cv-r7pm
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C language implementing standard based protocols such as SIP, SDP, RTP, STUN, TURN, and ICE. In versions 2.11.1 and prior, parsing an incoming SIP message that contains a malformed multipart can potentially cause out-of-bound read access. This issue affects all PJSIP users that accept SIP multipart. The patch is available as commit in the `master` branch. There are no known workarounds.
