commit_message:
Merge pull request from GHSA-48w2-rm65-62xx

* Fix HTTP request smuggling vulnerability

See GHSA-48w2-rm65-62xx or CVE-2021-41136 for more info.

* 4.3.9 release note

* 5.5.1 release note

* 5.5.1
cve_desc: Puma is a HTTP 1.1 server for Ruby/Rack applications. Prior to versions 5.5.1 and 4.3.9, using `puma` with a proxy which forwards HTTP header values which contain the LF character could allow HTTP request smugggling. A client could smuggle a request through a proxy, causing the proxy to send a response back to another unknown client. The only proxy which has this behavior, as far as the Puma team is aware of, is Apache Traffic Server. If the proxy uses persistent connections and the client adds another request in via HTTP pipelining, the proxy may mistake it as the first request's body. Puma, however, would see it as two requests, and when processing the second request, send back a response that the proxy does not expect. If the proxy has reused the persistent connection to Puma to send another request for a different client, the second response from the first client will be sent to the second client. This vulnerability was patched in Puma 5.5.1 and 4.3.9. As a workaround, do not use Apache Traffic Server with `puma`.

code_snippets: size_t puma_parser_execute(puma_parser *parser, const char *buffer, size_t len, size_t off)
vulnerabilities.short_description: Const-cast write to supposedly read-only input buffer can corrupt caller data or crash on read-only memory
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function accepts `buffer` as `const char *`, implying it will not modify the data. However, the Ragel-generated code casts away const via `(char *)p` and calls `snake_upcase_char` to modify header field name characters in-place. If the caller passes a buffer that resides in read-only memory (e.g., a string literal, mmap'd read-only page, or a frozen/immutable string from a managed runtime), this write will cause a segmentation fault or undefined behavior. Even when the memory is writable, the caller does not expect modification of its buffer, which can lead to data corruption in shared buffers, incorrect caching, or security bypasses if the original buffer is reused elsewhere with the assumption it is unmodified.

commit_message: 0.16.1: fix for dcraw ljpeg_start() vulnerability
cve_desc: Integer overflow in the ljpeg_start function in dcraw 7.00 and earlier allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via a crafted image, which triggers a buffer overflow, related to the len variable.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer underflow in JPEG marker length calculation leads to oversized read
vulnerabilities.long_description: The length field `len` is computed as `(data[2] << 8 | data[3]) - 2` using unsigned 16-bit arithmetic. If the marker length value is 0 or 1, the subtraction underflows, wrapping `len` to a very large value (e.g., 0xFFFE or 0xFFFF). This causes `fread` to attempt to read up to 65534/65535 bytes. While the `data` buffer is 0x10000 bytes, subsequent parsing (e.g., in the 0xffc4 case) uses `dp < data+len` as a loop bound, potentially processing uninitialized stack memory beyond what was actually read by `fread`.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read via attacker-controlled index in 0xffda marker parsing
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the `0xffda` case, array indices `1+data[0]*2` and `3+data[0]*2` are computed using the attacker-controlled value `data[0]`. Since `data[0]` can be 0-255, the index can reach up to 513 (3+255*2). While this is within the `data` buffer size, there is no check that `len` (the actual number of bytes read from the file) is large enough. If `len` is small, these accesses read data that was not populated by the current marker's `fread`, potentially using stale or uninitialized stack data to set critical decoding parameters `jh->psv` and `jh->bits`.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in calloc size calculation leads to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: Both `jh->wide` and `jh->clrs` are derived from attacker-controlled JPEG header data. The expression `jh->wide*jh->clrs` can overflow if both values are large, resulting in a much smaller allocation than expected. Subsequent code that writes `jh->wide * jh->clrs` elements into `jh->row` would then write far beyond the allocated heap buffer, leading to heap corruption and potential arbitrary code execution. The check `jh->clrs > 6` limits `clrs` but `wide` can be up to 65535, and even with small `clrs` values, overflow is possible depending on the platform's integer width and how the result is passed to `calloc`.

commit_message: Fix wrong boundary checks in inflections parser resulting in stack buffer over-read with corrupt input
cve_desc: Buffer Over-read in GitHub repository bfabiszewski/libmobi prior to 0.11. This vulnerability is capable of arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Off-by-one out-of-bounds read in the delete branch memmove
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the delete ('d') branch, the length `l` is computed as `*decoded_size - pos`, but the source pointer is `decoded + pos + 1`. This means memmove reads from `decoded[pos+1]` through `decoded[pos + *decoded_size - pos]` = `decoded[*decoded_size]`, which is one byte past the end of valid data. The correct length should be `*decoded_size - pos - 1`. The bounds check only verifies against `INDX_INFLBUF_SIZEMAX`, not against the actual data size, so this off-by-one read is not caught. An attacker who controls the rule input can trigger reads of uninitialized or stale data beyond valid content.

code_snippets: debug_print("Out of buffer in %s at pos: %i\n", decoded, pos);
debug_print("Character mismatch in %s at pos: %i (%c != %c)\n", decoded, pos, decoded[pos], c);
vulnerabilities.short_description: Potentially non-null-terminated buffer passed to printf-style format string as %s
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `decoded` buffer is a raw `unsigned char*` managed by size tracking (`*decoded_size`) and is not guaranteed to be null-terminated. When error conditions are triggered, `decoded` is passed to `debug_print` with `%s` format specifier, which will read until a null byte is found. This can cause an out-of-bounds read past the allocated buffer, potentially leaking sensitive memory contents or causing a crash. A crafted rule input that triggers these error paths can exploit this.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential negative index used in buffer access before adequate validation in edge cases
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `pos` can be decremented by up to 9 via `pos -= c - 10` (when c is 11-19), and further decremented by `pos--` in the delete-left branch. While bounds checks exist before memmove calls (`pos < 0`), in the delete branch `decoded[pos]` is accessed for the character mismatch comparison *after* the bounds check verifies `pos >= 0`, but `pos` could have been decremented to exactly 0 and then the `dir == '<'` decrement makes it -1. However, the `pos < 0` check does cover this. More critically, if `*decoded_size` is manipulated such that `l` computes to 0 and passes the check while `pos` is at a boundary, the subsequent memmove could operate on incorrect memory regions. Combined with the off-by-one in length calculation, crafted rules could cause writes outside intended buffer boundaries.

commit_message:
Fix null deref in xnu.kernelcache ##crash

* Reported by @xshad3 via huntr.dev
cve_desc: NULL Pointer Dereference in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.0.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Null pointer dereference of `obj` in the `beach` error handler
vulnerabilities.long_description: When the `goto beach` is reached from the first check (`if (!prelink_range)`), `obj` is still NULL (it hasn't been allocated yet). The `beach` label then dereferences `obj` via `obj->cache_buf = NULL`, causing a null pointer dereference. This leads to a crash (denial of service) when processing a malformed or crafted binary file.

commit_message: Fix #184: Heap overflow in S3M loader
cve_desc: XMFile::read in XMFile.cpp in milkyplay in MilkyTracker 1.02.00 has a heap-based buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Buffer overflow writing to fixed-size order list array
vulnerabilities.long_description: header->ordnum is read directly from the file (up to 65535 as a 16-bit word), and orders are copied into header->ord[j++] without checking if j exceeds the fixed-size ord[] array (typically 256 entries in XM/S3M module structures). A malicious file with a large ordnum value can cause a heap or stack buffer overflow, leading to arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds access on instr[] and smp[] arrays via unchecked insnum
vulnerabilities.long_description: header->insnum is read from the file without validation against the allocated sizes of module->instr and module->smp arrays. The loop iterates up to header->insnum, accessing instr[i] and smp[s] without bounds checking. A crafted file with a large insnum value will cause out-of-bounds reads and writes on these arrays, potentially leading to heap corruption and code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds access on phead[] array via unchecked patnum
vulnerabilities.long_description: header->patnum is read from the file without validation against the allocated size of module->phead. The pattern loading loop iterates up to header->patnum and accesses phead[i], which can go out of bounds if patnum exceeds the pre-allocated array size, leading to heap corruption.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in sample pointer offset calculation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The sample offset is computed as (((mp_uint32)bOffs<<16)+(mp_uint32)wOffs)*16. Since bOffs can be up to 255 and wOffs up to 65535, the intermediate sum can be up to 16,776,960. Multiplying by 16 yields up to 268,431,360, which fits in 32 bits. However, if the file provides extreme values, the multiplication by 16 on a 32-bit unsigned could wrap in edge cases, causing seeks to unexpected file positions and reading arbitrary data.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of orders array on early return paths
vulnerabilities.long_description: The 'orders' array is allocated early, then deleted after use. However, between allocation and deletion, if the f.readByte() != 16 check had failed earlier (before orders allocation), that's fine. But there's also a concern that if header->ordnum is 0, the allocation of a zero-size array and subsequent operations may have undefined behavior. More critically, the insParaPtrs allocation failure path returns without cleaning up previously allocated resources if the control flow is modified.

commit_message:
libnsgif: Fix null pointer deref on frameless GIF input

A crafted GIF file with no frame data could cause a null pointer
dereference leading to denial of service (crash). Reported by
@JieyongMa via huntr.dev.
cve_desc: chafa: NULL Pointer Dereference in function gif_internal_decode_frame at libnsgif.c:599 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via a crafted input file. in GitHub repository hpjansson/chafa prior to 1.10.2. chafa: NULL Pointer Dereference in function gif_internal_decode_frame at libnsgif.c:599 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via a crafted input file.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read on colour_table using unchecked LZW-decoded colour index
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable 'colour' is an unsigned char (0–255) obtained from the LZW decompression stream, but 'colour_table_size' can be as small as 2 (when the Size of Local Colour Table field is 0). There is no check that 'colour < colour_table_size' before indexing into 'colour_table[colour]'. A crafted GIF can produce LZW output bytes that exceed the colour table size, causing an out-of-bounds heap read. This can leak sensitive memory contents or cause a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read on colour_table using unchecked background_index
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the clear_image branch, 'gif->background_index' is used to index into 'colour_table' without verifying it is within 'colour_table_size'. A crafted GIF with a background_index larger than the colour table allows an out-of-bounds heap read, which could leak memory or crash the process.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Uncontrolled recursion via frame disposal methods can cause stack overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function recursively calls itself to handle GIF_FRAME_CLEAR and GIF_FRAME_RESTORE disposal methods. A crafted GIF with many frames using RESTORE disposal can cause deep recursion (the inner call with clear_image=false can itself recurse again), potentially exhausting the stack and crashing the application. There is no recursion depth limit.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential integer overflow in memset size calculation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expression 'gif->width * gif->height * sizeof(int)' is computed using unsigned integer arithmetic. If gif->width and gif->height are large (they are parsed from the GIF header as 16-bit values, but multiplied together with sizeof(int)), the result could wrap around, leading to an undersized memset that leaves parts of the buffer uninitialized, or conversely a memset that writes beyond the allocated buffer.

commit_message:
Fix oobread and unaligned casts in the NE entrypoint logic ##crash

* Reported by @hmsec via huntr.dev
* Reproducer: nepocaligns
* BountyID: ec538fa4-06c6-4050-a141-f60153ddeaac
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in r_bin_ne_get_entrypoints function in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.8. This vulnerability may allow attackers to read sensitive information or cause a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read on entry_table in the moveable bundle branch
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the moveable bundle type (0xff) branch, the code increments `off` by 2 and then reads from `bin->entry_table + off` to get `segnum`, increments again, and reads a ut16. The bounds check at the top of the for-loop (`tableat + off + 4 >= r_buf_size(bin->buf)`) does not accurately account for the number of bytes consumed inside this branch relative to the actual allocation size of `bin->entry_table`. The post-read check (`off > EntryTableLength`) occurs only after the read of `segnum`, meaning the read itself can be out-of-bounds. A crafted NE binary with a small EntryTableLength but large bundle_length can trigger reads past the end of the entry_table buffer.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of `segments` list on early return when entry allocation fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `R_NEW0(RBinAddr)` fails inside the inner for-loop, the code frees `entries` and returns NULL, but `segments` (allocated via `r_bin_ne_get_segments`) is never freed, causing a memory leak. This can be triggered under memory pressure conditions.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of allocated `entry` when breaking out of moveable or fixed bundle processing
vulnerabilities.long_description: In both the moveable (0xff) and fixed bundle type branches, an `entry` object is allocated with `R_NEW0` at the start of each loop iteration. If the subsequent bounds checks fail and a `break` is executed, the already-allocated `entry` is not freed, resulting in a memory leak. A malformed NE binary can trigger these break conditions repeatedly, leaking memory each time the function is called.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential out-of-bounds read of entry_table when reading bundle_length and bundle_type
vulnerabilities.long_description: The outer while-loop checks `tableat + off >= r_buf_size(bin->buf)` but this validates against the total file buffer size, not the actual allocated size of `bin->entry_table`. If entry_table was allocated with a size based on `EntryTableLength`, but `r_buf_size` is larger, the check is insufficient. Additionally, after reading `bundle_length`, `off` is incremented and `bundle_type` is read without re-checking bounds, potentially reading one byte past the entry_table buffer when `off` equals `EntryTableLength - 1` at the start of an iteration.

commit_message:
libnsgif: Fix null pointer deref on frameless GIF input

A crafted GIF file with no frame data could cause a null pointer
dereference leading to denial of service (crash). Reported by
@JieyongMa via huntr.dev.
cve_desc: chafa: NULL Pointer Dereference in function gif_internal_decode_frame at libnsgif.c:599 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via a crafted input file. in GitHub repository hpjansson/chafa prior to 1.10.2. chafa: NULL Pointer Dereference in function gif_internal_decode_frame at libnsgif.c:599 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via a crafted input file.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read on colour_table via unchecked LZW-decoded colour index
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `colour` is an unsigned char (0–255) obtained from LZW-decoded data. It is used directly as an index into `colour_table`, whose size (`colour_table_size`) can be as small as 2 entries (when the local/global colour table size field is 0, yielding `2 << 0 = 2`). A crafted GIF can produce colour indices larger than the colour table, causing an out-of-bounds read. This can leak heap memory or cause a crash. The same issue exists in the clear_image path with `colour_table[gif->background_index]`.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Off-by-one error in frame bounds check allows access one past valid frames
vulnerabilities.long_description: The check uses `>` instead of `>=`. If `frame_count_partial` represents the count of frames (0-indexed access), then `frame == gif->frame_count_partial` would be one past the last valid index but is still permitted. This can lead to out-of-bounds access on `gif->frames[frame]` and related data structures.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read on colour_table using unchecked background_index
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the clear_image path, `gif->background_index` is used to index into `colour_table` without verifying it is within `colour_table_size`. A crafted GIF with a background index larger than the colour table size causes an out-of-bounds read, potentially leaking memory or crashing.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Early return without restoring gif->buffer_position
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `bitmap_get_buffer` returns NULL, the function returns immediately without going through `gif_decode_frame_exit`, so `gif->buffer_position` is never restored to `save_buffer_position`. This corrupts the GIF decoder state. The same issue occurs at the second `return GIF_INSUFFICIENT_MEMORY` inside the FRAME_RESTORE handling. Subsequent operations on the gif object will use a wrong buffer position, potentially leading to out-of-bounds reads.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4359: crash when repeatedly using :retab

Problem:    crash when repeatedly using :retab.
Solution:   Bail out when the line is getting too long.
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

code_snippets:
old_len = (long)STRLEN(ptr);
new_line = alloc(old_len - col + start_col + len + 1);
vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in buffer size calculation can lead to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The allocation size `old_len - col + start_col + len + 1` is computed using `long` arithmetic where `len = num_spaces + num_tabs`. While `vcol` is checked against `MAXCOL`, the individual components (`old_len`, `col`, `start_col`, `len`) are all `long` values and their combination can overflow, especially on systems where `long` is 32-bit. If the arithmetic overflows and wraps to a small positive value (or a negative value that is then cast to a large `size_t`), `alloc()` will allocate an incorrectly sized buffer. The subsequent `mch_memmove` calls that copy `start_col` bytes and `old_len - col + 1` bytes into this undersized buffer would then cause a heap buffer overflow, potentially enabling arbitrary code execution.

commit_message:
Fix oobread and unaligned casts in the NE entrypoint logic ##crash

* Reported by @hmsec via huntr.dev
* Reproducer: nepocaligns
* BountyID: ec538fa4-06c6-4050-a141-f60153ddeaac
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in r_bin_ne_get_entrypoints function in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.8. This vulnerability may allow attackers to read sensitive information or cause a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read on segment_entries array due to missing upper bounds check on segnum
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the moveable bundle case (bundle_type == 0xff), `segnum` is read from attacker-controlled data in the entry table. The code only checks `segnum > 0` before indexing into `bin->segment_entries[segnum - 1]`, but never verifies that `segnum` is within the valid range (i.e., `segnum <= bin->ne_header->SegCount`). A crafted NE binary with a large segnum value would cause an out-of-bounds heap read, potentially leaking sensitive data or causing a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read on entry_table due to insufficient bounds checking during inner loop increments
vulnerabilities.long_description: The bounds check at the top of the for loop (`tableat + off + 4 >= r_buf_size(bin->buf)`) validates against the raw buffer size, but the actual accesses are performed on `bin->entry_table` which is a separate buffer of size `EntryTableLength`. Inside the loop body, `off` is incremented multiple times (up to 6 bytes in the moveable case) without re-checking that `off` remains within `EntryTableLength`. This means the code can read past the end of the `entry_table` allocation, leading to a heap buffer over-read.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read when bundle_type is 0 in the fixed segment branch (off-by-one via unsigned underflow)
vulnerabilities.long_description: Although the bundle_type == 0 case is handled with a break earlier, the condition `bundle_type < bin->ne_header->SegCount` allows bundle_type values that could be problematic. More critically, when bundle_type equals SegCount (which passes the `!bundle_type` check), the fixed branch is reached but the condition `bundle_type < SegCount` is false, so `entry` is appended with paddr=0 — this is benign. However, `*(ut16 *)(bin->entry_table + off)` is still an unaligned read from a potentially out-of-bounds offset with no independent size check on the entry_table buffer.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of segments list on allocation failure
vulnerabilities.long_description: When the first `R_NEW0(RBinAddr)` allocation (for csEntryPoint) fails, the code frees `entries` and returns NULL, but the `segments` list that was allocated by `r_bin_ne_get_segments` is never freed, causing a memory leak. The same leak occurs for the R_NEW0 failure inside the for loop.

commit_message:
HID: elo: fix memory leak in elo_probe

When hid_parse() in elo_probe() fails, it forgets to call usb_put_dev to
decrease the refcount.

Fix this by adding usb_put_dev() in the error handling code of elo_probe().

Fixes: fbf42729d0e9 ("HID: elo: update the reference count of the usb device structure")
Reported-by: syzkaller <syzkaller@googlegroups.com>
Signed-off-by: Dongliang Mu <mudongliangabcd@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Jiri Kosina <jkosina@suse.cz>
cve_desc: In drivers/hid/hid-elo.c in the Linux kernel before 5.16.11, a memory leak exists for a certain hid_parse error condition.

commit_message:
HID: elo: fix memory leak in elo_probe

When hid_parse() in elo_probe() fails, it forgets to call usb_put_dev to
decrease the refcount.

Fix this by adding usb_put_dev() in the error handling code of elo_probe().

Fixes: fbf42729d0e9 ("HID: elo: update the reference count of the usb device structure")
Reported-by: syzkaller <syzkaller@googlegroups.com>
Signed-off-by: Dongliang Mu <mudongliangabcd@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Jiri Kosina <jkosina@suse.cz>
cve_desc: In drivers/hid/hid-elo.c in the Linux kernel before 5.16.11, a memory leak exists for a certain hid_parse error condition.

vulnerabilities.short_description: USB device reference leak on error path
vulnerabilities.long_description: When hid_parse() or hid_hw_start() fails, the error path jumps to err_free which only calls kfree(priv) but does not call usb_put_dev(priv->usbdev) to release the reference obtained by usb_get_dev(). This leads to a reference count leak on the USB device, preventing it from ever being freed, which constitutes a resource leak (memory/device leak) that could be triggered repeatedly to exhaust kernel memory.

commit_message:
Fix null deref in xnu.kernelcache ##crash

* Reported by @xshad3 via huntr.dev
cve_desc: NULL Pointer Dereference in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.0.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of rebase_info on all error paths that jump to beach
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `rebase_info` object is allocated via `r_rebase_info_new_from_mach0` early in the function, but the `beach` error-handling label never frees it. Every `goto beach` after that allocation (e.g., when `prelink_range` is NULL, `obj` allocation fails, `prelink_info` parsing fails, or `pending_bin_files` allocation fails) will leak the `rebase_info` allocation. Repeated triggering of these error paths (e.g., by supplying malformed input) could lead to significant memory exhaustion.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Complex parsed dictionary prelink_info freed with R_FREE instead of its proper destructor
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the error path where `pending_bin_files` allocation fails, `prelink_info` (a `RCFValueDict*` returned by `r_cf_value_dict_parse`) is freed using `R_FREE`, which is essentially a plain `free()`. This only frees the top-level structure and leaks all internally-allocated members (strings, arrays, nested dicts, etc.). The correct destructor (e.g., `r_cf_value_dict_free`) should be used to avoid leaking the entire parsed property-list tree.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4253: using freed memory when substitute with function call

Problem:    Using freed memory when substitute uses a recursive function call.
Solution:   Make a copy of the substitute text.
cve_desc: Use After Free in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in substitution buffer size calculation can lead to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `needed_len` is declared as `int` while `new_start_len` is `unsigned int`. The calculation of `needed_len` involves adding multiple values (copy_len, string lengths, sublen) that could overflow a signed 32-bit integer, wrapping to a negative value. When the check `needed_len > (int)new_start_len` is performed, a negative `needed_len` will always be less than `new_start_len`, causing the reallocation to be skipped. Subsequent writes via `mch_memmove` and `vim_regsub_multi` into the undersized `new_start` buffer would then cause a heap buffer overflow. An attacker could craft a substitute command with a very long replacement string or operate on very large lines to trigger this overflow, potentially achieving arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Addition of constant 50 to needed_len can overflow unsigned, leading to undersized allocation
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `needed_len` is close to `UINT_MAX` (after being assigned to the unsigned `new_start_len`), adding 50 can cause an unsigned integer wrap-around, resulting in a very small allocation. The subsequent `mch_memmove` and string operations would then write far beyond the allocated buffer. This is a secondary overflow point related to the same buffer management logic, compounding the risk of heap corruption and potential remote code execution through a crafted substitute command.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4359: crash when repeatedly using :retab

Problem:    crash when repeatedly using :retab.
Solution:   Bail out when the line is getting too long.
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in allocation size can lead to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The allocation size `old_len - col + start_col + len + 1` is computed using `long` arithmetic. When `expandtab` is set, `len` equals `vcol - start_vcol` (the virtual column width of the whitespace), which can be significantly larger than the original byte count (`col - start_col`) due to tab expansion. If the resulting sum overflows a `long` (or wraps when cast to `size_t` for the allocator), a too-small buffer is allocated. The subsequent `mch_memmove` calls then write beyond the allocated buffer, leading to a heap buffer overflow. An attacker who can control the content of a file (e.g., a file with extremely long lines containing many tabs) and trigger `:retab` could exploit this to achieve arbitrary code execution.

commit_message:
Bug 701819: fixed ordering in if expression to avoid out-of-bounds access.

Fixes:
    ./sanbin/gs -dBATCH -dNOPAUSE -r965 -sOutputFile=tmp -sDEVICE=pcx16 ../bug-701819.pdf
cve_desc: A buffer overflow vulnerability in pcx_write_rle() in contrib/japanese/gdev10v.c of Artifex Software GhostScript v9.50 allows a remote attacker to cause a denial of service via a crafted PDF file. This is fixed in v9.51.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4253: using freed memory when substitute with function call

Problem:    Using freed memory when substitute uses a recursive function call.
Solution:   Make a copy of the substitute text.
cve_desc: Use After Free in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in substitution buffer size calculation leads to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variables `needed_len`, `len`, and `copy_len` are declared as `int`, but they are computed from `unsigned` values (via STRLEN casts) and `sublen`. On lines with very large content or after many substitution iterations, these values can overflow a signed `int`, causing `needed_len` to become negative or a small positive value. When `needed_len` is compared against `new_start_len` (an `unsigned int`) via `(int)new_start_len`, a negative `needed_len` will appear smaller, so no reallocation occurs. Similarly, `new_start_len = needed_len + 50` could wrap around to a small value, allocating a tiny buffer. Subsequent `mch_memmove` and `vim_regsub_multi` calls then write beyond the allocated buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow. An attacker could craft a file and substitute command to trigger this, potentially achieving arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in trailing count arithmetic for line range
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `getdigits` function parses a potentially very large number into a `long`. The expression `eap->line2 += i - 1` can overflow the `linenr_T` type (typically a `long` or `int`), wrapping to a negative value. Since the subsequent bounds check `eap->line2 > curbuf->b_ml.ml_line_count` only checks the upper bound, a wrapped negative value would pass this check but could cause out-of-bounds access when used as a line number in the substitution loop.

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/issues/1221
cve_desc: The ReadMATImageV4 function in coders/mat.c in ImageMagick 7.0.8-7 uses an uninitialized variable, leading to memory corruption.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in ReadBlob size calculation can lead to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expression `depth/8*image->columns` can overflow when `image->columns` (set from `HDR.nRows`, an unsigned int up to ~4 billion) is large. For example, with depth=64, depth/8=8, and a large `image->columns`, the multiplication can wrap around, causing ReadBlob to be called with a smaller size than expected. However, the pixel buffer from GetQuantumPixels was allocated based on the actual image dimensions. This mismatch could lead to reading into a smaller-than-expected allocation, or on a subsequent ImportQuantumPixels call, interpreting uninitialized/wrong data. On 32-bit systems this is especially dangerous as size_t is 32 bits.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in SeekBlob offset calculation in the ping code path
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the ping (metadata-only) path, `HDR.nCols*ldblk` is computed where HDR.nCols is an unsigned int read from the file and ldblk is a long derived from the type field. This multiplication can overflow, causing the seek to advance by an incorrect amount. This could cause the parser to read subsequent objects from wrong offsets, potentially interpreting arbitrary file data as image metadata, leading to further memory corruption or information disclosure.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Return values of quantum setup functions are overwritten without checking, allowing use of misconfigured quantum_info
vulnerabilities.long_description: The return status of SetQuantumFormat, SetQuantumDepth, and SetQuantumEndian are each assigned to `status` but never checked before proceeding. If any of these calls fail, the quantum_info object may be in an invalid or partially initialized state. Subsequent use of this misconfigured quantum_info in GetQuantumPixels and ImportQuantumPixels could lead to out-of-bounds memory access or other undefined behavior.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Complex imaginary data read uses pixel buffer potentially too small for double/float array interpretation
vulnerabilities.long_description: When HDR.imagf==1, the code reads complex data into the `pixels` buffer and casts it to `(double *)` or `(float *)`. The pixels buffer was allocated by AcquireQuantumInfo for single-channel gray quantum data. If the buffer size is insufficient for the full row interpreted as doubles (8 bytes each) or floats (4 bytes each) times the number of columns, InsertComplexDoubleRow/InsertComplexFloatRow will read beyond the buffer boundary, causing a heap-based buffer over-read.

commit_message:
Check configure file permissions even for non-default files if still privileged

(Bug 1044, CVE-2010-4345)
cve_desc: Exim 4.72 and earlier allows local users to gain privileges by leveraging the ability of the exim user account to specify an alternate configuration file with a directive that contains arbitrary commands, as demonstrated by the spool_directory directive.

code_snippets:
uschar *s = expand_string(host_number_string);
  long int n = Ustrtol(s, &end, 0);
vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference when expand_string fails for host_number_string
vulnerabilities.long_description: When host_number_string is not NULL, expand_string() is called on it. If expand_string() returns NULL (e.g., due to an expansion failure), the result is passed directly to Ustrtol() without a NULL check, leading to a NULL pointer dereference. Other expand_string() calls in the function properly check for NULL returns, but this one does not.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential buffer overflow when appending node name and euid to filename in big_buffer
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable 'suffix' points to the end of 'filename' within 'big_buffer'. The sprintf calls append up to ~280 additional characters (a long integer, a dot, and up to 256 characters of nodename). If the original filename is already close to big_buffer_size, this write will overflow big_buffer. The code does not check whether there is sufficient remaining space in big_buffer after the filename before appending the suffix via sprintf.

commit_message:
Check configure file permissions even for non-default files if still privileged

(Bug 1044, CVE-2010-4345)
cve_desc: Exim 4.72 and earlier allows local users to gain privileges by leveraging the ability of the exim user account to specify an alternate configuration file with a directive that contains arbitrary commands, as demonstrated by the spool_directory directive.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Buffer overflow when appending suffixes to filename in big_buffer
vulnerabilities.long_description: When CONFIGURE_FILE_USE_NODE or CONFIGURE_FILE_USE_EUID is defined, the code sets 'suffix' to point to the end of the filename string within 'big_buffer', then uses sprintf to append node name and/or euid suffixes. If the original filename is already close to big_buffer_size, these appended strings (up to ~256 bytes for the nodename plus the euid digits) can write past the end of big_buffer, causing a heap or stack buffer overflow. An attacker who controls config_main_filelist could craft a long path to trigger this overflow.

code_snippets:
uschar *s = expand_string(host_number_string);
  long int n = Ustrtol(s, &end, 0);
vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference if expand_string fails for host_number_string
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the host_number_string processing block, expand_string() is called and its return value is stored in 's'. If expand_string() fails and returns NULL, the code proceeds to call Ustrtol(s, &end, 0) with a NULL pointer without any NULL check, leading to a NULL pointer dereference and crash. Other similar expand_string calls in this function properly check for NULL before using the result.

commit_message:
Fix possible stack corruption with XM instrument headers claiming a size of less than 4

Closes #275
cve_desc: MilkyTracker v1.03.00 was discovered to contain a stack overflow via the component LoaderXM::load. This vulnerability is triggered when the program is supplied a crafted XM module file.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read in pattern data buffer due to missing bounds check on 'pc' index
vulnerabilities.long_description: When parsing pattern data, the variable 'pc' is used to index into 'buffer' (allocated with size phead[y].patdata) but is never validated against the buffer size before each access. A maliciously crafted XM file can provide pattern data that causes 'pc' to exceed the allocated buffer, leading to heap buffer over-read. This could leak sensitive memory contents or cause a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds array access via unvalidated 'nbu' values used as index into 'smpReloc'
vulnerabilities.long_description: The 'nbu' array contains byte values (0-255) read from file data, and these values are used as indices into 'smpReloc' which has MP_MAXINSSAMPS entries (typically 96 for XM format). Since nbu values can be up to 255 but smpReloc only has MP_MAXINSSAMPS slots, a crafted XM file can cause out-of-bounds reads on the stack-allocated smpReloc array. This can lead to information disclosure or potentially be leveraged for code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer underflow when instr[y].size < 4 causes massive read into stack buffer
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the version 0x104 instrument loading path, when instr[y].size < 29, the code computes 'instr[y].size - 4' as a read length. Since instr[y].size is an unsigned 32-bit integer, if its value is less than 4 (e.g., 0, 1, 2, or 3), the subtraction wraps around to a very large value, causing f.read to attempt to read a huge amount of data into a 29-byte stack buffer. This results in a stack buffer overflow that could enable arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds write to smp array: bounds check on 's' happens after writes, not before
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the sample loading loops, the code writes to smp[g+s] (setting flags, envelope numbers, reading sample data from file, etc.) before checking whether 's' has exceeded MP_MAXSAMPLES. The bounds check 'if (s>=MP_MAXSAMPLES)' only occurs after incrementing s and after all writes have been performed. A crafted file with many instruments and samples can cause g+s to exceed the smp array bounds, leading to heap buffer overflow with attacker-controlled data, potentially enabling arbitrary code execution.

commit_message:

In fastrpc_dma_buf_attach if dma_get_sgtable fails the allocated memory
for a should be released.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of 'a' when dma_get_sgtable fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: When dma_get_sgtable() fails, the function returns -EINVAL without freeing the previously allocated 'a' structure. This leads to a kernel memory leak. An attacker who can trigger repeated failures of dma_get_sgtable could exhaust kernel memory.

commit_message:
FSCTL_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA: Don't return 4 extra bytes at end

labels_data_count already accounts for the unicode null character at the
end of the array. There is no need in adding space for it again.

Signed-off-by: Christof Schmitt <christof.schmitt@us.ibm.com>
Reviewed-by: Jeremy Allison <jra@samba.org>
Reviewed-by: Simo Sorce <idra@samba.org>

Autobuild-User(master): Jeremy Allison <jra@samba.org>
Autobuild-Date(master): Tue Aug  6 04:03:17 CEST 2013 on sn-devel-104
cve_desc: Samba 3.6.6 through 3.6.23, 4.0.x before 4.0.18, and 4.1.x before 4.1.8, when a certain vfs shadow copy configuration is enabled, does not properly initialize the SRV_SNAPSHOT_ARRAY response field, which allows remote authenticated users to obtain potentially sensitive information from process memory via a (1) FSCTL_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA or (2) FSCTL_SRV_ENUMERATE_SNAPSHOTS request.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in FSCTL_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA leads to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the FSCTL_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA handler, `labels_data_count` is computed as `(shadow_data->num_volumes * 2 * sizeof(SHADOW_COPY_LABEL)) + 2` using uint32 arithmetic. If `shadow_data->num_volumes` (returned from VFS) is sufficiently large, this multiplication wraps around, producing a small `labels_data_count` and consequently a small `*out_len`. The subsequent `talloc_zero_array` allocates a too-small buffer. The loop then writes `num_volumes * 2 * sizeof(SHADOW_COPY_LABEL)` bytes into this undersized buffer via `srvstr_push`, causing a heap buffer overflow. The bounds check `max_out_len < *out_len` is ineffective because `*out_len` itself has already overflowed. An attacker who can influence the VFS shadow copy data (e.g., through a malicious VFS module or compromised snapshot source) could exploit this for remote code execution.

commit_message:
FSCTL_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA: Don't return 4 extra bytes at end

labels_data_count already accounts for the unicode null character at the
end of the array. There is no need in adding space for it again.

Signed-off-by: Christof Schmitt <christof.schmitt@us.ibm.com>
Reviewed-by: Jeremy Allison <jra@samba.org>
Reviewed-by: Simo Sorce <idra@samba.org>

Autobuild-User(master): Jeremy Allison <jra@samba.org>
Autobuild-Date(master): Tue Aug  6 04:03:17 CEST 2013 on sn-devel-104
cve_desc: Samba 3.6.6 through 3.6.23, 4.0.x before 4.0.18, and 4.1.x before 4.1.8, when a certain vfs shadow copy configuration is enabled, does not properly initialize the SRV_SNAPSHOT_ARRAY response field, which allows remote authenticated users to obtain potentially sensitive information from process memory via a (1) FSCTL_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA or (2) FSCTL_SRV_ENUMERATE_SNAPSHOTS request.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in FSCTL_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA leads to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the FSCTL_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA handler, `labels_data_count` is computed as `(shadow_data->num_volumes * 2 * sizeof(SHADOW_COPY_LABEL)) + 2` using uint32 arithmetic. If `num_volumes` (controlled by the VFS backend) is sufficiently large, this multiplication wraps around to a small value. The subsequent `*out_len = 12 + labels_data_count + 4` would also be small, causing `talloc_zero_array` to allocate a small buffer. However, the for-loop still iterates `num_volumes` times, writing `2 * sizeof(SHADOW_COPY_LABEL)` bytes per iteration, resulting in a massive heap buffer overflow. The size check `max_out_len < *out_len` is ineffective because `*out_len` itself has already overflowed. This could allow code execution if an attacker can influence the shadow copy data returned by the VFS layer.

commit_message:
Bug 701819: fixed ordering in if expression to avoid out-of-bounds access.

Fixes:
    ./sanbin/gs -dBATCH -dNOPAUSE -r965 -sOutputFile=tmp -sDEVICE=pcx16 ../bug-701819.pdf
cve_desc: A buffer overflow vulnerability in pcx_write_rle() in contrib/japanese/gdev10v.c of Artifex Software GhostScript v9.50 allows a remote attacker to cause a denial of service via a crafted PDF file. This is fixed in v9.51.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read when checking *from after advancing past end
vulnerabilities.long_description: After incrementing `from` by `step`, the code dereferences `*from` before checking whether `from == end`. Due to C's short-circuit evaluation of `||`, the left operand `data != *from` is evaluated first. If `from` has been advanced to or past `end`, this dereference reads out-of-bounds memory. An attacker providing crafted image data could trigger this to leak information or cause a crash.

commit_message:
Fix possible stack corruption with XM instrument headers claiming a size of less than 4

Closes #275
cve_desc: MilkyTracker v1.03.00 was discovered to contain a stack overflow via the component LoaderXM::load. This vulnerability is triggered when the program is supplied a crafted XM module file.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read in pattern data buffer due to missing bounds check on 'pc' index
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable 'pc' is used to index into 'buffer' (allocated with size phead[y].patdata) during pattern decompression, but is never validated against the buffer length. A crafted XM file with truncated or malformed pattern data can cause 'pc' to exceed the buffer bounds, leading to heap out-of-bounds reads. This could leak sensitive memory contents or cause a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds array access using nbu values as indices into smpReloc
vulnerabilities.long_description: The array 'nbu' contains byte values (0-255) read directly from the file and used as indices into 'smpReloc[MP_MAXINSSAMPS]'. If MP_MAXINSSAMPS is less than 256 (commonly 96 for XM format), any nbu value >= MP_MAXINSSAMPS causes an out-of-bounds read on the stack-allocated smpReloc array. An attacker can craft an XM file with nbu values exceeding the array size to read or influence stack data, potentially enabling information disclosure or code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Use of uninitialized smpReloc array in version 0x102/0x103 code path
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the version 0x102/0x103 code path, the stack-allocated array 'smpReloc' is never zero-initialized before use (unlike the 0x104 path which calls memset). Only entries for actual samples (indices 0 to instr[y].samp-1) are written, but the subsequent loop iterates over all MP_MAXINSSAMPS entries using nbu[sc] as an index. For indices beyond the number of actual samples, uninitialized stack values from smpReloc are used, leading to unpredictable behavior and potential use of attacker-influenced values for snum assignments.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer underflow when subtracting 4 from header size read from file
vulnerabilities.long_description: The header size is read directly from the file as an unsigned 32-bit value, then 4 is subtracted without checking if it's >= 4. If a crafted file provides hdrsize < 4, this causes an integer underflow, resulting in a very large value. This large value is then used to allocate a buffer and read data from the file, potentially causing excessive memory allocation or a heap buffer overflow if the allocation succeeds but the subsequent memset/read operates on the oversized value.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in pattern data allocation size calculation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expression 'phead[y].rows * header->channum * 6' can overflow if rows and channum are large values read from the file. Both phead[y].rows and header->channum come from untrusted file input. An overflow would result in a small allocation but a large memset or large indexing via 'bc', leading to a heap buffer overflow. An attacker could craft an XM file with large rows/channum values to trigger this.

commit_message:
selinux,smack: fix subjective/objective credential use mixups

Jann Horn reported a problem with commit eb1231f73c4d ("selinux:
clarify task subjective and objective credentials") where some LSM
hooks were attempting to access the subjective credentials of a task
other than the current task.  Generally speaking, it is not safe to
access another task's subjective credentials and doing so can cause
a number of problems.

Further, while looking into the problem, I realized that Smack was
suffering from a similar problem brought about by a similar commit
1fb057dcde11 ("smack: differentiate between subjective and objective
task credentials").

This patch addresses this problem by restoring the use of the task's
objective credentials in those cases where the task is other than the
current executing task.  Not only does this resolve the problem
reported by Jann, it is arguably the correct thing to do in these
cases.

Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Fixes: eb1231f73c4d ("selinux: clarify task subjective and objective credentials")
Fixes: 1fb057dcde11 ("smack: differentiate between subjective and objective task credentials")
Reported-by: Jann Horn <jannh@google.com>
Acked-by: Eric W. Biederman <ebiederm@xmission.com>
Acked-by: Casey Schaufler <casey@schaufler-ca.com>
Signed-off-by: Paul Moore <paul@paul-moore.com>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in the Linux kernel before 5.14.8. A use-after-free in selinux_ptrace_traceme (aka the SELinux handler for PTRACE_TRACEME) could be used by local attackers to cause memory corruption and escalate privileges, aka CID-a3727a8bac0a. This occurs because of an attempt to access the subjective credentials of another task.

commit_message:
rsi: fix a double free bug in rsi_91x_deinit()

`dev` (struct rsi_91x_usbdev *) field of adapter
(struct rsi_91x_usbdev *) is allocated  and initialized in
`rsi_init_usb_interface`. If any error is detected in information
read from the device side,  `rsi_init_usb_interface` will be
freed. However, in the higher level error handling code in
`rsi_probe`, if error is detected, `rsi_91x_deinit` is called
again, in which `dev` will be freed again, resulting double free.

This patch fixes the double free by removing the free operation on
`dev` in `rsi_init_usb_interface`, because `rsi_91x_deinit` is also
used in `rsi_disconnect`, in that code path, the `dev` field is not
 (and thus needs to be) freed.

This bug was found in v4.19, but is also present in the latest version
of kernel. Fixes CVE-2019-15504.

Reported-by: Hui Peng <benquike@gmail.com>
Reported-by: Mathias Payer <mathias.payer@nebelwelt.net>
Signed-off-by: Hui Peng <benquike@gmail.com>
Reviewed-by: Guenter Roeck <linux@roeck-us.net>
Signed-off-by: Kalle Valo <kvalo@codeaurora.org>
cve_desc: drivers/net/wireless/rsi/rsi_91x_usb.c in the Linux kernel through 5.2.9 has a Double Free via crafted USB device traffic (which may be remote via usbip or usbredir).

commit_message: Merge pull request from GHSA-f5qg-pqcg-765m
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C. Versions 2.12 and prior contain a stack buffer overflow vulnerability that affects PJSUA2 users or users that call the API `pjmedia_sdp_print(), pjmedia_sdp_media_print()`. Applications that do not use PJSUA2 and do not directly call `pjmedia_sdp_print()` or `pjmedia_sdp_media_print()` should not be affected. A patch is available on the `master` branch of the `pjsip/pjproject` GitHub repository. There are currently no known workarounds.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Buffer overflow due to insufficient length check that ignores format entries
vulnerabilities.long_description: The initial buffer length check only accounts for media, transport, and a fixed overhead (12+24 bytes), but does not account for the cumulative size of all format entries written in the fmt_count loop. If there are many or large format strings, the writes via pj_memcpy and pointer increments will go past the end of the buffer. An attacker who controls the SDP media description (e.g., by sending a crafted SDP message with many format entries) can trigger a heap or stack buffer overflow, potentially leading to code execution.

commit_message:
rsi: fix a double free bug in rsi_91x_deinit()

`dev` (struct rsi_91x_usbdev *) field of adapter
(struct rsi_91x_usbdev *) is allocated  and initialized in
`rsi_init_usb_interface`. If any error is detected in information
read from the device side,  `rsi_init_usb_interface` will be
freed. However, in the higher level error handling code in
`rsi_probe`, if error is detected, `rsi_91x_deinit` is called
again, in which `dev` will be freed again, resulting double free.

This patch fixes the double free by removing the free operation on
`dev` in `rsi_init_usb_interface`, because `rsi_91x_deinit` is also
used in `rsi_disconnect`, in that code path, the `dev` field is not
 (and thus needs to be) freed.

This bug was found in v4.19, but is also present in the latest version
of kernel. Fixes CVE-2019-15504.

Reported-by: Hui Peng <benquike@gmail.com>
Reported-by: Mathias Payer <mathias.payer@nebelwelt.net>
Signed-off-by: Hui Peng <benquike@gmail.com>
Reviewed-by: Guenter Roeck <linux@roeck-us.net>
Signed-off-by: Kalle Valo <kvalo@codeaurora.org>
cve_desc: drivers/net/wireless/rsi/rsi_91x_usb.c in the Linux kernel through 5.2.9 has a Double Free via crafted USB device traffic (which may be remote via usbip or usbredir).

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of rsi_dev on error paths
vulnerabilities.long_description: When the function fails and jumps to the fail_eps or fail_rx labels, the allocated rsi_dev (via kzalloc) is never freed. Every error path returns without calling kfree(rsi_dev), leading to a kernel memory leak. An attacker who can trigger repeated probe failures (e.g., by repeatedly connecting a specially crafted USB device) could exhaust kernel memory, causing a denial of service.
