commit_message:
Bug 702582, CVE 2020-15900 Memory Corruption in Ghostscript 9.52

Fix the 'rsearch' calculation for the 'post' size to give the correct
size.  Previous calculation would result in a size that was too large,
and could underflow to max uint32_t. Also fix 'rsearch' to return the
correct 'pre' string with empty string match.

A future change may 'undefine' this undocumented, non-standard operator
during initialization as we do with the many other non-standard internal
PostScript operators and procedures.
cve_desc: A memory corruption issue was found in Artifex Ghostscript 9.50 and 9.52. Use of a non-standard PostScript operator can allow overriding of file access controls. The 'rsearch' calculation for the 'post' size resulted in a size that was too large, and could underflow to max uint32_t. This was fixed in commit 5d499272b95a6b890a1397e11d20937de000d31b.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer underflow when searching backward with an empty pattern leads to out-of-bounds read
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `forward` is false and `size` is 0 (empty search pattern), the code jumps to `found` immediately. In the `found` block, the expression `count + (!forward ? (size - 1) : 0)` is computed. Since `size` is a `uint` with value 0, `size - 1` wraps around to `UINT_MAX` (4294967295). This is added to `count` (which equals `r_size(op1)`), resulting in a massive value being set as the size of the result string via `r_set_size(op1, ...)`. A subsequent read of this string object would allow reading far beyond the actual buffer bounds, potentially leaking sensitive memory contents. An attacker who can invoke this PostScript operator with a backward search and an empty pattern string can trigger this vulnerability.

commit_message:
Unsquashfs: additional write outside destination directory exploit fix

An issue on github (https://github.com/plougher/squashfs-tools/issues/72)
showed how some specially crafted Squashfs filesystems containing
invalid file names (with '/' and '..') can cause Unsquashfs to write
files outside of the destination directory.

Since then it has been shown that specially crafted Squashfs filesystems
that contain a symbolic link pointing outside of the destination directory,
coupled with an identically named file within the same directory, can
cause Unsquashfs to write files outside of the destination directory.

Specifically the symbolic link produces a pathname pointing outside
of the destination directory, which is then followed when writing the
duplicate identically named file within the directory.

This commit fixes this exploit by explictly checking for duplicate
filenames within a directory.  As directories in v2.1, v3.x, and v4.0
filesystems are sorted, this is achieved by checking for consecutively
identical filenames.  Additionally directories are checked to
ensure they are sorted, to avoid attempts to evade the duplicate
check.

Version 1.x and 2.0 filesystems (where the directories were unsorted)
are sorted and then the above duplicate filename check is applied.

Signed-off-by: Phillip Lougher <phillip@squashfs.org.uk>
cve_desc: squashfs_opendir in unsquash-2.c in Squashfs-Tools 4.5 allows Directory Traversal, a different vulnerability than CVE-2021-40153. A squashfs filesystem that has been crafted to include a symbolic link and then contents under the same filename in a filesystem can cause unsquashfs to first create the symbolic link pointing outside the expected directory, and then the subsequent write operation will cause the unsquashfs process to write through the symbolic link elsewhere in the filesystem.

vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference if read_inode returns NULL
vulnerabilities.long_description: The return value of read_inode() is stored in *i but is never checked for NULL before being dereferenced multiple times (e.g., (*i)->mode, (*i)->uid, (*i)->data, etc.). If read_inode fails and returns NULL, this leads to a NULL pointer dereference, causing a crash (denial of service). A crafted squashfs image with an invalid inode reference could trigger this.

commit_message:
Bluetooth: virtio_bt: fix memory leak in virtbt_rx_handle()

On the reception of packets with an invalid packet type, the memory of
the allocated socket buffers is never freed. Add a default case that frees
these to avoid a memory leak.

Fixes: afd2daa26c7a ("Bluetooth: Add support for virtio transport driver")
Signed-off-by: Soenke Huster <soenke.huster@eknoes.de>
Signed-off-by: Marcel Holtmann <marcel@holtmann.org>
cve_desc: drivers/bluetooth/virtio_bt.c in the Linux kernel before 5.16.3 has a memory leak (socket buffers have memory allocated but not freed).

commit_message:
atlantic: Fix OOB read and write in hw_atl_utils_fw_rpc_wait

This bug report shows up when running our research tools. The
reports is SOOB read, but it seems SOOB write is also possible
a few lines below.

In details, fw.len and sw.len are inputs coming from io. A len
over the size of self->rpc triggers SOOB. The patch fixes the
bugs by adding sanity checks.

The bugs are triggerable with compromised/malfunctioning devices.
They are potentially exploitable given they first leak up to
0xffff bytes and able to overwrite the region later.

The patch is tested with QEMU emulater.
This is NOT tested with a real device.

Attached is the log we found by fuzzing.

	hw_atl_utils_fw_upload_dwords+0x393/0x3c0 [atlantic]

commit_message:
Bluetooth: virtio_bt: fix memory leak in virtbt_rx_handle()

On the reception of packets with an invalid packet type, the memory of
the allocated socket buffers is never freed. Add a default case that frees
these to avoid a memory leak.

Fixes: afd2daa26c7a ("Bluetooth: Add support for virtio transport driver")
Signed-off-by: Soenke Huster <soenke.huster@eknoes.de>
Signed-off-by: Marcel Holtmann <marcel@holtmann.org>
cve_desc: drivers/bluetooth/virtio_bt.c in the Linux kernel before 5.16.3 has a memory leak (socket buffers have memory allocated but not freed).

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read due to missing skb length check before accessing skb->data
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function dereferences skb->data to read the packet type byte without first verifying that skb->len is at least 1. If the virtio device (or a malicious/buggy backend) delivers a zero-length skb, this results in an out-of-bounds read. Additionally, skb_pull with a length of 1 on a zero-length buffer can lead to an underflow of skb->len (wrapping to a very large value), which could then cause further out-of-bounds access when hci_recv_frame processes the corrupted skb. An attacker controlling the virtio backend could exploit this to leak kernel memory or cause a denial of service.

commit_message:
Unsquashfs: additional write outside destination directory exploit fix

An issue on github (https://github.com/plougher/squashfs-tools/issues/72)
showed how some specially crafted Squashfs filesystems containing
invalid file names (with '/' and '..') can cause Unsquashfs to write
files outside of the destination directory.

Since then it has been shown that specially crafted Squashfs filesystems
that contain a symbolic link pointing outside of the destination directory,
coupled with an identically named file within the same directory, can
cause Unsquashfs to write files outside of the destination directory.

Specifically the symbolic link produces a pathname pointing outside
of the destination directory, which is then followed when writing the
duplicate identically named file within the directory.

This commit fixes this exploit by explictly checking for duplicate
filenames within a directory.  As directories in v2.1, v3.x, and v4.0
filesystems are sorted, this is achieved by checking for consecutively
identical filenames.  Additionally directories are checked to
ensure they are sorted, to avoid attempts to evade the duplicate
check.

Version 1.x and 2.0 filesystems (where the directories were unsorted)
are sorted and then the above duplicate filename check is applied.

Signed-off-by: Phillip Lougher <phillip@squashfs.org.uk>
cve_desc: squashfs_opendir in unsquash-2.c in Squashfs-Tools 4.5 allows Directory Traversal, a different vulnerability than CVE-2021-40153. A squashfs filesystem that has been crafted to include a symbolic link and then contents under the same filename in a filesystem can cause unsquashfs to first create the symbolic link pointing outside the expected directory, and then the subsequent write operation will cause the unsquashfs process to write through the symbolic link elsewhere in the filesystem.

vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference when read_inode returns NULL
vulnerabilities.long_description: The return value of read_inode() is stored in *i but never checked for NULL before being dereferenced to access ->mode, ->uid, ->gid, ->time, ->xattr, ->data, ->start, and ->offset. If read_inode() fails and returns NULL (e.g., due to a corrupted filesystem image), this leads to a NULL pointer dereference, causing a crash or potentially exploitable behavior. An attacker crafting a malicious squashfs image could trigger this by providing invalid block_start/offset values.

commit_message:
Remove double free() in contrib/shpsrt, issue #39

This fixes issue #39
cve_desc: A double-free condition exists in contrib/shpsort.c of shapelib 1.5.0 and older releases. This issue may allow an attacker to cause a denial of service or have other unspecified impact via control over malloc.

vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference when realloc fails on first allocation (result is NULL)
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `i` is 0 and `result` is NULL, if `realloc(NULL, ...)` fails and returns NULL, the condition `!tmp && result` is false (since result is also NULL). Execution continues, setting `result = tmp` (NULL), then `result[i++] = dupstr(cptr)` dereferences a NULL pointer, causing undefined behavior / crash.

commit_message:
Unsquashfs: additional write outside destination directory exploit fix

An issue on github (https://github.com/plougher/squashfs-tools/issues/72)
showed how some specially crafted Squashfs filesystems containing
invalid file names (with '/' and '..') can cause Unsquashfs to write
files outside of the destination directory.

Since then it has been shown that specially crafted Squashfs filesystems
that contain a symbolic link pointing outside of the destination directory,
coupled with an identically named file within the same directory, can
cause Unsquashfs to write files outside of the destination directory.

Specifically the symbolic link produces a pathname pointing outside
of the destination directory, which is then followed when writing the
duplicate identically named file within the directory.

This commit fixes this exploit by explictly checking for duplicate
filenames within a directory.  As directories in v2.1, v3.x, and v4.0
filesystems are sorted, this is achieved by checking for consecutively
identical filenames.  Additionally directories are checked to
ensure they are sorted, to avoid attempts to evade the duplicate
check.

Version 1.x and 2.0 filesystems (where the directories were unsorted)
are sorted and then the above duplicate filename check is applied.

Signed-off-by: Phillip Lougher <phillip@squashfs.org.uk>
cve_desc: squashfs_opendir in unsquash-2.c in Squashfs-Tools 4.5 allows Directory Traversal, a different vulnerability than CVE-2021-40153. A squashfs filesystem that has been crafted to include a symbolic link and then contents under the same filename in a filesystem can cause unsquashfs to first create the symbolic link pointing outside the expected directory, and then the subsequent write operation will cause the unsquashfs process to write through the symbolic link elsewhere in the filesystem.

vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference when read_inode returns NULL
vulnerabilities.long_description: The return value of read_inode(block_start, offset) is stored in *i but is never checked for NULL before being dereferenced to access fields like mode, uid, gid, time, and xattr. If read_inode fails (e.g., due to a corrupted or malicious filesystem image), it may return NULL, leading to a NULL pointer dereference. This could cause a crash (denial of service) or, in some environments, exploitable behavior if the NULL page is mappable.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential integer overflow in directory size calculation leading to excessive loop iteration
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable 'size' is computed as (*i)->data + bytes - 3, where (*i)->data comes from an on-disk inode and is attacker-controlled in a malicious filesystem image. If (*i)->data is a very large or specifically crafted value, the arithmetic could overflow the signed int 'size', potentially causing the while loop to iterate far beyond the actual directory data. This could lead to out-of-bounds reads from directory data buffers, information disclosure, or crashes when processing a crafted squashfs image.

commit_message:
Unsquashfs: additional write outside destination directory exploit fix

An issue on github (https://github.com/plougher/squashfs-tools/issues/72)
showed how some specially crafted Squashfs filesystems containing
invalid file names (with '/' and '..') can cause Unsquashfs to write
files outside of the destination directory.

Since then it has been shown that specially crafted Squashfs filesystems
that contain a symbolic link pointing outside of the destination directory,
coupled with an identically named file within the same directory, can
cause Unsquashfs to write files outside of the destination directory.

Specifically the symbolic link produces a pathname pointing outside
of the destination directory, which is then followed when writing the
duplicate identically named file within the directory.

This commit fixes this exploit by explictly checking for duplicate
filenames within a directory.  As directories in v2.1, v3.x, and v4.0
filesystems are sorted, this is achieved by checking for consecutively
identical filenames.  Additionally directories are checked to
ensure they are sorted, to avoid attempts to evade the duplicate
check.

Version 1.x and 2.0 filesystems (where the directories were unsorted)
are sorted and then the above duplicate filename check is applied.

Signed-off-by: Phillip Lougher <phillip@squashfs.org.uk>
cve_desc: squashfs_opendir in unsquash-2.c in Squashfs-Tools 4.5 allows Directory Traversal, a different vulnerability than CVE-2021-40153. A squashfs filesystem that has been crafted to include a symbolic link and then contents under the same filename in a filesystem can cause unsquashfs to first create the symbolic link pointing outside the expected directory, and then the subsequent write operation will cause the unsquashfs process to write through the symbolic link elsewhere in the filesystem.

vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference if read_inode returns NULL
vulnerabilities.long_description: The return value of read_inode(block_start, offset) is stored into *i but is never checked for NULL before being dereferenced multiple times (e.g., (*i)->mode, (*i)->uid, (*i)->data, etc.). If the inode data on disk is corrupted or the block_start/offset are invalid, read_inode could return NULL, leading to a NULL pointer dereference and a crash. On a crafted squashfs image this would cause denial of service.

commit_message:
Bug 702582, CVE 2020-15900 Memory Corruption in Ghostscript 9.52

Fix the 'rsearch' calculation for the 'post' size to give the correct
size.  Previous calculation would result in a size that was too large,
and could underflow to max uint32_t. Also fix 'rsearch' to return the
correct 'pre' string with empty string match.

A future change may 'undefine' this undocumented, non-standard operator
during initialization as we do with the many other non-standard internal
PostScript operators and procedures.
cve_desc: A memory corruption issue was found in Artifex Ghostscript 9.50 and 9.52. Use of a non-standard PostScript operator can allow overriding of file access controls. The 'rsearch' calculation for the 'post' size resulted in a size that was too large, and could underflow to max uint32_t. This was fixed in commit 5d499272b95a6b890a1397e11d20937de000d31b.

commit_message: Merge pull request from GHSA-3qx3-cg72-wrh9
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C language implementing standard based protocols such as SIP, SDP, RTP, STUN, TURN, and ICE. In affected versions if the incoming RTCP BYE message contains a reason's length, this declared length is not checked against the actual received packet size, potentially resulting in an out-of-bound read access. This issue affects all users that use PJMEDIA and RTCP. A malicious actor can send a RTCP BYE message with an invalid reason length. Users are advised to upgrade as soon as possible. There are no known workarounds.

cve_desc: Access of Memory Location Before Start of Buffer in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

commit_message: Merge pull request from GHSA-3qx3-cg72-wrh9
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C language implementing standard based protocols such as SIP, SDP, RTP, STUN, TURN, and ICE. In affected versions if the incoming RTCP BYE message contains a reason's length, this declared length is not checked against the actual received packet size, potentially resulting in an out-of-bound read access. This issue affects all users that use PJMEDIA and RTCP. A malicious actor can send a RTCP BYE message with an invalid reason length. Users are advised to upgrade as soon as possible. There are no known workarounds.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Heap/buffer over-read when the BYE reason length byte exceeds the actual remaining packet data
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code reads the reason length from the byte at offset 8 in the packet and clamps it only against the size of the destination buffer (peer_sdes_buf_), but never checks that offset 9 + reason.slen <= size. An attacker can craft a malicious RTCP BYE packet where `size` is, say, 10 (just barely > 8) but the length byte at offset 8 is set to a large value (e.g., 255). The memcpy will then read up to 255 bytes starting from pkt+9, far beyond the actual packet buffer. This leads to an out-of-bounds read that can leak sensitive memory contents or cause a crash.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4206: condition with many "(" causes a crash

Problem:    Condition with many "(" causes a crash.
Solution:   Limit recursion to 1000.

commit_message:
SplashOutputDev::tilingPatternFill: Fix crash on broken file

Issue #802
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in Poppler through 0.78.0. There is a divide-by-zero error in the function SplashOutputDev::tilingPatternFill at SplashOutputDev.cc.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Division by zero when both ctm[0] and ctm[1] (or matc[0] and matc[1]) are zero or near-zero
vulnerabilities.long_description: When both ctm[0] and ctm[1] are zero, the condition `splashAbs(ctm[1]) > splashAbs(ctm[0])` is false, so the else branch executes and divides by ctm[0] which is zero. The same pattern occurs later with matc[0] and matc[1]. A crafted PDF with a degenerate transformation matrix can trigger this, causing a floating-point division by zero which may result in a crash or undefined behavior depending on the platform's floating-point exception handling.

code_snippets:
result_width = tBitmap->getWidth() * imgData.repeatX;
  result_height = tBitmap->getHeight() * imgData.repeatY;
vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in result_width/result_height computation used for image rendering
vulnerabilities.long_description: The multiplication of tBitmap->getWidth() (int) by imgData.repeatX (int) and tBitmap->getHeight() by imgData.repeatY is performed as signed int arithmetic with no overflow check. If the values are large enough, the multiplication overflows, producing a small or negative value. This corrupted result_width/result_height is then passed to splash->drawImage(), which could lead to heap buffer overflows, incorrect memory allocations, or other memory corruption issues. An attacker could craft a PDF with specific tiling pattern parameters to trigger this overflow.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in surface area size check before bitmap allocation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The check `(unsigned long) surface_width * surface_height > 0x800000L` only casts surface_width to unsigned long; if surface_width is negative (from a double-to-int conversion overflow in `(int) ceil(fabs(kx))`), the cast to unsigned long produces a very large number, but if both are large positive ints the multiplication can still overflow on platforms where unsigned long is 32-bit. On such platforms the overflow wraps around, potentially passing the size check and allowing allocation of a very large bitmap. This could lead to excessive memory allocation or heap-based buffer overflows during subsequent rendering operations.

commit_message: check max member count in class
cve_desc: sqclass.cpp in Squirrel through 2.2.5 and 3.x through 3.1 allows an out-of-bounds read (in the core interpreter) that can lead to Code Execution. If a victim executes an attacker-controlled squirrel script, it is possible for the attacker to break out of the squirrel script sandbox even if all dangerous functionality such as File System functions has been disabled. An attacker might abuse this bug to target (for example) Cloud services that allow customization via SquirrelScripts, or distribute malware through video games that embed a Squirrel Engine.

commit_message: ITS#9454 fix issuerAndThisUpdateCheck
cve_desc: In OpenLDAP through 2.4.57 and 2.5.x through 2.5.1alpha, an assertion failure in slapd can occur in the issuerAndThisUpdateCheck function via a crafted packet, resulting in a denial of service (daemon exit) via a short timestamp. This is related to schema_init.c and checkTime.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read when checking for escaped double-quote in issuer field
vulnerabilities.long_description: When scanning the issuer string for a closing double-quote, the loop condition is `is->bv_len < x.bv_len`. If a double-quote character is found at position `is->bv_len == x.bv_len - 1` (the last valid index), the code accesses `is->bv_val[is->bv_len + 1]` which equals `is->bv_val[x.bv_len]`, reading one byte beyond the logical boundary of the buffer tracked by `x.bv_len`. While this may still fall within the original allocated buffer in many cases (e.g., the stripped `}` character), it constitutes an out-of-bounds read relative to the validated region and could lead to incorrect parsing logic or, depending on memory layout, information disclosure.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Buffer read before length check in space-eating loops
vulnerabilities.long_description: Multiple space-eating loops test `x.bv_val[0] == ' '` before checking `x.bv_len`. Due to left-to-right evaluation of `&&`, when `x.bv_len` is 0 the code still dereferences `x.bv_val[0]`, reading past the logical end of the buffer. This affects all the space-eating `for` loops in the function. An attacker providing a carefully crafted input could trigger reads beyond the intended buffer boundaries, potentially causing incorrect control flow or, in edge cases, reading sensitive adjacent memory.

commit_message:
SplashOutputDev::tilingPatternFill: Fix crash on broken file

Issue #802
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in Poppler through 0.78.0. There is a divide-by-zero error in the function SplashOutputDev::tilingPatternFill at SplashOutputDev.cc.

code_snippets:
result_width = tBitmap->getWidth() * imgData.repeatX;
  result_height = tBitmap->getHeight() * imgData.repeatY;
vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow when computing tiled result dimensions
vulnerabilities.long_description: The multiplication of tBitmap->getWidth() * imgData.repeatX (and height * repeatY) is performed using int arithmetic. If repeatX/repeatY are large (they can be derived from x1-x0, y1-y0 which come from external input), this multiplication can overflow, resulting in a small or negative value for result_width/result_height. This corrupted value is then passed to splash->drawImage() which could lead to heap buffer overflows or other memory corruption when rendering the tiled pattern.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in surface area check before cast to unsigned long
vulnerabilities.long_description: surface_width and surface_height are both int types. The multiplication `surface_width * surface_height` is performed as int*int before the result is cast to unsigned long. If both values are large enough (e.g., each around 46341), the int multiplication overflows, potentially producing a small or negative value that then gets cast to unsigned long. This could bypass the size limit check, allowing allocation of an excessively large bitmap.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Division by zero when both CTM diagonal and off-diagonal elements are zero
vulnerabilities.long_description: When splashAbs(ctm[1]) is not greater than splashAbs(ctm[0]), the else branch divides by ctm[0]. However, if both ctm[0] and ctm[1] are zero (e.g., from a degenerate transformation matrix), this results in a division by zero. The same pattern occurs later with matc[0] and matc[1]. While the finite check catches NaN/Inf for the initial CTM values, subsequent matrix concatenations could produce zero values in matc, leading to floating-point division by zero and undefined behavior or crashes.

code_snippets:
result_width = (int) ceil(fabs(kx * width * (x1 - x0)));
  result_height = (int) ceil(fabs(ky * height * (y1 - y0)));
vulnerabilities.short_description: Undefined behavior casting large double to int
vulnerabilities.long_description: The result of ceil(fabs(...)) could be a very large double value that exceeds INT_MAX. Casting such a value to int is undefined behavior in C/C++. Since kx, width, and (x1-x0) all come from potentially attacker-controlled PDF input, a crafted document could trigger this, leading to unpredictable values for result_width and result_height which are used in subsequent size calculations and memory operations.

commit_message:
bpf, ringbuf: Deny reserve of buffers larger than ringbuf

A BPF program might try to reserve a buffer larger than the ringbuf size.
If the consumer pointer is way ahead of the producer, that would be
successfully reserved, allowing the BPF program to read or write out of
the ringbuf allocated area.

Reported-by: Ryota Shiga (Flatt Security)
Fixes: 457f44363a88 ("bpf: Implement BPF ring buffer and verifier support for it")
Signed-off-by: Thadeu Lima de Souza Cascardo <cascardo@canonical.com>
Signed-off-by: Daniel Borkmann <daniel@iogearbox.net>
Acked-by: Andrii Nakryiko <andrii@kernel.org>
Acked-by: Alexei Starovoitov <ast@kernel.org>
cve_desc: The eBPF RINGBUF bpf_ringbuf_reserve() function in the Linux kernel did not check that the allocated size was smaller than the ringbuf size, allowing an attacker to perform out-of-bounds writes within the kernel and therefore, arbitrary code execution. This issue was fixed via commit 4b81ccebaeee ("bpf, ringbuf: Deny reserve of buffers larger than ringbuf") (v5.13-rc4) and backported to the stable kernels in v5.12.4, v5.11.21, and v5.10.37. It was introduced via 457f44363a88 ("bpf: Implement BPF ring buffer and verifier support for it") (v5.8-rc1).

commit_message:
CVE-2022-31214: fixing the fix, one more time

the previous commit "CVE-2022-31214: fixing the fix"
made private-etc=fonts,fonts and similar commands
fail with an error

fix that regression by tolerating already existing

cve_desc: A Privilege Context Switching issue was discovered in join.c in Firejail 0.9.68. By crafting a bogus Firejail container that is accepted by the Firejail setuid-root program as a join target, a local attacker can enter an environment in which the Linux user namespace is still the initial user namespace, the NO_NEW_PRIVS prctl is not activated, and the entered mount namespace is under the attacker's control. In this way, the filesystem layout can be adjusted to gain root privileges through execution of available setuid-root binaries such as su or sudo.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds write on `dst` buffer due to `q` advancing in lockstep with `p` without bounds checking.
vulnerabilities.long_description: The pointer `q` (into the `dst` buffer) is incremented in lockstep with `p` (into the `src` buffer) on every loop iteration (`p++, q++`). If the source path after `src_prefix_len` is longer than the space available in the `dst` buffer after `dst_prefix_len`, the writes `*q = '\0'` and `*r = '/'` (where `r` is set to `q`) will occur beyond the bounds of the `dst` buffer. There is no size parameter or bounds check for the `dst` buffer anywhere in the function. An attacker who controls the `src` path (e.g., via a crafted filesystem or archive) can trigger a heap or stack buffer overflow, potentially leading to code execution.

commit_message:
[bgpd] cleanup, compact and consolidate capability parsing code

2007-07-26 Paul Jakma <paul.jakma@sun.com>

	* (general) Clean up and compact capability parsing slightly.
	  Consolidate validation of length and logging of generic TLV, and
	  memcpy of capability data, thus removing such from cap specifc
	  code (not always present or correct).
	* bgp_open.h: Add structures for the generic capability TLV header
	  and for the data formats of the various specific capabilities we
	  support.  Hence remove the badly named, or else misdefined, struct
	  capability.
	* bgp_open.c: (bgp_capability_vty_out) Use struct capability_mp_data.
	  Do the length checks *before* memcpy()'ing based on that length
	  (stored capability - should have been validated anyway on input,
	  but..).
	  (bgp_afi_safi_valid_indices) new function to validate (afi,safi)
	  which is about to be used as index into arrays, consolidates
	  several instances of same, at least one of which appeared to be
	  incomplete..
	  (bgp_capability_mp) Much condensed.
	  (bgp_capability_orf_entry) New, process one ORF entry
	  (bgp_capability_orf) Condensed. Fixed to process all ORF entries.
	  (bgp_capability_restart) Condensed, and fixed to use a
	  cap-specific type, rather than abusing capability_mp.
	  (struct message capcode_str) added to aid generic logging.
	  (size_t cap_minsizes[]) added to aid generic validation of
	  capability length field.
	  (bgp_capability_parse) Generic logging and validation of TLV
	  consolidated here. Code compacted as much as possible.
	* bgp_packet.c: (bgp_open_receive) Capability parsers now use
	  streams, so no more need here to manually fudge the input stream
	  getp.
	  (bgp_capability_msg_parse) use struct capability_mp_data. Validate
	  lengths /before/ memcpy. Use bgp_afi_safi_valid_indices.
	  (bgp_capability_receive) Exported for use by test harness.
	* bgp_vty.c: (bgp_show_summary) fix conversion warning
	  (bgp_show_peer) ditto
	* bgp_debug.h: Fix storage 'extern' after type 'const'.
        * lib/log.c: (mes_lookup) warning about code not being in
          same-number array slot should be debug, not warning. E.g. BGP
          has several discontigious number spaces, allocating from
          different parts of a space is not uncommon (e.g. IANA
          assigned versus vendor-assigned code points in some number
          space).
cve_desc: An out-of-bounds read in the BGP daemon of FRRouting FRR before 8.4 may lead to a segmentation fault and denial of service. This occurs in bgp_capability_msg_parse in bgpd/bgp_packet.c.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read when hdr->length is smaller than sizeof(struct capability_mp_data)
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code validates that `pnt + hdr->length + 3` does not exceed `end`, but then unconditionally copies `sizeof(struct capability_mp_data)` bytes from `pnt + 3` into `mpc`. If `hdr->length` (controlled by the remote peer) is smaller than `sizeof(struct capability_mp_data)`, the memcpy reads beyond the bounds validated by the length check, potentially reading past the end of the buffer. A malicious BGP peer could craft a CAPABILITY message with a small `hdr->length` value to trigger this out-of-bounds read, potentially leaking memory contents or causing a crash.

commit_message:
[bgpd] cleanup, compact and consolidate capability parsing code

2007-07-26 Paul Jakma <paul.jakma@sun.com>

	* (general) Clean up and compact capability parsing slightly.
	  Consolidate validation of length and logging of generic TLV, and
	  memcpy of capability data, thus removing such from cap specifc
	  code (not always present or correct).
	* bgp_open.h: Add structures for the generic capability TLV header
	  and for the data formats of the various specific capabilities we
	  support.  Hence remove the badly named, or else misdefined, struct
	  capability.
	* bgp_open.c: (bgp_capability_vty_out) Use struct capability_mp_data.
	  Do the length checks *before* memcpy()'ing based on that length
	  (stored capability - should have been validated anyway on input,
	  but..).
	  (bgp_afi_safi_valid_indices) new function to validate (afi,safi)
	  which is about to be used as index into arrays, consolidates
	  several instances of same, at least one of which appeared to be
	  incomplete..
	  (bgp_capability_mp) Much condensed.
	  (bgp_capability_orf_entry) New, process one ORF entry
	  (bgp_capability_orf) Condensed. Fixed to process all ORF entries.
	  (bgp_capability_restart) Condensed, and fixed to use a
	  cap-specific type, rather than abusing capability_mp.
	  (struct message capcode_str) added to aid generic logging.
	  (size_t cap_minsizes[]) added to aid generic validation of
	  capability length field.
	  (bgp_capability_parse) Generic logging and validation of TLV
	  consolidated here. Code compacted as much as possible.
	* bgp_packet.c: (bgp_open_receive) Capability parsers now use
	  streams, so no more need here to manually fudge the input stream
	  getp.
	  (bgp_capability_msg_parse) use struct capability_mp_data. Validate
	  lengths /before/ memcpy. Use bgp_afi_safi_valid_indices.
	  (bgp_capability_receive) Exported for use by test harness.
	* bgp_vty.c: (bgp_show_summary) fix conversion warning
	  (bgp_show_peer) ditto
	* bgp_debug.h: Fix storage 'extern' after type 'const'.
        * lib/log.c: (mes_lookup) warning about code not being in
          same-number array slot should be debug, not warning. E.g. BGP
          has several discontigious number spaces, allocating from
          different parts of a space is not uncommon (e.g. IANA
          assigned versus vendor-assigned code points in some number
          space).
cve_desc: An out-of-bounds read in the BGP daemon of FRRouting FRR before 8.4 may lead to a segmentation fault and denial of service. This occurs in bgp_capability_msg_parse in bgpd/bgp_packet.c.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read via memcpy of sizeof(struct capability) bytes before validating sufficient data is available
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function copies sizeof(struct capability) bytes from `pnt + 1` into the local `cap` structure, but at this point only 3 bytes of availability have been validated (`pnt + 3 > end` check). If the remaining data in the buffer (`end - pnt - 1`) is less than `sizeof(struct capability)`, the memcpy will read beyond the bounds of the input buffer. The `cap.length` field used for the proper bounds check is only validated *after* the memcpy has already occurred. An attacker can craft a BGP CAPABILITY message with a small `length` parameter such that the input buffer is shorter than `sizeof(struct capability)`, triggering an out-of-bounds read that can leak memory contents or cause a crash.

commit_message: ITS#9454 fix issuerAndThisUpdateCheck
cve_desc: In OpenLDAP through 2.4.57 and 2.5.x through 2.5.1alpha, an assertion failure in slapd can occur in the issuerAndThisUpdateCheck function via a crafted packet, resulting in a denial of service (daemon exit) via a short timestamp. This is related to schema_init.c and checkTime.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer underflow on x.bv_len when issuer closing quote is not found
vulnerabilities.long_description: If the scanning loop for the issuer field terminates because is->bv_len reaches x.bv_len (no closing double-quote found), the code proceeds to execute `x.bv_len -= is->bv_len + 1`. Since is->bv_len == x.bv_len at that point, this becomes `x.bv_len -= x.bv_len + 1`, which underflows the unsigned ber_len_t to a very large value. This corrupted length is then used in subsequent string comparisons and memory accesses, leading to out-of-bounds reads. A similar issue exists in the thisUpdate parsing section.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read when checking for double-quote escape at end of buffer
vulnerabilities.long_description: When the loop finds a double-quote character at position is->bv_len == x.bv_len - 1 (the last valid index), the code reads is->bv_val[is->bv_len + 1] which is at offset x.bv_len from is->bv_val, one byte past the logical end of the parsed region. While this may read the trailing '}' from the original buffer in most cases, it is still an unchecked out-of-bounds access. Additionally, if is->bv_len + 1 happens to match (the byte beyond equals '"'), is->bv_len is incremented by 2, exceeding x.bv_len and causing further corruption of length calculations.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Dereference before length check in space-eating loops
vulnerabilities.long_description: Multiple space-eating loops evaluate `x.bv_val[0] == ' '` before checking `x.bv_len` (non-zero). Due to C's left-to-right short-circuit evaluation, when x.bv_len is 0, the code dereferences x.bv_val[0] before the length guard can prevent it. If x.bv_len has been corrupted (e.g., via the underflow above) or the pointer has advanced past valid memory, this results in an out-of-bounds read. The condition should be `x.bv_len && (x.bv_val[0] == ' ')` to check length first.

commit_message:
io_uring: fix off-by-one in sync cancelation file check

The passed in index should be validated against the number of registered
files we have, it needs to be smaller than the index value to avoid going
one beyond the end.

commit_message:
io_uring: fix off-by-one in sync cancelation file check

The passed in index should be validated against the number of registered
files we have, it needs to be smaller than the index value to avoid going
one beyond the end.

Fixes: 78a861b94959 ("io_uring: add sync cancelation API through io_uring_register()")
Reported-by: Luo Likang <luolikang@nsfocus.com>
Signed-off-by: Jens Axboe <axboe@kernel.dk>
cve_desc: off-by-one in io_uring module.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Off-by-one error allows out-of-bounds access to fixed file table
vulnerabilities.long_description: The boundary check uses `fd > ctx->nr_user_files` instead of `fd >= ctx->nr_user_files`. Since `nr_user_files` represents the count of files (indexed 0 to nr_user_files-1), an `fd` value equal to `nr_user_files` passes the check but results in an out-of-bounds access when used in `io_fixed_file_slot(&ctx->file_table, fd)` and `array_index_nospec(fd, ctx->nr_user_files)`. Note that `array_index_nospec(fd, ctx->nr_user_files)` when fd == nr_user_files returns 0 (clamped), which mitigates the OOB read in practice, but this still leads to incorrect behavior by operating on the wrong file descriptor (index 0 instead of the intended one), potentially cancelling the wrong I/O operation.

commit_message:
CVE-2022-31214: fixing the fix, one more time

the previous commit "CVE-2022-31214: fixing the fix"
made private-etc=fonts,fonts and similar commands
fail with an error

fix that regression by tolerating already existing
directories
cve_desc: A Privilege Context Switching issue was discovered in join.c in Firejail 0.9.68. By crafting a bogus Firejail container that is accepted by the Firejail setuid-root program as a join target, a local attacker can enter an environment in which the Linux user namespace is still the initial user namespace, the NO_NEW_PRIVS prctl is not activated, and the entered mount namespace is under the attacker's control. In this way, the filesystem layout can be adjusted to gain root privileges through execution of available setuid-root binaries such as su or sudo.

vulnerabilities.short_description: TOCTOU race condition between mkdir and chmod allows symlink attack on arbitrary files
vulnerabilities.long_description: When mkdir fails with EEXIST, the code proceeds to call chmod on the existing path. An attacker could replace the existing directory with a symlink between the mkdir and chmod calls (or before chmod when EEXIST is returned). Since chmod() follows symlinks, this allows an attacker to change the permissions of an arbitrary file or directory to the mode of the source directory. Even in the non-EEXIST case, there is a race window between mkdir creating the directory with 0700 and chmod setting the final permissions, during which the directory could be replaced with a symlink. This could be used for privilege escalation or to make sensitive files world-readable/writable.

vulnerabilities.short_description: chmod uses full st_mode including setuid/setgid bits from source
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code passes `s.st_mode` directly to chmod, which includes not only permission bits but also setuid (S_ISUID), setgid (S_ISGID), and sticky (S_ISVTX) bits. If the source directory has setuid or setgid bits set, these will be blindly replicated to the destination directory. This could lead to privilege escalation if an attacker can control the source path or its permissions. The code should mask the mode with 07777 or, more conservatively, with 0777 to strip special bits.

vulnerabilities.short_description: TOCTOU race on stat() of source path allows symlink-based manipulation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function uses stat() which follows symlinks to check whether each source path component is a directory. An attacker who can modify the source path (e.g., by placing a symlink) can make stat() resolve to a different directory, causing the function to use that directory's mode bits (potentially including setuid/setgid) when creating and setting permissions on the destination. The time gap between the stat check and the subsequent mkdir/chmod operations creates a race window that can be exploited.

commit_message:
drivers: hamradio: 6pack: fix UAF bug caused by mod_timer()

When a 6pack device is detaching, the sixpack_close() will act to cleanup
necessary resources. Although del_timer_sync() in sixpack_close()
won't return if there is an active timer, one could use mod_timer() in
sp_xmit_on_air() to wake up timer again by calling userspace syscall such
as ax25_sendmsg(), ax25_connect() and ax25_ioctl().

This unexpected waked handler, sp_xmit_on_air(), realizes nothing about
the undergoing cleanup and may still call pty_write() to use driver layer
resources that have already been released.

One of the possible race conditions is shown below:

      (USE)                      |      (FREE)
ax25_sendmsg()                   |
 ax25_queue_xmit()               |
  ...                            |
  sp_xmit()                      |
   sp_encaps()                   | sixpack_close()
    sp_xmit_on_air()             |  del_timer_sync(&sp->tx_t)
     mod_timer(&sp->tx_t,...)    |  ...
                                 |  unregister_netdev()
                                 |  ...
     (wait a while)              | tty_release()
                                 |  tty_release_struct()
                                 |   release_tty()
    sp_xmit_on_air()             |    tty_kref_put(tty_struct) //FREE
     pty_write(tty_struct) //USE |    ...

The corresponding fail log is shown below:
===============================================================
BUG: KASAN: use-after-free in __run_timers.part.0+0x170/0x470
Write of size 8 at addr ffff88800a652ab8 by task swapper/2/0
...
Call Trace:
  ...
  queue_work_on+0x3f/0x50
  pty_write+0xcd/0xe0pty_write+0xcd/0xe0
  sp_xmit_on_air+0xb2/0x1f0
  call_timer_fn+0x28/0x150
  __run_timers.part.0+0x3c2/0x470
  run_timer_softirq+0x3b/0x80
  __do_softirq+0xf1/0x380
  ...

This patch reorders the del_timer_sync() after the unregister_netdev()
to avoid UAF bugs. Because the unregister_netdev() is well synchronized,
it flushs out any pending queues, waits the refcount of net_device
decreases to zero and removes net_device from kernel. There is not any
running routines after executing unregister_netdev(). Therefore, we could
not arouse timer from userspace again.

Signed-off-by: Duoming Zhou <duoming@zju.edu.cn>
Reviewed-by: Lin Ma <linma@zju.edu.cn>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: A use-after-free vulnerabilitity was discovered in drivers/net/hamradio/6pack.c of linux that allows an attacker to crash linux kernel by simulating ax25 device using 6pack driver from user space.

commit_message:
ovl: prevent private clone if bind mount is not allowed

Add the following checks from __do_loopback() to clone_private_mount() as
well:

 - verify that the mount is in the current namespace

 - verify that there are no locked children

Reported-by: Alois Wohlschlager <alois1@gmx-topmail.de>
Fixes: c771d683a62e ("vfs: introduce clone_private_mount()")
Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org> # v3.18
Signed-off-by: Miklos Szeredi <mszeredi@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in the Linux kernel's OverlayFS subsystem in the way the user mounts the TmpFS filesystem with OverlayFS. This flaw allows a local user to gain access to hidden files that should not be accessible.

commit_message:
Fix heap buffer overflow in stl_update_connects_remove_1


- Add argument value check to the stl_update_connects_remove_1
- Add neighbor value check in stl_remove_degenerate

Fixes https://github.com/admesh/admesh/issues/28
Merges https://github.com/admesh/admesh/pull/55
cve_desc: ADMesh through 0.98.4 has a heap-based buffer over-read in stl_update_connects_remove_1 (called from stl_remove_degenerate) in connect.c in libadmesh.a.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Array index used without bounds validation for neighbor indices
vulnerabilities.long_description: The values neighbor1, neighbor2, and neighbor3 are read from the stl->neighbors_start data structure and used directly as array indices into stl->neighbors_start[] without any upper-bound validation (only checked against -1). If the data is corrupted or maliciously crafted, these values could be negative (other than -1) or exceed the array bounds, leading to out-of-bounds read/write. This could allow arbitrary memory corruption and potential code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: No bounds check on the 'facet' parameter before array access
vulnerabilities.long_description: The 'facet' parameter is used as an index into stl->facet_start[] and stl->neighbors_start[] without any validation that it is within the valid range (0 to stl->stats.number_of_facets - 1). A caller passing an out-of-range value would cause out-of-bounds memory access, leading to information disclosure or memory corruption.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4151: reading beyond the end of a line

Problem:    Reading beyond the end of a line.
Solution:   For block insert only use the offset for correcting the length.
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in vim/vim prior to 8.2.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Truncation of size_t to unsigned int for string length can cause undersized allocation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `s_len` is declared as `unsigned` (typically 32 bits) but is assigned from `STRLEN(s)` which returns `size_t` (64 bits on 64-bit platforms). If the string `s` is longer than UINT_MAX (approximately 4 GB), the value silently wraps around. This truncated `s_len` is then used in the allocation size calculation (`alloc(STRLEN(oldp) + spaces + s_len + ...)`). Since `STRLEN(oldp)` is `size_t` and `s_len` is a truncated `unsigned`, the computed allocation size will be too small. While the subsequent `mch_memmove` also uses the truncated `(size_t)s_len` (which zero-extends from `unsigned`, thus also truncated), the overall allocation arithmetic mixing `size_t` and the truncated `unsigned` could yield a buffer smaller than what subsequent operations (e.g., `STRMOVE` copying the rest of `oldp`) expect, potentially leading to a heap buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Signed integer `count` can be negative, causing allocation size underflow via unsigned wrap
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `count` is a signed `int` that participates in the allocation size expression which evaluates as `size_t` (unsigned). In certain code paths, `count` can remain set to `ts_val - 1` even when `spaces` is subsequently clamped to zero (e.g., if `bdp->startspaces` or `bdp->endspaces` was negative). If `ts_val` is 0, then `count` becomes -1. When -1 is implicitly converted to `size_t` in the addition, it wraps to a very large value, likely causing `alloc` to fail and return NULL (handled by `continue`). However, in edge cases where the arithmetic wraps to a small positive value (e.g., on 32-bit systems with specific input sizes), the allocation could succeed with an undersized buffer, and the subsequent memory operations (`mch_memmove`, `vim_memset`, `STRMOVE`) would write beyond the allocated buffer, leading to heap corruption.

commit_message:
Fix heap buffer overflow in stl_update_connects_remove_1


- Add argument value check to the stl_update_connects_remove_1
- Add neighbor value check in stl_remove_degenerate

Fixes https://github.com/admesh/admesh/issues/28
Merges https://github.com/admesh/admesh/pull/55
cve_desc: ADMesh through 0.98.4 has a heap-based buffer over-read in stl_update_connects_remove_1 (called from stl_remove_degenerate) in connect.c in libadmesh.a.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds array access via unvalidated neighbor indices and vnot values
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variables neighbor1, neighbor2, neighbor3 are read from the stl->neighbors_start array and used directly as indices into the same array without any bounds validation (only checked against -1). If the STL data is malformed or corrupt, these values could be negative (other than -1) or exceed the allocated array size, leading to out-of-bounds reads and writes. Similarly, vnot1/vnot2/vnot3 are used in the expression (vnot + 1) % 3 without validation. In C, if vnot is negative, the result of the modulo operation is implementation-defined and could yield a negative index, causing further out-of-bounds access. An attacker providing a crafted STL file could exploit this to achieve arbitrary memory read/write.

vulnerabilities.short_description: No bounds validation on the 'facet' parameter before array access
vulnerabilities.long_description: The 'facet' parameter is used to index into stl->facet_start and stl->neighbors_start arrays without any check that it is within valid bounds (0 to stl->stats.number_of_facets - 1). A caller passing a negative or overly large facet index would cause out-of-bounds memory access, potentially leading to information disclosure or code execution.

commit_message:
fuse: fix live lock in fuse_iget()

Commit 5d069dbe8aaf ("fuse: fix bad inode") replaced make_bad_inode()
in fuse_iget() with a private implementation fuse_make_bad().

The private implementation fails to remove the bad inode from inode
cache, so the retry loop with iget5_locked() finds the same bad inode
and marks it bad forever.

kmsg snip:

[ ] rcu: INFO: rcu_sched self-detected stall on CPU
...
[ ]  ? bit_wait_io+0x50/0x50
[ ]  ? fuse_init_file_inode+0x70/0x70
[ ]  ? find_inode.isra.32+0x60/0xb0
[ ]  ? fuse_init_file_inode+0x70/0x70

commit_message:
fuse: fix live lock in fuse_iget()

Commit 5d069dbe8aaf ("fuse: fix bad inode") replaced make_bad_inode()
in fuse_iget() with a private implementation fuse_make_bad().

The private implementation fails to remove the bad inode from inode
cache, so the retry loop with iget5_locked() finds the same bad inode
and marks it bad forever.

kmsg snip:

[ ] rcu: INFO: rcu_sched self-detected stall on CPU
...
[ ]  ? bit_wait_io+0x50/0x50
[ ]  ? fuse_init_file_inode+0x70/0x70
[ ]  ? find_inode.isra.32+0x60/0xb0
[ ]  ? fuse_init_file_inode+0x70/0x70
[ ]  ilookup5_nowait+0x65/0x90
[ ]  ? fuse_init_file_inode+0x70/0x70
[ ]  ilookup5.part.36+0x2e/0x80
[ ]  ? fuse_init_file_inode+0x70/0x70
[ ]  ? fuse_inode_eq+0x20/0x20
[ ]  iget5_locked+0x21/0x80
[ ]  ? fuse_inode_eq+0x20/0x20
[ ]  fuse_iget+0x96/0x1b0

Fixes: 5d069dbe8aaf ("fuse: fix bad inode")

A user opening a malicious PYC file could be affected by this vulnerability, allowing an attacker to execute code on the user's machine. vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of 'ret' when malloc(n1 + 1) for s1 fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: When the allocation of s1 fails, the function returns NULL without freeing the previously allocated 'ret' object, causing a memory leak.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of 'ret' and 's1' when n2 error/overflow check fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: When the error or overflow check for n2 triggers, the function returns NULL without freeing the previously allocated 'ret' and 's1', causing memory leaks.

commit_message:
ipf: release unhandled packets from the batch

Since 640d4db788ed ("ipf: Fix a use-after-free error, ...") the ipf
framework unconditionally allocates a new dp_packet to track
individual fragments.  This prevents a use-after-free.  However, an
additional issue was present - even when the packet buffer is cloned,
if the ip fragment handling code keeps it, the original buffer is
leaked during the refill loop.  Even in the original processing code,
the hardcoded dnsteal branches would always leak a packet buffer from
the refill loop.

This can be confirmed with valgrind:

==717566== 16,672 (4,480 direct, 12,192 indirect) bytes in 8 blocks are definitely lost in loss record 390 of 390
==717566==    at 0x484086F: malloc (vg_replace_malloc.c:380)
==717566==    by 0x537BFD: xmalloc__ (util.c:137)
==717566==    by 0x537BFD: xmalloc (util.c:172)
==717566==    by 0x46DDD4: dp_packet_new (dp-packet.c:153)
==717566==    by 0x46DDD4: dp_packet_new_with_headroom (dp-packet.c:163)
==717566==    by 0x550AA6: netdev_linux_batch_rxq_recv_sock.constprop.0 (netdev-linux.c:1262)
==717566==    by 0x5512AF: netdev_linux_rxq_recv (netdev-linux.c:1511)
==717566==    by 0x4AB7E0: netdev_rxq_recv (netdev.c:727)
==717566==    by 0x47F00D: dp_netdev_process_rxq_port (dpif-netdev.c:4699)
==717566==    by 0x47FD13: dpif_netdev_run (dpif-netdev.c:5957)
==717566==    by 0x4331D2: type_run (ofproto-dpif.c:370)
==717566==    by 0x41DFD8: ofproto_type_run (ofproto.c:1768)
==717566==    by 0x40A7FB: bridge_run__ (bridge.c:3245)
==717566==    by 0x411269: bridge_run (bridge.c:3310)
==717566==    by 0x406E6C: main (ovs-vswitchd.c:127)

The fix is to delete the original packet when it isn't able to be
reinserted into the packet batch.  Subsequent valgrind runs show that
the packets are not leaked from the batch any longer.

Fixes: 640d4db788ed ("ipf: Fix a use-after-free error, and remove the 'do_not_steal' flag.")
Fixes: 4ea96698f667 ("Userspace datapath: Add fragmentation handling.")
Reported-by: Wan Junjie <wanjunjie@bytedance.com>
Reported-at: https://github.com/openvswitch/ovs-issues/issues/226
Signed-off-by: Aaron Conole <aconole@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: David Marchand <david.marchand@redhat.com>
Tested-by: Wan Junjie <wanjunjie@bytedance.com>
Signed-off-by: Alin-Gabriel Serdean <aserdean@ovn.org>
cve_desc: A memory leak was found in Open vSwitch (OVS) during userspace IP fragmentation processing. An attacker could use this flaw to potentially exhaust available memory by keeping sending packet fragments.

commit_message:
media: em28xx: initialize refcount before kref_get

The commit 47677e51e2a4("[media] em28xx: Only deallocate struct
em28xx after finishing all extensions") adds kref_get to many init
functions (e.g., em28xx_audio_init). However, kref_init is called too
late in em28xx_usb_probe, since em28xx_init_dev before will invoke
those init functions and call kref_get function. Then refcount bug
occurs in my local syzkaller instance.

Fix it by moving kref_init before em28xx_init_dev. This issue occurs
not only in dev but also dev->dev_next.

Fixes: 47677e51e2a4 ("[media] em28xx: Only deallocate struct em28xx after finishing all extensions")
Reported-by: syzkaller <syzkaller@googlegroups.com>
Signed-off-by: Dongliang Mu <mudongliangabcd@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Hans Verkuil <hverkuil-cisco@xs4all.nl>
cve_desc: A flaw use after free in the Linux kernel video4linux driver was found in the way user triggers em28xx_usb_probe() for the Empia 28xx based TV cards. A local user could use this flaw to crash the system or potentially escalate their privileges on the system.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Use-after-free: usb_set_intfdata stores a pointer to dev that is not cleared on the err_free path
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function calls usb_set_intfdata(intf, dev) to store a pointer to the device structure in the USB interface. If em28xx_init_dev() or subsequent operations fail and execution jumps to err_free, the dev structure is freed via kfree(dev), but usb_set_intfdata(intf, NULL) is never called. This leaves a dangling pointer in the USB interface data. If any other code (e.g., the USB core's disconnect handler) subsequently calls usb_get_intfdata(intf), it will obtain a pointer to freed memory, leading to a use-after-free condition. An attacker with physical access could trigger this by using a specially crafted USB device that causes em28xx_init_dev to fail.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing cleanup of dev->dev_next on error after em28xx_duplicate_dev succeeds
vulnerabilities.long_description: When em28xx_duplicate_dev() succeeds, it allocates a second device structure stored in dev->dev_next. If the subsequent em28xx_init_dev() call for dev->dev_next fails, the code jumps to err_free, which only frees dev and dev->alt_max_pkt_size_isoc. The dev->dev_next structure and its associated resources (including its alt_max_pkt_size_isoc, devno bit, etc.) are leaked. Additionally, the first device's em28xx_init_dev() has already been called successfully, but its cleanup path is not invoked, potentially leaving partially-initialized resources behind. This constitutes a resource leak and could lead to further issues if the leaked devno slot is reused inconsistently.

commit_message:
CamelGpgContext: Enclose email addresses in brackets.

The recipient list for encrypting can be specified by either key ID or
email address.  Enclose email addresses in brackets to ensure an exact
match, as per the gpg man page:

HOW TO SPECIFY A USER ID

       ...

       By exact match on an email address.
              This is indicated by enclosing the email address in the
              usual way with left and right angles.

         <heinrichh@uni-duesseldorf.de>

Without the brackets gpg uses a substring match, which risks selecting
the wrong recipient.
cve_desc: The gpg_ctx_add_recipient function in camel/camel-gpg-context.c in GNOME Evolution 3.8.4 and earlier and Evolution Data Server 3.9.5 and earlier does not properly select the GPG key to use for email encryption, which might cause the email to be encrypted with the wrong key and allow remote attackers to obtain sensitive information.

commit_message:
CamelGpgContext: Enclose email addresses in brackets.

The recipient list for encrypting can be specified by either key ID or
email address.  Enclose email addresses in brackets to ensure an exact
match, as per the gpg man page:

HOW TO SPECIFY A USER ID

       ...

       By exact match on an email address.
              This is indicated by enclosing the email address in the
              usual way with left and right angles.

         <heinrichh@uni-duesseldorf.de>

Without the brackets gpg uses a substring match, which risks selecting
the wrong recipient.
cve_desc: The gpg_ctx_add_recipient function in camel/camel-gpg-context.c in GNOME Evolution 3.8.4 and earlier and Evolution Data Server 3.9.5 and earlier does not properly select the GPG key to use for email encryption, which might cause the email to be encrypted with the wrong key and allow remote attackers to obtain sensitive information.

commit_message:
media: em28xx: initialize refcount before kref_get

The commit 47677e51e2a4("[media] em28xx: Only deallocate struct
em28xx after finishing all extensions") adds kref_get to many init
functions (e.g., em28xx_audio_init). However, kref_init is called too
late in em28xx_usb_probe, since em28xx_init_dev before will invoke
those init functions and call kref_get function. Then refcount bug
occurs in my local syzkaller instance.

Fix it by moving kref_init before em28xx_init_dev. This issue occurs
not only in dev but also dev->dev_next.

Fixes: 47677e51e2a4 ("[media] em28xx: Only deallocate struct em28xx after finishing all extensions")
Reported-by: syzkaller <syzkaller@googlegroups.com>
Signed-off-by: Dongliang Mu <mudongliangabcd@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Hans Verkuil <hverkuil-cisco@xs4all.nl>
cve_desc: A flaw use after free in the Linux kernel video4linux driver was found in the way user triggers em28xx_usb_probe() for the Empia 28xx based TV cards. A local user could use this flaw to crash the system or potentially escalate their privileges on the system.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Use-after-free: usb_set_intfdata stores pointer to dev which is freed in error path without clearing it
vulnerabilities.long_description: After `usb_set_intfdata(intf, dev)` is called, several subsequent error conditions (e.g., failure in `em28xx_init_dev`, speed check failure, dual-ts init failure) jump to `err_free`, which calls `kfree(dev)` without first calling `usb_set_intfdata(intf, NULL)`. This leaves a dangling pointer stored in the USB interface's driver data. When the USB core later accesses this pointer (e.g., during disconnect callbacks or other interface operations), it dereferences freed memory, leading to a use-after-free condition. An attacker with physical access could craft a malicious USB device that triggers these error paths, potentially achieving kernel code execution or denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Incomplete cleanup in error path after dual-ts initialization leaks dev_next and resources from first em28xx_init_dev
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `em28xx_duplicate_dev(dev)` succeeds (allocating `dev->dev_next`) and then `em28xx_init_dev(dev->dev_next, ...)` fails, the code jumps to `err_free` which only frees `dev->alt_max_pkt_size_isoc` and `dev`. It does not free `dev->dev_next` or tear down any resources initialized by the first successful `em28xx_init_dev(dev, ...)` call. This leads to memory leaks and potentially leaves hardware or kernel resources in an inconsistent state. Similarly, any error after the first `em28xx_init_dev` succeeds (such as the has_dual_ts path failing) frees `dev` without properly uninitializing the device, which could leave dangling references in subsystems that were registered.

commit_message: fix #2963 - oob write (1 byte) in pyc/marshal.c
cve_desc: Rizin is a UNIX-like reverse engineering framework and command-line toolset. Versions 0.4.0 and prior are vulnerable to an out-of-bounds write when getting data from PYC(python) files. A user opening a malicious PYC file could be affected by this vulnerability, allowing an attacker to execute code on the user's machine. Commit number 68948017423a12786704e54227b8b2f918c2fd27 contains a patch.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in malloc size leads to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `get_st32` is used (for magic_int > 62061), it returns a signed 32-bit value stored in a ut32. A value like -1 (0xFFFFFFFF) causes `n1 + 1` or `n2 + 1` to wrap around to 0. `malloc(0)` may return a valid small pointer, after which `rz_buf_read(buffer, s1, 0xFFFFFFFF)` attempts to write up to ~4GB into the tiny buffer, causing a massive heap buffer overflow. An attacker crafting a malicious .pyc file can control the size field to trigger this and achieve arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of s1 and ret on multiple early-return error paths
vulnerabilities.long_description: After allocating and populating `s1` and `ret`, several error paths return NULL without freeing them. Specifically: (1) when `get_ut8`/`get_st32` for n2 fails (`error` is true), both `s1` and `ret` are leaked; (2) when `malloc(n2 + 1)` fails, both `s1` and `ret` are leaked. While primarily a resource leak, in long-running processes parsing many malicious inputs, this can lead to memory exhaustion (denial of service).

commit_message: use mkstemp for sdb
cve_desc: In Singular before 4.3.1, a predictable /tmp pathname is used (e.g., by sdb.cc), which allows local users to gain the privileges of other users via a procedure in a file under /tmp. NOTE: this CVE Record is about sdb.cc and similar files in the Singular interface that have predictable /tmp pathnames; this CVE Record is not about the lack of a safe temporary-file creation capability in the Singular language.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Buffer overflow when formatting PID into fixed-size filename buffer
vulnerabilities.long_description: The filename buffer is allocated to hold exactly 14 bytes (strlen("/tmp/sd000000") + 1). The sprintf writes the PID starting at offset 7, leaving only 7 bytes (6 characters + null). On systems where PIDs can be larger than 999999 (e.g., Linux allows up to ~4 million with pid_max), the PID string can exceed 6 characters, causing a heap buffer overflow. This could lead to heap corruption, crashes, or potentially arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Insecure temporary file creation with predictable name in /tmp
vulnerabilities.long_description: The temporary file is created in /tmp with a predictable name based on the PID. This is vulnerable to symlink attacks (TOCTOU race condition): an attacker can create a symbolic link at /tmp/sd<PID> pointing to a sensitive file before this function runs. When the function opens the file for writing, it will overwrite the target of the symlink. Later, the function reads the file back and uses its contents, enabling potential data injection. Functions like mkstemp() should be used instead.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Command injection via EDITOR environment variable passed to system()
vulnerabilities.long_description: When the EDITOR environment variable contains spaces, the code constructs a command string and passes it to system(), which invokes a shell. A malicious user (or attacker who can influence the environment) can set EDITOR to something like 'cmd; malicious_command' to execute arbitrary commands. Even in the execlp path, no validation is done, though that path is less exploitable. The system() call is the primary concern as it allows full shell injection.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer truncation casting long file length to int for allocation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The file length is obtained as a long from ftell() but cast to int when passed to omAlloc(). If the file is larger than INT_MAX bytes, this cast truncates the value, potentially resulting in a much smaller allocation than needed. The subsequent myfread() would then write beyond the allocated buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow. Additionally, if len is negative (ftell error), the cast to int and addition of 1 could produce unexpected allocation sizes.

commit_message: use mkstemp for sdb
cve_desc: In Singular before 4.3.1, a predictable /tmp pathname is used (e.g., by sdb.cc), which allows local users to gain the privileges of other users via a procedure in a file under /tmp. NOTE: this CVE Record is about sdb.cc and similar files in the Singular interface that have predictable /tmp pathnames; this CVE Record is not about the lack of a safe temporary-file creation capability in the Singular language.

vulnerabilities.short_description: OS command injection via EDITOR/VISUAL environment variable
vulnerabilities.long_description: The EDITOR or VISUAL environment variable is passed to system() without any sanitization. An attacker who can control these environment variables can inject arbitrary shell commands (e.g., EDITOR="vi; rm -rf /"). The code only checks if the editor string contains a space to decide whether to use execlp or system(), but a malicious value with spaces will be passed directly to system() which invokes a shell, enabling full command injection.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer truncation when casting ftell result from long to int
vulnerabilities.long_description: The file length is obtained as a `long` from ftell() but is cast to `int` when passed to omAlloc. If the file is larger than INT_MAX, this truncation could result in a much smaller buffer being allocated, leading to a heap buffer overflow when myfread writes `len` bytes into the undersized buffer.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Temporary file created in /tmp without secure directory, potential for symlink attacks after unlink/reopen
vulnerabilities.long_description: While mkstemp itself is safe, the file is closed and later reopened with fopen(filename, "r"). Between the close and the fopen, an attacker could replace the file (e.g., via a symlink attack or race condition), causing the program to read attacker-controlled content into the procedure body. The file is also edited by a forked child process, creating a window for TOCTOU issues.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Assigning NULL (0) to int file descriptor instead of -1, and missing cleanup of temp file on error path
vulnerabilities.long_description: When pi->language != LANG_SINGULAR, the file descriptor is closed but the temporary file is not unlinked before falling through to the end of the function. Also, assigning NULL to an int is semantically wrong (sets f to 0, which is a valid fd: stdin), though this is more of a correctness issue. The temp file leak is a minor resource issue.

commit_message:
Make ZlibInStream more robust against failures

Move the checks around to avoid missing cases where we might access
memory that is no longer valid. Also avoid touching the underlying
stream implicitly (e.g. via the destructor) as it might also no
longer be valid.

A malicious server could theoretically use this for remote code
execution in the client.

Issue found by Pavel Cheremushkin from Kaspersky Lab
cve_desc: TigerVNC version prior to 1.10.1 is vulnerable to stack use-after-return, which occurs due to incorrect usage of stack memory in ZRLEDecoder. If decoding routine would throw an exception, ZRLEDecoder may try to access stack variable, which has been already freed during the process of stack unwinding. Exploitation of this vulnerability could potentially result into remote code execution. This attack appear to be exploitable via network connectivity.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read of uninitialized palette entries via attacker-controlled index
vulnerabilities.long_description: In both the packed-pixels and palette-RLE decoding paths, the index used to look up into the `palette` array is derived from attacker-controlled stream data and is only masked to 0-127, but the palette is only populated with `palSize` entries (which can be as low as 2). Since `palette` is a stack-allocated array of 128 elements, accessing `palette[index]` where `index >= palSize` reads uninitialized stack memory. A malicious server can craft a ZRLE-encoded rectangle with a small palette size but embed indices that reference uninitialized palette slots, causing uninitialized stack data (potentially containing sensitive information like pointers or other secrets) to be written into the framebuffer and displayed to the user. This is an information disclosure vulnerability.

commit_message:
Bug 790784 - (CVE-2017-17784) heap overread in gbr parser / load_image.

We were assuming the input name was well formed, hence was
nul-terminated. As any data coming from external input, this has to be
thorougly checked.
Similar to commit 06d24a79af94837d615d0024916bb95a01bf3c59 but adapted
to older gimp-2-8 code.
cve_desc: In GIMP 2.8.22, there is a heap-based buffer over-read in load_image in plug-ins/common/file-gbr.c in the gbr import parser, related to mishandling of UTF-8 data.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in allocation size when reading .gpb pattern data
vulnerabilities.long_description: When a pattern follows the brush data (obsolete .gpb format), the code allocates `4 * bh.width * bh.height` bytes without an overflow check. The original sanitization only verified `G_MAXSIZE / bh.width / bh.height / bh.bytes < 1` with `bh.bytes == 1`, meaning `bh.width * bh.height` can be up to G_MAXSIZE. Multiplying by 4 can then overflow, resulting in a small allocation followed by out-of-bounds writes when filling brush_buf.

vulnerabilities.short_description: File descriptor leak when header sanitization fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: If the header data validation check fails (invalid width, height, bytes, or overflow check), the function returns -1 without calling `close(fd)`. The file descriptor opened earlier via `g_open()` is leaked. Repeated exploitation could exhaust file descriptors, leading to denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Signed/unsigned comparison between read() return value and gsize
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `read()` system call returns `ssize_t` (signed), which is compared against `size` of type `gsize` (unsigned). If `size` exceeds `SSIZE_MAX`, `read()` may return a truncated or implementation-defined value, and the comparison could incorrectly succeed, leading to use of partially initialized buffer data. This is possible since the overflow check allows `width * height * bytes` up to `G_MAXSIZE`.

commit_message: do not attempt to write a null image list (thanks to Vinay Rohila)
cve_desc: In ImageMagick, a crafted file could trigger an assertion failure when a call to WriteImages was made in MagickWand/operation.c, due to a NULL image list. This could potentially cause a denial of service. This was fixed in upstream ImageMagick version 7.1.0-30.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential double-free of arg1 when the 'read' option is processed with percent-escaped arguments
vulnerabilities.long_description: When percent escape interpretation is enabled, arg1 is set to a newly allocated string (different from arg1n). In the 'read' handler, argv is initialized to point at arg1, and after ExpandFilenames, the code destroys each argv[i] via DestroyString. If ExpandFilenames does not actually expand (e.g., no glob characters), argv[0] may still reference the same allocated string as arg1. After argv[0] is destroyed, arg1 becomes a dangling pointer. The cleanup code at the end of the function then checks `arg1 != arg1n` (which is true since arg1 was the interpreted version) and calls DestroyString on the already-freed pointer, resulting in a double-free. This could lead to heap corruption and potentially arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of initially percent-escaped arg1/arg2 when 'set' option re-interprets arguments
vulnerabilities.long_description: When percent escape interpretation is enabled at the top of the function, arg1 and arg2 are set to newly allocated interpreted strings. In the 'set' handler, arg1 is unconditionally overwritten by a new call to InterpretImageProperties(arg1n), and arg2 is overwritten (either set to NULL or a new interpretation). The previously allocated interpreted strings from the top of the function are never freed, causing a memory leak. While primarily a resource leak, repeated exploitation could lead to denial of service through memory exhaustion.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.5162: reading before the start of the line with BS in Replace mode

Problem:    Reading before the start of the line with BS in Replace mode.
Solution:   Check the cursor column is more than zero.
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

commit_message:
Make ZlibInStream more robust against failures

Move the checks around to avoid missing cases where we might access
memory that is no longer valid. Also avoid touching the underlying
stream implicitly (e.g. via the destructor) as it might also no
longer be valid.

A malicious server could theoretically use this for remote code
execution in the client.

Issue found by Pavel Cheremushkin from Kaspersky Lab
cve_desc: TigerVNC version prior to 1.10.1 is vulnerable to stack use-after-return, which occurs due to incorrect usage of stack memory in ZRLEDecoder. If decoding routine would throw an exception, ZRLEDecoder may try to access stack variable, which has been already freed during the process of stack unwinding. Exploitation of this vulnerability could potentially result into remote code execution. This attack appear to be exploitable via network connectivity.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read on palette array due to insufficient index validation
vulnerabilities.long_description: In both the packed-pixels and palette-RLE decoding paths, the index used to access the `palette[128]` array is not validated against the actual `palSize`. In the packed-pixels path, the index is masked to fit `bppp` bits and ANDed with 127, but a crafted stream can still produce an index value >= palSize. In the palette-RLE path, the index is only masked with `& 127`. Since `palette` is a stack-allocated array of 128 entries and only the first `palSize` entries are initialized, an out-of-range index reads uninitialized stack memory. This could leak sensitive stack data into the framebuffer, or cause unpredictable behavior.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in RLE run length accumulation can bypass bounds check
vulnerabilities.long_description: In both the plain-RLE and palette-RLE decoding paths, `len` is an `int` that starts at 1 and is incremented by up to 255 per iteration in a loop that continues as long as bytes of value 255 are read from the stream. A malicious server can send a long sequence of 0xFF bytes causing `len` to overflow the 32-bit signed integer, resulting in undefined behavior. If `len` wraps to a negative value, the subsequent check `end - ptr < len` will not trigger (since a positive value is not less than a negative value), and the following write loop `while (len-- > 0)` may behave unpredictably. Depending on the compiler, this could lead to a heap/stack buffer overflow when writing pixels.
