commit_message:
Fix #73280: Stack Buffer Overflow in GD dynamicGetbuf

We make sure to never pass a negative `rlen` as size to memcpy().

Cf. <https://github.com/libgd/libgd/commit/53110871>.
cve_desc: Integer signedness error in the dynamicGetbuf function in gd_io_dp.c in the GD Graphics Library (aka libgd) through 2.2.3, as used in PHP before 5.6.28 and 7.x before 7.0.13, allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (stack-based buffer overflow) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a crafted imagecreatefromstring call.

commit_message:
Fix #73280: Stack Buffer Overflow in GD dynamicGetbuf

We make sure to never pass a negative `rlen` as size to memcpy().

Cf. <https://github.com/libgd/libgd/commit/53110871>.
cve_desc: Integer signedness error in the dynamicGetbuf function in gd_io_dp.c in the GD Graphics Library (aka libgd) through 2.2.3, as used in PHP before 5.6.28 and 7.x before 7.0.13, allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (stack-based buffer overflow) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a crafted imagecreatefromstring call.

commit_message: Fixed bug #22965 (Crash in gd lib's ImageFillToBorder()).
cve_desc: Stack consumption vulnerability in GD in PHP before 5.6.12 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service via a crafted imagefilltoborder call.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Unbounded recursion can cause stack overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function uses recursive calls to fill adjacent lines above and below. For a large image with no border pixels, this can recurse to a depth proportional to the image height (potentially thousands of frames), exhausting the stack and causing a crash (denial of service). An attacker who controls the image dimensions and content can craft an image that triggers deep recursion leading to a stack overflow.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing validation for negative x and y coordinates leads to out-of-bounds access
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function clamps x and y when they exceed the image dimensions but does not check for negative values. If a caller passes negative x or y, the function will call gdImageGetPixel and gdImageSetPixel with negative coordinates, potentially resulting in out-of-bounds memory reads and writes. This could be exploited to read or corrupt memory outside the image pixel buffer, leading to information disclosure, crashes, or arbitrary code execution.

commit_message:
Fix UAF in aaaa on arm/thumb switching ##crash

* Reported by @peacock-doris via huntr.dev
* Reproducer tests_65185
* This is a logic fix, but not the fully safe as changes in the code
  can result on UAF again, to properly protect r2 from crashing we
  need to break the ABI and add refcounting to RRegItem, which can't
  happen in 5.6.x because of abi-compat rules
cve_desc: Use After Free in op_is_set_bp in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.6.

code_snippets: for (i = from; stack_rev ? i >= to : i < to; stack_rev ? i-- : i++) {
vulnerabilities.short_description: Unsigned integer underflow in loop counter causes infinite loop or out-of-bounds access
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `i` is of type `size_t` (unsigned). When `stack_rev` is true, the loop decrements `i` with the condition `i >= to`. If `to` is 0 (which occurs when `r_anal_cc_max_arg` returns 0 or `fcn->cc` is NULL), then when `i` decrements past 0, it wraps around to SIZE_MAX due to unsigned integer underflow. Since SIZE_MAX >= 0 is always true, the loop becomes effectively infinite, reading arbitrary type/argument data via `r_type_func_args_type` and `r_type_func_args_name` with ever-increasing (wrapped) index values. This can lead to denial of service (infinite loop / excessive memory consumption) or potentially out-of-bounds memory reads depending on how those functions handle large index values.

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0025: accessing beyond allocated memory with the cmdline window

Problem:    Accessing beyond allocated memory when using the cmdline window in
            Ex mode.
Solution:   Use "*" instead of "'<,'>" for Visual mode.
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Write in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Off-by-one heap buffer overflow when rewriting visual range with plus command
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `has_visual_range` is true and `use_plus_cmd` is true and modifiers were parsed (eap->cmd > cmd_start), the code rewrites the command buffer in-place. The original buffer layout is: "'<,'>" (5 bytes) + cmd_start content (len bytes) + '\0' (1 byte) = len + 6 bytes total. After the transformation, the code writes: cmd_start content (len bytes) + "'<,'>+" (6 bytes) + '\0' (1 byte) = len + 7 bytes. This exceeds the original buffer size by 1 byte, causing an out-of-bounds write. The extra '+' character that is appended was not present in the original string, so the buffer is not large enough to hold it. An attacker who can control command input in visual mode (e.g., via a crafted modeline or script) could trigger this overflow to corrupt adjacent heap memory, potentially leading to code execution.

commit_message:
Fix UAF in aaaa on arm/thumb switching ##crash

* Reported by @peacock-doris via huntr.dev
* Reproducer tests_65185
* This is a logic fix, but not the fully safe as changes in the code
  can result on UAF again, to properly protect r2 from crashing we
  need to break the ABI and add refcounting to RRegItem, which can't
  happen in 5.6.x because of abi-compat rules
cve_desc: Use After Free in op_is_set_bp in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.6.

code_snippets: for (i = from; stack_rev ? i >= to : i < to; stack_rev ? i-- : i++) {
vulnerabilities.short_description: Unsigned integer underflow in stack_rev loop causes infinite loop or out-of-bounds access
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `stack_rev` is true, the loop variable `i` is of type `size_t` (unsigned). The variable `to` can be 0 (from `r_anal_cc_max_arg`). When `i` is decremented past 0 via `i--`, it wraps around to SIZE_MAX due to unsigned integer underflow. The loop condition `i >= to` (i.e., `i >= 0`) will always be true for an unsigned type, leading to an effectively infinite loop that reads out-of-bounds data via `r_type_func_args_type` and `r_type_func_args_name` with extremely large index values. This could cause denial of service or memory corruption depending on how those functions handle the invalid index.

commit_message:
Calcualte max nspace declarations correctly for XML writer

(raptor_xml_writer_start_element_common): Calculate max including for
each attribute a potential name and value.

Fixes Issues #0000617 http://bugs.librdf.org/mantis/view.php?id=617
and #0000618 http://bugs.librdf.org/mantis/view.php?id=618
cve_desc: raptor_xml_writer_start_element_common in raptor_xml_writer.c in Raptor RDF Syntax Library 2.0.15 miscalculates the maximum nspace declarations for the XML writer, leading to heap-based buffer overflows (sometimes seen in raptor_qname_format_as_xml).

vulnerabilities.short_description: Heap buffer overflow due to undersized nspace_declarations allocation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The allocation size `nspace_max_count` is computed as `attribute_count + 1 + declared_nspaces + (xml_language ? 1 : 0)`. However, in the attribute processing loop, each attribute can contribute up to TWO entries to `nspace_declarations`: one for its namespace declaration and one for the attribute key=value itself. This means the actual maximum needed is `2 * attribute_count + 1 + ...`. When many attributes have out-of-scope namespaces, writes to `nspace_declarations[nspace_declarations_count]` will exceed the allocated buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow that could lead to arbitrary code execution.

code_snippets:
for(j = 0; j < nspace_declarations_count; j++)
            if(nspace_declarations[j].nspace == element->attributes[j]->nspace) {
vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read due to using wrong index variable `j` instead of `i` for element->attributes
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the inner deduplication loop, the code checks `element->attributes[j]->nspace` but should check `element->attributes[i]->nspace` (the current outer loop attribute). As `j` iterates up to `nspace_declarations_count`, which can exceed `element->attribute_count`, this reads `element->attributes` out of bounds. This is both a logic bug (wrong deduplication) and a memory safety issue that could cause a crash or information disclosure.

code_snippets: unsigned char* buffer = RAPTOR_MALLOC(unsigned char*, buf_length + 1);
vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference if malloc fails for xml:lang buffer
vulnerabilities.long_description: When allocating the buffer for the `xml:lang` attribute, if `RAPTOR_MALLOC` returns NULL, the code does not check for failure and immediately calls `memcpy(buffer, ...)`, resulting in a NULL pointer dereference. Other allocations in this function have NULL checks that jump to the error handler, but this one is missing.

commit_message: do not attempt to write a null image list (thanks to Vinay Rohila)
cve_desc: In ImageMagick, a crafted file could trigger an assertion failure when a call to WriteImages was made in MagickWand/operation.c, due to a NULL image list. This could potentially cause a denial of service. This was fixed in upstream ImageMagick version 7.1.0-30.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak when 'set' handler re-interprets arg1/arg2 already allocated by initial percent-escape expansion
vulnerabilities.long_description: At the top of the function, if process flags dictate it, arg1 and arg2 are set to newly allocated strings via InterpretImageProperties (different from arg1n/arg2n). Later, the 'set' option handler unconditionally calls InterpretImageProperties again on arg1n, overwriting the arg1 pointer with a new allocation. The originally allocated string from the initial expansion is leaked because no code frees it before the reassignment. The same pattern applies to arg2 in several sub-paths of the 'set' handler. An attacker who can invoke repeated 'set' operations (e.g., via a crafted script or batch command) could cause unbounded memory growth, leading to denial of service through memory exhaustion.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak in '+clone' path when arg1 was previously allocated by percent-escape expansion
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the 'clone' handler, when the option is '+clone', arg1 is unconditionally overwritten with AcquireString("-1"). If the initial percent-escape expansion block at the top of the function had already allocated a new string for arg1 (different from arg1n), that allocation is leaked because the pointer is overwritten without being freed first. Repeated invocations can exhaust memory.

commit_message:
netfilter: nf_tables: disallow binding to already bound chain

Update nft_data_init() to report EINVAL if chain is already bound.

Fixes: d0e2c7de92c7 ("netfilter: nf_tables: add NFT_CHAIN_BINDING")
Reported-by: Gwangun Jung <exsociety@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Pablo Neira Ayuso <pablo@netfilter.org>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in net/netfilter/nf_tables_api.c in the Linux kernel before 5.19.6. A denial of service can occur upon binding to an already bound chain.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Reference count overflow on chain->use allows use-after-free
vulnerabilities.long_description: The chain->use reference counter is incremented without any overflow check. An attacker can repeatedly create rules/set elements with NFT_JUMP or NFT_GOTO verdicts referencing the same chain, eventually overflowing the use counter back to zero. Once the counter wraps, the chain can be freed while still being referenced by existing rules, leading to a use-after-free condition. This can be exploited for local privilege escalation. This is related to CVE-2022-32250.

commit_message:
netfilter: nf_tables: disallow binding to already bound chain

Update nft_data_init() to report EINVAL if chain is already bound.

Fixes: d0e2c7de92c7 ("netfilter: nf_tables: add NFT_CHAIN_BINDING")
Reported-by: Gwangun Jung <exsociety@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Pablo Neira Ayuso <pablo@netfilter.org>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in net/netfilter/nf_tables_api.c in the Linux kernel before 5.19.6. A denial of service can occur upon binding to an already bound chain.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Reference counter overflow on chain->use allows use-after-free
vulnerabilities.long_description: The chain->use reference counter is incremented without any overflow check. An attacker could repeatedly create rules with NFT_JUMP or NFT_GOTO verdicts referencing the same chain, eventually wrapping the use counter (e.g., a u32) back to zero. Once the counter overflows to zero, the chain can be freed while still referenced by existing rules, leading to a use-after-free condition. This is a known class of nftables vulnerability (related to CVE-2022-32250 and similar) that can be exploited by a local unprivileged user (with user namespace access) to achieve kernel code execution or privilege escalation.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4418: crash when using special multi-byte character

Problem:    Crash when using special multi-byte character.
Solution:   Don't use isalpha() for an arbitrary character.
cve_desc: Use of Out-of-range Pointer Offset in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.4418.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Heap buffer overflow when directory entry name exceeds BASENAMELEN
vulnerabilities.long_description: The buffer `buf` is allocated with size `STRLEN(path) + BASENAMELEN + 5`, assuming directory entry names will not exceed BASENAMELEN. However, `dp->d_name` from `readdir()` can be up to NAME_MAX (typically 255) bytes or longer on some filesystems, and there is no length check before `STRCPY(s, dp->d_name)` copies the name into `buf`. If a filename is longer than BASENAMELEN, this write overflows the heap buffer. Subsequently, additional data (`path_end`, `"/**"`) is appended to `buf + len`, further extending the overflow. An attacker who can create files with long names in a directory being expanded can trigger this to corrupt heap metadata, potentially achieving arbitrary code execution.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4418: crash when using special multi-byte character

Problem:    Crash when using special multi-byte character.
Solution:   Don't use isalpha() for an arbitrary character.
cve_desc: Use of Out-of-range Pointer Offset in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.4418.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Heap buffer overflow when directory entry name exceeds BASENAMELEN
vulnerabilities.long_description: The buffer `buf` is allocated with size `STRLEN(path) + BASENAMELEN + 5`, assuming directory entry names will not exceed BASENAMELEN characters. However, `dp->d_name` from `readdir()` can be up to NAME_MAX (typically 255) characters or even longer on some filesystems, and BASENAMELEN may be smaller. When `STRCPY(s, dp->d_name)` copies the directory entry name into `buf` at offset `s`, and subsequently `STRCPY(buf + len, "/**")` and `STRCPY(buf + len + 3, path_end)` append additional data, the total length can exceed the allocated buffer size. An attacker who can create files or directories with long names in a directory being glob-expanded can trigger a heap buffer overflow, potentially leading to arbitrary code execution.

commit_message:
Calcualte max nspace declarations correctly for XML writer

(raptor_xml_writer_start_element_common): Calculate max including for
each attribute a potential name and value.

Fixes Issues #0000617 http://bugs.librdf.org/mantis/view.php?id=617
and #0000618 http://bugs.librdf.org/mantis/view.php?id=618
cve_desc: raptor_xml_writer_start_element_common in raptor_xml_writer.c in Raptor RDF Syntax Library 2.0.15 miscalculates the maximum nspace declarations for the XML writer, leading to heap-based buffer overflows (sometimes seen in raptor_qname_format_as_xml).

code_snippets:
for(j = 0; j < nspace_declarations_count; j++)
            if(nspace_declarations[j].nspace == element->attributes[j]->nspace) {
vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read due to wrong index variable used for element->attributes array
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the loop that checks for earlier duplicate namespace declarations, the code uses `element->attributes[j]->nspace` instead of `element->attributes[i]->nspace`. The variable `j` iterates over `nspace_declarations_count`, which can grow beyond `element->attribute_count`. When `j >= element->attribute_count`, accessing `element->attributes[j]` is an out-of-bounds read, potentially leading to a crash or information disclosure. An attacker who can control the XML structure (e.g., by providing an element with declared namespaces that inflate `nspace_declarations_count` beyond `attribute_count`) could trigger this bug.

vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference when RAPTOR_MALLOC fails for xml_language buffer
vulnerabilities.long_description: When processing `element->xml_language`, the code allocates a buffer via RAPTOR_MALLOC but never checks if the allocation returned NULL before using it in `memcpy`. If the allocation fails, a NULL pointer will be passed to memcpy, causing undefined behavior / crash. Other allocation failures in this function are properly checked and jump to the error label, but this one is missing the check.

commit_message:
Bug 790784 - (CVE-2017-17784) heap overread in gbr parser / load_image.

We were assuming the input name was well formed, hence was
nul-terminated. As any data coming from external input, this has to be
thorougly checked.
Similar to commit 06d24a79af94837d615d0024916bb95a01bf3c59 but adapted
to older gimp-2-8 code.
cve_desc: In GIMP 2.8.22, there is a heap-based buffer over-read in load_image in plug-ins/common/file-gbr.c in the gbr import parser, related to mishandling of UTF-8 data.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow when allocating RGBA brush buffer in .gpb pattern handling
vulnerabilities.long_description: When a grayscale brush (bh.bytes=1) is followed by a pattern (.gpb format), the code changes bh.bytes to 4 and allocates `4 * bh.width * bh.height`. The original overflow check `G_MAXSIZE / bh.width / bh.height / bh.bytes < 1` was performed with bh.bytes=1, so it only ensured `width * height <= G_MAXSIZE`. On 32-bit systems, `4 * bh.width * bh.height` can overflow, resulting in a small allocation. The subsequent loop writes `4 * width * height` bytes into this undersized buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Heap buffer over-read due to passing non-null-terminated buffer with length -1 to gimp_any_to_utf8
vulnerabilities.long_description: The brush name is read from the file into `temp` with exactly `bn_size` bytes, but the buffer is not guaranteed to be null-terminated. `gimp_any_to_utf8` is then called with a length of -1, which means it treats `temp` as a null-terminated string and reads until a null byte is found. If the file data does not contain a null terminator within `bn_size` bytes, the function will read past the end of the heap-allocated buffer, potentially leaking sensitive memory contents or causing a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential integer overflow in size calculation and type mismatch with read() return value
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `size` is of type `gsize` (unsigned) and is compared against the return value of `read()` which is `ssize_t` (signed). If `size` exceeds `SSIZE_MAX`, `read()` may return a truncated or negative value, and the signed/unsigned comparison would not correctly detect a short read. This could lead to using partially-initialized buffer data. Additionally, on 32-bit platforms with the given overflow check using sequential division, edge cases in the division rounding could still allow an overflow in the multiplication `bh.width * bh.height * bh.bytes`.

commit_message: Fixed bug #22965 (Crash in gd lib's ImageFillToBorder()).
cve_desc: Stack consumption vulnerability in GD in PHP before 5.6.12 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service via a crafted imagefilltoborder call.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Uncontrolled recursion can cause stack overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function uses unbounded recursion to perform flood fill. For large images or adversarial inputs, the recursive calls (for rows above and below) can exhaust the stack, leading to a stack overflow and crash (denial of service). An attacker who controls the image dimensions and pixel content can craft an image that triggers deep recursion.

code_snippets: gdImageFillToBorder (gdImagePtr im, int x, int y, int border, int color)
vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing bounds check on x and y parameters allows out-of-bounds access
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function does not validate that the initial x and y coordinates are within the image bounds (0 <= x < im->sx, 0 <= y < im->sy). If negative or out-of-range values are passed, gdImageGetPixel and gdImageSetPixel may access memory outside the image buffer, leading to out-of-bounds reads/writes and potential code execution or crashes.

commit_message: check vertexIndex which might be -1 for broken DXF
cve_desc: A code execution vulnerability exists in the DL_Dxf::handleLWPolylineData functionality of Ribbonsoft dxflib 3.17.0. A specially-crafted .dxf file can lead to a heap buffer overflow. An attacker can provide a malicious file to trigger this vulnerability.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in allocation size leads to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expression `4*maxVertices` is computed as an `int` multiplication. If `maxVertices` is large (e.g., greater than INT_MAX/4), this overflows, resulting in a small allocation. The subsequent initialization loop and later vertex writes use the original `maxVertices` value, writing far beyond the allocated buffer. An attacker supplying a crafted DXF file with a large group-90 value can trigger heap corruption and potentially achieve arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds write when groupCode==42 with vertexIndex==-1
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `groupCode==42` is encountered before any `groupCode==10` (so `vertexIndex` is still -1), the condition `vertexIndex<maxVertices` is satisfied but there is no `vertexIndex>=0` check. This results in writing to `vertices[4*(-1)+3] = vertices[-1]`, an out-of-bounds write before the allocated buffer. A crafted DXF file that sends a group-42 record before any group-10 record triggers this, potentially corrupting heap metadata and enabling code execution.

commit_message:
server: reset meta context replies on starttls

Related to CVE-2021-3716, but not as severe.  No compliant client will
send NBD_CMD_BLOCK_STATUS unless it first negotiates
NBD_OPT_SET_META_CONTEXT.  If an attacker injects a premature
SET_META_CONTEXT, either the client will never notice (because it
never uses BLOCK_STATUS), or the client will overwrite the attacker's
attempt with the client's own SET_META_CONTEXT request after
encryption is enabled.  So I don't class this as having the potential
to trigger denial-of-service due to any protocol mismatch between
compliant client and server (I don't care what happens with
non-compliant clients).

Fixes: 26455d45 (server: protocol: Implement Block Status "base:allocation".)
cve_desc: A flaw was found in nbdkit due to to improperly caching plaintext state across the STARTTLS encryption boundary. A MitM attacker could use this flaw to inject a plaintext NBD_OPT_STRUCTURED_REPLY before proxying everything else a client sends to the server, potentially leading the client to terminate the NBD session. The highest threat from this vulnerability is to system availability.

commit_message: check vertexIndex which might be -1 for broken DXF
cve_desc: A code execution vulnerability exists in the DL_Dxf::handleLWPolylineData functionality of Ribbonsoft dxflib 3.17.0. A specially-crafted .dxf file can lead to a heap buffer overflow. An attacker can provide a malicious file to trigger this vulnerability.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in allocation size leads to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expression `4*maxVertices` is computed using `int` arithmetic. If `maxVertices` is a large value (e.g., parsed from a malicious DXF file via `toInt(groupValue)`), the multiplication `4*maxVertices` can overflow a 32-bit integer, resulting in a much smaller allocation than expected. Subsequent writes in the initialization loop and in the vertex-processing branch will then write out of bounds of the allocated buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow. An attacker can craft a DXF file with a large vertex count in group code 90 to trigger this, potentially achieving arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Uncontrolled allocation size from untrusted input
vulnerabilities.long_description: The value of `maxVertices` comes directly from user-controlled file input (`groupValue`) with no upper-bound validation. An attacker can specify an extremely large value, causing an enormous heap allocation (`4 * maxVertices * sizeof(double)` bytes) that could exhaust memory and lead to a denial of service. Even if `new[]` throws `std::bad_alloc`, no exception handling is present, so the application will crash.

commit_message:
Harden /tmp/tmate directory

Suggested by Matthias Gerstner
cve_desc: World-writable permissions on the /tmp/tmate/sessions directory in tmate-ssh-server 2.3.0 allow a local attacker to compromise the integrity of session handling, or obtain the read-write session ID from a read-only session symlink in this directory.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential NULL pointer dereference when environment is empty
vulnerabilities.long_description: If the environment variable list is empty, `envp` points to a NULL terminator, meaning `*envp` would be NULL. This value is assigned to `cmdline_end` and any subsequent use of it (e.g., for process title rewriting) could lead to a NULL pointer dereference or unexpected behavior.

code_snippets:
if ((mkdir(TMATE_WORKDIR, 0700)             < 0 && errno != EEXIST) ||
    (mkdir(TMATE_WORKDIR "/sessions", 0700) < 0 && errno != EEXIST) ||
    (mkdir(TMATE_WORKDIR "/jail", 0700)     < 0 && errno != EEXIST))
		tmate_fatal("Cannot prepare session in " TMATE_WORKDIR);

	if ((chmod(TMATE_WORKDIR, 0700)             < 0) ||
	    (chmod(TMATE_WORKDIR "/sessions", 0700) < 0) ||
	    (chmod(TMATE_WORKDIR "/jail", 0700)     < 0))
		tmate_fatal("Cannot prepare session in " TMATE_WORKDIR);

	if (check_owned_directory_mode(TMATE_WORKDIR, 0700) ||
vulnerabilities.short_description: TOCTOU race condition between mkdir/chmod and ownership check on directories
vulnerabilities.long_description: There is a time-of-check-time-of-use race condition across the mkdir, chmod, and check_owned_directory_mode calls. Between these operations, a local attacker could replace one of the directories (e.g., TMATE_WORKDIR/jail) with a symlink pointing to an arbitrary location. The subsequent chmod would then change permissions on the symlink target, and the server might operate on attacker-controlled directories. This is especially concerning for a server running with elevated privileges.

vulnerabilities.short_description: No validation on port number inputs parsed via atoi()
vulnerabilities.long_description: The use of atoi() provides no error checking: it returns 0 on failure, accepts negative numbers, and silently truncates values exceeding INT_MAX. An attacker or misconfiguration could supply out-of-range values (e.g., negative ports, ports > 65535, or non-numeric strings) leading to undefined behavior when these values are later used in network socket operations. The function strtol() with proper bounds checking should be used instead.

commit_message:
ALSA: control: Re-order bounds checking in get_ctl_id_hash()

These two checks are in the reverse order so it might read one element
beyond the end of the array.  First check if the "i" is within bounds
before using it.

Fixes: 6ab55ec0a938 ("ALSA: control: Fix an out-of-bounds bug in get_ctl_id_hash()")
Signed-off-by: Dan Carpenter <dan.carpenter@oracle.com>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/YwjgNh/gkG1hH7po@kili
Signed-off-by: Takashi Iwai <tiwai@suse.de>
cve_desc: An out-of-bounds access issue was found in the Linux kernel sound subsystem. It could occur when the 'id->name' provided by the user did not end with '\0'. A privileged local user could pass a specially crafted name through ioctl() interface and crash the system or potentially escalate their privileges on the system.

commit_message:
Validate and require subkey binding signatures on PGP public keys

All subkeys must be followed by a binding signature by the primary key
as per the OpenPGP RFC, enforce the presence and validity in the parser.

The implementation is as kludgey as they come to work around our
simple-minded parser structure without touching API, to maximise
backportability. Store all the raw packets internally as we decode them
to be able to access previous elements at will, needed to validate ordering
and access the actual data. Add testcases for manipulated keys whose
import previously would succeed.

Depends on the two previous commits:
7b399fcb8f52566e6f3b4327197a85facd08db91 and
236b802a4aa48711823a191d1b7f753c82a89ec5

Fixes CVE-2021-3521.
cve_desc: There is a flaw in RPM's signature functionality. OpenPGP subkeys are associated with a primary key via a "binding signature." RPM does not check the binding signature of subkeys prior to importing them. If an attacker is able to add or socially engineer another party to add a malicious subkey to a legitimate public key, RPM could wrongly trust a malicious signature. The greatest impact of this flaw is to data integrity. To exploit this flaw, an attacker must either compromise an RPM repository or convince an administrator to install an untrusted RPM or public key. It is strongly recommended to only use RPMs and public keys from trusted sources.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of `selfsig` when the loop exits early via `break` after allocation but before freeing.
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `selfsig` is allocated via `pgpDigParamsNew` and the loop subsequently breaks due to `pgpPrtPkt` failing or the `PGPSIGTYPE_SUBKEY_BINDING` check failing, `selfsig` is never freed. The code after the loop only frees `digp` and `all`, but not `selfsig`. An attacker providing crafted PGP packets that trigger these early-exit paths can cause repeated memory leaks, potentially leading to resource exhaustion / denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in `alloced *= 2` can lead to undersized heap allocation and subsequent buffer overflow.
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `alloced` is a signed `int`. With a sufficiently large number of packets, repeated doubling (`alloced *= 2`) can cause a signed integer overflow, resulting in undefined behavior. If `alloced` wraps to a small or negative value, the `xrealloc` call will allocate a buffer much smaller than expected, while the index `i` continues to grow. Subsequent accesses to `all[i]` will write out of bounds on the heap, potentially allowing arbitrary code execution. While exploiting this requires a very large input, it is a real risk when processing untrusted PGP data.

commit_message:
ALSA: control: Re-order bounds checking in get_ctl_id_hash()

These two checks are in the reverse order so it might read one element
beyond the end of the array.  First check if the "i" is within bounds
before using it.

Fixes: 6ab55ec0a938 ("ALSA: control: Fix an out-of-bounds bug in get_ctl_id_hash()")
Signed-off-by: Dan Carpenter <dan.carpenter@oracle.com>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/YwjgNh/gkG1hH7po@kili
Signed-off-by: Takashi Iwai <tiwai@suse.de>
cve_desc: An out-of-bounds access issue was found in the Linux kernel sound subsystem. It could occur when the 'id->name' provided by the user did not end with '\0'. A privileged local user could pass a specially crafted name through ioctl() interface and crash the system or potentially escalate their privileges on the system.
The ReadTGAImage function in coders\tga.c in ImageMagick 7.0.5-6 has a memory leak vulnerability that can cause memory exhaustion via invalid colors data in the header of a TGA or VST file.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds colormap access when colormap_type != 0 on non-PseudoClass images
vulnerabilities.long_description: When tga_info.colormap_type is non-zero but the image type is not colormap-based (e.g., TGARGB), the image storage class is DirectClass, so the PseudoClass initialization block that computes image->colors as colormap_index + colormap_length is skipped. image->colors defaults to 0. Later, in the colormap reading section, image->colors is only set to colormap_index (if colormap_index > 0), or remains 0. AcquireImageColormap is then called with this potentially smaller (or zero) value. The colormap_length entries from the file are never consumed, and the colormap is undersized. During pixel reading with 8bpp, if colormap_type != 0, the code accesses image->colormap via an index. When image->colors is 0, ConstrainColormapIndex returns 0 but colormap[0] is out-of-bounds on an empty colormap, leading to a heap buffer over-read. An attacker can craft a TGA file with image_type=TGARGB, colormap_type=1, colormap_index=0, colormap_length=0, and bits_per_pixel=8 to trigger this.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing validation of colormap_size leads to under-reading colormap entries and file offset misalignment
vulnerabilities.long_description: The tga_info.colormap_size value is read from the file as an arbitrary byte but is never validated to be one of the expected values (8, 15, 16, 24, 32). If an attacker sets colormap_size to an unexpected value, the default case reads only 1 byte per colormap entry. If the actual intended colormap entry size is larger (e.g., 24-bit or 32-bit entries), fewer bytes than expected are consumed from the file stream. This misaligns the file pointer, causing subsequent pixel data to be read from the wrong offset. While the colormap writes themselves are bounded by image->colors, the file pointer misalignment means attacker-controlled colormap data is interpreted as pixel data, potentially causing information disclosure or unexpected image content.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential large allocation and colormap size issue with unconstrained bits_per_pixel for monochrome images
vulnerabilities.long_description: For PseudoClass images with colormap_type == 0 (e.g., TGAMonochrome), image->colors is computed as 1 << bits_per_pixel. The bits_per_pixel validation allows values 2-16, so this could allocate up to 65536 colormap entries. While not an overflow on 64-bit systems, on 32-bit systems with bits_per_pixel values close to the size_t limit (if validation were bypassed or extended), this could lead to integer overflow. More importantly, for a monochrome image, having 65536 colormap entries is abnormal and could waste significant memory, enabling a denial-of-service via resource exhaustion with a small crafted file.

commit_message:
PCI: rpadlpar: Fix potential drc_name corruption in store functions

Both add_slot_store() and remove_slot_store() try to fix up the
drc_name copied from the store buffer by placing a NUL terminator at
nbyte + 1 or in place of a '\n' if present. However, the static buffer
that we copy the drc_name data into is not zeroed and can contain
anything past the n-th byte.

This is problematic if a '\n' byte appears in that buffer after nbytes
and the string copied into the store buffer was not NUL terminated to
start with as the strchr() search for a '\n' byte will mark this
incorrectly as the end of the drc_name string resulting in a drc_name
string that contains garbage data after the n-th byte.

Additionally it will cause us to overwrite that '\n' byte on the stack
with NUL, potentially corrupting data on the stack.

The following debugging shows an example of the drmgr utility writing
"PHB 4543" to the add_slot sysfs attribute, but add_slot_store()
logging a corrupted string value.

  drmgr: drmgr: -c phb -a -s PHB 4543 -d 1
  add_slot_store: drc_name = PHB 4543°|<82>!, rc = -19

Fix this by using strscpy() instead of memcpy() to ensure the string
is NUL terminated when copied into the static drc_name buffer.
Further, since the string is now NUL terminated the code only needs to
change '\n' to '\0' when present.

Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Signed-off-by: Tyrel Datwyler <tyreld@linux.ibm.com>
[mpe: Reformat change log and add mention of possible stack corruption]
Signed-off-by: Michael Ellerman <mpe@ellerman.id.au>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20210315214821.452959-1-tyreld@linux.ibm.com
cve_desc: In drivers/pci/hotplug/rpadlpar_sysfs.c in the Linux kernel through 5.11.8, the RPA PCI Hotplug driver has a user-tolerable buffer overflow when writing a new device name to the driver from userspace, allowing userspace to write data to the kernel stack frame directly. This occurs because add_slot_store and remove_slot_store mishandle drc_name '\0' termination, aka CID-cc7a0bb058b8.

commit_message: fix in thread.call
cve_desc: Heap-based buffer overflow in sqbaselib.cpp in SQUIRREL 3.2 due to lack of a certain sq_reservestack call.

commit_message:
udf: Restore i_lenAlloc when inode expansion fails

When we fail to expand inode from inline format to a normal format, we
restore inode to contain the original inline formatting but we forgot to
set i_lenAlloc back. The mismatch between i_lenAlloc and i_size was then
causing further problems such as warnings and lost data down the line.

Reported-by: butt3rflyh4ck <butterflyhuangxx@gmail.com>
CC: stable@vger.kernel.org
Fixes: 7e49b6f2480c ("udf: Convert UDF to new truncate calling sequence")
Reviewed-by: Christoph Hellwig <hch@lst.de>
Signed-off-by: Jan Kara <jack@suse.cz>
cve_desc: A flaw null pointer dereference in the Linux kernel UDF file system functionality was found in the way user triggers udf_file_write_iter function for the malicious UDF image. A local user could use this flaw to crash the system. Actual from Linux kernel 4.2-rc1 till 5.17-rc2.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds write if i_lenAlloc exceeds PAGE_SIZE due to missing bounds check
vulnerabilities.long_description: If a corrupted or malicious UDF filesystem image has i_lenAlloc set to a value larger than PAGE_SIZE, the expression `PAGE_SIZE - iinfo->i_lenAlloc` will underflow (both are unsigned), resulting in a very large memset that writes far beyond the mapped page. Additionally, the memcpy will copy more than PAGE_SIZE bytes into the page buffer. An attacker who crafts a malicious UDF image could trigger kernel heap/memory corruption, potentially leading to privilege escalation or denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Buffer overflow in error recovery path using inode->i_size without bounds validation
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the error recovery path after filemap_fdatawrite fails, the code copies `inode->i_size` bytes from the page into `iinfo->i_data + iinfo->i_lenEAttr`. The i_data buffer in the UDF inode info structure has a fixed size. If inode->i_size is larger than the available space in the i_data buffer (i.e., larger than the i_data buffer size minus i_lenEAttr), this will write beyond the bounds of i_data, corrupting adjacent kernel heap memory. This could be triggered by a crafted filesystem image and may lead to privilege escalation.

commit_message: fix in thread.call
cve_desc: Heap-based buffer overflow in sqbaselib.cpp in SQUIRREL 3.2 due to lack of a certain sq_reservestack call.

commit_message:
server: reset meta context replies on starttls

Related to CVE-2021-3716, but not as severe.  No compliant client will
send NBD_CMD_BLOCK_STATUS unless it first negotiates
NBD_OPT_SET_META_CONTEXT.  If an attacker injects a premature
SET_META_CONTEXT, either the client will never notice (because it
never uses BLOCK_STATUS), or the client will overwrite the attacker's
attempt with the client's own SET_META_CONTEXT request after
encryption is enabled.  So I don't class this as having the potential
to trigger denial-of-service due to any protocol mismatch between
compliant client and server (I don't care what happens with
non-compliant clients).

Fixes: 26455d45 (server: protocol: Implement Block Status "base:allocation".)
cve_desc: A flaw was found in nbdkit due to to improperly caching plaintext state across the STARTTLS encryption boundary. A MitM attacker could use this flaw to inject a plaintext NBD_OPT_STRUCTURED_REPLY before proxying everything else a client sends to the server, potentially leading the client to terminate the NBD session. The highest threat from this vulnerability is to system availability.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Option payload data not consumed from socket before continuing, causing protocol desynchronization
vulnerabilities.long_description: When the early guard checks (non-fixed-newstyle client sending a non-EXPORT_NAME option, or forced-TLS mode with a non-STARTTLS/non-ABORT option) reject an option, the code sends a reply and continues to the next loop iteration without first reading and discarding the `optlen` bytes of option payload data from the socket. In the NBD protocol, each option header is followed by `optlen` bytes of data. Since this data is never consumed, the next iteration's `conn_recv_full(&new_option, ...)` will read the stale option payload data as if it were the next option header, causing protocol desynchronization. A malicious client could craft option payloads that, when reinterpreted as an option header, cause unexpected behavior, potentially bypassing security checks (e.g., the TLS requirement check) or causing the server to process attacker-controlled data as protocol commands.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak when client sends multiple NBD_OPT_SET_META_CONTEXT options without freeing previous allocation
vulnerabilities.long_description: When handling NBD_OPT_SET_META_CONTEXT, the code assigns a new `strndup` result to `conn->exportname_from_set_meta_context` without first freeing the previously stored value. If a client sends multiple NBD_OPT_SET_META_CONTEXT options within the same negotiation (up to MAX_NR_OPTIONS times), each previous allocation is leaked. While bounded by MAX_NR_OPTIONS, a malicious client could repeatedly trigger this to waste server memory, especially if `exportnamelen` is large (up to MAX_REQUEST_SIZE - 8 bytes per iteration).

commit_message:
PCI: rpadlpar: Fix potential drc_name corruption in store functions

Both add_slot_store() and remove_slot_store() try to fix up the
drc_name copied from the store buffer by placing a NUL terminator at
nbyte + 1 or in place of a '\n' if present. However, the static buffer
that we copy the drc_name data into is not zeroed and can contain
anything past the n-th byte.

This is problematic if a '\n' byte appears in that buffer after nbytes
and the string copied into the store buffer was not NUL terminated to
start with as the strchr() search for a '\n' byte will mark this
incorrectly as the end of the drc_name string resulting in a drc_name
string that contains garbage data after the n-th byte.

Additionally it will cause us to overwrite that '\n' byte on the stack
with NUL, potentially corrupting data on the stack.

The following debugging shows an example of the drmgr utility writing
"PHB 4543" to the add_slot sysfs attribute, but add_slot_store()
logging a corrupted string value.

  drmgr: drmgr: -c phb -a -s PHB 4543 -d 1
  add_slot_store: drc_name = PHB 4543°|<82>!, rc = -19

Fix this by using strscpy() instead of memcpy() to ensure the string
is NUL terminated when copied into the static drc_name buffer.
Further, since the string is now NUL terminated the code only needs to
change '\n' to '\0' when present.

Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Signed-off-by: Tyrel Datwyler <tyreld@linux.ibm.com>
[mpe: Reformat change log and add mention of possible stack corruption]
Signed-off-by: Michael Ellerman <mpe@ellerman.id.au>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20210315214821.452959-1-tyreld@linux.ibm.com
cve_desc: In drivers/pci/hotplug/rpadlpar_sysfs.c in the Linux kernel through 5.11.8, the RPA PCI Hotplug driver has a user-tolerable buffer overflow when writing a new device name to the driver from userspace, allowing userspace to write data to the kernel stack frame directly. This occurs because add_slot_store and remove_slot_store mishandle drc_name '\0' termination, aka CID-cc7a0bb058b8.

vulnerabilities.short_description: strchr called on a buffer that is not guaranteed to be null-terminated, leading to out-of-bounds read.
vulnerabilities.long_description: After memcpy copies 'nbytes' bytes into the stack buffer 'drc_name', the remaining bytes in the buffer are uninitialized. strchr then scans 'drc_name' looking for '\n', but because there is no null terminator placed before this call, strchr may read past the copied data into uninitialized stack memory. If neither '\n' nor a coincidental '\0' is found within the MAX_DRC_NAME_LEN-sized buffer, strchr will continue reading beyond the buffer boundary, resulting in a stack-based out-of-bounds read. This could lead to information disclosure (reading sensitive stack data) or a crash. The fix should null-terminate the buffer before calling strchr, e.g., by setting drc_name[nbytes] = '\0' immediately after the memcpy.

commit_message:
udf: Restore i_lenAlloc when inode expansion fails

When we fail to expand inode from inline format to a normal format, we
restore inode to contain the original inline formatting but we forgot to
set i_lenAlloc back. The mismatch between i_lenAlloc and i_size was then
causing further problems such as warnings and lost data down the line.

Reported-by: butt3rflyh4ck <butterflyhuangxx@gmail.com>
CC: stable@vger.kernel.org
Fixes: 7e49b6f2480c ("udf: Convert UDF to new truncate calling sequence")
Reviewed-by: Christoph Hellwig <hch@lst.de>
Signed-off-by: Jan Kara <jack@suse.cz>
cve_desc: A flaw null pointer dereference in the Linux kernel UDF file system functionality was found in the way user triggers udf_file_write_iter function for the malicious UDF image. A local user could use this flaw to crash the system. Actual from Linux kernel 4.2-rc1 till 5.17-rc2.

vulnerabilities.short_description: No bounds check on i_lenAlloc against PAGE_SIZE leading to potential integer underflow and buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The value of iinfo->i_lenAlloc is used without validating that it does not exceed PAGE_SIZE. If a corrupted or malicious UDF filesystem image provides an i_lenAlloc value larger than PAGE_SIZE, the expression `PAGE_SIZE - iinfo->i_lenAlloc` will undergo unsigned integer underflow, resulting in a very large size passed to memset, causing an out-of-bounds write on the mapped page. Additionally, the memcpy with i_lenAlloc as the size could write beyond the page boundary. This can lead to kernel heap/stack corruption and potential code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Error recovery path copies inode->i_size bytes into inline data buffer without bounds checking
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the error recovery path after filemap_fdatawrite fails, the code copies inode->i_size bytes from the page into iinfo->i_data + iinfo->i_lenEAttr. There is no validation that inode->i_size fits within the remaining space in the iinfo->i_data buffer (which is limited to the inline area of the inode). If inode->i_size is larger than the available inline space, this results in a buffer overflow that can corrupt adjacent kernel memory, potentially leading to privilege escalation.

commit_message:
PCI: rpadlpar: Fix potential drc_name corruption in store functions

Both add_slot_store() and remove_slot_store() try to fix up the
drc_name copied from the store buffer by placing a NUL terminator at
nbyte + 1 or in place of a '\n' if present. However, the static buffer
that we copy the drc_name data into is not zeroed and can contain
anything past the n-th byte.

This is problematic if a '\n' byte appears in that buffer after nbytes
and the string copied into the store buffer was not NUL terminated to
start with as the strchr() search for a '\n' byte will mark this
incorrectly as the end of the drc_name string resulting in a drc_name
string that contains garbage data after the n-th byte.

Additionally it will cause us to overwrite that '\n' byte on the stack
with NUL, potentially corrupting data on the stack.

The following debugging shows an example of the drmgr utility writing
"PHB 4543" to the add_slot sysfs attribute, but add_slot_store()
logging a corrupted string value.

  drmgr: drmgr: -c phb -a -s PHB 4543 -d 1
  add_slot_store: drc_name = PHB 4543°|<82>!, rc = -19

Fix this by using strscpy() instead of memcpy() to ensure the string
is NUL terminated when copied into the static drc_name buffer.
Further, since the string is now NUL terminated the code only needs to
change '\n' to '\0' when present.

Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Signed-off-by: Tyrel Datwyler <tyreld@linux.ibm.com>
[mpe: Reformat change log and add mention of possible stack corruption]
Signed-off-by: Michael Ellerman <mpe@ellerman.id.au>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20210315214821.452959-1-tyreld@linux.ibm.com
cve_desc: In drivers/pci/hotplug/rpadlpar_sysfs.c in the Linux kernel through 5.11.8, the RPA PCI Hotplug driver has a user-tolerable buffer overflow when writing a new device name to the driver from userspace, allowing userspace to write data to the kernel stack frame directly. This occurs because add_slot_store and remove_slot_store mishandle drc_name '\0' termination, aka CID-cc7a0bb058b8.

vulnerabilities.short_description: strchr operates on a non-null-terminated buffer, reading uninitialized stack memory
vulnerabilities.long_description:
The function copies `nbytes` bytes from `buf` into the stack buffer `drc_name` using `memcpy`, which does not append a null terminator. It then calls `strchr(drc_name, '
')` which scans until it finds a '
' or '\0'. Since the buffer is not null-terminated after the copy, `strchr` may read beyond the copied data into uninitialized stack memory. If a newline byte happens to exist in the uninitialized portion of the stack, `strchr` will return a pointer to it (beyond the valid data), and the subsequent `*end = '\0'` will null-terminate at the wrong position. This could lead to `dlpar_remove_slot` processing incorrect or uninitialized data from the stack, potentially causing unexpected behavior. The fix is to null-terminate the buffer before calling `strchr`, or use `memchr(drc_name, '
', nbytes)` instead.

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/issues/1540
cve_desc: In ImageMagick 7.0.8-40 Q16, there is a heap-based buffer over-read in the function WritePNMImage of coders/pnm.c, which allows an attacker to cause a denial of service or possibly information disclosure via a crafted image file. This is related to SetGrayscaleImage in MagickCore/quantize.c.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds access on colormap_index in the PseudoClass path via ScaleQuantumToMap
vulnerabilities.long_description: When image->storage_class is PseudoClass, colormap_index is allocated with only image->colors+1 entries. However, in the second parallel loop, the code accesses colormap_index[ScaleQuantumToMap(GetPixelIndex(image,q))]. ScaleQuantumToMap can return values up to MaxMap (e.g. 65535), which can be far larger than image->colors. This results in a heap-based out-of-bounds read (and potentially write via the SetPixelIndex that follows), leading to possible information disclosure, crashes, or arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Unbounded increment of image->colors can cause out-of-bounds write to image->colormap
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the OpenMP parallel loop, image->colors is incremented inside a critical section each time a new intensity is encountered, but there is no bounds check to ensure image->colors does not exceed MaxColormapSize (the allocated size of image->colormap). If an image has more than MaxColormapSize distinct intensity values, image->colormap[image->colors] writes past the end of the allocated colormap buffer, resulting in a heap buffer overflow. This can lead to memory corruption and potential code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Off-by-one: memset clears MaxColormapSize entries but MaxColormapSize+1 were allocated
vulnerabilities.long_description: The colormap_index buffer is allocated with MaxColormapSize+1 entries, but the memset only initializes MaxColormapSize entries to -1. The last element (at index MaxColormapSize) is left uninitialized. If ScaleQuantumToMap returns MaxColormapSize (i.e., MaxMap which equals MaxColormapSize), the uninitialized value is read and used as a colormap index, leading to undefined behavior and potential out-of-bounds access on the colormap array.

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/issues/1540
cve_desc: In ImageMagick 7.0.8-40 Q16, there is a heap-based buffer over-read in the function WritePNMImage of coders/pnm.c, which allows an attacker to cause a denial of service or possibly information disclosure via a crafted image file. This is related to SetGrayscaleImage in MagickCore/quantize.c.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Off-by-one heap buffer overflow: colormap_index allocated with MaxMap elements but indexed up to MaxMap
vulnerabilities.long_description: When the image is PseudoClass, colormap_index is allocated with MagickMax(image->colors+1, MaxMap) elements. ScaleQuantumToMap() can return values in the range [0, MaxMap], meaning index MaxMap is valid. However, the allocation uses MaxMap (not MaxMap+1), so the array has indices 0 through MaxMap-1. Accessing colormap_index[MaxMap] is a one-element out-of-bounds heap write/read. This occurs in the final pixel-remapping loop where colormap_index[ScaleQuantumToMap(GetPixelIndex(image,q))] is accessed. An attacker supplying a crafted PseudoClass grayscale image could trigger heap corruption, potentially leading to code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential out-of-bounds write to image->colormap via unbounded image->colors increment
vulnerabilities.long_description: Inside the OpenMP parallel loop for non-PseudoClass images, image->colors is incremented within a critical section without any bounds check against MaxColormapSize (the size of the allocated colormap). If the number of unique grayscale intensity values exceeds the allocated colormap size, image->colormap[image->colors] writes past the end of the allocated buffer. While in typical configurations MaxMap+1 == MaxColormapSize, this lacks a defensive bounds check and can be exploited if the constants differ or under unusual quantum depth configurations, leading to heap buffer overflow and potential remote code execution.

commit_message:
red fixes (#468)

* red fixes

* remove magic number
cve_desc: A race condition exists in Eternal Terminal prior to version 6.2.0 that allows an authenticated attacker to hijack other users' SSH authorization socket, enabling the attacker to login to other systems as the targeted users. The bug is in UserTerminalRouter::getInfoForId().

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0218: reading before the start of the line

Problem:    Reading before the start of the line.
Solution:   When displaying "$" check the column is not negative.
cve_desc: Improper Validation of Specified Quantity in Input in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.0218.

commit_message:
red fixes (#468)

* red fixes

* remove magic number
cve_desc: A race condition exists in Eternal Terminal prior to version 6.2.0 that allows an authenticated attacker to hijack other users' SSH authorization socket, enabling the attacker to login to other systems as the targeted users. The bug is in UserTerminalRouter::getInfoForId().

vulnerabilities.short_description: Buffer overflow via unbounded strcpy into fixed-size sun_path field
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function copies the user-controlled pipePath string into local.sun_path using strcpy without checking that the string length fits within the fixed-size sun_path buffer (typically 104 or 108 bytes). If pipePath exceeds this size, a stack buffer overflow occurs, potentially allowing arbitrary code execution or denial of service.

commit_message:
input-bmp: Increase header buffer in some cases

Signed-off-by: Peter Lemenkov <lemenkov@gmail.com>
cve_desc: AutoTrace v0.40.0 was discovered to contain a heap overflow via the ReadImage function at input-bmp.c:660.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Stack buffer overflow when reading BMP v4/v5 headers (biSize 108 or 124)
vulnerabilities.long_description: The local buffer is only 64 bytes, but when Bitmap_File_Head.biSize is 108 or 124, the code reads (biSize - 4) bytes (i.e., 104 or 120 bytes) into this 64-byte stack buffer. This results in a stack-based buffer overflow. An attacker can craft a malicious BMP file with a biSize of 108 or 124 to overwrite adjacent stack memory, potentially achieving arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: fclose called on NULL file pointer when fopen fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: If fopen fails and returns NULL, the code jumps to the cleanup label which unconditionally calls fclose(fd). Passing NULL to fclose is undefined behavior per the C standard and can cause a crash or other unexpected behavior.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer truncation when casting image dimensions to unsigned short
vulnerabilities.long_description: Bitmap_Head.biWidth and biHeight can be up to ~2 billion (validated as fitting in a 32-bit signed integer), but they are cast to unsigned short (max 65535) when initializing the bitmap. This silently truncates large dimensions, causing the bitmap metadata to describe a much smaller image than the actual allocated pixel data from ReadImage, leading to inconsistent state that could cause out-of-bounds access or other memory corruption in downstream consumers.

commit_message:
red fixes (#468)

* red fixes

* remove magic number
cve_desc: A race condition exists in Eternal Terminal prior to version 6.2.0 that allows an authenticated attacker to hijack other users' SSH authorization socket, enabling the attacker to login to other systems as the targeted users. The bug is in UserTerminalRouter::getInfoForId().

commit_message:
red fixes (#468)

* red fixes

* remove magic number
cve_desc: A race condition exists in Eternal Terminal prior to version 6.2.0 that allows an authenticated attacker to hijack other users' SSH authorization socket, enabling the attacker to login to other systems as the targeted users. The bug is in UserTerminalRouter::getInfoForId().

vulnerabilities.short_description: strncpy may not null-terminate sun_path, leading to buffer over-read and path issues
vulnerabilities.long_description: If pipePath.c_str() length is >= sizeof(local.sun_path) (typically 108 bytes), strncpy will not null-terminate local.sun_path. This unterminated buffer is then passed to unlink(), bind(), and chmod(), which expect null-terminated strings. This can cause out-of-bounds reads when those functions scan for the null terminator, potentially operating on unintended file paths or causing undefined behavior. An attacker who controls the endpoint name could craft a path of exactly the right length to exploit this.

commit_message:
lib/iov_iter: initialize "flags" in new pipe_buffer

The functions copy_page_to_iter_pipe() and push_pipe() can both
allocate a new pipe_buffer, but the "flags" member initializer is
missing.

Fixes: 241699cd72a8 ("new iov_iter flavour: pipe-backed")
To: Alexander Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
To: linux-fsdevel@vger.kernel.org
To: linux-kernel@vger.kernel.org
Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Signed-off-by: Max Kellermann <max.kellermann@ionos.com>
Signed-off-by: Al Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in the way the "flags" member of the new pipe buffer structure was lacking proper initialization in copy_page_to_iter_pipe and push_pipe functions in the Linux kernel and could thus contain stale values. An unprivileged local user could use this flaw to write to pages in the page cache backed by read only files and as such escalate their privileges on the system.

commit_message: Fix oob write for dwarf with abbrev with count 0 (Fix #2083) (#2086)
cve_desc: Rizin is a UNIX-like reverse engineering framework and command-line toolset. In versions up to and including 0.3.1 there is a heap-based out of bounds write in parse_die() when reversing an AMD64 ELF binary with DWARF debug info. When a malicious AMD64 ELF binary is opened by a victim user, Rizin may crash or execute unintended actions. No workaround are known and users are advised to upgrade.

code_snippets:
for (i = 0; i < abbrev->count - 1; i++) {
	memset(&die->attr_values[i], 0, sizeof(die->attr_values[i]));
vulnerabilities.short_description: No bounds check on die->attr_values array index against its allocated capacity
vulnerabilities.long_description: The loop iterates up to abbrev->count - 1 times, indexing into die->attr_values[i] without verifying that i is within the bounds of the allocated attr_values array. If abbrev->count exceeds the allocated size of die->attr_values, this leads to an out-of-bounds write (via memset and subsequent assignment) and read, potentially causing heap corruption, information disclosure, or code execution when processing a crafted DWARF binary.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Pointer arithmetic buf_end - buf can underflow if buf advances past buf_end
vulnerabilities.long_description: After each call to parse_attr_value, buf may advance beyond buf_end (e.g., due to a malformed attribute). On the next iteration, the expression buf_end - buf would produce a very large value (interpreted as an unsigned size) or a negative value (if signed), effectively disabling bounds checking inside parse_attr_value. There is no check that buf != NULL or buf <= buf_end after parse_attr_value returns, allowing subsequent iterations to read out-of-bounds memory from a crafted DWARF input.

commit_message: Fix oob write for dwarf with abbrev with count 0 (Fix #2083) (#2086)
cve_desc: Rizin is a UNIX-like reverse engineering framework and command-line toolset. In versions up to and including 0.3.1 there is a heap-based out of bounds write in parse_die() when reversing an AMD64 ELF binary with DWARF debug info. When a malicious AMD64 ELF binary is opened by a victim user, Rizin may crash or execute unintended actions. No workaround are known and users are advised to upgrade.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer underflow when abbrev->count is 0 leads to massive out-of-bounds access
vulnerabilities.long_description: If abbrev->count is 0, the expression `abbrev->count - 1` underflows (since size_t is unsigned) to a very large value (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF on 64-bit). This causes the loop to iterate an enormous number of times, leading to out-of-bounds reads and writes on die->attr_values, abbrev->defs, and potentially any memory accessible from the process. An attacker crafting a malicious DWARF binary with an abbreviation declaration that has a count of 0 could exploit this for code execution or information disclosure.

vulnerabilities.short_description: No NULL check on return value of parse_attr_value before continued use of buf
vulnerabilities.long_description: If parse_attr_value returns NULL (e.g., on a parsing error or when the buffer is exhausted), the returned NULL pointer is assigned to buf and then passed into the next iteration's call to parse_attr_value with `buf_end - buf` as the length. Subtracting a NULL pointer from buf_end is undefined behavior, and passing NULL as a buffer pointer will lead to a NULL pointer dereference, causing a crash (denial of service).

vulnerabilities.short_description: Pointer arithmetic underflow if buf exceeds buf_end
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expression `buf_end - buf` is passed as a size/length to parse_attr_value. If buf has been advanced past buf_end (which can happen if parse_attr_value overshoots), this subtraction wraps to a very large value when interpreted as an unsigned size, effectively disabling bounds checking inside parse_attr_value. This could allow out-of-bounds reads from the buffer.

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0218: reading before the start of the line

Problem:    Reading before the start of the line.
Solution:   When displaying "$" check the column is not negative.
cve_desc: Improper Validation of Specified Quantity in Input in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.0218.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read when col exceeds the length of the current line
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function computes `p + col` where `p` is the pointer to the current line's buffer, but `col` (derived from `col_arg`) is never validated against the actual length of the string returned by `ml_get_curline()`. If `col_arg` is larger than the line length, `p + col` points past the end of the buffer. This pointer is then passed to `mb_head_off`, which walks backwards through memory to find a multi-byte character head byte, potentially reading out-of-bounds memory. An attacker who can influence the column value (e.g., via crafted editor commands or file content) could trigger an out-of-bounds read, potentially leaking sensitive heap/stack data or causing a crash.

commit_message:
input-bmp: Increase header buffer in some cases

Signed-off-by: Peter Lemenkov <lemenkov@gmail.com>
cve_desc: AutoTrace v0.40.0 was discovered to contain a heap overflow via the ReadImage function at input-bmp.c:660.

vulnerabilities.short_description: fclose called on NULL file pointer when fopen fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: If fopen() fails, fd is NULL and the code jumps to the cleanup label via goto. At cleanup, fclose(fd) is called with a NULL pointer, which is undefined behavior according to the C standard and can cause a crash. The check `if (!fd)` sends control to `goto cleanup` but the cleanup block unconditionally calls fclose(fd).

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer truncation when casting biWidth/biHeight to unsigned short
vulnerabilities.long_description: Bitmap_Head.biWidth and biHeight are 32-bit integers but are cast to unsigned short (16-bit) when passed to at_bitmap_init. If the BMP declares dimensions larger than 65535 (which passes the earlier sanity checks), the values silently truncate. This causes a mismatch between the actual allocated image buffer size (from ReadImage using full 32-bit dimensions) and the dimensions recorded in the bitmap struct, potentially leading to out-of-bounds reads/writes by consumers of the returned bitmap.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential integer overflow in rowbytes calculation
vulnerabilities.long_description: Although there is an overflow check for `biWidth * biBitCnt` using unsigned long, the actual `rowbytes` computation is performed using signed int arithmetic. The variable `rowbytes` is a signed int, and the intermediate expression `Bitmap_Head.biWidth * Bitmap_Head.biBitCnt` is computed in the native int type (not unsigned long as in overflowTest), which can overflow for large but valid width/bitcount combinations. This could yield an incorrect (too small) rowbytes value, leading to heap buffer overflows during image reading.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer underflow in ColormapSize calculation with crafted bfOffs
vulnerabilities.long_description: If a malicious BMP file has bfOffs smaller than (biSize + 14), the subtraction underflows. Depending on the signedness of the types involved, this could result in a very large ColormapSize value. Although it is later capped to 256, the intermediate value is used in the condition check and could cause ReadColorMap to read more data than expected if the capping logic is bypassed or if ColormapSize is used elsewhere before capping.

commit_message: Fix segfault in older versions of OpenSSL (before 0.9.8i)
cve_desc: The openssl_encrypt function in ext/openssl/openssl.c in PHP 5.3.9 through 5.3.13 does not initialize a certain variable, which allows remote attackers to obtain sensitive information from process memory by providing zero bytes of input data.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in output buffer size calculation leads to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The calculation `data_len + EVP_CIPHER_block_size(cipher_type)` can overflow when `data_len` is a large value (close to INT_MAX). This would result in a small value for `outlen`, causing `emalloc(outlen + 1)` to allocate a very small buffer. The subsequent `EVP_EncryptUpdate` call would then write far beyond the allocated buffer, causing a heap-based buffer overflow. An attacker who controls the `data` parameter length could exploit this to achieve arbitrary code execution.

commit_message:
random32: update the net random state on interrupt and activity

This modifies the first 32 bits out of the 128 bits of a random CPU's
net_rand_state on interrupt or CPU activity to complicate remote
observations that could lead to guessing the network RNG's internal
state.

Note that depending on some network devices' interrupt rate moderation
or binding, this re-seeding might happen on every packet or even almost
never.

In addition, with NOHZ some CPUs might not even get timer interrupts,
leaving their local state rarely updated, while they are running
networked processes making use of the random state.  For this reason, we
also perform this update in update_process_times() in order to at least
update the state when there is user or system activity, since it's the
only case we care about.

Reported-by: Amit Klein <aksecurity@gmail.com>
Suggested-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
Cc: Eric Dumazet <edumazet@google.com>
Cc: "Jason A. Donenfeld" <Jason@zx2c4.com>
Cc: Andy Lutomirski <luto@kernel.org>
Cc: Kees Cook <keescook@chromium.org>
Cc: Thomas Gleixner <tglx@linutronix.de>
Cc: Peter Zijlstra <peterz@infradead.org>
Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org>
Signed-off-by: Willy Tarreau <w@1wt.eu>
Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
cve_desc: The Linux kernel through 5.7.11 allows remote attackers to make observations that help to obtain sensitive information about the internal state of the network RNG, aka CID-f227e3ec3b5c. This is related to drivers/char/random.c and kernel/time/timer.c.

commit_message: Fixed an issue affecting recursions in JIT
cve_desc: An out-of-bounds read vulnerability was discovered in the PCRE2 library in the get_recurse_data_length() function of the pcre2_jit_compile.c file. This issue affects recursions in JIT-compiled regular expressions caused by duplicate data transfers.

commit_message:
xfrm: xfrm_policy: fix a possible double xfrm_pols_put() in xfrm_bundle_lookup()

xfrm_policy_lookup() will call xfrm_pol_hold_rcu() to get a refcount of
pols[0]. This refcount can be dropped in xfrm_expand_policies() when
xfrm_expand_policies() return error. pols[0]'s refcount is balanced in
here. But xfrm_bundle_lookup() will also call xfrm_pols_put() with
num_pols == 1 to drop this refcount when xfrm_expand_policies() return
error.

This patch also fix an illegal address access. pols[0] will save a error
point when xfrm_policy_lookup fails. This lead to xfrm_pols_put to resolve
an illegal address in xfrm_bundle_lookup's error path.

Fix these by setting num_pols = 0 in xfrm_expand_policies()'s error path.

Fixes: 80c802f3073e ("xfrm: cache bundles instead of policies for outgoing flows")
Signed-off-by: Hangyu Hua <hbh25y@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Steffen Klassert <steffen.klassert@secunet.com>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in the Linux kernel through 5.18.14. xfrm_expand_policies in net/xfrm/xfrm_policy.c can cause a refcount to be dropped twice.

commit_message:
xfrm: xfrm_policy: fix a possible double xfrm_pols_put() in xfrm_bundle_lookup()

xfrm_policy_lookup() will call xfrm_pol_hold_rcu() to get a refcount of
pols[0]. This refcount can be dropped in xfrm_expand_policies() when
xfrm_expand_policies() return error. pols[0]'s refcount is balanced in
here. But xfrm_bundle_lookup() will also call xfrm_pols_put() with
num_pols == 1 to drop this refcount when xfrm_expand_policies() return
error.

This patch also fix an illegal address access. pols[0] will save a error
point when xfrm_policy_lookup fails. This lead to xfrm_pols_put to resolve
an illegal address in xfrm_bundle_lookup's error path.

Fix these by setting num_pols = 0 in xfrm_expand_policies()'s error path.

Fixes: 80c802f3073e ("xfrm: cache bundles instead of policies for outgoing flows")
Signed-off-by: Hangyu Hua <hbh25y@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Steffen Klassert <steffen.klassert@secunet.com>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in the Linux kernel through 5.18.14. xfrm_expand_policies in net/xfrm/xfrm_policy.c can cause a refcount to be dropped twice.

commit_message:
Fix oobread in dyldcache ##crash

* Reported by @hdthky via huntr.dev
* Reproducers: poc1
* BountyID: 8ae2c61a-2220-47a5-bfe8-fe6d41ab1f82
cve_desc: heap-buffer-overflow in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.8. This vulnerability is capable of inducing denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read on depArray and out-of-bounds write on deps via unchecked dep_index
vulnerabilities.long_description: The loop iterating over depArray uses a sentinel value (0xffff) as the termination condition but never checks that the index 'k' stays within the bounds of the allocated depArray (size cache->accel->depListCount). If the sentinel is missing or corrupt, this reads past the buffer. Additionally, dep_index (depArray[k] & 0x7fff, max value 32767) is used to index into 'deps' which is allocated with cache->hdr->imagesCount elements. There is no bounds check, so a malicious or corrupt cache file can cause an out-of-bounds write via deps[dep_index]++, potentially leading to code execution.

code_snippets:
for (j = 0; j < hdr->imagesCount; j++) {
	bool printing = !deps[j];
vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds access on deps array when iterating over multiple headers with different imagesCount
vulnerabilities.long_description: The 'deps' array is allocated based on cache->hdr->imagesCount (the first header's image count). However, the outer loop iterates over cache->n_hdr headers, and the inner loop uses hdr->imagesCount from each respective header. If a subsequent header (cache->hdr[i] for i>0) has a larger imagesCount than cache->hdr[0].imagesCount, the accesses deps[j] will go out of bounds, causing heap buffer overflow. An attacker crafting a malicious dyld cache could exploit this for arbitrary memory corruption.

commit_message:
Fix oobread in dyldcache ##crash

* Reported by @hdthky via huntr.dev
* Reproducers: poc1
* BountyID: 8ae2c61a-2220-47a5-bfe8-fe6d41ab1f82
cve_desc: heap-buffer-overflow in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.8. This vulnerability is capable of inducing denial of service.

code_snippets:
for (j = 0; j < hdr->imagesCount; j++) {
				bool printing = !deps[j];
vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds access on `deps` array when iterating over multiple headers with different image counts
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `deps` array is allocated using `cache->hdr->imagesCount`, which refers to the first header's image count (`cache->hdr[0].imagesCount`). However, the inner loop iterates using `hdr->imagesCount` where `hdr = &cache->hdr[i]` for each header in the cache. If a subsequent header has a larger `imagesCount` than the first, the accesses `deps[j]` and `deps[j]++` will read/write out of bounds of the allocated array, leading to heap corruption or information disclosure.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read on `depArray` due to missing bounds check on index `k`
vulnerabilities.long_description: The loop iterating over `depArray` starts at `extras[j].dependentsStartArrayIndex` and continues until `depArray[k] == 0xffff`. There is no check that `k` remains within `cache->accel->depListCount`. If the sentinel value `0xffff` is missing or the start index is corrupted, `k` will exceed the allocated size of `depArray`, causing a heap buffer over-read.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds access using unchecked `dep_index` to index `deps` and `img` arrays
vulnerabilities.long_description: The value `dep_index` is derived from untrusted data (`depArray[k] & 0x7fff`) and can be up to 32767. It is used without any bounds validation to index both `deps` (sized `cache->hdr->imagesCount`) and `img` (sized `hdr->imagesCount`). An attacker who controls the dyld cache file can craft a `dep_index` value that exceeds these array sizes, causing out-of-bounds reads and writes, potentially leading to arbitrary code execution.

commit_message:
mount: sanitize paths from non-root users

 $ mount /root/.ssh/../../dev/sda2
 mount: only root can mount UUID=17bc65ec-4125-4e7c-8a7d-e2795064c736 on /boot

this is too promiscuous. It seems better to ignore on command line
specified paths which are not resolve-able for non-root users.

Fixed version:

  $ mount /root/.ssh/../../dev/sda2
  mount: /root/.ssh/../../dev/sda2: Permission denied

  $ mount /dev/sda2
  mount: only root can mount UUID=17bc65ec-4125-4e7c-8a7d-e2795064c736 on /boot

Note that this bug has no relation to mount(2) permissions evaluation
in suid mode. The way how non-root user specifies paths on command
line is completely irrelevant for comparison with fstab entries.

Signed-off-by: Karel Zak <kzak@redhat.com>
cve_desc: (a) mount and (b) umount in util-linux 2.14.1, 2.17.2, and probably other versions allow local users to determine the existence of restricted directories by (1) using the --guess-fstype command-line option or (2) attempting to mount a non-existent device, which generates different error messages depending on whether the directory exists.

this is too promiscuous. It seems better to ignore on command line
