commit_message: Fix segfault in older versions of OpenSSL (before 0.9.8i)
cve_desc: The openssl_encrypt function in ext/openssl/openssl.c in PHP 5.3.9 through 5.3.13 does not initialize a certain variable, which allows remote attackers to obtain sensitive information from process memory by providing zero bytes of input data.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in output buffer size calculation leads to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The calculation `data_len + EVP_CIPHER_block_size(cipher_type)` can overflow when data_len is a large value (close to INT_MAX). This would result in a very small buffer being allocated via emalloc, while EVP_DecryptUpdate subsequently writes up to the original (large) data_len bytes into this undersized buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow. An attacker could exploit this by providing a very large input to openssl_decrypt.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing NULL check on php_base64_decode return value leads to NULL pointer dereference
vulnerabilities.long_description: When raw_input is false, php_base64_decode is called and its return value is assigned to base64_str and then to data. If php_base64_decode fails and returns NULL (e.g., due to invalid base64 input or allocation failure), data becomes NULL. This NULL pointer is subsequently passed to EVP_DecryptUpdate, causing a NULL pointer dereference or potentially undefined behavior. An attacker can trigger this by providing malformed base64 data.

vulnerabilities.short_description: EVP_CIPHER_CTX used without prior initialization via EVP_CIPHER_CTX_init
vulnerabilities.long_description: The EVP_CIPHER_CTX structure is declared on the stack but never initialized with EVP_CIPHER_CTX_init() (or EVP_CIPHER_CTX_reset in newer APIs) before being passed to EVP_DecryptInit. The structure may contain uninitialized memory, which could lead to undefined behavior within OpenSSL's internals. Additionally, EVP_CIPHER_CTX_cleanup at the end may attempt to free pointers from uninitialized fields if an error occurred before full initialization.

commit_message:
lib/iov_iter: initialize "flags" in new pipe_buffer

The functions copy_page_to_iter_pipe() and push_pipe() can both
allocate a new pipe_buffer, but the "flags" member initializer is
missing.

Fixes: 241699cd72a8 ("new iov_iter flavour: pipe-backed")
To: Alexander Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
To: linux-fsdevel@vger.kernel.org
To: linux-kernel@vger.kernel.org
Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Signed-off-by: Max Kellermann <max.kellermann@ionos.com>
Signed-off-by: Al Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in the way the "flags" member of the new pipe buffer structure was lacking proper initialization in copy_page_to_iter_pipe and push_pipe functions in the Linux kernel and could thus contain stale values. An unprivileged local user could use this flaw to write to pages in the page cache backed by read only files and as such escalate their privileges on the system.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Uninitialized pipe buffer flags allows writing to page cache pages (CVE-2022-0847 / Dirty Pipe)
vulnerabilities.long_description: When initializing a new pipe buffer entry, the code sets buf->ops, buf->page, buf->offset, and buf->len, but never clears buf->flags. This means the PIPE_BUF_FLAG_CAN_MERGE flag from a previous use of that pipe ring slot can persist. When a page cache page is placed into the pipe via this function (e.g., during splice), a subsequent write() to the pipe will see PIPE_BUF_FLAG_CAN_MERGE still set on the buffer and will write directly into the page cache page rather than allocating a new anonymous page. This allows an unprivileged user to overwrite the contents of read-only files (including SUID binaries, /etc/passwd, etc.) in the page cache, leading to local privilege escalation. The fix is to add 'buf->flags = 0;' when initializing the new pipe buffer.

commit_message:
Remove double free() in contrib/shpsrt, issue #39

This fixes issue #39
cve_desc: A double-free condition exists in contrib/shpsort.c of shapelib 1.5.0 and older releases. This issue may allow an attacker to cause a denial of service or have other unspecified impact via control over malloc.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Double free of `copy` when the second realloc fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `copy` is freed after the tokenization loop. If the subsequent realloc (to add the NULL terminator) fails, the error-handling path calls `free(copy)` again. This is a double-free vulnerability that can lead to heap corruption, potentially enabling arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference when initial realloc (acting as malloc) fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: On the first iteration of the loop, `result` is NULL, so `realloc(NULL, size)` acts as `malloc`. If this allocation fails, `tmp` is NULL and `result` is also NULL, so the condition `!tmp && result` is false, and execution falls through to `result = tmp; result[i++] = dupstr(cptr);`, which dereferences a NULL pointer. This causes a crash (denial of service) under low-memory conditions.

Avoid possible segfault when handling networkstatus vote with bad flavor

Fix for 6530; fix on 0.2.2.6-alpha.
cve_desc: Use-after-free vulnerability in dns.c in Tor before 0.2.2.38 might allow remote attackers to cause a denial of service (daemon crash) via vectors related to failed DNS requests.

commit_message:
net: xilinx_emaclite: Do not print real IOMEM pointer

Printing kernel pointers is discouraged because they might leak kernel
memory layout.  This fixes smatch warning:

drivers/net/ethernet/xilinx/xilinx_emaclite.c:1191 xemaclite_of_probe() warn:
 argument 4 to %08lX specifier is cast from pointer

Signed-off-by: YueHaibing <yuehaibing@huawei.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: drivers/net/ethernet/xilinx/xilinx_emaclite.c in the Linux kernel before 5.13.3 makes it easier for attackers to defeat an ASLR protection mechanism because it prints a kernel pointer (i.e., the real IOMEM pointer).

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential NULL pointer dereference when platform_get_resource returns NULL
vulnerabilities.long_description: The return value of platform_get_resource(ofdev, IORESOURCE_MEM, 0) is not checked for NULL before being passed to devm_ioremap_resource(). While devm_ioremap_resource() in modern kernels internally handles a NULL resource by returning an ERR_PTR, after the IS_ERR check on lp->base_addr, the code unconditionally dereferences res->start and res->end. If the internal behavior of devm_ioremap_resource were to change, or if the compiler reorders operations, res could be NULL at that point, leading to a NULL pointer dereference. This is a well-known anti-pattern in kernel code. An attacker who can influence device tree configuration or platform resource registration could trigger this.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Resource leak of phy_node OF reference on error path
vulnerabilities.long_description: of_parse_phandle() returns a device_node with an incremented reference count. If register_netdev() fails after of_parse_phandle() has been called, the error path jumps to the 'error' label which only calls free_netdev(). The reference obtained by of_parse_phandle() on lp->phy_node is never released via of_node_put(), causing a reference count leak. Repeated failures could lead to resource exhaustion over time.

commit_message:
net: xilinx_emaclite: Do not print real IOMEM pointer

Printing kernel pointers is discouraged because they might leak kernel
memory layout.  This fixes smatch warning:

drivers/net/ethernet/xilinx/xilinx_emaclite.c:1191 xemaclite_of_probe() warn:
 argument 4 to %08lX specifier is cast from pointer

Signed-off-by: YueHaibing <yuehaibing@huawei.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: drivers/net/ethernet/xilinx/xilinx_emaclite.c in the Linux kernel before 5.13.3 makes it easier for attackers to defeat an ASLR protection mechanism because it prints a kernel pointer (i.e., the real IOMEM pointer).

vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference if platform_get_resource for IORESOURCE_MEM returns NULL
vulnerabilities.long_description: platform_get_resource(ofdev, IORESOURCE_MEM, 0) can return NULL. While devm_ioremap_resource internally checks for a NULL resource and returns an ERR_PTR, in some older kernel versions this check may not exist, leading to a NULL dereference inside devm_ioremap_resource. More critically, even if the IS_ERR check passes in a race or edge case, res->start and res->end are accessed without a prior NULL check on res. This is a missing validation that could cause a kernel oops/panic.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Resource leak of phy_node reference and MDIO bus on register_netdev failure
vulnerabilities.long_description: If register_netdev() fails, the error path only calls free_netdev(). It does not call of_node_put() on lp->phy_node (obtained via of_parse_phandle, which increments the refcount) and does not tear down the MDIO bus set up by xemaclite_mdio_setup(). This leads to a device-tree node reference leak and potentially an unfreed MDIO bus structure, which could be exploited for resource exhaustion if the probe path can be triggered repeatedly.

commit_message:
Avoid possible segfault when handling networkstatus vote with bad flavor

Fix for 6530; fix on 0.2.2.6-alpha.
cve_desc: Use-after-free vulnerability in dns.c in Tor before 0.2.2.38 might allow remote attackers to cause a denial of service (daemon crash) via vectors related to failed DNS requests.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read when accessing tok->args[2] instead of tok->args[1]
vulnerabilities.long_description: When tok->n_args is exactly 2 (i.e., > 1), the code correctly parses tok->args[1] to get the flavor name, but in the error-handling path it logs tok->args[2] instead of tok->args[1]. Since the args array only has indices 0 and 1 when n_args is 2, accessing tok->args[2] is an out-of-bounds read. This could lead to reading uninitialized or adjacent heap memory, potentially causing a crash or information disclosure. An attacker could craft a malicious network status document with an invalid flavor name and exactly 2 arguments to trigger this bug.

commit_message:
Fix potential divide by zero error when executing FractionalMaxPool, when pooling ratio is higher than input size for a particular dimension.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 412151722
Change-Id: I06e57cbb8eca43816eff79eac264fa7aae8f7163
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `FractionalMaxPool` can be made to crash a TensorFlow process via a division by 0. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

code_snippets:
ConstEigenMatrixMap in_mat(tensor_in.flat<T>().data(), input_size[3],
                               input_size[2] * input_size[1] * input_size[0]);

EigenMatrixMap out_mat(output_tensor->flat<T>().data(), output_size[3],
                           output_size[2] * output_size[1] * output_size[0]);
vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in Eigen matrix map dimension computation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expressions `input_size[2] * input_size[1] * input_size[0]` and `output_size[2] * output_size[1] * output_size[0]` multiply three `int` values together without any overflow check. If the tensor dimensions are large enough, this multiplication can overflow a 32-bit integer, resulting in a much smaller (or negative) value being used as the column count for the Eigen matrix map. This would cause the map to misinterpret the underlying buffer size, and subsequent column accesses in the nested loops would read/write out-of-bounds memory, potentially leading to information disclosure or code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Output size validated only with debug assertion, allowing zero or negative sizes in production
vulnerabilities.long_description: The check `DCHECK_GT(output_size[i], 0)` is a debug-only assertion that is compiled out in release/production builds. If `pooling_ratio_[i]` is larger than `input_size[i]` in a way that passes the earlier check but yields a zero from `std::floor(input_size[i] / pooling_ratio_[i])`, `output_size[i]` can be zero. This leads to allocating a zero-sized output tensor while the loop logic (e.g., `height_cum_seq.size() - 1` underflowing for an unsigned type) may still attempt to write data, causing out-of-bounds memory access.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential out-of-bounds read/write from unvalidated pooling sequence values
vulnerabilities.long_description: The values in `height_cum_seq` and `width_cum_seq` returned by `GeneratePoolingSequence` are used directly as `height_start`/`width_start` indices into the input matrix without validating that they fall within [0, input_size). If the generated sequence contains negative values or values exceeding input dimensions, the computed `in_offset` or `out_offset` will be out of bounds for the Eigen matrix maps, resulting in out-of-bounds reads from `in_mat` and out-of-bounds writes to `out_mat`. An attacker who can control the seed or input dimensions could exploit this to read or corrupt adjacent memory.

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0018: going over the end of the typahead

Problem:    Going over the end of the typahead.
Solution:   Put a NUL after the typeahead.
cve_desc: Integer Overflow or Wraparound in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds write of NUL byte past buffer boundary
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code writes `tp[len] = NUL` to NUL-terminate the buffer for subsequent string comparison operations. When `buf` is non-NULL, `tp = buf + offset` and `len = *buflen - offset`, so the write goes to `buf[*buflen]`. If `*buflen == bufsize` (the buffer is full), this writes one byte past the allocated buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow. Similarly, when `buf` is NULL, it writes at `typebuf.tb_buf + typebuf.tb_off + typebuf.tb_len`, which may also be past the allocated boundary. An attacker who can influence terminal input to fill the buffer could trigger this overflow, potentially leading to code execution or denial of service.

commit_message:
virtio-serial: fix ANY_LAYOUT

Don't assume a specific layout for control messages.
Required by virtio 1.

Signed-off-by: Michael S. Tsirkin <mst@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Amit Shah <amit.shah@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Jason Wang <jasowang@redhat.com>
cve_desc: Buffer overflow in the send_control_msg function in hw/char/virtio-serial-bus.c in QEMU before 2.4.0 allows guest users to cause a denial of service (QEMU process crash) via a crafted virtio control message.

code_snippets:
/* TODO: detect a buffer that's too short, set NEEDS_RESET */
    iov_from_buf(elem.in_sg, elem.in_num, 0, buf, len);
vulnerabilities.short_description: Guest buffer overflow due to missing length validation before writing control message
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function writes 'len' bytes into the guest-provided scatter-gather buffers without checking that the total capacity of elem.in_sg (elem.in_num entries) is sufficient to hold 'len' bytes. The TODO comment explicitly acknowledges this gap. If the guest supplies a buffer smaller than 'len', the write via iov_from_buf could overflow the guest's buffer, and the subsequent virtqueue_push reports 'len' bytes written even though fewer were actually copied. This could lead to incorrect guest state, information disclosure (guest sees stale memory), or be leveraged by a malicious guest to trigger unexpected host behavior depending on how iov_from_buf handles the short buffer internally.

commit_message: fix #2964 - double-free in bin_qnx.c

It only affects hosts with a PCI device and driver that supports mediated devices (e.g., GRID driver). commit_message: Fix use-after-free in PlayerGeneric destructor
cve_desc: PlayerGeneric.cpp in MilkyTracker through 1.02.00 has a use-after-free in the PlayerGeneric destructor.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Use-after-free: mixer is deleted then dereferenced
vulnerabilities.long_description: The destructor deletes `mixer` first, then in the `if (player)` block it dereferences `mixer` by calling `mixer->isActive()` and `mixer->isDeviceRemoved(player)`. Since `mixer` was already freed, this is a use-after-free vulnerability. If `mixer` is non-null and `player` is also non-null, the code will access freed memory, leading to undefined behavior which could be exploited for code execution or cause a crash.

Signed-off-by: Jonathon Jongsma <jjongsma@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Ján Tomko <jtomko@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: Ján Tomko <jtomko@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in libvirt in the virConnectListAllNodeDevices API in versions before 7.0.0. It only affects hosts with a PCI device and driver that supports mediated devices (e.g., GRID driver). This flaw could be used by an unprivileged client with a read-only connection to crash the libvirt daemon by executing the 'nodedev-list' virsh command. The highest threat from this vulnerability is to system availability.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Operator precedence bug causes out-of-bounds pointer dereference when freeing old mdev types
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expression `*mdev_types[i]` is parsed as `*(mdev_types[i])` due to C operator precedence ([] binds tighter than *), when the intended expression is `(*mdev_types)[i]`. This means instead of indexing into the array pointed to by *mdev_types, it indexes `i` elements past the `mdev_types` pointer itself (a pointer to a local variable on the caller's stack), then dereferences the result. For i > 0, this reads out-of-bounds memory from the caller's stack, which could lead to a crash (denial of service) or, in certain scenarios, could be leveraged for information disclosure or code execution if the incorrectly-read pointer value is passed to the free-like function.

commit_message:
- Fix Integer Overflows in Size Calculations,
  reported by X41 D-Sec.
cve_desc: Unbound before 1.9.5 allows an integer overflow in a size calculation in dnscrypt/dnscrypt.c. NOTE: The vendor disputes that this is a vulnerability. Although the code may be vulnerable, a running Unbound installation cannot be remotely or locally exploited

code_snippets:
rrlen = strlen(dnscenv->provider_name) +
                         strlen(ttl_class_type) +
                         4 * sizeof(struct SignedCert) + // worst case scenario
                         1 + // trailing double quote
                         1;
vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in uint16_t rrlen leads to undersized heap allocation and buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `rrlen` is declared as `uint16_t` (max 65535), but the guard only rejects provider names with strlen >= 0xffff0000 (~4 billion). If the sum of `strlen(provider_name) + strlen(ttl_class_type) + 4*sizeof(SignedCert) + 2` exceeds 65535 but is below the guard threshold, the result silently wraps around when stored in `uint16_t`. This causes `malloc(rrlen)` to allocate a much smaller buffer than needed, and the subsequent `snprintf` calls write beyond the allocated buffer, resulting in a heap-based buffer overflow. An attacker who controls `provider_name` could exploit this for arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: strdup return values not checked for NULL before being passed to cfg_str2list_insert
vulnerabilities.long_description: Both `strdup(dnscenv->provider_name)` and `strdup("deny")` may return NULL on memory allocation failure. These NULL pointers are passed directly to `cfg_str2list_insert`, which likely dereferences them, leading to a NULL pointer dereference and crash. Similarly, `strdup(rr)` passed to `cfg_strlist_insert` later in the function is also unchecked.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing semicolon after `continue` statement causes a compilation error or unintended behavior
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `continue` statement inside the overflow guard block is missing its terminating semicolon. Depending on the compiler, this may cause a compilation error or could be parsed in an unexpected way, potentially causing the guard to not function as intended and allowing the integer overflow to proceed unchecked.

commit_message:
iwlwifi: pcie: fix rb_allocator workqueue allocation

We don't handle failures in the rb_allocator workqueue allocation
correctly. To fix that, move the code earlier so the cleanup is
easier and we don't have to undo all the interrupt allocations in
this case.

Signed-off-by: Johannes Berg <johannes.berg@intel.com>
Signed-off-by: Luca Coelho <luciano.coelho@intel.com>
cve_desc: drivers/net/wireless/intel/iwlwifi/pcie/trans.c in the Linux kernel 5.2.14 does not check the alloc_workqueue return value, leading to a NULL pointer dereference.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing NULL check on alloc_workqueue return value
vulnerabilities.long_description: The return value of alloc_workqueue() is not checked for NULL. If the allocation fails, trans_pcie->rba.alloc_wq will be NULL, and subsequent use of this workqueue (e.g., queue_work) will dereference a NULL pointer, causing a kernel panic/oops. The function should check for NULL and jump to an error cleanup path.

commit_message:
Fixed int overflow in PresentationContext_new

Thanks to hac425 CVE-2020-11038
cve_desc: In FreeRDP less than or equal to 2.0.0, an Integer Overflow to Buffer Overflow exists. When using /video redirection, a manipulated server can instruct the client to allocate a buffer with a smaller size than requested due to an integer overflow in size calculation. With later messages, the server can manipulate the client to write data out of bound to the previously allocated buffer. This has been patched in 2.1.0.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in buffer size calculation for surfaceData allocation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expression `width * height * 4` is computed using UINT32 arithmetic, which can overflow if width and height are sufficiently large (e.g., width=65536, height=65536 would wrap around). This could result in allocating a much smaller buffer than expected, leading to subsequent heap buffer overflows when pixel data is written into surfaceData. An attacker who controls the presentation dimensions could exploit this to achieve remote code execution.

Thanks to hac425 CVE-2020-11038
cve_desc: In FreeRDP less than or equal to 2.0.0, an Integer Overflow to Buffer Overflow exists. When using /video redirection, a manipulated server can instruct the client to allocate a buffer with a smaller size than requested due to an integer overflow in size calculation. With later messages, the server can manipulate the client to write data out of bound to the previously allocated buffer. This has been patched in 2.1.0.

commit_message:
- Fix Integer Overflows in Size Calculations,
  reported by X41 D-Sec.
cve_desc: Unbound before 1.9.5 allows an integer overflow in a size calculation in dnscrypt/dnscrypt.c. NOTE: The vendor disputes that this is a vulnerability. Although the code may be vulnerable, a running Unbound installation cannot be remotely or locally exploited

code_snippets:
rrlen = strlen(dnscenv->provider_name) +
                         strlen(ttl_class_type) +
                         4 * sizeof(struct SignedCert) + // worst case scenario
                         1 + // trailing double quote
                         1;
vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in uint16_t rrlen leads to undersized heap allocation and buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `rrlen` is declared as `uint16_t`, which can hold a maximum value of 65535. If `dnscenv->provider_name` is sufficiently long, the sum of `strlen(provider_name) + strlen(ttl_class_type) + 4 * sizeof(struct SignedCert) + 2` can exceed 65535, causing the value to wrap around due to integer overflow. This results in `malloc(rrlen)` allocating a much smaller buffer than needed. The subsequent `snprintf` calls then write well beyond the allocated buffer, causing a heap-based buffer overflow. An attacker who can control the provider name (e.g., via configuration) could exploit this to achieve arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Unchecked strdup return values may pass NULL pointers to functions
vulnerabilities.long_description: Multiple calls to `strdup()` are made without checking for NULL return values. If memory allocation fails, `strdup` returns NULL, which is then passed directly to `cfg_str2list_insert` or `cfg_strlist_insert`. This can lead to NULL pointer dereferences within those functions, potentially crashing the application. Additionally, the return value of `cfg_strlist_insert` for local_data is never checked, so failures are silently ignored.

commit_message:
seq_file: disallow extremely large seq buffer allocations

There is no reasonable need for a buffer larger than this, and it avoids
int overflow pitfalls.

Fixes: 058504edd026 ("fs/seq_file: fallback to vmalloc allocation")
Suggested-by: Al Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
Reported-by: Qualys Security Advisory <qsa@qualys.com>
Signed-off-by: Eric Sandeen <sandeen@redhat.com>
Cc: stable@kernel.org
Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
cve_desc: fs/seq_file.c in the Linux kernel 3.16 through 5.13.x before 5.13.4 does not properly restrict seq buffer allocations, leading to an integer overflow, an Out-of-bounds Write, and escalation to root by an unprivileged user, aka CID-8cae8cd89f05.

commit_message:
aaef on arm/thumb switches causes uaf ##crash

* Reported by peacock-doris via huntr.dev
* Reproducer: poc_uaf_r_reg_get
cve_desc: Use After Free in r_reg_get_name_idx in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.6.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of `buf` on multiple early return paths after allocation
vulnerabilities.long_description: After `buf` is allocated via `malloc`, there are several early `return` statements that do not call `free(buf)`. Specifically, the returns for: (1) ESIL not initialized, (2) no SP register alias found, and (3) no PC register found all leak the allocated buffer. In a long-running process like radare2, repeated invocations of this function hitting these error paths would accumulate leaked memory, potentially leading to denial of service through memory exhaustion.

code_snippets: if (!r_anal_op (core->anal, &op, cur, buf + i, iend - i, ...))
vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer truncation when computing `iend` from 64-bit subtraction into a 32-bit int
vulnerabilities.long_description: `iend` is declared as `int` but is assigned the result of `end - start` where both are `ut64` (unsigned 64-bit). If the difference exceeds INT_MAX but is still below MAX_SCAN_SIZE (depending on its definition), the value stored in `iend` could be truncated or wrap around to a negative value. While there is a check `if (iend < 0) return;`, the truncation itself could cause `iend` to appear valid (e.g., a large ut64 difference could truncate to a small positive int), leading to an undersized buffer allocation followed by an out-of-bounds read via `r_io_read_at` and `r_anal_op`.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential out-of-bounds read when `i` is close to `iend`
vulnerabilities.long_description: The length passed to `r_anal_op` is `iend - i`. Although there is a check `if (i > iend) goto repeat`, when `i == iend` the length becomes 0, and `buf + i` points to the last valid byte. The `r_anal_op` function may still attempt to read beyond the buffer with a zero or very small length parameter. Additionally, after a failed `r_anal_op` call, `i += minopsize - 1` (typically 3) is added without re-checking bounds before the next iteration accesses `buf + i`, potentially reading past the allocated buffer.

commit_message:
openvswitch: fix OOB access in reserve_sfa_size()

Given a sufficiently large number of actions, while copying and
reserving memory for a new action of a new flow, if next_offset is
greater than MAX_ACTIONS_BUFSIZE, the function reserve_sfa_size() does
not return -EMSGSIZE as expected, but it allocates MAX_ACTIONS_BUFSIZE
bytes increasing actions_len by req_size. This can then lead to an OOB
write access, especially when further actions need to be copied.

Fix it by rearranging the flow action size check.

Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Fixes: f28cd2af22a0 ("openvswitch: fix flow actions reallocation")
Signed-off-by: Paolo Valerio <pvalerio@redhat.com>
Acked-by: Eelco Chaudron <echaudro@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: An integer coercion error was found in the openvswitch kernel module. Given a sufficiently large number of actions, while copying and reserving memory for a new action of a new flow, the reserve_sfa_size() function does not return -EMSGSIZE as expected, potentially leading to an out-of-bounds write access. This flaw allows a local user to crash or potentially escalate their privileges on the system.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow when computing new_acts_size due to size_t to int truncation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `new_acts_size` is declared as `int`, but `next_offset + req_size` and `ksize(*sfa) * 2` are computed as `size_t` (unsigned, typically 64-bit on 64-bit systems). When the result of `max()` exceeds INT_MAX, assigning it to `int new_acts_size` causes truncation/undefined behavior, potentially resulting in a small or negative value. If the truncated `new_acts_size` wraps to a small positive value that passes the `MAX_ACTIONS_BUFSIZE` check, `nla_alloc_flow_actions` would allocate an undersized buffer. The subsequent `memcpy(acts->actions, (*sfa)->actions, (*sfa)->actions_len)` would then write beyond the allocated buffer, leading to a heap buffer overflow. This could be exploited for privilege escalation or denial of service in the kernel.

commit_message:
aaef on arm/thumb switches causes uaf ##crash

* Reported by peacock-doris via huntr.dev
* Reproducer: poc_uaf_r_reg_get
cve_desc: Use After Free in r_reg_get_name_idx in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.6.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Multiple early return paths leak allocated memory (buf, spname, pcname).
vulnerabilities.long_description: After `buf` is allocated via malloc, several early return paths fail to free it. For example, if ESIL initialization fails, the function returns without freeing `buf`. Similarly, after `spname = strdup(kspname)`, if the pcname check fails, both `buf` and `spname` are leaked. After `pcname = strdup(kpcname)`, if `core->anal->cur->arch` causes issues, `buf`, `spname`, and `pcname` could all leak. In a long-running process repeatedly calling this function, these leaks accumulate and could lead to denial of service through memory exhaustion.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer truncation when ut64 subtraction result is stored in int `iend`, potentially leading to undersized buffer allocation.
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `iend` is declared as `int`, but `end` and `start` are both `ut64` (unsigned 64-bit). The subtraction `end - start` produces a ut64 result which is then implicitly truncated to int. If the ut64 result has specific bit patterns (e.g., the lower 32 bits represent a small positive number while the actual 64-bit value is very large), the truncation could bypass both the `iend < 0` and `iend > MAX_SCAN_SIZE` checks. This would result in a small buffer being allocated via `malloc((size_t)iend + 2)`, while `r_io_read_at` reads `iend + 1` bytes into it, and the loop iterates over what it believes is a larger range, causing a heap buffer overflow when accessing `buf + i`.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential out-of-bounds read when `i` approaches `iend` due to unsigned/signed mismatch.
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `i` is `size_t` (unsigned) and `iend` is `int`. The expression `iend - i` is computed with mixed signedness. When `i` equals `iend`, `iend - i` evaluates to 0, and `buf + i` points near the end of the allocated buffer. However, after `r_anal_op` fails, `i += minopsize - 1` increments `i` without a subsequent bounds check before the next iteration may access `buf + i` again. The check `if (i > iend)` at the top of the loop uses `>` rather than `>=`, allowing one iteration where `i == iend` and `iend - i == 0` is passed to `r_anal_op`, which may attempt to read beyond the buffer. Additionally, if `i` exceeds `iend` but wraps or the comparison misbehaves due to unsigned/signed conversion, further out-of-bounds access is possible.

commit_message:
kdc: validate sname in TGS-REQ

In tgs_build_reply(), validate the server name in the TGS-REQ is present before
dereferencing.
cve_desc: A null pointer de-reference was found in the way samba kerberos server handled missing sname in TGS-REQ (Ticket Granting Server - Request). An authenticated user could use this flaw to crash the samba server.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Type confusion: Realm string passed as request object to _kdc_set_e_text
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `r` is of type `Realm` (a `char *` string, set from `b->realm`), but `_kdc_set_e_text()` expects a request/context object as its first argument (similar to how `_kdc_audit_addreason` takes `(kdc_request_t)priv`). Passing the realm string as if it were a request structure pointer leads to type confusion, where the function will dereference the string data as a structure. This can cause a crash (denial of service) or potentially memory corruption. An attacker could trigger this by sending a TGS-REQ with no sname and without the enc_tkt_in_skey option.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of S4U2Self principal and name when constrained delegation overwrites them
vulnerabilities.long_description: When both the S4U2Self (PA-FOR-USER) and constrained delegation code paths execute, the S4U2Self code allocates new `tp` (principal) and `tpn` (name string) values. The constrained delegation code then overwrites both `tp` and `tpn` without first freeing the S4U2Self-allocated values. The cleanup code `if (tpn != cpn) free(tpn)` and `if (tp && tp != cp) krb5_free_principal(context, tp)` only frees the final values. The intermediate allocations from S4U2Self are leaked. Repeated exploitation could lead to memory exhaustion (denial of service).

vulnerabilities.short_description: Client fetch error silently ignored for cross-realm clients, allowing processing with NULL client
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `_kdc_db_fetch` for the client principal fails with an error other than `HDB_ERR_NOT_FOUND_HERE`, and the client's realm differs from the krbtgt realm, the code logs the error but does NOT jump to `out`. Execution falls through with `client` potentially NULL and `ret` set to an error value. The error return is subsequently overwritten by `check_PAC()`. This means the invalid/disabled client flags check (`else if (ret == 0 && ...)`) is also skipped for these cross-realm clients. Processing continues with a potentially NULL or invalid client pointer, which could lead to authorization bypass or null pointer dereferences in subsequent operations that don't guard against NULL client.

commit_message: Improve handling of Gopher responses (#1022)
cve_desc: In Squid 3.x through 3.5.28, 4.x through 4.17, and 5.x before 5.6, due to improper buffer management, a Denial of Service can occur when processing long Gopher server responses.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Off-by-one buffer overflow when null-terminating the line buffer
vulnerabilities.long_description: After copying `llen` bytes into `line` (a stack buffer of TEMP_BUF_SIZE), the code null-terminates at index `llen + 1` instead of `llen`. This writes one byte beyond the intended data. In the worst case, when the overflow-truncation logic sets `llen = TEMP_BUF_SIZE - gopherState->len - 1` and then `llen += gopherState->len` makes it `TEMP_BUF_SIZE - 1`, the write to `line[TEMP_BUF_SIZE]` is one byte past the end of the LOCAL_ARRAY stack buffer, causing a stack buffer overflow.

code_snippets: outbuf.appendf(" %s\n", icon_url, rfc1738_escape_unescaped(selector), html_quote(name));
vulnerabilities.short_description: Format string argument mismatch: 5 specifiers but only 3 arguments provided for GOPHER_WWW case
vulnerabilities.long_description: The format string contains five format specifiers (%s, %s, %c, %s, %s) but only three arguments are passed (icon_url, rfc1738_escape_unescaped(selector), html_quote(name)). The missing two arguments will cause reads from uninitialized stack memory, leading to undefined behavior, potential information disclosure, or crashes. This is likely a copy-paste bug from the adjacent code path.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Null pointer dereference when strtok returns NULL for s_code or s_recno
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the HTML_CSO_RESULT parsing, `strtok` may return NULL for `s_code` or `s_recno` if the input line doesn't contain enough delimiters. The code only checks whether `result` (the third token) is NULL before calling `atoi(s_code)` and `atoi(s_recno)`. If either `s_code` or `s_recno` is NULL, `atoi` will dereference a null pointer, causing a crash. A malicious gopher server could send specially crafted CSO result lines to trigger this.

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0240: crash when using ":mkspell" with an empty .dic file

Problem:    Crash when using ":mkspell" with an empty .dic file.
Solution:   Check for an empty word tree.
cve_desc: NULL Pointer Dereference in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.0240.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Stack buffer overflow due to unbounded depth increment without bounds check against MAXWLEN
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable 'depth' is incremented when descending into the trie without any check that it remains less than MAXWLEN. The arrays arridx, curi, tword, and wordcount are all stack-allocated with size MAXWLEN. If the trie stored in slang->sl_fbyts/sl_fidxs has a depth exceeding MAXWLEN (e.g., from a maliciously crafted spell file), depth will exceed the array bounds, causing out-of-bounds writes on the stack. This can overwrite the return address and other stack data, potentially leading to arbitrary code execution.

commit_message: SERVER-59071 Treat '$sample' as unsharded when connecting directly to shards
cve_desc: An authorized user may trigger an invariant which may result in denial of service or server exit if a relevant aggregation request is sent to a shard. Usually, the requests are sent via mongos and special privileges are required in order to know the address of the shards and to log in to the shards of an auth enabled environment.

commit_message:
openvswitch: fix OOB access in reserve_sfa_size()

Given a sufficiently large number of actions, while copying and
reserving memory for a new action of a new flow, if next_offset is
greater than MAX_ACTIONS_BUFSIZE, the function reserve_sfa_size() does
not return -EMSGSIZE as expected, but it allocates MAX_ACTIONS_BUFSIZE
bytes increasing actions_len by req_size. This can then lead to an OOB
write access, especially when further actions need to be copied.

Fix it by rearranging the flow action size check.

KASAN splat below:

Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Fixes: f28cd2af22a0 ("openvswitch: fix flow actions reallocation")
Signed-off-by: Paolo Valerio <pvalerio@redhat.com>
Acked-by: Eelco Chaudron <echaudro@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: An integer coercion error was found in the openvswitch kernel module. Given a sufficiently large number of actions, while copying and reserving memory for a new action of a new flow, the reserve_sfa_size() function does not return -EMSGSIZE as expected, potentially leading to an out-of-bounds write access. This flaw allows a local user to crash or potentially escalate their privileges on the system.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer truncation when assigning size_t result to int new_acts_size can lead to undersized allocation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `new_acts_size` is declared as `int`, but the expression `max(next_offset + req_size, ksize(*sfa) * 2)` is computed as `size_t` (unsigned, typically 64-bit on 64-bit systems). If the computed value exceeds INT_MAX, assigning it to `int` causes truncation/sign change. A truncated (possibly negative) `new_acts_size` would bypass the `new_acts_size > MAX_ACTIONS_BUFSIZE` check (signed comparison against a positive constant would pass if new_acts_size is negative), resulting in `nla_alloc_flow_actions` being called with a small or negative size. This leads to a heap buffer overflow when `memcpy(acts->actions, (*sfa)->actions, (*sfa)->actions_len)` copies data into the undersized buffer, potentially allowing kernel heap corruption and privilege escalation.

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0240: crash when using ":mkspell" with an empty .dic file

Problem:    Crash when using ":mkspell" with an empty .dic file.
Solution:   Check for an empty word tree.
cve_desc: NULL Pointer Dereference in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.0240.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Stack buffer overflow due to missing bounds check on 'depth' before incrementing into fixed-size arrays.
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable 'depth' is incremented without checking that it remains within the bounds of the stack-allocated arrays arridx[], curi[], tword[], and wordcount[], all sized MAXWLEN. If the input trie (slang->sl_fbyts / sl_fidxs) contains a path longer than MAXWLEN bytes, depth will exceed MAXWLEN-1, causing out-of-bounds writes to all four stack arrays. This can corrupt adjacent stack memory including the return address, potentially allowing arbitrary code execution. A maliciously crafted spell file with a deeply nested trie would trigger this overflow.

commit_message: Limit the number of elements in a vector (found by oss-fuzz)
cve_desc: cdf_read_property_info in cdf.c in file through 5.37 does not restrict the number of CDF_VECTOR elements, which allows a heap-based buffer overflow (4-byte out-of-bounds write).

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in string length rounding and offset calculation can lead to out-of-bounds read
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the CDF_LENGTH32_STRING/CDF_LENGTH32_WSTRING case, the bounds check `o4 + l > left` is performed with the original length `l`, but then `l` is incremented if odd (`if (l & 1) l++`). The rounded-up value is used to advance `slen` and recalculate `o4`. This means the actual offset advances past what was validated. Furthermore, `slen += l >> 1` and `o4 = slen * sizeof(uint32_t)` can overflow on 32-bit platforms (where size_t is 32 bits), causing `o4` to wrap around to a small value. In the next loop iteration, the bounds check `o4 + sizeof(uint32_t) > left` would then pass, leading to out-of-bounds reads from `q[o4]` with a wrapped-around offset. An attacker can craft a malicious CDF file with specific string lengths to trigger this overflow and read memory beyond the buffer boundaries.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Addition overflow in bounds check `o4 + l` can bypass the out-of-bounds protection
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expression `o4 + l` where both are `size_t` values can wrap around to a small value on 32-bit systems, causing the comparison `o4 + l > left` to incorrectly evaluate to false. This would allow subsequent accesses like `&q[o4]` with an `o4` value that, combined with `l`, actually exceeds the buffer size `left`. An attacker can craft a CDF file with carefully chosen property lengths to trigger this integer wraparound and bypass the bounds check, leading to out-of-bounds memory reads.

commit_message: Improve handling of Gopher responses (#1022)
cve_desc: In Squid 3.x through 3.5.28, 4.x through 4.17, and 5.x before 5.6, due to improper buffer management, a Denial of Service can occur when processing long Gopher server responses.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Off-by-one error in null termination writes past intended buffer boundary
vulnerabilities.long_description: The null terminator is written at `line[llen + 1]` instead of `line[llen]`. Since `llen` already represents the total length of the copied data (including the newline character), the null byte should go at index `llen`, not `llen + 1`. When `llen` is close to `TEMP_BUF_SIZE - 1` (after the overflow adjustment clamps it), `line[llen + 1]` can write one byte past the end of the `TEMP_BUF_SIZE`-sized buffer, causing a stack-based buffer overflow. Additionally, the byte at `line[llen]` is left uninitialized, potentially including stale data from previous iterations in subsequent string operations.

code_snippets: snprintf(tmpbuf, TEMP_BUF_SIZE, " %s\n", icon_url, host, rfc1738_escape_unescaped(selector + 5), html_quote(name));
snprintf(tmpbuf, TEMP_BUF_SIZE, " %s\n", icon_url, host, gtype, escaped_selector, html_quote(name));
vulnerabilities.short_description: Cross-site scripting via unescaped host and port values from gopher server response
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `host` and `port` values are parsed directly from a gopher server's response and are inserted into generated HTML without any HTML entity escaping. A malicious gopher server could craft responses containing JavaScript or HTML injection payloads in the host or port fields. For example, the `host` variable is used directly in href attributes and link text contexts in multiple snprintf calls. An attacker controlling a gopher server could inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript that executes in the victim's browser context.

vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference when strtok returns NULL for s_code or s_recno
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the HTML_CSO_RESULT handling, `strtok` is called three times to parse `s_code`, `s_recno`, and `result`. Only `result` is checked for NULL before use. If the line doesn't contain enough colon-delimited fields, `s_code` or `s_recno` can be NULL, but they are passed directly to `atoi()` without null checks, causing a NULL pointer dereference and crash. A malicious gopher server can send a crafted CSO response line starting with '-' but lacking the expected colon-separated fields to trigger this.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Static buffer reuse when multiple functions returning static buffers are called in same expression
vulnerabilities.long_description: Functions like `html_quote()`, `rfc1738_escape_part()`, and `rfc1738_escape_unescaped()` typically return pointers to internal static buffers. When multiple such functions are called as arguments to the same `snprintf()` call, later calls overwrite the static buffer used by earlier calls. This leads to corrupted output where multiple arguments may point to the same buffer content (the last call's result). This can cause incorrect HTML generation and potentially be leveraged for security bypass if the escaping of one field is effectively lost.

commit_message:
i2c-ddc: fix oob read

Suggested-by: Michael Hanselmann <public@hansmi.ch>
Signed-off-by: Gerd Hoffmann <kraxel@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Michael Hanselmann <public@hansmi.ch>
Reviewed-by: Philippe Mathieu-Daudé <philmd@redhat.com>
Message-id: 20190108102301.1957-1-kraxel@redhat.com
cve_desc: QEMU, through version 2.10 and through version 3.1.0, is vulnerable to an out-of-bounds read of up to 128 bytes in the hw/i2c/i2c-ddc.c:i2c_ddc() function. A local attacker with permission to execute i2c commands could exploit this to read stack memory of the qemu process on the host.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read due to missing bounds check on s->reg index
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function accesses s->edid_blob[s->reg] without validating that s->reg is within the valid bounds of the edid_blob array. Since s->reg is incremented on every call and there is no bounds check or wrap-around, repeated reads will eventually cause s->reg to exceed the array size, leading to an out-of-bounds read. An attacker or malicious guest able to trigger repeated I2C read transactions could read memory beyond the edid_blob buffer, potentially leaking sensitive host memory contents.

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/issues/4988
cve_desc: ImageMagick 7.1.0-27 is vulnerable to Buffer Overflow.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in image dimension validation allows bypass of size check
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expression `image->columns * image->rows` is computed as `size_t` multiplication before the cast to `MagickSizeType`. On 32-bit platforms, if `image->columns` and `image->rows` (both read directly from the file without prior validation) are large enough, their product can overflow a 32-bit `size_t`, wrapping to a small value. After dividing by 8, this small value would pass the `> GetBlobSize(image)` check, allowing an image with impossibly large dimensions to proceed. Subsequent pixel processing (e.g., `QueueAuthenticPixels`, `SetImageExtent`) would then operate on these attacker-controlled dimensions, potentially causing heap buffer overflows or excessive memory allocation leading to denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Image depth read from file without validation may cause undefined behavior
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `image->depth` is set directly from `cin.image.channel[0].bits_per_pixel`, which is a single byte read from the file with no validation. A value of 0, or an unsupported/very large depth value, is passed directly to `GetBytesPerRow` and subsequently to `ImportQuantumPixels`. Depending on how these functions handle unusual depth values, this could lead to division by zero, incorrect buffer size calculations, out-of-bounds reads during pixel import, or other undefined behavior. An attacker crafting a CIN file with a malicious `bits_per_pixel` value could exploit this to crash the application or potentially achieve code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Image dimensions set from file with no zero-check before processing
vulnerabilities.long_description: The image width and height are read directly from file data without checking for zero values. If `pixels_per_line` or `lines_per_image` is zero, subsequent operations like `GetBytesPerRow` with zero columns, or `SetImageExtent` with zero dimensions, could lead to unexpected behavior, division by zero, or allocation of zero-length buffers that are subsequently written to. A crafted CIN file could exploit this for denial of service.

commit_message:
i2c-ddc: fix oob read

Suggested-by: Michael Hanselmann <public@hansmi.ch>
Signed-off-by: Gerd Hoffmann <kraxel@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Michael Hanselmann <public@hansmi.ch>
Reviewed-by: Philippe Mathieu-Daudé <philmd@redhat.com>
Message-id: 20190108102301.1957-1-kraxel@redhat.com
cve_desc: QEMU, through version 2.10 and through version 3.1.0, is vulnerable to an out-of-bounds read of up to 128 bytes in the hw/i2c/i2c-ddc.c:i2c_ddc() function. A local attacker with permission to execute i2c commands could exploit this to read stack memory of the qemu process on the host.

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/issues/4988
cve_desc: ImageMagick 7.1.0-27 is vulnerable to Buffer Overflow.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in image dimension validation allows bypass of size check
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expression `image->columns*image->rows` can overflow before the division by 8, since `image->columns` and `image->rows` are derived directly from attacker-controlled file data (cin.image.channel[0].pixels_per_line and cin.image.channel[0].lines_per_image). If the multiplication wraps around, the result divided by 8 could be a small value that passes the size check against GetBlobSize, allowing the code to proceed with enormous image dimensions. This could lead to excessive memory allocation in SetImageExtent/QueueAuthenticPixels or out-of-bounds memory access during pixel processing.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Unvalidated bits_per_pixel value used as image depth
vulnerabilities.long_description: The image depth is set directly from cin.image.channel[0].bits_per_pixel (a single byte from the file) without validation. A value of 0 or an unusually large value could cause unexpected behavior in GetBytesPerRow, AcquireQuantumInfo, and ImportQuantumPixels. Depending on how downstream functions handle an invalid depth, this could lead to division by zero, incorrect buffer size calculations, or out-of-bounds reads/writes during pixel import.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Attacker-controlled user_length can cause excessive memory allocation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The cin.file.user_length is read from the file and only checked against GetBlobSize(image). For files where GetBlobSize returns a large value (or in certain streaming scenarios), an attacker can craft a CIN file with a very large user_length that passes the check but causes BlobToStringInfo to attempt allocation of gigabytes of memory, leading to denial of service through memory exhaustion. The subsequent ReadBlob would also fail to read the claimed amount of data, leaving the profile partially uninitialized.

commit_message:
Add more validation to `RequantizationRangePerChannel`.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387693946
Change-Id: Ife8dcbdb021bec4787eef6a4361dd08f17c14bd6
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions due to incomplete validation in MKL implementation of requantization, an attacker can trigger undefined behavior via binding a reference to a null pointer or can access data outside the bounds of heap allocated arrays. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/mkl/mkl_requantization_range_per_channel_op.cc) does not validate the dimensions of the `input` tensor. A similar issue occurs in `MklRequantizePerChannelOp`. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/mkl/mkl_requantize_per_channel_op.cc) does not perform full validation for all the input arguments. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 9e62869465573cb2d9b5053f1fa02a81fce21d69 and in the Github commit 203214568f5bc237603dbab6e1fd389f1572f5c9. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow via std::abs on INT32_MIN
vulnerabilities.long_description: If min_per_channel equals INT32_MIN (-2147483648), calling std::abs on it is undefined behavior in C/C++ because the result (2147483648) cannot be represented as an int32_t. This could lead to unexpected values for abs_max, corrupting the computed ranges and output min/max values. An attacker who crafts input tensors where the minimum qint32 value is INT32_MIN can trigger this UB.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Data race on is_non_negative in OpenMP parallel region
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable is_non_negative is written by multiple threads concurrently in the OpenMP parallel for loop without any synchronization or reduction clause. Unlike out_min_max which has a reduction(max:) directive, is_non_negative has no such protection. This constitutes a data race (undefined behavior per C++ standard), which could result in incorrect output_min values. An attacker could exploit this to cause non-deterministic or incorrect quantization calibration results.

commit_message:
vhost: fix FD leak with inflight messages

Even if unlikely, a buggy vhost-user master might attach fds to inflight
messages. Add checks like for other types of vhost-user messages.

Fixes: d87f1a1cb7b6 ("vhost: support inflight info sharing")
Cc: stable@dpdk.org

Signed-off-by: David Marchand <david.marchand@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Maxime Coquelin <maxime.coquelin@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in dpdk. This flaw allows a malicious vhost-user master to attach an unexpected number of fds as ancillary data to VHOST_USER_GET_INFLIGHT_FD / VHOST_USER_SET_INFLIGHT_FD messages that are not closed by the vhost-user slave. By sending such messages continuously, the vhost-user master exhausts available fd in the vhost-user slave process, leading to a denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in mmap_size calculation from user-controlled num_queues and queue_size
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variables num_queues and queue_size are read directly from the client message payload without validation. The multiplication `num_queues * pervq_inflight_size` (where pervq_inflight_size is derived from queue_size) can overflow a uint64_t or, more practically, produce a smaller-than-expected value when the intermediate computation in get_pervq_shm_size_* overflows. This could result in a smaller allocation than expected, while the subsequent loop iterates num_queues times writing at offsets based on the non-overflowed pervq_inflight_size, causing an out-of-bounds write via memset or the packed descriptor initialization loop. A malicious vhost-user client can craft num_queues and queue_size values to trigger heap corruption.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing validation of num_queues and queue_size from untrusted input
vulnerabilities.long_description: num_queues and queue_size are taken directly from the vhost-user message without any bounds checking. A zero queue_size or an excessively large num_queues could lead to unexpected behavior: zero queue_size may cause get_pervq_shm_size_* to return a size that doesn't account for the actual structure, and large values could cause excessive memory allocation or the integer overflow described above. A malicious vhost-user frontend/backend could exploit this to cause denial of service or memory corruption.

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0104: going beyond allocated memory when evaluating string constant

Problem:    Going beyond allocated memory when evaluating string constant.
Solution:   Properly skip over <Key> form.
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.0102.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in buffer size calculation can lead to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `len` is declared as `int` and computed as `(int)(p - *arg + extra)`. For very large input strings, `p - *arg` (a `ptrdiff_t`) combined with `extra` (which grows by 5 for every `\<` escape sequence) can overflow the `int` type. If the result wraps to a small positive value, `alloc(len)` allocates a small heap buffer, but the subsequent copy loop writes the full string content into it, causing a heap buffer overflow. This could be exploited for arbitrary code execution.

code_snippets: extra = trans_special(&p, end, flags, FALSE, NULL);
vulnerabilities.short_description: Buffer overflow check occurs after the write, not before
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the second parsing loop's `\<` special key handling, `trans_special` writes data into the buffer at `end`, and only afterward does the code check whether `end >= rettv->vval.v_string + len`. If the buffer was actually too small (e.g., due to a discrepancy between `find_special_key` and `trans_special` behavior, or due to the integer overflow in `len`), the out-of-bounds write has already occurred by the time the check runs. The `iemsg` call merely logs a message rather than preventing the corruption, meaning heap metadata or adjacent allocations could be silently corrupted.

commit_message:
vhost: fix FD leak with inflight messages

Even if unlikely, a buggy vhost-user master might attach fds to inflight
messages. Add checks like for other types of vhost-user messages.

Fixes: d87f1a1cb7b6 ("vhost: support inflight info sharing")
Cc: stable@dpdk.org

Signed-off-by: David Marchand <david.marchand@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Maxime Coquelin <maxime.coquelin@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in dpdk. This flaw allows a malicious vhost-user master to attach an unexpected number of fds as ancillary data to VHOST_USER_GET_INFLIGHT_FD / VHOST_USER_SET_INFLIGHT_FD messages that are not closed by the vhost-user slave. By sending such messages continuously, the vhost-user master exhausts available fd in the vhost-user slave process, leading to a denial of service.

code_snippets:
for (i = 0; i < num_queues; i++) {
		vq = dev->virtqueue[i];
vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds access on dev->virtqueue[] using attacker-controlled num_queues without bounds check against dev->nr_vring
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable num_queues comes directly from the vhost-user message payload (ctx->msg.payload.inflight.num_queues) and is never validated against dev->nr_vring (the actual number of allocated virtqueues). An attacker (malicious guest/frontend) can supply a num_queues value larger than dev->nr_vring, causing the loop to access dev->virtqueue[i] out of bounds, leading to arbitrary memory read/write and potential code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: No validation that num_queues * pervq_inflight_size fits within mmap_size, allowing out-of-bounds access in the mmap'd region
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code advances the addr pointer by pervq_inflight_size for each queue iteration but never checks that the total size (num_queues * pervq_inflight_size + mmap_offset) stays within the mmap'd region of mmap_size bytes. A malicious frontend could provide a small mmap_size with a large num_queues or queue_size, causing reads and writes beyond the mapped memory, potentially leading to crashes or memory corruption.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential integer overflow when computing pervq_inflight_size from attacker-controlled queue_size
vulnerabilities.long_description: The queue_size value comes from the untrusted message payload and is used to compute pervq_inflight_size (stored as uint32_t). Depending on the implementation of get_pervq_shm_size_split/packed, a large queue_size could cause an integer overflow in the size calculation, resulting in a small pervq_inflight_size. This would then lead to the inflight structures overlapping each other in the mmap'd region, causing memory corruption when the structures are accessed.

commit_message:
vhost: fix FD leak with inflight messages

Even if unlikely, a buggy vhost-user master might attach fds to inflight
messages. Add checks like for other types of vhost-user messages.

Fixes: d87f1a1cb7b6 ("vhost: support inflight info sharing")
Cc: stable@dpdk.org

Signed-off-by: David Marchand <david.marchand@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Maxime Coquelin <maxime.coquelin@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in dpdk. This flaw allows a malicious vhost-user master to attach an unexpected number of fds as ancillary data to VHOST_USER_GET_INFLIGHT_FD / VHOST_USER_SET_INFLIGHT_FD messages that are not closed by the vhost-user slave. By sending such messages continuously, the vhost-user master exhausts available fd in the vhost-user slave process, leading to a denial of service.

code_snippets:
for (i = 0; i < num_queues; i++) {
		vq = dev->virtqueue[i];
vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds access on dev->virtqueue[] using unchecked num_queues from user input
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable num_queues is read directly from the message payload (ctx->msg.payload.inflight.num_queues) and used as the loop bound to index into dev->virtqueue[i] without any validation against dev->nr_vring (the actual number of allocated virtqueues). A malicious or buggy frontend can supply a num_queues value larger than dev->nr_vring, causing out-of-bounds read/write on the virtqueue array, potentially leading to code execution or a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: No validation that mmap'd region is large enough for num_queues * pervq_inflight_size
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code mmaps a region of mmap_size bytes, then iterates num_queues times advancing the addr pointer by pervq_inflight_size each time. There is no check that mmap_size >= mmap_offset-relative-start + num_queues * pervq_inflight_size, nor that pervq_inflight_size * num_queues doesn't overflow. A malicious frontend can provide a small mmap_size with a large num_queues or queue_size, causing the inflight_packed/inflight_split pointers to reference memory beyond the mmap'd region, leading to out-of-bounds reads and writes when those pointers are later dereferenced.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential integer overflow in pervq_inflight_size calculation from user-controlled queue_size
vulnerabilities.long_description: The queue_size value is taken directly from user input and passed to get_pervq_shm_size_packed/split which likely multiply it by a per-descriptor structure size. If queue_size is large enough, the multiplication can overflow the uint32_t pervq_inflight_size, resulting in a much smaller value than expected. This would cause the addr pointer in the loop to advance by an insufficient amount, making multiple virtqueues' inflight regions overlap or point within valid mmap'd memory but at incorrect offsets, leading to memory corruption.

commit_message:
vhost: fix FD leak with inflight messages

Even if unlikely, a buggy vhost-user master might attach fds to inflight
messages. Add checks like for other types of vhost-user messages.

Fixes: d87f1a1cb7b6 ("vhost: support inflight info sharing")
Cc: stable@dpdk.org

Signed-off-by: David Marchand <david.marchand@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Maxime Coquelin <maxime.coquelin@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in dpdk. This flaw allows a malicious vhost-user master to attach an unexpected number of fds as ancillary data to VHOST_USER_GET_INFLIGHT_FD / VHOST_USER_SET_INFLIGHT_FD messages that are not closed by the vhost-user slave. By sending such messages continuously, the vhost-user master exhausts available fd in the vhost-user slave process, leading to a denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in mmap_size calculation from untrusted num_queues and queue_size
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variables num_queues and queue_size are read directly from the message payload (controlled by the vhost-user client). The multiplication `num_queues * pervq_inflight_size` can overflow a uint64_t (or more practically, pervq_inflight_size itself could be very large depending on queue_size). If the result wraps around to a small value, a small buffer is allocated via inflight_mem_alloc, but the subsequent loop iterates num_queues times writing pervq_inflight_size bytes per iteration, causing a heap buffer overflow. Additionally, num_queues=0 would result in a zero-size allocation with no writes, but queue_size=0 is not validated either. An attacker controlling the vhost-user frontend/backend messages could exploit this to corrupt heap memory.

code_snippets:
for (j = 0; j < queue_size; j++)
	inflight_packed->desc[j].next = j + 1;
vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds write via desc[] flexible array member when queue_size exceeds allocated capacity
vulnerabilities.long_description: The desc[] array is a flexible array member at the end of rte_vhost_inflight_info_packed. The allocated size per queue (pervq_inflight_size) is computed by get_pervq_shm_size_packed(queue_size), but if that function has any rounding or overflow issues, or if the mmap_size overflowed, the loop writing to inflight_packed->desc[j] for j up to queue_size-1 could write beyond the allocated region. Combined with the integer overflow above, this enables heap corruption and potentially arbitrary code execution.

patch 8.2.5151: reading beyond the end of the line with lisp indenting

commit_message:
patch 8.2.3884: crash when clearing the argument list while using it

Problem:    Crash when clearing the argument list while using it.
Solution:   Lock the argument list for ":all".
cve_desc: vim is vulnerable to Out-of-bounds Read

code_snippets:
(void)do_ecmd(0, alist_name(&AARGLIST(alist)[i]), NULL, NULL,
		      ECMD_ONE,
		      ((buf_hide(curwin->w_buffer)
			   || bufIsChanged(curwin->w_buffer)) ? ECMD_HIDE : 0)
						       + ECMD_OLDBUF, curwin);
vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read on argument list due to autocommand-induced size change
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `opened` array and `count`/`opened_len` are sized based on the initial ARGCOUNT. The code acknowledges that autocommands may modify the argument list ('ARGCOUNT may change while doing this, because of autocommands'). However, in the second loop, `AARGLIST(alist)[i]` is accessed with `i` ranging up to the original `opened_len - 1`, without re-checking that `i` is still within the current `alist->al_ga.ga_len`. If an autocommand triggered by `do_ecmd`, `win_split`, or other operations shrinks the argument list, `i` can exceed the current argument list bounds, leading to a heap out-of-bounds read. This could result in a crash or potential information disclosure.

commit_message:
can: mcba_usb: mcba_usb_start_xmit(): fix double dev_kfree_skb in error path

There is no need to call dev_kfree_skb() when usb_submit_urb() fails
because can_put_echo_skb() deletes original skb and
can_free_echo_skb() deletes the cloned skb.

Fixes: 51f3baad7de9 ("can: mcba_usb: Add support for Microchip CAN BUS Analyzer")

io_uring: reexpand under-reexpanded iters

[   74.211232] BUG: KASAN: stack-out-of-bounds in iov_iter_revert+0x809/0x900
[   74.212778] Read of size 8 at addr ffff888025dc78b8 by task
syz-executor.0/828
[   74.214756] CPU: 0 PID: 828 Comm: syz-executor.0 Not tainted
5.14.0-rc3-next-20210730 #1
[   74.216525] Hardware name: QEMU Standard PC (i440FX + PIIX, 1996),
BIOS rel-1.14.0-0-g155821a1990b-prebuilt.qemu.org 04/01/2014
[   74.219033] Call Trace:
[   74.219683]  dump_stack_lvl+0x8b/0xb3
[   74.220706]  print_address_description.constprop.0+0x1f/0x140
[   74.224226]  kasan_report.cold+0x7f/0x11b
[   74.226085]  iov_iter_revert+0x809/0x900
[   74.227960]  io_write+0x57d/0xe40
[   74.232647]  io_issue_sqe+0x4da/0x6a80
[   74.242578]  __io_queue_sqe+0x1ac/0xe60
[   74.245358]  io_submit_sqes+0x3f6e/0x76a0
[   74.248207]  __do_sys_io_uring_enter+0x90c/0x1a20
[   74.257167]  do_syscall_64+0x3b/0x90
[   74.257984]  entry_SYSCALL_64_after_hwframe+0x44/0xae

old_size = iov_iter_count();
...
iov_iter_revert(old_size - iov_iter_count());

If iov_iter_revert() is done base on the initial size as above, and the
iter is truncated and not reexpanded in the middle, it miscalculates
borders causing problems. This trace is due to no one reexpanding after
generic_write_checks().

Now iters store how many bytes has been truncated, so reexpand them to
the initial state right before reverting.

Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Reported-by: Palash Oswal <oswalpalash@gmail.com>
Reported-by: Sudip Mukherjee <sudipm.mukherjee@gmail.com>
Reported-and-tested-by: syzbot+9671693590ef5aad8953@syzkaller.appspotmail.com
Signed-off-by: Pavel Begunkov <asml.silence@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Al Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
cve_desc: An out-of-bounds read flaw was found in the Linux kernel’s io_uring module in the way a user triggers the io_read() function with some special parameters. This flaw allows a local user to read some memory out of bounds.
