commit_message:
Prevent heap OOB in sparse reduction ops.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387934524
Change-Id: I894aa30f1e454f09b471d565b4a325da49322c1a
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of sparse reduction operations in TensorFlow can trigger accesses outside of bounds of heap allocated data. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/a1bc56203f21a5a4995311825ffaba7a670d7747/tensorflow/core/kernels/sparse_reduce_op.cc#L217-L228) fails to validate that each reduction group does not overflow and that each corresponding index does not point to outside the bounds of the input tensor. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 87158f43f05f2720a374f3e6d22a7aaa3a33f750. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/issues/1337
cve_desc: In ImageMagick 7.0.8-13 Q16, there is an infinite loop in the ReadBMPImage function of the coders/bmp.c file. Remote attackers could leverage this vulnerability to cause a denial of service via a crafted bmp file.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in bytes_per_line and length calculations can lead to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The calculation of `bytes_per_line` involves `image->columns * bmp_info.bits_per_pixel` which can overflow for large image dimensions. Subsequently, `length = bytes_per_line * image->rows` can also overflow, resulting in a much smaller value than expected. The sanity check `((MagickSizeType) length/8) > GetBlobSize(image)` divides the already-overflowed length by 8, making it even weaker. The memory allocation via `AcquireVirtualMemory(image->rows, MagickMax(bytes_per_line, ...))` may also overflow internally. If the allocated buffer is smaller than expected, the subsequent `ReadBlob(image, length, pixels)` or the pixel processing loops that index into the buffer using `pixels+(image->rows-y-1)*bytes_per_line` will write/read beyond the allocated heap buffer, potentially enabling arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: RLE-decoded data may exceed allocated buffer boundaries leading to out-of-bounds read
vulnerabilities.long_description: For RLE-compressed images, the buffer is allocated with `image->rows * MagickMax(bytes_per_line, image->columns+256UL)` bytes, and DecodeImage is called with a limit of `image->columns * image->rows`. However, `image->columns * image->rows` can overflow for large dimensions. Additionally, the DecodeImage function populates the pixel buffer, and later the code accesses `pixels+(image->rows-y-1)*bytes_per_line` where `bytes_per_line` is set to `image->columns`. If DecodeImage doesn't properly validate bounds or if the multiplication overflows, out-of-bounds memory access can occur during the subsequent pixel conversion loops.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in bytes_per_line for 32-bit alpha detection loop
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the alpha detection code path for 32-bit BMP3 images, `bytes_per_line` is recalculated as `4*(image->columns)`. If `image->columns` is large enough (e.g., greater than SIZE_MAX/4), this multiplication overflows, producing a small `bytes_per_line`. The subsequent pointer arithmetic `p=pixels+(image->rows-y-1)*bytes_per_line` would then access incorrect memory locations, potentially reading out-of-bounds data from the heap. This could lead to information disclosure or a crash.

commit_message:
bgpd: Make sure hdr length is at a minimum of what is expected

Ensure that if the capability length specified is enough data.

Signed-off-by: Donald Sharp <sharpd@nvidia.com>
cve_desc: An out-of-bounds read in the BGP daemon of FRRouting FRR before 8.4 may lead to a segmentation fault and denial of service. This occurs in bgp_capability_msg_parse in bgpd/bgp_packet.c.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Heap buffer over-read when hdr->length is smaller than sizeof(struct capability_mp_data)
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code validates that `pnt + hdr->length + 3` does not exceed `end`, but then unconditionally copies `sizeof(struct capability_mp_data)` bytes from `pnt + 3` into `mpc`. If `hdr->length` is smaller than `sizeof(struct capability_mp_data)` (e.g., 0 or 1), the memcpy will read beyond the validated boundary, causing an out-of-bounds read. A remote peer can craft a BGP CAPABILITY message with a small `length` field in the capability header to trigger this, potentially leaking memory contents or crashing the process.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4074: going over the end of NameBuff

Problem:    Going over the end of NameBuff.
Solution:   Check length when appending a space.
cve_desc: vim is vulnerable to Heap-based Buffer Overflow

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential buffer overflow when NameBuff is nearly full due to negative size calculation in vim_snprintf
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `get_trans_bufname` fills NameBuff (size MAXPATHL+1) with a string of length close to or equal to MAXPATHL, the line `*(p + len++) = ' '` increments `len` beyond what subsequent `vim_snprintf` calls expect. The size argument `MAXPATHL - len` can become negative (e.g., if len becomes MAXPATHL+1 after the space is appended). Since `vim_snprintf` takes a `size_t` parameter, this negative value wraps to a very large unsigned value, effectively removing the bounds check and allowing the snprintf calls to write well beyond the end of NameBuff. An attacker who can control the buffer name (e.g., through a specially crafted long filename) could trigger a heap buffer overflow, potentially leading to code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Write into the middle of a multi-byte character sequence in NameBuff can corrupt adjacent data
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the multibyte branch, after scanning forward by multi-byte character lengths (i += mb_ptr2len), the code sets `p = p + i - 1` and writes `*p = '<'`. Since `i` is advanced by multi-byte character widths (2, 3, or 4 bytes), `p + i - 1` points into the middle of the last skipped multi-byte character. Overwriting this byte corrupts the multi-byte sequence. While primarily a correctness issue, when `i` is large and the buffer content is attacker-controlled, this could lead to subsequent string operations misinterpreting the corrupted data, potentially reading out of bounds or producing unexpected behavior downstream.

commit_message: libarchive: sanitize filenames before extracting
cve_desc: Directory traversal vulnerability in File Roller 3.6.x before 3.6.4, 3.8.x before 3.8.3, and 3.9.x before 3.9.3, when libarchive is used, allows remote attackers to create arbitrary files via a crafted archive that is not properly handled in a "Keep directory structure" action, related to fr-archive-libarchive.c and fr-window.c.

vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference when calling g_object_unref on a NULL info pointer
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the else branch of `if (info != NULL)`, the variable `info` is necessarily NULL (since `g_file_query_info` returned NULL). However, the code calls `g_object_unref(info)` on the error path where `local_error` does not match `G_IO_ERROR_NOT_FOUND`. This will dereference a NULL pointer, causing a crash (denial of service). An attacker could trigger this by crafting an archive and causing g_file_query_info to fail with a non-NOT_FOUND error.

code_snippets:
case AE_IFLNK:
				if (! g_file_make_symbolic_link (file, archive_entry_symlink (entry), cancellable, &local_error)) {

case AE_IFREG:
				ostream = (GOutputStream *) g_file_replace (file, NULL, FALSE, G_FILE_CREATE_REPLACE_DESTINATION, cancellable, &load_data->error);
vulnerabilities.short_description: Symlink-based path traversal (Zip Slip variant) allows writing files outside the destination directory
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code creates symbolic links from archive entries without validating that the symlink target stays within the extraction destination. A malicious archive can contain a symlink entry pointing to an arbitrary directory (e.g., `foo -> /etc/`), followed by a regular file entry at `foo/cron.d/malicious`. When extracted sequentially, the symlink is created first, and the subsequent file is written through the symlink to an arbitrary location on the filesystem. This allows an attacker to overwrite arbitrary files with controlled content, potentially leading to code execution.

code_snippets:
fullpath = (*pathname == '/') ? g_strdup (pathname) : g_strconcat ("/", pathname, NULL);
		file = g_file_get_child (extract_data->destination, _g_path_get_relative_basename (fullpath, extract_data->base_dir, extract_data->junk_paths));
vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential path traversal via crafted archive entry pathnames with directory traversal sequences
vulnerabilities.long_description: The pathname from the archive entry is used to construct a file path under the extraction destination. If `_g_path_get_relative_basename` does not properly sanitize path components containing `..` sequences, an attacker could craft an archive with entries like `../../etc/passwd` to write files outside the intended extraction directory. This is a classic Zip Slip vulnerability that can lead to arbitrary file overwrite, potentially enabling remote code execution depending on what files are overwritten.

Ensure that if the capability length specified is enough data.

Signed-off-by: Donald Sharp <sharpd@nvidia.com>

commit_message: Fixed bug #68665 (Invalid free)
cve_desc: The apprentice_load function in libmagic/apprentice.c in the Fileinfo component in PHP through 5.6.4 attempts to perform a free operation on a stack-based character array, which allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (memory corruption or application crash) or possibly have unspecified other impact via unknown vectors.  NOTE: this is disputed by the vendor because the standard erealloc behavior makes the free operation unreachable

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in maxfiles calculation can lead to undersized heap allocation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `maxfiles` (size_t) is doubled repeatedly via `(maxfiles + 1) * 2`. On 32-bit systems, if a very large number of directory entries are processed, `maxfiles * sizeof(*filearr)` can overflow, resulting in a small allocation. Subsequent writes via `filearr[files++]` would then write beyond the allocated buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow. An attacker who can control the contents of the magic directory (e.g., by placing many files) could exploit this for code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: snprintf truncation not detected leads to operations on unintended truncated path
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code checks if snprintf returns a negative value but does not check if the return value is >= sizeof(mfn), which indicates truncation. When the combined path exceeds MAXPATHLEN, the path in `mfn` is silently truncated. The truncated path is then passed to stat() and later loaded, potentially operating on a different file than intended. This could allow an attacker to craft directory/file names such that the truncated path resolves to a different, attacker-controlled file.

commit_message: Fix https://github.com/Exiv2/exiv2/issues/55
cve_desc: In Exiv2 0.26, there is a heap-based buffer over-read in the Exiv2::Image::byteSwap4 function in image.cpp. Remote attackers can exploit this vulnerability to disclose memory data or cause a denial of service via a crafted TIFF file.

code_snippets: DataBuf  buf(size*count + pad+20);  // allocate a buffer
vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in buffer size calculation leads to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expression `size*count + pad+20` uses uint32_t arithmetic and can overflow when `size` and `count` (both attacker-controlled from the TIFF file) are large. For example, if size=4 and count=0x40000001, the multiplication wraps to a small value, resulting in a tiny heap allocation. Subsequent reads (`io.read(buf.pData_,count*size)`) and byte-swap accesses will write/read far beyond the allocated buffer, enabling heap corruption, code execution, or information disclosure.

code_snippets:
if ( bOffsetIsPointer ) {         // read into buffer
                    size_t   restore = io.tell();  // save
                    io.seek(offset,BasicIo::beg);  // position
                    io.read(buf.pData_,count*size);// read
                    io.seek(restore,BasicIo::beg); // restore
                }
vulnerabilities.short_description: Unvalidated file offset allows out-of-bounds seek and read
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `offset` value is read directly from the file and used in `io.seek(offset, BasicIo::beg)` without validating it against `io.size()`. The commented-out line `// if ( offset > io.size() ) offset = 0; // Denial of service?` confirms this was known but left unmitigated. An attacker can craft a file with an offset pointing beyond file bounds, causing reads of uninitialized or out-of-bounds memory into the buffer.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Heap buffer over-read when printing values due to kount/buf size mismatch
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `kount` variable is capped (e.g., to 5 or 32 for strings) to limit output, but the buffer `buf` was allocated with `size*count` (which may have overflowed). Even without overflow, when `bOffsetIsPointer` is false (count*size <= 4), only 4 bytes are copied into buf from the directory entry, yet `kount` can be up to 5 for non-string types. Accessing `byteSwap4(buf, k*size, bSwap)` with k>=1 reads beyond the 4 valid bytes, causing a heap buffer over-read that may leak sensitive memory contents.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Uncontrolled recursion depth via crafted IFD chains can cause stack overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `option == kpsRecursive`, the function recurses into sub-IFDs without limiting `depth`. A malicious file can create circular or deeply nested IFD references (e.g., an IFD entry pointing back to itself or to a chain of IFDs), exhausting the stack and crashing the process. There is no check that `depth` stays within a reasonable bound, nor that previously visited offsets are not revisited.

commit_message: Fixed bug #68665 (Invalid free)
cve_desc: The apprentice_load function in libmagic/apprentice.c in the Fileinfo component in PHP through 5.6.4 attempts to perform a free operation on a stack-based character array, which allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (memory corruption or application crash) or possibly have unspecified other impact via unknown vectors.  NOTE: this is disputed by the vendor because the standard erealloc behavior makes the free operation unreachable

vulnerabilities.short_description: Calling efree() on stack-allocated buffer `mfn` leads to heap corruption
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `mfn` is declared as `char mfn[MAXPATHLEN]`, a stack-allocated array. In the error-handling path when `erealloc` fails, the code calls `efree(mfn)`, which passes a stack address to the heap deallocator. This causes undefined behavior, typically heap metadata corruption or a crash. An attacker who can trigger the memory allocation failure (e.g., by causing many directory entries to be enumerated) could potentially exploit heap corruption for code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in maxfiles calculation can lead to undersized heap allocation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `maxfiles` variable is of type `size_t`, but `(maxfiles + 1) * 2` can overflow on 32-bit platforms when maxfiles is large. If an attacker can cause a directory to contain a very large number of files, the overflow results in a small value for `maxfiles` and consequently a small `mlen` allocation. Subsequent writes to `filearr[files++]` would then write beyond the allocated buffer, leading to a heap buffer overflow that could be exploited for arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: snprintf truncation not checked, leading to use of truncated path
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `snprintf` returns a value >= `sizeof(mfn)`, the output is silently truncated but the code continues using the truncated path. The code only checks for `mflen < 0` (format error) but not for `mflen >= sizeof(mfn)` (truncation). This means a truncated path is passed to `stat()` and potentially loaded, which could cause the wrong file to be processed. While the `estrndup` call partially handles the length, the truncated path itself could reference an unintended file, potentially leading to security issues if an attacker controls directory/file names.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.3610: crash when ModeChanged triggered too early

Problem:    Crash when ModeChanged triggered too early.
Solution:   Trigger ModeChanged after setting VIsual.
cve_desc: vim is vulnerable to Heap-based Buffer Overflow

commit_message:
patch 8.2.3610: crash when ModeChanged triggered too early

Problem:    Crash when ModeChanged triggered too early.
Solution:   Trigger ModeChanged after setting VIsual.
cve_desc: vim is vulnerable to Heap-based Buffer Overflow

commit_message:
Avoid an integer overflow in the xbm loader

At the same time, reject some silly input, such as negative
width or height.

https://bugzilla.gnome.org/show_bug.cgi?id=672811
cve_desc: Multiple integer overflows in the read_bitmap_file_data function in io-xbm.c in gdk-pixbuf before 2.26.1 allow remote attackers to cause a denial of service (application crash) via a negative (1) height or (2) width in an XBM file, which triggers a heap-based buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in bitmap size calculation leads to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variables ww and hh are parsed from user-controlled input and stored as guint (unsigned int). The calculation `bytes_per_line * hh` can overflow a signed int (size is declared as int), resulting in a small or negative allocation size. The subsequent loop then writes far more data than allocated into the heap buffer. An attacker can craft a malicious bitmap file with large width/height values to trigger this overflow, leading to heap corruption and potential arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Negative values from sscanf are cast to unsigned, producing large width/height
vulnerabilities.long_description: The 'value' variable is a signed int populated by sscanf. A malicious file could specify a negative width or height (e.g., #define foo_width -1), which when cast to unsigned int becomes a very large value (e.g., 4294967295). This feeds into the size calculation and can cause an undersized allocation followed by a massive heap buffer overflow during the data-reading loops.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.3949: using freed memory with /\%V

Problem:    Using freed memory with /\%V.
Solution:   Get the line again after getvvcol().
cve_desc: vim is vulnerable to Use After Free

commit_message: Fix: index entry label not being zero-terminated with corrupt input
cve_desc: Buffer Over-read in GitHub repository bfabiszewski/libmobi prior to 0.11.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read on control_bytes pointer with no bounds checking
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `control_bytes` pointer is set to `buf->data + buf->offset` and is subsequently dereferenced (`control_bytes[0]`) and incremented (`control_bytes++`) inside the while loop iterating over `tagx->tags_count` entries. There is no bounds check to ensure `control_bytes` remains within the valid buffer region. If the tagx structure contains many tags with `control_byte == 1`, the pointer is incremented repeatedly and can point beyond the allocated buffer, leading to an out-of-bounds read. An attacker crafting a malicious MOBI file with specially crafted TAGX data could trigger this to read sensitive heap memory or cause a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential integer overflow in malloc size calculation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expressions `tagx->tags_count * sizeof(MOBIPtagx)` and `tagx->tags_count * sizeof(MOBIIndexTag)` could overflow if `tagx->tags_count` is a large value from a crafted file. On 32-bit platforms especially, this could wrap around to a small allocation size while the code proceeds to write `tagx->tags_count` entries into the undersized buffer, resulting in a heap buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Buffer maxlen not restored on multiple error return paths
vulnerabilities.long_description: After `buf->maxlen` is modified to `buf->offset + entry_length`, several error return paths (e.g., label length check, malloc failures, tag allocation failures) do not restore `buf->maxlen` to the original `buf_maxlen` value. This leaves the shared buffer object in an inconsistent state with a truncated maxlen, which could cause subsequent buffer operations by callers to malfunction, potentially leading to out-of-bounds accesses or incorrect parsing of data.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.3949: using freed memory with /\%V

Problem:    Using freed memory with /\%V.
Solution:   Get the line again after getvvcol().
cve_desc: vim is vulnerable to Use After Free

commit_message:
Fix a bug when getting a gzip header extra field with inflate().

If the extra field was larger than the space the user provided with
inflateGetHeader(), and if multiple calls of inflate() delivered
the extra header data, then there could be a buffer overflow of the
provided space. This commit assures that provided space is not
exceeded.
cve_desc: zlib through 1.2.12 has a heap-based buffer over-read or buffer overflow in inflate in inflate.c via a large gzip header extra field. NOTE: only applications that call inflateGetHeader are affected. Some common applications bundle the affected zlib source code but may be unable to call inflateGetHeader (e.g., see the nodejs/node reference).

vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference when accessing state->head->extra_len before NULL check
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the EXTRA case, the code computes `len = state->head->extra_len - state->length;` before checking whether `state->head != Z_NULL` on the very next line. If `state->head` is NULL, this dereference causes undefined behavior, most likely a crash. An attacker could craft a gzip stream with the FEXTRA flag set (0x0400) that reaches this code path when `state->head` is NULL, triggering a denial of service.

commit_message: Fix: index entry label not being zero-terminated with corrupt input
cve_desc: Buffer Over-read in GitHub repository bfabiszewski/libmobi prior to 0.11.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read on control_bytes pointer with no bounds checking
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `control_bytes` pointer is set to a position within `buf->data`, and is subsequently dereferenced (`control_bytes[0]`) and incremented (`control_bytes++`) inside the tag-parsing loop without any bounds checking against the buffer's limits. If the parsed data contains many tags with `control_byte == 1`, or if `tagx->tags_count` is large, `control_bytes` can be advanced past the end of the buffer, leading to an out-of-bounds heap read. An attacker could craft a malicious MOBI file with specially crafted TAGX data to trigger this, potentially leaking sensitive heap data or causing a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in entry_length computation and subsequent bounds check
vulnerabilities.long_description: If `idxt.offsets[curr_number + 1]` is less than `idxt.offsets[curr_number]`, the subtraction wraps around to a very large `size_t` value. Furthermore, the subsequent check `buf->offset + entry_length >= buf_maxlen` can itself overflow when `buf->offset` is added to the wrapped `entry_length`, causing the sum to wrap to a small value that passes the bounds check. This would set `buf->maxlen` to a wrapped value and allow subsequent reads to operate on incorrect buffer boundaries, potentially leading to out-of-bounds reads or writes. A crafted MOBI file with manipulated IDXT offsets could exploit this.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of previously allocated resources on error paths
vulnerabilities.long_description: When memory allocation fails for `indx->entries[entry_number].tags[i].tagvalues`, the function frees `ptagx` and returns, but does not free `indx->entries[entry_number].label` or any previously allocated `tagvalues` from earlier iterations of the loop. Similarly, other error return paths after the label allocation don't free the label. Repeated triggering of these error paths (e.g., via crafted files that cause allocation failures) could lead to memory exhaustion.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Buffer maxlen not restored on error return paths, corrupting buffer state
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function temporarily sets `buf->maxlen` to a restricted value (`buf->offset + entry_length`) for the duration of parsing. However, all error return paths after this assignment fail to restore `buf->maxlen` to `buf_maxlen`. This leaves the buffer in an inconsistent state for subsequent callers, who may then perform reads or writes based on an incorrect maximum length, potentially leading to out-of-bounds memory access in later operations.

commit_message:
exec: --cap do not set inheritable capabilities

Closes: CVE-2022-27650

Signed-off-by: Giuseppe Scrivano <gscrivan@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in crun where containers were incorrectly started with non-empty default permissions. A vulnerability was found in Moby (Docker Engine) where containers were started incorrectly with non-empty inheritable Linux process capabilities. This flaw allows an attacker with access to programs with inheritable file capabilities to elevate those capabilities to the permitted set when execve(2) runs.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Off-by-one error causes out-of-bounds read of argv[argc] (NULL) and incorrect args_len
vulnerabilities.long_description: The loop iterates with i from 0 to argc-first_arg-1. The maximum array index accessed is argv[first_arg + (argc - first_arg - 1) + 1] = argv[argc]. By the C standard argv[argc] is NULL, so xstrdup(NULL) is called which is undefined behavior and will likely crash. Additionally, process->args_len is set to argc (the total argument count) rather than the actual number of exec arguments (argc - first_arg - 1), which misrepresents the array length to downstream consumers and could lead to out-of-bounds reads when the array is later iterated using args_len.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4074: going over the end of NameBuff

Problem:    Going over the end of NameBuff.
Solution:   Check length when appending a space.
cve_desc: vim is vulnerable to Heap-based Buffer Overflow

commit_message:
Avoid an integer overflow in the xbm loader

At the same time, reject some silly input, such as negative
width or height.

https://bugzilla.gnome.org/show_bug.cgi?id=672811
cve_desc: Multiple integer overflows in the read_bitmap_file_data function in io-xbm.c in gdk-pixbuf before 2.26.1 allow remote attackers to cause a denial of service (application crash) via a negative (1) height or (2) width in an XBM file, which triggers a heap-based buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in (ww+7) can lead to undersized heap allocation
vulnerabilities.long_description: When ww is a large guint value close to UINT_MAX (e.g., 0xFFFFFFF9 through 0xFFFFFFFF), the expression (ww+7) wraps around due to unsigned integer overflow, producing a very small value. This causes bytes_per_line to be much smaller than expected, leading to an undersized buffer allocation via g_malloc(size). The subsequent loop that writes bitmap data will then write far beyond the allocated buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow. The existing overflow check (size / bytes_per_line != hh) does not catch this because bytes_per_line itself is already incorrect. Additionally, if (ww+7) wraps to produce bytes_per_line == 0, a division by zero occurs in the overflow check.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Heap buffer overflow in version10p parsing path due to writing more bytes than allocated
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the version10p code path, the loop iterates with bytes incrementing by 2, but in each iteration it writes either 1 or 2 bytes via ptr. When padding is 0 (!padding is true), it always writes 2 bytes per iteration, meaning ptr advances by 2 each time, totaling 'size' bytes written. However, when padding is 1, the conditional write means sometimes 1 byte is written and sometimes 2. The pointer 'ptr' can advance beyond the 'size' bytes allocated for the 'bits' buffer because the loop counter 'bytes' does not accurately track the number of bytes written through 'ptr'. This mismatch can lead to out-of-bounds heap writes, potentially allowing code execution.

commit_message: [TALOS-CAN-0063] avoid buffer overrun in ntpq
cve_desc: ntpq in NTP 4.2.x before 4.2.8p4, and 4.3.x before 4.3.77 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via crafted mode 6 response packets.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Off-by-one heap/stack buffer overflow when appending character to bv
vulnerabilities.long_description: The buffer `bv` is declared as `char bv[2 * MAXVALLEN]`. After `atoascii(value, MAXVALLEN, bv, sizeof(bv))` fills it, `strlen(bv)` could be up to `sizeof(bv) - 1` (i.e., `2 * MAXVALLEN - 1`). In that case, `bv[len]` writes at index `2 * MAXVALLEN - 1` (the last valid byte), but `bv[len + 1]` writes the null terminator at index `2 * MAXVALLEN`, which is one byte past the end of the stack-allocated buffer. This is a classic off-by-one stack buffer overflow that could allow an attacker who controls the NTP variable value to corrupt adjacent stack memory, potentially overwriting the saved return address or other critical data to achieve code execution.

commit_message:
Fix a bug when getting a gzip header extra field with inflate().

If the extra field was larger than the space the user provided with
inflateGetHeader(), and if multiple calls of inflate() delivered
the extra header data, then there could be a buffer overflow of the
provided space. This commit assures that provided space is not
exceeded.
cve_desc: zlib through 1.2.12 has a heap-based buffer over-read or buffer overflow in inflate in inflate.c via a large gzip header extra field. NOTE: only applications that call inflateGetHeader are affected. Some common applications bundle the affected zlib source code but may be unable to call inflateGetHeader (e.g., see the nodejs/node reference).

code_snippets: unsigned in, out;           /* save starting available input and output */
vulnerabilities.short_description: Unsigned integer overflow in total byte counters when accumulating input/output counts
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variables 'in' and 'out' are declared as 'unsigned' (typically 32-bit), and they track the difference in available input/output. The values are then added to strm->total_in and strm->total_out (unsigned long). Over many calls to inflate(), total_in and total_out can overflow, which could cause incorrect tracking of total bytes processed. While this is a known limitation of the zlib API, it can lead to incorrect checksum computations or length checks in callers that rely on these values, particularly in the CHECK and LENGTH states where state->total is used for gzip length verification.

cve_desc: Integer underflow in the cli_scanpe function in pe.c in ClamAV before 0.97.8 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via a skewed offset larger than the size of the PE section in a UPX packed executable, which triggers an out-of-bounds read.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Unchecked return value of fseek before reading into buffer
vulnerabilities.long_description: The return value of fseek() is not checked. If the seek fails (e.g., due to an invalid or out-of-range ptr value, especially since uint32_t can represent values larger than LONG_MAX on some platforms, or if f is a non-seekable stream), the subsequent fread() will read from an unintended file position. This could lead to processing arbitrary file data as a string, potentially causing incorrect behavior or information disclosure. An attacker crafting a malicious SIS file could supply a ptr value that causes fseek to fail, leading to data from an unexpected file offset being interpreted as the string.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Implicit conversion of uint32_t to long in fseek may cause sign issues
vulnerabilities.long_description: The parameter ptr is uint32_t, but fseek() expects a long for its offset argument. On platforms where long is 32 bits (e.g., 32-bit systems or Windows), a uint32_t value greater than LONG_MAX (2147483647) will be implicitly converted to a negative long value. When passed to fseek with SEEK_SET, this results in undefined behavior or an error, and since the return value is unchecked, execution continues with fread reading from an unintended file position. An attacker could craft a SIS file with a large ptr value to trigger this behavior.

commit_message:
nfc: st21nfca: Fix potential buffer overflows in EVT_TRANSACTION

It appears that there are some buffer overflows in EVT_TRANSACTION.
This happens because the length parameters that are passed to memcpy
come directly from skb->data and are not guarded in any way.

Signed-off-by: Jordy Zomer <jordy@pwning.systems>
Reviewed-by: Krzysztof Kozlowski <krzysztof.kozlowski@canonical.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: st21nfca_connectivity_event_received in drivers/nfc/st21nfca/se.c in the Linux kernel through 5.16.12 has EVT_TRANSACTION buffer overflows because of untrusted length parameters.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Unchecked aid_len from skb allows heap buffer overflow and out-of-bounds read
vulnerabilities.long_description: The aid_len is taken directly from attacker-controlled skb->data[1] without any validation against the allocated buffer size (skb->len - 2) or the size of transaction->aid. A crafted packet with a large aid_len value can cause memcpy to write past the bounds of the transaction allocation, leading to heap corruption. It can also read beyond skb->data bounds.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Unchecked params_len allows heap buffer overflow and out-of-bounds read
vulnerabilities.long_description: Similar to aid_len, params_len is taken directly from attacker-controlled data without validation. The memcpy uses this length to copy into transaction->params, which can overflow the allocated buffer. Additionally, aid_len + 3 or aid_len + 4 + params_len could exceed skb->len, causing an out-of-bounds read from skb->data.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Insufficient allocation size for transaction structure
vulnerabilities.long_description: The allocation size is skb->len - 2, but struct nfc_evt_transaction contains fixed-size fields (aid_len, params_len, aid[], params[]) whose total size may exceed skb->len - 2. Since aid_len and params_len are taken from the skb data and not validated against the allocation, the memcpy operations can write beyond the allocated region.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Logical error using AND instead of OR allows processing of too-short packets
vulnerabilities.long_description: The condition uses && instead of ||. If skb->len is less than NFC_MIN_AID_LENGTH + 2 but skb->data[0] happens to equal NFC_EVT_TRANSACTION_AID_TAG, the check passes and the code proceeds to access skb->data[1], skb->data[2], etc. on a too-short buffer. The check should use || to reject the packet if EITHER condition fails.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of devm-allocated transaction on early return
vulnerabilities.long_description: When the PARAMETERS tag check fails, the function returns -EPROTO without freeing the transaction object allocated with devm_kzalloc. While devm resources are freed on device removal, this is still a leak until that point. The skb is also leaked on this path since kfree_skb is only called at the end.

cve_desc: Integer underflow in the cli_scanpe function in pe.c in ClamAV before 0.97.8 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via a skewed offset larger than the size of the PE section in a UPX packed executable, which triggers an out-of-bounds read.

code_snippets:
for (i = 0 ; i < len; i+=2) name[i/2] = name[i];
  name[i/2]='\0';
vulnerabilities.short_description: Off-by-one heap buffer overflow when len is odd (especially len=1)
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `len` is odd, the for-loop increments `i` by 2, so after the loop terminates, `i` equals `len + 1` (the first even number >= len). The null terminator is then written at `name[(len+1)/2]`. For example, when `len = 1`, the buffer is 1 byte (valid index 0), but the null terminator is written at `name[1]`, which is one byte past the allocated heap buffer. This is a heap-based off-by-one write that could corrupt heap metadata or adjacent heap objects, potentially leading to arbitrary code execution.

commit_message: [TALOS-CAN-0063] avoid buffer overrun in ntpq
cve_desc: ntpq in NTP 4.2.x before 4.2.8p4, and 4.3.x before 4.3.77 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via crafted mode 6 response packets.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Off-by-one heap/stack buffer overflow when appending output_raw character to bv
vulnerabilities.long_description: When output_raw is not '*', atoascii is called with a max output size of sizeof(bv) - 1. If atoascii interprets this size as the maximum number of characters it can write (excluding the null terminator), it could produce a string of length sizeof(bv) - 1. Then len = sizeof(bv) - 1, and the write bv[len+1] = '\0' writes to index sizeof(bv), which is one byte past the end of the stack-allocated buffer bv[2 * MAXVALLEN]. This is a stack-based off-by-one buffer overflow that could corrupt adjacent stack variables or the saved frame pointer, potentially leading to code execution.

commit_message:
netfilter: nf_tables_offload: incorrect flow offload action array size

immediate verdict expression needs to allocate one slot in the flow offload
action array, however, immediate data expression does not need to do so.

fwd and dup expression need to allocate one slot, this is missing.

Add a new offload_action interface to report if this expression needs to
allocate one slot in the flow offload action array.

Fixes: be2861dc36d7 ("netfilter: nft_{fwd,dup}_netdev: add offload support")
Reported-and-tested-by: Nick Gregory <Nick.Gregory@Sophos.com>
Signed-off-by: Pablo Neira Ayuso <pablo@netfilter.org>
cve_desc: net/netfilter/nf_dup_netdev.c in the Linux kernel 5.4 through 5.6.10 allows local users to gain privileges because of a heap out-of-bounds write. This is related to nf_tables_offload.

commit_message:
netfilter: nf_tables_offload: incorrect flow offload action array size

immediate verdict expression needs to allocate one slot in the flow offload
action array, however, immediate data expression does not need to do so.

fwd and dup expression need to allocate one slot, this is missing.

Add a new offload_action interface to report if this expression needs to
allocate one slot in the flow offload action array.

Fixes: be2861dc36d7 ("netfilter: nft_{fwd,dup}_netdev: add offload support")
Reported-and-tested-by: Nick Gregory <Nick.Gregory@Sophos.com>
Signed-off-by: Pablo Neira Ayuso <pablo@netfilter.org>
cve_desc: net/netfilter/nf_dup_netdev.c in the Linux kernel 5.4 through 5.6.10 allows local users to gain privileges because of a heap out-of-bounds write. This is related to nf_tables_offload.

commit_message: lib: Protect against malicious namespace declarations (CVE-2022-25236)
cve_desc: xmlparse.c in Expat (aka libexpat) before 2.4.5 allows attackers to insert namespace-separator characters into namespace URIs.

commit_message:
nfc: st21nfca: Fix potential buffer overflows in EVT_TRANSACTION

It appears that there are some buffer overflows in EVT_TRANSACTION.
This happens because the length parameters that are passed to memcpy
come directly from skb->data and are not guarded in any way.

Signed-off-by: Jordy Zomer <jordy@pwning.systems>
Reviewed-by: Krzysztof Kozlowski <krzysztof.kozlowski@canonical.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: st21nfca_connectivity_event_received in drivers/nfc/st21nfca/se.c in the Linux kernel through 5.16.12 has EVT_TRANSACTION buffer overflows because of untrusted length parameters.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Logical AND (&&) instead of OR (||) allows short skb to bypass length validation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The condition uses && (AND) instead of || (OR). For the error to trigger, BOTH conditions must be true: the skb must be too short AND the tag must be wrong. If the skb is too short but data[0] happens to match NFC_EVT_TRANSACTION_AID_TAG, the check is bypassed, and subsequent accesses to skb->data at various offsets (e.g., skb->data[1], skb->data[transaction->aid_len + 2], etc.) will read out of bounds. An attacker can craft a very short skb with the correct first byte to trigger heap out-of-bounds reads.

vulnerabilities.short_description: No validation of aid_len or subsequent offsets against skb->len causes out-of-bounds reads
vulnerabilities.long_description: transaction->aid_len is read from skb->data[1] and checked only against sizeof(transaction->aid) (max 16), but never against skb->len. A crafted skb could be long enough to pass the initial (broken) length check but still too short for the aid_len value provided. Accesses at skb->data[2 .. 2+aid_len-1], skb->data[aid_len+2], skb->data[aid_len+3], and the params memcpy all rely on the skb being large enough, but this is never verified. This allows an attacker to cause heap out-of-bounds reads, potentially leaking kernel memory or causing a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory allocated with devm_kzalloc leaks on early error return paths
vulnerabilities.long_description: After the transaction buffer is allocated via devm_kzalloc, several error-return paths (returning -EINVAL or -EPROTO) exit the function without freeing the allocation. While devm-managed memory is freed when the device is removed, in this context the device is long-lived, so repeated triggering of these error paths leads to an unbounded memory leak until the device is unbound, which could be exploited for denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer underflow in allocation size when skb->len < 2
vulnerabilities.long_description: Due to the broken initial length check (using && instead of ||), it is possible to reach this allocation with skb->len being 0 or 1. Since skb->len is unsigned, `skb->len - 2` wraps to a very large value, causing either a huge allocation attempt (likely failing and returning -ENOMEM) or, in theory, an undersized allocation leading to subsequent buffer overflows if it somehow succeeds.

commit_message: Avoid maxrlvls more than upper bound to cause heap-buffer-overflow
cve_desc: There's a flaw in jasper's jpc encoder in versions prior to 2.0.23. Crafted input provided to jasper by an attacker could cause an arbitrary out-of-bounds write. This could potentially affect data confidentiality, integrity, or application availability.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in LCM step calculation loop
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variables hsteplcm and vsteplcm are multiplied in a loop by component step values without any overflow check. Since these are uint_fast32_t, repeated multiplication can wrap around, producing an incorrect (small) value. This corrupted value is later used to scale cp->imgareatlx and cp->imgareatly, leading to incorrect grid dimensions that could cause out-of-bounds memory access in later processing stages.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in image area and reference grid calculations
vulnerabilities.long_description: The multiplication of cp->imgareatlx by hsteplcm and the subsequent addition of image dimensions can overflow uint_fast32_t. This can produce smaller-than-expected values for refgrdwidth/refgrdheight, leading to incorrect tile counts and potential heap buffer overflows when tiles are allocated and processed later.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in tile count multiplication
vulnerabilities.long_description: The multiplication of numhtiles by numvtiles has no overflow check. If an attacker can control tile width/height to be very small relative to the grid dimensions, both numhtiles and numvtiles can be large, and their product can overflow. This leads to an undersized allocation for tile-related arrays, causing heap buffer overflows when the code iterates over the actual number of tiles.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Use of atoi for unsigned fields allows negative or overflowed values
vulnerabilities.long_description: Multiple option values are parsed with atoi(), which returns a signed int, and then stored into unsigned (uint_fast32_t/uint_fast16_t) fields. A negative input string yields a negative int that, when implicitly converted to unsigned, becomes a very large value. This can bypass validation checks (e.g., tile grid offset checks) and produce unexpected large dimensions, leading to integer overflows and potential memory corruption in subsequent calculations. Additionally, atoi provides no error detection for non-numeric or overflowing input.

vulnerabilities.short_description: sampgrdsubstepy is never initialized due to copy-paste bug
vulnerabilities.long_description: The field ccp->sampgrdsubstepx is set twice while ccp->sampgrdsubstepy is never initialized. This leaves sampgrdsubstepy with an indeterminate value from the heap allocation (jas_alloc2 does not zero memory). Depending on how sampgrdsubstepy is used later, this uninitialized memory read can cause incorrect computations, information disclosure, or undefined behavior.

commit_message: lib: Protect against malicious namespace declarations (CVE-2022-25236)
cve_desc: xmlparse.c in Expat (aka libexpat) before 2.4.5 allows attackers to insert namespace-separator characters into namespace URIs.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of BINDING struct on integer overflow error paths
vulnerabilities.long_description: When a new BINDING is allocated via MALLOC but the subsequent integer overflow checks for `len + EXPAND_SPARE` fail, the function returns XML_ERROR_NO_MEMORY without freeing the already-allocated BINDING struct `b`. There are two such error return paths (the `len > INT_MAX - EXPAND_SPARE` check and the `UINT_MAX >= SIZE_MAX` check) that both leak the BINDING allocation. An attacker who can control the URI length to trigger these overflow conditions repeatedly could cause unbounded memory consumption, potentially leading to denial of service.

commit_message: Fix https://github.com/Exiv2/exiv2/issues/55
cve_desc: In Exiv2 0.26, there is a heap-based buffer over-read in the Exiv2::Image::byteSwap4 function in image.cpp. Remote attackers can exploit this vulnerability to disclose memory data or cause a denial of service via a crafted TIFF file.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in size*count leads to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expression `size*count` is computed using uint32_t arithmetic before being cast to `long long`. If `size` and `count` (both attacker-controlled from the file) multiply to a value exceeding UINT32_MAX, the result wraps around to a small value. The bounds check `allocate > io.size()` then passes with a small `allocate`, a small buffer is allocated, but subsequent operations like `io.read(buf.pData_, count*size)` (which also overflows to the same small value) and the printing loops that access `buf` at offsets `k*size` for `k` up to `kount` (which can be up to 5, meaning offsets up to 40) can read/write beyond the undersized heap buffer. This can lead to information disclosure or code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read when accessing buf in printing loops
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `bOffsetIsPointer` is false (count*size <= 4), only 4 bytes are copied from the directory entry into `buf`. However, `kount` can be up to 5 for non-string types. For types with size 2 (short), accessing `k*size` at k=2 reads at offset 4, which is beyond the 4 copied bytes. For rational types (size=8), even k=0 accesses 8 bytes but only 4 were copied. Although the buffer is allocated larger than 4 bytes, the data beyond the first 4 bytes is uninitialized, causing an information leak of heap memory contents.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Unvalidated file offset allows seeking to arbitrary positions
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `offset` value is read directly from the file without validation against the file size. The commented-out line `// if ( offset > io.size() ) offset = 0;` suggests this was a known concern. An attacker can craft a file with an offset pointing beyond file boundaries, and while `io.read` may return fewer bytes, the buffer contents would be partially uninitialized, leading to information disclosure. Additionally, for recursive IFD parsing, attacker-controlled offsets can cause infinite recursion or processing of arbitrary file regions.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Uncontrolled recursion depth can cause stack overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function recurses via `printIFDStructure` for ExifTag, SubIFDs, tiffIfd type, and MakerNote entries. A crafted file can create circular IFD references (e.g., an IFD offset pointing back to a parent IFD), causing unbounded recursion. The `depth` variable is incremented but never checked against a maximum. This leads to stack exhaustion and a crash (denial of service).

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in address calculation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The address computation `start + 2 + i*12` uses uint32_t arithmetic where `start` is attacker-controlled and `i` can be up to 499 (dirLength-1). While this only affects display output, an overflow here can produce misleading address information. More critically, the iteration itself assumes sequential directory entries at `start + 2 + i*12` but doesn't validate these offsets are within the file.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4436: crash with weird 'vartabstop' value

Problem:    Crash with weird 'vartabstop' value.
Solution:   Check for running into the end of the line.
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.4436.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Heap buffer overflow when truncating new_line at cursor column
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the VREPLACE block, `new_line` is allocated as a copy of the current line via `vim_strsave`. Then `new_line[curwin->w_cursor.col] = NUL` is used to truncate it. If `curwin->w_cursor.col` is greater than or equal to the allocated size of `new_line` (i.e., beyond `strlen(ml_get_curline())`), this write goes out of bounds on the heap. After the extensive cursor column manipulation earlier in the function (including potential insertion of spaces, indent changes, etc.), the cursor column may end up beyond the actual length of the current line string, leading to a heap buffer overflow. An attacker who can influence the cursor position and indent operations could potentially achieve arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of orig_line when new_line allocation fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: When in VREPLACE mode, `orig_line` is allocated at the top of the function via `vim_strsave`. Later, `new_line` is allocated, and if that allocation fails, the function returns immediately without freeing `orig_line`. At this point `ml_replace` has not yet been called (which would have taken ownership of `orig_line`), so the memory is leaked. While this is a minor issue triggered only under memory pressure, repeated triggering could exhaust memory.

commit_message:
jp2_dec: fix `numchans` mixup

When iterating over `dec->cdef->data.cdef.ents`, we need to use its
`numchans` variable, not the one in `jp2_dec_t`.

Fixes CVE-2018-19543
Fixes CVE-2017-9782

Closes https://github.com/jasper-maint/jasper/issues/13
Closes https://github.com/jasper-maint/jasper/issues/18
Closes https://github.com/mdadams/jasper/issues/140
Closes https://github.com/mdadams/jasper/issues/182
cve_desc: JasPer 2.0.12 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (heap-based buffer over-read and application crash) via a crafted image, related to the jp2_decode function in libjasper/jp2/jp2_dec.c.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Null pointer dereference of dec->cdef when CMAP box is present but CDEF box is absent
vulnerabilities.long_description: When a CMAP box is present (dec->cmap is non-NULL), the code unconditionally dereferences dec->cdef to compute `cdefd = &dec->cdef->data.cdef`. However, there is no guarantee that a CDEF box was encountered during parsing — dec->cdef could be NULL. This leads to a null pointer dereference, which can cause a crash (denial of service). An attacker can craft a JP2 file with a CMAP and PCLR box but no CDEF box to trigger this.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Null pointer dereference when jas_alloc2 for lutents fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: The return value of jas_alloc2() when allocating lutents is never checked for NULL. If the allocation fails (e.g., due to memory pressure or a very large numlutents value), the subsequent loop will dereference a NULL pointer, leading to a crash. An attacker can craft a JP2 file with a large numlutents value in the PCLR box to trigger an allocation failure and denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read on CDEF entries array when CDEF numchans differs from dec->numchans
vulnerabilities.long_description: The loop iterates up to dec->numchans and indexes into dec->cdef->data.cdef.ents[i]. However, the CDEF box may have fewer entries than dec->numchans (the number of entries is dec->cdef->data.cdef.numchans, which is independently parsed). If dec->numchans exceeds the actual number of CDEF entries, this results in an out-of-bounds read. An attacker can craft a JP2 file where the CDEF box has fewer entries than the CMAP box has channels, leading to information disclosure or a crash.

commit_message: squash heap-buffer-overflow, PoC TIFF from Hardik
cve_desc: A heap buffer overflow issue was found in ImageMagick. When an application processes a malformed TIFF file, it could lead to undefined behavior or a crash causing a denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Incomplete overflow check in ReadGenericMethod: only checks rows, not columns*rows
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the ReadGenericMethod path, HeapOverflowSanityCheck is called with (image->rows, sizeof(*pixels)) which only validates that rows * sizeof(uint32) doesn't overflow. However, the actual allocation uses image->columns * image->rows * sizeof(uint32). If image->columns * image->rows overflows, a much smaller buffer is allocated, and the subsequent TIFFReadRGBAImage call writes beyond the allocated buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow. An attacker can craft a TIFF with large width and height values to trigger this.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in strip extent calculation leading to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the ReadStripMethod path, the extent is computed as 4*((image->depth+7)/8)*(samples_per_pixel+1)*TIFFStripSize(tiff). Since all operands are size_t and TIFFStripSize can return a large value, this multiplication can silently wrap around, resulting in a small allocation. Subsequent TIFFReadEncodedStrip calls write TIFFStripSize(tiff) bytes into this undersized buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow. An attacker can craft a TIFF with specific strip sizes and sample parameters to exploit this.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in tile extent calculation leading to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the ReadTileMethod path, the extent is computed by multiplying 4*(samples_per_pixel+1) with the maximum of rows*TIFFTileRowSize(tiff) and TIFFTileSize(tiff). This multiplication can overflow for large tile dimensions, resulting in an undersized allocation. The subsequent TIFFReadTile calls then write beyond the allocated buffer. A specially crafted TIFF with large tile dimensions can trigger this vulnerability.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential out-of-bounds read in ReadYCCKMethod when scanline is smaller than expected
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the ReadYCCKMethod, the code reads 4 bytes per pixel (p, p+1, p+2, p+3) and advances p by 4 for each of image->columns pixels. This assumes the scanline buffer contains at least image->columns*4 bytes. However, the pixels buffer is allocated based on scanline_size from TIFFScanlineSize, which may be smaller than image->columns*4 if the TIFF metadata is inconsistent. This can result in reading past the end of the allocated buffer.

commit_message: Avoid maxrlvls more than upper bound to cause heap-buffer-overflow
cve_desc: There's a flaw in jasper's jpc encoder in versions prior to 2.0.23. Crafted input provided to jasper by an attacker could cause an arbitrary out-of-bounds write. This could potentially affect data confidentiality, integrity, or application availability.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds write via unchecked maxrlvls before array bounds validation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The user-controlled option OPT_MAXRLVLS sets tccp->maxrlvls via atoi() without any bounds checking. This value is then used as the loop bound when writing to the fixed-size arrays tccp->prcwidthexpns[] and tccp->prcheightexpns[]. The validation that checks if maxrlvls < 1 occurs AFTER this loop has already executed. If a very large value is provided, the loop will write far beyond the array bounds, causing a heap buffer overflow. This can lead to code execution or denial of service.

code_snippets:
hsteplcm *= jas_image_cmpthstep(image, cmptno);
vsteplcm *= jas_image_cmptvstep(image, cmptno);
vulnerabilities.short_description: Multiple integer overflow vulnerabilities in arithmetic operations on image dimensions
vulnerabilities.long_description: Several arithmetic operations are performed without overflow checking: (1) hsteplcm and vsteplcm are repeatedly multiplied in a loop, easily overflowing. (2) cp->imgareatlx is multiplied by the overflowed hsteplcm. (3) cp->refgrdwidth/refgrdheight are computed via addition that can overflow. (4) cp->numtiles = numhtiles * numvtiles can overflow. These overflows can lead to undersized allocations later, heap buffer overflows, or incorrect program behavior.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in tile grid offset validation allows bypass of safety checks
vulnerabilities.long_description: The tile grid offset validation computes cp->tilegrdoffx + cp->tilewidth and cp->tilegrdoffy + cp->tileheight, both of which can wrap around on overflow (since they are unsigned). This means the validation check can pass even with invalid values, leading to incorrect tile calculations and potential memory corruption downstream.

commit_message:
jp2_dec: fix `numchans` mixup

When iterating over `dec->cdef->data.cdef.ents`, we need to use its
`numchans` variable, not the one in `jp2_dec_t`.

Fixes CVE-2018-19543
Fixes CVE-2017-9782

Closes https://github.com/jasper-maint/jasper/issues/13
Closes https://github.com/jasper-maint/jasper/issues/18
Closes https://github.com/mdadams/jasper/issues/140
Closes https://github.com/mdadams/jasper/issues/182
cve_desc: JasPer 2.0.12 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (heap-based buffer over-read and application crash) via a crafted image, related to the jp2_decode function in libjasper/jp2/jp2_dec.c.

vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference when dec->cdef is NULL but dec->cmap is present
vulnerabilities.long_description: When a CMAP box is present but no CDEF box exists, dec->cdef is NULL. The code unconditionally dereferences dec->cdef via `cdefd = &dec->cdef->data.cdef;` without checking for NULL. This is undefined behavior and can lead to a crash. An attacker can craft a JP2 file with a CMAP and PCLR box but no CDEF box to trigger this.

vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference when lutents allocation fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: The return value of jas_alloc2 for lutents is never checked for NULL. If the allocation fails (e.g., due to a large numlutents value in a crafted file), the subsequent loop dereferences a NULL pointer, causing a crash. This can be triggered by an attacker providing a JP2 file with palette mapping that causes a large allocation to fail.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing validation of channo as a valid component index for direct mapping
vulnerabilities.long_description: When a CMAP entry specifies JP2_CMAP_DIRECT, the code assigns `dec->chantocmptlut[channo] = channo` without verifying that channo is a valid component index (i.e., less than the number of image components). The validation loop above only checks cmptno for all entries, but for direct-mapped entries, channo is used as the component number. Later, dec->chantocmptlut[channo] is used to index into image components, which could cause out-of-bounds access if channo exceeds the number of components.

commit_message: commit bash-20190628 snapshot
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in disable_priv_mode in shell.c in GNU Bash through 5.0 patch 11. By default, if Bash is run with its effective UID not equal to its real UID, it will drop privileges by setting its effective UID to its real UID. However, it does so incorrectly. On Linux and other systems that support "saved UID" functionality, the saved UID is not dropped. An attacker with command execution in the shell can use "enable -f" for runtime loading of a new builtin, which can be a shared object that calls setuid() and therefore regains privileges. However, binaries running with an effective UID of 0 are unaffected.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Failure to drop privileges does not abort execution, allowing continued operation with elevated privileges.
vulnerabilities.long_description: When setresuid/setuid or setresgid/setgid fails, the function logs an error but continues execution. Without EXIT_ON_SETUID_FAILURE defined, or when the errno is not EAGAIN, the function proceeds and even updates current_user.euid/egid to the unprivileged values, masking the fact that privilege dropping failed. This means the process continues running with elevated (e.g., root) privileges while believing it has dropped them, which could be exploited by an attacker to perform privileged operations that should have been denied.

commit_message:
glob: fix heap buffer overflow

* lib/glob.c (glob): Fix off-by-one error introduced into
glibc in commit dd7d45e838a42b0ed470c44b55901ea98d0c2bab
dated 1997-10-29 20:33:40.  Problem reported by Tim Rühsen in:
https://sourceware.org/bugzilla/show_bug.cgi?id=22320
Fix suggested by Bruno Haible.
cve_desc: The GNU C Library (aka glibc or libc6) before 2.27 contains an off-by-one error leading to a heap-based buffer overflow in the glob function in glob.c, related to the processing of home directories using the ~ operator followed by a long string.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Off-by-one heap/stack buffer overflow when extracting user name in ~user expansion
vulnerabilities.long_description: When processing a `~username/...` pattern, the code allocates `end_name - dirname` bytes for `newp` to hold the user name. It then copies `end_name - dirname` bytes from `dirname + 1` via `mempcpy`, and writes a NUL terminator at the returned pointer (one byte past the copied data). The user name occupies `end_name - dirname - 1` characters (from `dirname + 1` up to but not including `end_name`), so the mempcpy writes `end_name - dirname` bytes which already fills the buffer completely. The subsequent NUL terminator write overflows the buffer by one byte. If `malloc` was used (when `glob_use_alloca` returns false), this is a one-byte heap buffer overflow that can corrupt heap metadata, potentially leading to arbitrary code execution. If `alloca` was used, it corrupts the stack. An attacker controlling the glob pattern (e.g., `~username/...`) can trigger this vulnerability.

commit_message:
Do not call http_hdr_flags() on pseudo-headers

In http_EstimateWS(), all headers are passed to the http_isfiltered()
function to calculate how many bytes is needed to serialize the entire
struct http. http_isfiltered() will check the headers for whether they are
going to be filtered out later and if so skip them.

However http_isfiltered() would attempt to treat all elements of struct
http as regular headers with an implicit structure. That does not hold for
the first three pseudo-header entries, which would lead to asserts in
later steps.

This patch skips the filter step for pseudo-headers.

Fixes: #3830
cve_desc: In Varnish Cache 7.0.0, 7.0.1, 7.0.2, and 7.1.0, it is possible to cause the Varnish Server to assert and automatically restart through forged HTTP/1 backend responses. An attack uses a crafted reason phrase of the backend response status line. This is fixed in 7.0.3 and 7.1.1.

we need to make sure @q and @chain local variables are cleared again,

For consistency, apply the same fix in tc_ctl_chain()

commit_message:
Fix: Worked around buffer overflows in URL auth's cURL interface

This is only a workaround that keeps compatibility with 2.4.x mainline.
A real fix has been implemented in 2.5.x (master).
cve_desc: A buffer overflow was discovered in the URL-authentication backend of the Icecast before 2.4.4. If the backend is enabled, then any malicious HTTP client can send a request for that specific resource including a crafted header, leading to denial of service and potentially remote code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in size * nmemb multiplication
vulnerabilities.long_description: The multiplication of size and nmemb is performed without overflow checking. If both values are large, the result can wrap around, causing `bytes` to be a small value while `ptr` points to a much larger (or smaller) buffer. Although the return value `bytes` is primarily used by libcurl to verify the callback processed all data, the overflow itself means the function's understanding of the buffer size is wrong. More critically, the subsequent string operations (strncasecmp, sscanf, snprintf, strchr) all treat `ptr` as a C string without any length bounds derived from the actual buffer size, so a mismatch could contribute to out-of-bounds reads.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read due to treating non-null-terminated buffer as a string
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `ptr` buffer from libcurl's header callback is not guaranteed to be null-terminated. Functions like sscanf, snprintf with %s, and strchr all expect null-terminated strings. If the buffer at `ptr` is not null-terminated, these functions will read beyond the allocated buffer. Additionally, strncasecmp compares up to `url->auth_header_len` or `url->timelimit_header_len` bytes without verifying that the buffer `ptr` (of size size*nmemb) is at least that long, potentially reading out of bounds. Similarly, `ptr+22` and `ptr+url->timelimit_header_len` are used without checking that the buffer is large enough, leading to potential out-of-bounds access.
