commit_message:
i2c: ismt: prevent memory corruption in ismt_access()

The "data->block[0]" variable comes from the user and is a number
between 0-255.  It needs to be capped to prevent writing beyond the end
of dma_buffer[].

Fixes: 5e9a97b1f449 ("i2c: ismt: Adding support for I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL")
Reported-and-tested-by: Zheyu Ma <zheyuma97@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Dan Carpenter <dan.carpenter@oracle.com>
Reviewed-by: Mika Westerberg <mika.westerberg@linux.intel.com>
Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
cve_desc: A buffer overflow vulnerability was found in the Linux kernel Intel’s iSMT SMBus host controller driver in the way it handled the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL case (via the ioctl I2C_SMBUS) with malicious input data. This flaw could allow a local user to crash the system.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow of u8 dma_size in SMBUS_BLOCK_DATA write can bypass DMA mapping and cause buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_DATA write case, dma_size is a u8 and is assigned data->block[0] + 1. If data->block[0] is 255, dma_size wraps to 0 due to u8 overflow. This means the DMA mapping is skipped (since the check is dma_size != 0), yet the memcpy still writes dma_size - 1 (which as a u8 wraps to 255) bytes from data->block[1] into dma_buffer. This causes an out-of-bounds write on the priv->buffer array. Even without the wrap to 0, data->block[0] is not bounds-checked against I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_MAX (32), so any value above 32 can overflow the DMA buffer.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing bounds check on data->block[0] in SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL allows heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL case, data->block[0] is used directly without validation as the size for memcpy into dma_buffer. Since data->block[0] can be up to 255 but dma_buffer points into the fixed-size priv->buffer, a large value causes an out-of-bounds heap write. Unlike the I2C_SMBUS_I2C_BLOCK_DATA case which explicitly clamps data->block[0] to I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_MAX, no such check exists here. An attacker who can influence SMBus transaction parameters could corrupt adjacent kernel heap memory, potentially leading to privilege escalation.

commit_message: commit bash-20190628 snapshot
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in disable_priv_mode in shell.c in GNU Bash through 5.0 patch 11. By default, if Bash is run with its effective UID not equal to its real UID, it will drop privileges by setting its effective UID to its real UID. However, it does so incorrectly. On Linux and other systems that support "saved UID" functionality, the saved UID is not dropped. An attacker with command execution in the shell can use "enable -f" for runtime loading of a new builtin, which can be a shared object that calls setuid() and therefore regains privileges. However, binaries running with an effective UID of 0 are unaffected.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Failure to drop privileges: cached euid/egid updated even when setuid/setgid fails, and execution continues.
vulnerabilities.long_description: When setuid() or setgid() fails, the function logs an error but continues execution without aborting (EXIT_ON_SETUID_FAILURE only covers EAGAIN). Critically, the cached values current_user.euid and current_user.egid are unconditionally updated to the unprivileged uid/gid at the end of the function. This means the rest of the program believes privileges have been dropped when they have not, potentially allowing privileged operations to continue under the assumption of reduced rights. An attacker who can cause setuid/setgid to fail (e.g., via resource limits) could keep elevated privileges while the program thinks it is unprivileged.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Incorrect order of privilege dropping: setuid() called before setgid().
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function calls setuid() to drop the user ID before calling setgid() to drop the group ID. Once the effective UID is changed to an unprivileged user, the process may no longer have permission to call setgid() successfully, causing the group privilege drop to fail silently (only a log message is emitted). The correct and well-established practice is to call setgid() first while still running with elevated privileges, and then call setuid(). This ordering bug can leave the process running with an elevated effective GID.

commit_message:
i2c: ismt: prevent memory corruption in ismt_access()

The "data->block[0]" variable comes from the user and is a number
between 0-255.  It needs to be capped to prevent writing beyond the end
of dma_buffer[].

Fixes: 5e9a97b1f449 ("i2c: ismt: Adding support for I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL")
Reported-and-tested-by: Zheyu Ma <zheyuma97@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Dan Carpenter <dan.carpenter@oracle.com>
Reviewed-by: Mika Westerberg <mika.westerberg@linux.intel.com>
Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
cve_desc: A buffer overflow vulnerability was found in the Linux kernel Intel’s iSMT SMBus host controller driver in the way it handled the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL case (via the ioctl I2C_SMBUS) with malicious input data. This flaw could allow a local user to crash the system.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing validation of data->block[0] in I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_DATA write path allows buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_DATA write case, data->block[0] is used directly without bounds checking. Since data->block[0] is a u8, it can be up to 255. The memcpy copies data->block[0] bytes into dma_buffer (which is derived from priv->buffer, a fixed-size buffer). In contrast, the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL case correctly validates that data->block[0] <= I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_MAX (32) before proceeding. A value larger than expected (e.g., 255) would cause an out-of-bounds write on the DMA buffer, potentially corrupting adjacent kernel memory. This could lead to privilege escalation or denial of service.

commit_message: squash heap-buffer-overflow, PoC TIFF from Hardik
cve_desc: A heap buffer overflow issue was found in ImageMagick. When an application processes a malformed TIFF file, it could lead to undefined behavior or a crash causing a denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in strip buffer extent calculation leads to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the ReadStripMethod case, the `extent` variable is computed as `4*(samples_per_pixel+1)*TIFFStripSize(tiff)`. Since `samples_per_pixel` can be up to 32 (MaxPixelChannels) and `TIFFStripSize()` can return a large value, this multiplication can overflow a `size_t` (especially on 32-bit platforms), resulting in a much smaller allocation than intended. The subsequent `memset` and `TIFFReadEncodedStrip` operations then write beyond the allocated buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow. An attacker can craft a TIFF file with specific strip size and samples_per_pixel values to trigger this overflow for code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in tile buffer extent calculation leads to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the ReadTileMethod case, `extent` is calculated as `4*(samples_per_pixel+1)*MagickMax(rows*TIFFTileRowSize(tiff), TIFFTileSize(tiff))`. Both the inner multiplication `rows*TIFFTileRowSize(tiff)` and the outer multiplication can overflow, resulting in a small heap allocation. The subsequent `memset` zeroing and `TIFFReadTile` calls write past the end of the undersized buffer, causing heap corruption. A crafted TIFF with large tile dimensions and high samples_per_pixel can exploit this for arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Heap buffer over-read in ReadYCCKMethod due to unchecked pixel buffer access
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the ReadYCCKMethod case, the code reads a scanline into the `pixels` buffer and then iterates over `image->columns`, accessing 4 bytes per pixel (p[0] through p[3]) and advancing the pointer by 4 each iteration. The `pixels` buffer is allocated based on `number_pixels*sizeof(uint32)` which is derived from scanline_size, but there is no validation that `image->columns * 4` does not exceed the buffer size. If the scanline size reported by libtiff is smaller than `image->columns * 4`, this results in an out-of-bounds read, potentially leaking sensitive memory contents or causing a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Incomplete overflow check in ReadGenericMethod - only rows checked, not columns*rows product
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the ReadGenericMethod, `HeapOverflowSanityCheck` is called with only `image->rows` and `sizeof(*pixels)`, but the actual allocation is for `image->columns * image->rows` elements. The check does not account for the columns dimension, so the product `image->columns * image->rows` can overflow (particularly on 32-bit platforms where size_t is 32-bit). This leads to a small allocation while `TIFFReadRGBAImage` writes a much larger amount of data, causing a heap buffer overflow. A crafted TIFF with large dimensions could exploit this.

commit_message: Bug 705156(1): Avoid double free of PCL error buffer
cve_desc: A vulnerability classified as problematic was found in GhostPCL 9.55.0. This vulnerability affects the function chunk_free_object of the file gsmchunk.c. The manipulation with a malicious file leads to a memory corruption. The attack can be initiated remotely but requires user interaction. The exploit has been disclosed to the public as a POC and may be used. It is recommended to apply the patches to fix this issue.

commit_message:
Do not call http_hdr_flags() on pseudo-headers

In http_EstimateWS(), all headers are passed to the http_isfiltered()
function to calculate how many bytes is needed to serialize the entire
struct http. http_isfiltered() will check the headers for whether they are
going to be filtered out later and if so skip them.

However http_isfiltered() would attempt to treat all elements of struct
http as regular headers with an implicit structure. That does not hold for
the first three pseudo-header entries, which would lead to asserts in
later steps.

This patch skips the filter step for pseudo-headers.

Fixes: #3830
cve_desc: In Varnish Cache 7.0.0, 7.0.1, 7.0.2, and 7.1.0, it is possible to cause the Varnish Server to assert and automatically restart through forged HTTP/1 backend responses. An attack uses a crafted reason phrase of the backend response status line. This is fixed in 7.0.3 and 7.1.1.

commit_message:
bpf: Fix integer overflow in prealloc_elems_and_freelist()

In prealloc_elems_and_freelist(), the multiplication to calculate the
size passed to bpf_map_area_alloc() could lead to an integer overflow.
As a result, out-of-bounds write could occur in pcpu_freelist_populate()
as reported by KASAN:

[...]
[   16.968613] BUG: KASAN: slab-out-of-bounds in pcpu_freelist_populate+0xd9/0x100
[   16.969408] Write of size 8 at addr ffff888104fc6ea0 by task crash/78
[   16.970038]
[   16.970195] CPU: 0 PID: 78 Comm: crash Not tainted 5.15.0-rc2+ #1
[   16.970878] Hardware name: QEMU Standard PC (i440FX + PIIX, 1996), BIOS 1.13.0-1ubuntu1.1 04/01/2014
[   16.972026] Call Trace:
[   16.972306]  dump_stack_lvl+0x34/0x44
[   16.972687]  print_address_description.constprop.0+0x21/0x140
[   16.973297]  ? pcpu_freelist_populate+0xd9/0x100
[   16.973777]  ? pcpu_freelist_populate+0xd9/0x100
[   16.974257]  kasan_report.cold+0x7f/0x11b
[   16.974681]  ? pcpu_freelist_populate+0xd9/0x100
[   16.975190]  pcpu_freelist_populate+0xd9/0x100
[   16.975669]  stack_map_alloc+0x209/0x2a0
[   16.976106]  __sys_bpf+0xd83/0x2ce0
[...]

The possibility of this overflow was originally discussed in [0], but
was overlooked.

Fix the integer overflow by changing elem_size to u64 from u32.

  [0] https://lore.kernel.org/bpf/728b238e-a481-eb50-98e9-b0f430ab01e7@gmail.com/

Fixes: 557c0c6e7df8 ("bpf: convert stackmap to pre-allocation")
Signed-off-by: Tatsuhiko Yasumatsu <th.yasumatsu@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Daniel Borkmann <daniel@iogearbox.net>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/bpf/20210930135545.173698-1-th.yasumatsu@gmail.com
cve_desc: prealloc_elems_and_freelist in kernel/bpf/stackmap.c in the Linux kernel before 5.14.12 allows unprivileged users to trigger an eBPF multiplication integer overflow with a resultant out-of-bounds write.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in allocation size calculation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The multiplication `elem_size * smap->map.max_entries` can overflow a u32/u64 boundary. `elem_size` is a u32 derived from `sizeof(struct stack_map_bucket) + smap->map.value_size`, and `smap->map.max_entries` is also a u32. If both values are sufficiently large, their product wraps around, resulting in a much smaller allocation than expected. Subsequent use in `pcpu_freelist_populate` would then operate on memory beyond the allocated buffer, leading to heap out-of-bounds writes. An attacker who can control `value_size` and `max_entries` (e.g., via the bpf syscall) could exploit this for privilege escalation or denial of service.

commit_message: Merge pull request from GHSA-2qpg-f6wf-w984
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C language implementing standard based protocols such as SIP, SDP, RTP, STUN, TURN, and ICE. In affected versions if the incoming STUN message contains an ERROR-CODE attribute, the header length is not checked before performing a subtraction operation, potentially resulting in an integer underflow scenario. This issue affects all users that use STUN. A malicious actor located within the victim’s network may forge and send a specially crafted UDP (STUN) message that could remotely execute arbitrary code on the victim’s machine. Users are advised to upgrade as soon as possible. There are no known workarounds.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer underflow in reason string length leads to heap buffer over-read
vulnerabilities.long_description: If `attr->hdr.length` (parsed from the network buffer) is less than 4, the subtraction `attr->hdr.length - 4` underflows. If `slen` is an unsigned type, this produces a very large value; if signed, it becomes negative but `pj_strdup` may interpret it incorrectly. In either case, the subsequent `pj_strdup` call will attempt to copy a huge or invalid amount of memory from the buffer, causing an out-of-bounds read from the input buffer and potentially an oversized heap allocation or write. An attacker can craft a STUN message with a small `length` field in the ERROR-CODE attribute to trigger this, potentially crashing the application or leaking memory contents.

commit_message: Bug 705156(1): Avoid double free of PCL error buffer
cve_desc: A vulnerability classified as problematic was found in GhostPCL 9.55.0. This vulnerability affects the function chunk_free_object of the file gsmchunk.c. The manipulation with a malicious file leads to a memory corruption. The attack can be initiated remotely but requires user interaction. The exploit has been disclosed to the public as a POC and may be used. It is recommended to apply the patches to fix this issue.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Unsigned integer underflow when read_pos exceeds write_pos leads to out-of-bounds read
vulnerabilities.long_description: If `pcs->status.read_pos` is greater than `pcs->status.write_pos`, the subtraction `write_pos - read_pos` underflows because both operands are unsigned, producing a very large value. The `min` with `max_data` would then yield `max_data`, and the subsequent `memcpy` would read `max_data` bytes starting at an offset of `read_pos` into the buffer. This could read well beyond the allocated buffer, leaking sensitive memory contents or causing a crash. An attacker who can influence the state's read/write positions (e.g., through malformed input or repeated calls) could exploit this for information disclosure or denial of service.

commit_message:
Fix: Worked around buffer overflows in URL auth's cURL interface

This is only a workaround that keeps compatibility with 2.4.x mainline.
A real fix has been implemented in 2.5.x (master).
cve_desc: A buffer overflow was discovered in the URL-authentication backend of the Icecast before 2.4.4. If the backend is enabled, then any malicious HTTP client can send a request for that specific resource including a crafted header, leading to denial of service and potentially remote code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in size * nmemb multiplication
vulnerabilities.long_description: The multiplication of size and nmemb can overflow a size_t value. If an attacker can influence the values returned by the upstream server (e.g., via a malicious auth backend), a crafted response could cause len to wrap around to a small value, while the actual data pointed to by ptr is much larger. All subsequent length checks and memory operations based on len would then be incorrect, potentially leading to buffer over-reads or other memory corruption.

vulnerabilities.short_description: sscanf used on a non-null-terminated buffer, causing potential out-of-bounds read
vulnerabilities.long_description: The ptr buffer provided by the libcurl header callback is not guaranteed to be null-terminated. The code passes input (a pointer into this buffer) to sscanf, which expects a null-terminated string and will read until it finds a '\0' byte. This can cause sscanf to read past the end of the provided buffer into adjacent memory, potentially leading to information disclosure or a crash. An attacker controlling the auth backend could craft headers to trigger this condition.

commit_message:
bpf: Fix integer overflow in prealloc_elems_and_freelist()

In prealloc_elems_and_freelist(), the multiplication to calculate the
size passed to bpf_map_area_alloc() could lead to an integer overflow.
As a result, out-of-bounds write could occur in pcpu_freelist_populate()
as reported by KASAN:

[...]
[   16.968613] BUG: KASAN: slab-out-of-bounds in pcpu_freelist_populate+0xd9/0x100
[   16.969408] Write of size 8 at addr ffff888104fc6ea0 by task crash/78
[   16.970038]
[   16.970195] CPU: 0 PID: 78 Comm: crash Not tainted 5.15.0-rc2+ #1
[   16.970878] Hardware name: QEMU Standard PC (i440FX + PIIX, 1996), BIOS 1.13.0-1ubuntu1.1 04/01/2014
[   16.972026] Call Trace:
[   16.972306]  dump_stack_lvl+0x34/0x44
[   16.972687]  print_address_description.constprop.0+0x21/0x140
[   16.973297]  ? pcpu_freelist_populate+0xd9/0x100
[   16.973777]  ? pcpu_freelist_populate+0xd9/0x100
[   16.974257]  kasan_report.cold+0x7f/0x11b
[   16.974681]  ? pcpu_freelist_populate+0xd9/0x100
[   16.975190]  pcpu_freelist_populate+0xd9/0x100
[   16.975669]  stack_map_alloc+0x209/0x2a0
[   16.976106]  __sys_bpf+0xd83/0x2ce0
[...]

The possibility of this overflow was originally discussed in [0], but
was overlooked.

Fix the integer overflow by changing elem_size to u64 from u32.

  [0] https://lore.kernel.org/bpf/728b238e-a481-eb50-98e9-b0f430ab01e7@gmail.com/

Fixes: 557c0c6e7df8 ("bpf: convert stackmap to pre-allocation")
Signed-off-by: Tatsuhiko Yasumatsu <th.yasumatsu@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Daniel Borkmann <daniel@iogearbox.net>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/bpf/20210930135545.173698-1-th.yasumatsu@gmail.com
cve_desc: prealloc_elems_and_freelist in kernel/bpf/stackmap.c in the Linux kernel before 5.14.12 allows unprivileged users to trigger an eBPF multiplication integer overflow with a resultant out-of-bounds write.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in elem_size * max_entries allocation size calculation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The multiplication `elem_size * smap->map.max_entries` is performed in 64-bit arithmetic (u64 * u32), but for sufficiently large values of both operands, it can still wrap around to a small value. For example, if value_size is crafted so that elem_size is large and max_entries is also large, the product can overflow u64 and wrap to a small number. This would cause bpf_map_area_alloc to allocate a small buffer, while pcpu_freelist_populate would then treat the buffer as containing max_entries elements of elem_size bytes each, leading to massive out-of-bounds memory access. An attacker with the ability to create BPF maps (e.g., via BPF syscall) could exploit this to corrupt kernel memory, potentially achieving privilege escalation.

patch 9.0.0322: crash when no errors and 'quickfixtextfunc' is set

Problem:    Crash when no errors and 'quickfixtextfunc' is set.
Solution:   Do not handle errors if there aren't any.
cve_desc: Use After Free in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.0322.

commit_message:
sungem: switch to use qemu_receive_packet() for loopback

This patch switches to use qemu_receive_packet() which can detect
reentrancy and return early.

This is intended to address CVE-2021-3416.

Cc: Prasad J Pandit <ppandit@redhat.com>
Cc: qemu-stable@nongnu.org
Reviewed-by: Mark Cave-Ayland <mark.cave-ayland@ilande.co.uk>
Reviewed-by: Philippe Mathieu-Daudé <philmd@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Alistair Francis <alistair.francis@wdc.com>
Signed-off-by: Jason Wang <jasowang@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A potential stack overflow via infinite loop issue was found in various NIC emulators of QEMU in versions up to and including 5.2.0. The issue occurs in loopback mode of a NIC wherein reentrant DMA checks get bypassed. A guest user/process may use this flaw to consume CPU cycles or crash the QEMU process on the host resulting in DoS scenario.

commit_message: Merge pull request from GHSA-2qpg-f6wf-w984
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C language implementing standard based protocols such as SIP, SDP, RTP, STUN, TURN, and ICE. In affected versions if the incoming STUN message contains an ERROR-CODE attribute, the header length is not checked before performing a subtraction operation, potentially resulting in an integer underflow scenario. This issue affects all users that use STUN. A malicious actor located within the victim’s network may forge and send a specially crafted UDP (STUN) message that could remotely execute arbitrary code on the victim’s machine. Users are advised to upgrade as soon as possible. There are no known workarounds.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer underflow in STUN error-code attribute length leads to heap buffer over-read
vulnerabilities.long_description: If `attr->hdr.length` (parsed from the network) is an unsigned type (e.g., uint16_t) with a value less than 4, the subtraction `attr->hdr.length - 4` wraps around to a large positive value (e.g., 65534 for a uint16_t with value 2). When this is assigned to `value.slen` (a signed `pj_ssize_t`, typically 32 or 64 bits), the result remains a large positive number because it fits within the signed range. The subsequent `value.slen < 0` guard therefore fails to catch this case, and `pj_strdup` is called with an excessively large length, causing a heap buffer over-read. An attacker can craft a malicious STUN packet with a small attribute length to read out-of-bounds memory, potentially leaking sensitive data or causing a crash.

commit_message:
glob: fix heap buffer overflow

* lib/glob.c (glob): Fix off-by-one error introduced into
glibc in commit dd7d45e838a42b0ed470c44b55901ea98d0c2bab
dated 1997-10-29 20:33:40.  Problem reported by Tim Rühsen in:
https://sourceware.org/bugzilla/show_bug.cgi?id=22320
Fix suggested by Bruno Haible.
cve_desc: The GNU C Library (aka glibc or libc6) before 2.27 contains an off-by-one error leading to a heap-based buffer overflow in the glob function in glob.c, related to the processing of home directories using the ~ operator followed by a long string.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in tilde expansion buffer size calculation leads to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: When expanding `~/...` patterns, the code computes `home_len + dirlen` without checking for integer overflow. If the sum of strlen(home_dir) and dirlen wraps around to a small value, a too-small buffer is allocated via malloc, but the subsequent mempcpy calls write the full `home_len` and `dirlen` bytes, causing a heap buffer overflow. An attacker who can control the HOME environment variable and the glob pattern could exploit this to achieve arbitrary code execution. This is related to CVE-2017-15670.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in ~NAME expansion buffer size calculation leads to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: When expanding `~username/...` patterns, the code computes `home_len + rest_len + 1` without checking for overflow. If pw_dir length and rest path length sum to a value that wraps around, a too-small buffer is allocated while the mempcpy operations copy the full original sizes, causing a heap buffer overflow. An attacker could exploit this via a crafted passwd entry (e.g., very long home directory) combined with a long pattern suffix.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of malloc'd user_name on error path during ~NAME expansion
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `malloc_user_name` is set (i.e., user_name was heap-allocated) and `scratch_buffer_grow` fails inside the getpwnam_r loop, the code jumps to `goto out` without freeing `user_name`. The `out` label only frees `dirname`, so the heap-allocated `user_name` buffer is leaked. While primarily a memory leak, in long-running processes this could lead to memory exhaustion (denial of service) if an attacker can repeatedly trigger this path.

commit_message:
Sanitize inputs to bookmark-cmd (#591)

Newsbeuter didn't properly shell-escape the arguments passed to
bookmarking command, which allows a remote attacker to perform remote
code execution by crafting an RSS item whose title and/or URL contain
something interpretable by the shell (most notably subshell
invocations.)

This has been reported by Jeriko One <jeriko.one@gmx.us>, complete with
PoC and a patch.

This vulnerability was assigned CVE-2017-12904.
cve_desc: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command in bookmarking function of Newsbeuter versions 0.7 through 2.9 allows remote attackers to perform user-assisted code execution by crafting an RSS item that includes shell code in its title and/or URL.

code_snippets:
std::string cmdline = strprintf::fmt("%s '%s' '%s' '%s' '%s'",
		                                       bookmark_cmd,
		                                       utils::replace_all(url,"'", "%27"),
		                                       utils::replace_all(title,"'", "%27"),
		                                       utils::replace_all(description,"'", "%27"),
		                                       utils::replace_all(feed_title,"'", "%27"));
vulnerabilities.short_description: OS command injection via incomplete shell argument escaping
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code attempts to sanitize user-controlled inputs (url, title, description, feed_title) by replacing single quotes with %27 before embedding them in single-quoted shell arguments. However, this escaping is insufficient. While single quotes are replaced, the values are placed inside single quotes in the shell command, and a crafted input could still break out. More critically, if any of these strings contain characters like newlines or other control characters, they can alter shell behavior. Additionally, the bookmark_cmd itself is taken from configuration and concatenated directly without validation. A feed providing a malicious title or URL such as one containing `'$(malicious_command)'` patterns (after the single-quote replacement is applied the surrounding quotes could still be manipulated depending on shell interpretation) could lead to arbitrary command execution. The proper fix is to use a robust shell-escaping function (e.g., escaping all special characters or passing arguments via execv-style APIs without shell interpretation).

commit_message:
Sanitize inputs to bookmark-cmd (#591)

Newsbeuter didn't properly shell-escape the arguments passed to
bookmarking command, which allows a remote attacker to perform remote
code execution by crafting an RSS item whose title and/or URL contain
something interpretable by the shell (most notably subshell
invocations.)

This has been reported by Jeriko One <jeriko.one@gmx.us>, complete with
PoC and a patch.

This vulnerability was assigned CVE-2017-12904.
cve_desc: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command in bookmarking function of Newsbeuter versions 0.7 through 2.9 allows remote attackers to perform user-assisted code execution by crafting an RSS item that includes shell code in its title and/or URL.

code_snippets:
std::string cmdline = strprintf::fmt("%s '%s' %s %s %s",
		                                       bookmark_cmd,
		                                       utils::replace_all(url,"'", "%27"),
		                                       quote_empty(stfl::quote(title)),
		                                       quote_empty(stfl::quote(description)),
		                                       quote_empty(stfl::quote(feed_title)));
vulnerabilities.short_description: OS command injection via unsanitized title, description, and feed_title parameters passed to a shell command.
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function constructs a shell command line by concatenating user-controlled inputs (title, description, feed_title) into a string that is later executed via /bin/sh -c. While the 'url' parameter is placed within single quotes and has single-quote characters escaped, the title, description, and feed_title parameters are only processed through stfl::quote() and quote_empty(), which are UI quoting functions not designed for shell escaping. An attacker who controls an RSS feed's title, item title, or description can inject arbitrary shell metacharacters (e.g., backticks, $(), semicolons, pipes) to execute arbitrary commands when a user bookmarks the item. This could lead to full system compromise under the privileges of the running process.

commit_message:
nfc: nfcmrvl: main: reorder destructive operations in nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev to avoid bugs

There are destructive operations such as nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_abort and
gpio_free in nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev. The resources such as firmware,
gpio and so on could be destructed while the upper layer functions such as
nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start and nfcmrvl_nci_recv_frame is executing, which leads
to double-free, use-after-free and null-ptr-deref bugs.

There are three situations that could lead to double-free bugs.

The first situation is shown below:

   (Thread 1)                 |      (Thread 2)
nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start         |
 ...                          |  nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev
 release_firmware()           |   nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_abort
  kfree(fw) //(1)             |    fw_dnld_over
                              |     release_firmware
  ...                         |      kfree(fw) //(2)
                              |     ...

The second situation is shown below:

   (Thread 1)                 |      (Thread 2)
nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start         |
 ...                          |
 mod_timer                    |
 (wait a time)                |
 fw_dnld_timeout              |  nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev
   fw_dnld_over               |   nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_abort
    release_firmware          |    fw_dnld_over
     kfree(fw) //(1)          |     release_firmware
     ...                      |      kfree(fw) //(2)

The third situation is shown below:

       (Thread 1)               |       (Thread 2)
nfcmrvl_nci_recv_frame          |
 if(..->fw_download_in_progress)|
  nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_recv_frame    |
   queue_work                   |
                                |
fw_dnld_rx_work                 | nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev
 fw_dnld_over                   |  nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_abort
  release_firmware              |   fw_dnld_over
   kfree(fw) //(1)              |    release_firmware
                                |     kfree(fw) //(2)

The firmware struct is deallocated in position (1) and deallocated
in position (2) again.

The crash trace triggered by POC is like below:

BUG: KASAN: double-free or invalid-free in fw_dnld_over
Call Trace:
  kfree
  fw_dnld_over
  nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev
  nci_uart_tty_close
  tty_ldisc_kill
  tty_ldisc_hangup
  __tty_hangup.part.0
  tty_release
  ...

What's more, there are also use-after-free and null-ptr-deref bugs
in nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start. If we deallocate firmware struct, gpio or
set null to the members of priv->fw_dnld in nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev,
then, we dereference firmware, gpio or the members of priv->fw_dnld in
nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start, the UAF or NPD bugs will happen.

This patch reorders destructive operations after nci_unregister_device
in order to synchronize between cleanup routine and firmware download
routine.

The nci_unregister_device is well synchronized. If the device is
detaching, the firmware download routine will goto error. If firmware
download routine is executing, nci_unregister_device will wait until
firmware download routine is finished.

Fixes: 3194c6870158 ("NFC: nfcmrvl: add firmware download support")
Signed-off-by: Duoming Zhou <duoming@zju.edu.cn>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: A flaw in Linux Kernel found in nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev() in drivers/nfc/nfcmrvl/main.c can lead to use after free both read or write when non synchronized between cleanup routine and firmware download routine.

commit_message: imptcp: fix Segmentation Fault when octet count is to high
cve_desc: A denial of service vulnerability was found in rsyslog in the imptcp module. An attacker could send a specially crafted message to the imptcp socket, which would cause rsyslog to crash. Versions before 8.27.0 are vulnerable.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Buffer overflow when parsing octet count digits — no bounds check on iMsg
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the `eInOctetCnt` state, each received digit character is appended to `pThis->pMsg` at offset `pThis->iMsg` which is then incremented, but there is no check that `pThis->iMsg` is within the bounds of the `pMsg` buffer (e.g., less than `iMaxLine`). An attacker could send a very long sequence of digits (up to ~200 million characters before the size guard triggers), causing `iMsg` to grow far beyond the allocated buffer size, resulting in a heap buffer overflow. This could allow remote code execution or denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer underflow in octet-counting truncation when iMsg already exceeds iMaxLine
vulnerabilities.long_description: If `pThis->iMsg` is already greater than `iMaxLine` (possible because the octet-count digit parsing path increments `iMsg` without a bounds check), the expression `iMaxLine - pThis->iMsg` produces a negative value (signed) or a very large value (unsigned). This corrupts `octatesToCopy` and `octatesToDiscard`, which are then used to control a `memcpy` and pointer arithmetic. Depending on signedness and types, this can lead to a large `memcpy` causing a heap buffer overflow, or incorrect pointer advancement causing further protocol desynchronization and memory corruption.

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0322: crash when no errors and 'quickfixtextfunc' is set

Problem:    Crash when no errors and 'quickfixtextfunc' is set.
Solution:   Do not handle errors if there aren't any.
cve_desc: Use After Free in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.0322.

commit_message:
gsocketclient: Fix criticals

This ensures the parent GTask is kept alive as long as an enumeration
is running and trying to connect.

Closes #1646
Closes #1649
cve_desc: gio/gsocketclient.c in GNOME GLib 2.59.2 does not ensure that a parent GTask remains alive during the execution of a connection-attempting enumeration, which allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (g_socket_client_connected_callback mishandling and application crash) via a crafted web site, as demonstrated by GNOME Web (aka Epiphany).

commit_message: Fix bug #79037 (global buffer-overflow in `mbfl_filt_conv_big5_wchar`)
cve_desc: When using certain mbstring functions to convert multibyte encodings, in PHP versions 7.2.x below 7.2.27, 7.3.x below 7.3.14 and 7.4.x below 7.4.2 it is possible to supply data that will cause function mbfl_filt_conv_big5_wchar to read past the allocated buffer. This may lead to information disclosure or crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read on cp950_pua_tbl when no matching entry is found
vulnerabilities.long_description: If the for-loop iterates through all entries of cp950_pua_tbl without finding a match (i.e., the break is never hit), k will equal the table size. The code then proceeds to access cp950_pua_tbl[k] without checking whether k is still within bounds, resulting in an out-of-bounds array read. This can lead to reading arbitrary memory, potentially causing a crash or information disclosure.

commit_message:
cgroup: verify that source is a string

The following sequence can be used to trigger a UAF:

    int fscontext_fd = fsopen("cgroup");
    int fd_null = open("/dev/null, O_RDONLY);
    int fsconfig(fscontext_fd, FSCONFIG_SET_FD, "source", fd_null);
    close_range(3, ~0U, 0);

The cgroup v1 specific fs parser expects a string for the "source"
parameter.  However, it is perfectly legitimate to e.g.  specify a file
descriptor for the "source" parameter.  The fs parser doesn't know what
a filesystem allows there.  So it's a bug to assume that "source" is
always of type fs_value_is_string when it can reasonably also be
fs_value_is_file.

This assumption in the cgroup code causes a UAF because struct
fs_parameter uses a union for the actual value.  Access to that union is
guarded by the param->type member.  Since the cgroup paramter parser
didn't check param->type but unconditionally moved param->string into
fc->source a close on the fscontext_fd would trigger a UAF during
put_fs_context() which frees fc->source thereby freeing the file stashed
in param->file causing a UAF during a close of the fd_null.

Fix this by verifying that param->type is actually a string and report
an error if not.

In follow up patches I'll add a new generic helper that can be used here
and by other filesystems instead of this error-prone copy-pasta fix.
But fixing it in here first makes backporting a it to stable a lot
easier.

Fixes: 8d2451f4994f ("cgroup1: switch to option-by-option parsing")
Reported-by: syzbot+283ce5a46486d6acdbaf@syzkaller.appspotmail.com
Cc: Christoph Hellwig <hch@lst.de>
Cc: Alexander Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
Cc: Dmitry Vyukov <dvyukov@google.com>
Cc: <stable@kernel.org>
Cc: syzkaller-bugs <syzkaller-bugs@googlegroups.com>
Signed-off-by: Christian Brauner <christian.brauner@ubuntu.com>
Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
cve_desc: A use-after-free flaw was found in cgroup1_parse_param in kernel/cgroup/cgroup-v1.c in the Linux kernel's cgroup v1 parser. A local attacker with a user privilege could cause a privilege escalation by exploiting the fsconfig syscall parameter leading to a container breakout and a denial of service on the system.

commit_message: imptcp: fix Segmentation Fault when octet count is to high
cve_desc: A denial of service vulnerability was found in rsyslog in the imptcp module. An attacker could send a specially crafted message to the imptcp socket, which would cause rsyslog to crash. Versions before 8.27.0 are vulnerable.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in octet count parsing allows unbounded multiplication
vulnerabilities.long_description: The octet count accumulator `iOctetsRemain` is multiplied by 10 and added to for each digit character received, with no upper bound check. If an attacker sends a very long sequence of digits, this value can overflow its integer type, wrapping around to a small or negative value. This could lead to incorrect framing, buffer over-reads or under-reads in the subsequent `memcpy` call in the octet-counting branch, or bypass of the `iOctetsRemain > iMaxLine` truncation check.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Invalid octet count (zero or negative) is logged but processing continues, leading to incorrect state
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `iOctetsRemain` is less than 1 (zero, or negative due to integer overflow), the code logs an error but does not abort or reset the input state. It falls through to set `inputState = eInMsg`. On the next data byte, the octet-counting branch is entered with `iOctetsRemain <= 0`. The condition `pThis->iOctetsRemain == 0` will immediately trigger message submission of potentially empty or stale buffer content. If `iOctetsRemain` is negative (from overflow), the subtraction `pThis->iOctetsRemain -= (octatesToCopy + octatesToDiscard)` will never reach zero, causing the session to never properly frame messages again, potentially leading to denial of service or data corruption.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential negative octatesToCopy passed to memcpy when iMsg already exceeds iMaxLine
vulnerabilities.long_description: If through repeated calls or due to the integer overflow in `iOctetsRemain`, `pThis->iMsg` has already reached or exceeded `iMaxLine`, the expression `iMaxLine - pThis->iMsg` becomes zero or negative. When assigned to the int `octatesToCopy`, a negative value is then passed to `memcpy` as a `size_t` parameter, which converts it to a very large unsigned value. This would cause a massive heap buffer overflow, allowing an attacker to overwrite adjacent memory, potentially achieving remote code execution.

commit_message: Fix bug #72750: wddx_deserialize null dereference
cve_desc: The php_wddx_pop_element function in ext/wddx/wddx.c in PHP before 5.6.25 and 7.x before 7.0.10 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (NULL pointer dereference and application crash) or possibly have unspecified other impact via an invalid base64 binary value, as demonstrated by a wddx_deserialize call that mishandles a binary element in a wddxPacket XML document.

vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference when ent2->data is NULL for non-ST_FIELD entry types
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code only guards against ent2->data being NULL when ent2->type is ST_FIELD. If ent2->data is NULL but ent2->type is any other value (e.g., ST_ARRAY, ST_STRUCT), the early return is skipped and the code proceeds to dereference ent2->data via Z_TYPE_P(ent2->data), causing a NULL pointer dereference. A crafted WDDX input with nested elements that lead to a non-ST_FIELD entry with NULL data on the stack could trigger a crash (denial of service).

vulnerabilities.short_description: Deserialization of untrusted data allows arbitrary class instantiation and __wakeup invocation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The WDDX deserialization code instantiates arbitrary PHP classes based on a class name supplied in the input (via the PHP_CLASS_NAME_VAR field), and also unconditionally calls __wakeup() on any deserialized object. An attacker who controls the WDDX input can instantiate any loaded class and trigger its constructor and __wakeup() method, potentially leading to arbitrary side effects, property injection, or further exploitation depending on available classes (POP chain gadgets).

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of ent1->data when ent2->data is neither array nor object
vulnerabilities.long_description: When ent2->data is not IS_ARRAY or IS_OBJECT, the entire block that inserts ent1->data into a target hash or frees it is skipped. The code only calls efree(ent1) but never calls zval_ptr_dtor on ent1->data, and ent1->varname is also not freed. This leaks the zval and potentially the varname string. A crafted WDDX payload with nested elements of unexpected types could trigger repeated memory leaks, leading to resource exhaustion.

commit_message:
tpm2: Initialize a whole OBJECT before using it

Initialize a whole OBJECT before using it. This is necessary since
an OBJECT may also be used as a HASH_OBJECT via the ANY_OBJECT
union and that HASH_OBJECT can leave bad size inidicators in TPM2B
buffer in the OBJECT. To get rid of this problem we reset the whole
OBJECT to 0 before using it. This is as if the memory for the
OBJECT was just initialized.

Signed-off-by: Stefan Berger <stefanb@linux.ibm.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in the libtpms code that may cause access beyond the boundary of internal buffers. The vulnerability is triggered by specially-crafted TPM2 command packets that then trigger the issue when the state of the TPM2's volatile state is written. The highest threat from this vulnerability is to system availability. This issue affects libtpms versions before 0.8.5, before 0.7.9 and before 0.6.6.

commit_message:
tpm2: Initialize a whole OBJECT before using it

Initialize a whole OBJECT before using it. This is necessary since
an OBJECT may also be used as a HASH_OBJECT via the ANY_OBJECT
union and that HASH_OBJECT can leave bad size inidicators in TPM2B
buffer in the OBJECT. To get rid of this problem we reset the whole
OBJECT to 0 before using it. This is as if the memory for the
OBJECT was just initialized.

Signed-off-by: Stefan Berger <stefanb@linux.ibm.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in the libtpms code that may cause access beyond the boundary of internal buffers. The vulnerability is triggered by specially-crafted TPM2 command packets that then trigger the issue when the state of the TPM2's volatile state is written. The highest threat from this vulnerability is to system availability. This issue affects libtpms versions before 0.8.5, before 0.7.9 and before 0.6.6.

commit_message: Fix bug #72750: wddx_deserialize null dereference
cve_desc: The php_wddx_pop_element function in ext/wddx/wddx.c in PHP before 5.6.25 and 7.x before 7.0.10 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (NULL pointer dereference and application crash) or possibly have unspecified other impact via an invalid base64 binary value, as demonstrated by a wddx_deserialize call that mishandles a binary element in a wddxPacket XML document.

vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference when php_base64_decode fails on binary element
vulnerabilities.long_description: If php_base64_decode() returns NULL (e.g., due to invalid base64 input), the result is assigned directly to Z_STRVAL_P(ent1->data) without any NULL check. The old string is already freed via STR_FREE at that point. Subsequent operations that access Z_STRVAL_P(ent1->data) (such as strlen, string comparisons, or zval destruction) will dereference a NULL pointer, leading to a crash (denial of service). An attacker can trigger this by providing malformed base64 data within a WDDX <binary> element.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Unsafe deserialization allows instantiation of arbitrary PHP classes from untrusted WDDX input
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code instantiates arbitrary PHP classes based on a class name extracted from the WDDX input (the PHP_CLASS_NAME_VAR field). An attacker can craft WDDX data specifying any class name, causing that class to be instantiated and its __wakeup() method to be called. This is a classic insecure deserialization vulnerability. Depending on which classes are available in the application (e.g., classes with destructors or __wakeup methods that perform dangerous operations), this can lead to arbitrary code execution, file manipulation, or other severe consequences.

vulnerabilities.short_description: __wakeup() callback can manipulate WDDX deserialization state, leading to use-after-free
vulnerabilities.long_description: After deserializing an object, __wakeup() is called via call_user_function_ex before the entry is fully processed and merged into the parent container. The __wakeup() method executes arbitrary user-defined PHP code which could manipulate or destroy zvals referenced by the stack (e.g., by triggering garbage collection, modifying global state, or re-entering the WDDX parser). After __wakeup() returns, the code continues to use ent1->data and ent2->data without re-validation, which could result in use-after-free conditions. An attacker who controls the WDDX input and has suitable PHP classes available could exploit this for code execution.

commit_message:
ax25: Fix UAF bugs in ax25 timers

There are race conditions that may lead to UAF bugs in
ax25_heartbeat_expiry(), ax25_t1timer_expiry(), ax25_t2timer_expiry(),
ax25_t3timer_expiry() and ax25_idletimer_expiry(), when we call
ax25_release() to deallocate ax25_dev.

One of the UAF bugs caused by ax25_release() is shown below:

      (Thread 1)                    |      (Thread 2)
ax25_dev_device_up() //(1)          |
...                                 | ax25_kill_by_device()
ax25_bind()          //(2)          |
ax25_connect()                      | ...
 ax25_std_establish_data_link()     |
  ax25_start_t1timer()              | ax25_dev_device_down() //(3)
   mod_timer(&ax25->t1timer,..)     |
                                    | ax25_release()
   (wait a time)                    |  ...
                                    |  ax25_dev_put(ax25_dev) //(4)FREE
   ax25_t1timer_expiry()            |
    ax25->ax25_dev->values[..] //USE|  ...
     ...                            |

We increase the refcount of ax25_dev in position (1) and (2), and
decrease the refcount of ax25_dev in position (3) and (4).
The ax25_dev will be freed in position (4) and be used in
ax25_t1timer_expiry().

The fail log is shown below:
==============================================================

[  106.116942] BUG: KASAN: use-after-free in ax25_t1timer_expiry+0x1c/0x60
[  106.116942] Read of size 8 at addr ffff88800bda9028 by task swapper/0/0
[  106.116942] CPU: 0 PID: 0 Comm: swapper/0 Not tainted 5.17.0-06123-g0905eec574
[  106.116942] Hardware name: QEMU Standard PC (i440FX + PIIX, 1996), BIOS rel-14
[  106.116942] Call Trace:
...
[  106.116942]  ax25_t1timer_expiry+0x1c/0x60
[  106.116942]  call_timer_fn+0x122/0x3d0
[  106.116942]  __run_timers.part.0+0x3f6/0x520
[  106.116942]  run_timer_softirq+0x4f/0xb0
[  106.116942]  __do_softirq+0x1c2/0x651
...

This patch adds del_timer_sync() in ax25_release(), which could ensure
that all timers stop before we deallocate ax25_dev.

Signed-off-by: Duoming Zhou <duoming@zju.edu.cn>
Signed-off-by: Paolo Abeni <pabeni@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A NULL pointer dereference flaw was found in the Linux kernel’s Amateur Radio AX.25 protocol functionality in the way a user connects with the protocol. This flaw allows a local user to crash the system.

vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference when ax25_dev is NULL in AX25_STATE_3/AX25_STATE_4
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the AX25_STATE_3 and AX25_STATE_4 cases, the code dereferences ax25->ax25_dev (via ax25->ax25_dev->values[AX25_VALUES_PROTOCOL]) without first checking that ax25_dev is non-NULL. Earlier in the function, ax25_dev is assigned from ax25->ax25_dev, but it is never checked for NULL before this dereference. If ax25_dev is NULL (e.g., the underlying device was removed), this results in a kernel NULL pointer dereference, causing a denial of service (kernel panic/oops). The NULL check for ax25_dev only occurs later, near the end of the function, for the dev_put_track/ax25_dev_put calls.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Race condition due to dropping and re-acquiring socket lock around ax25_disconnect
vulnerabilities.long_description: In states 0, 1, and 2, the code drops the socket lock via release_sock(sk), calls ax25_disconnect(ax25, 0), and then re-acquires the lock with lock_sock(sk). During the window when the lock is released, another thread or a network event could concurrently modify the ax25 control block state, free resources, or change ax25->sk flags. After re-acquiring the lock, the code proceeds to check flags (e.g., SOCK_DESTROY) or call ax25_destroy_socket without re-validating the state, which could lead to use-after-free or double-free conditions.

commit_message:
nfc: nfcmrvl: main: reorder destructive operations in nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev to avoid bugs

There are destructive operations such as nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_abort and
gpio_free in nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev. The resources such as firmware,
gpio and so on could be destructed while the upper layer functions such as
nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start and nfcmrvl_nci_recv_frame is executing, which leads
to double-free, use-after-free and null-ptr-deref bugs.

There are three situations that could lead to double-free bugs.

The first situation is shown below:

   (Thread 1)                 |      (Thread 2)
nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start         |
 ...                          |  nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev
 release_firmware()           |   nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_abort
  kfree(fw) //(1)             |    fw_dnld_over
                              |     release_firmware
  ...                         |      kfree(fw) //(2)
                              |     ...

The second situation is shown below:

   (Thread 1)                 |      (Thread 2)
nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start         |
 ...                          |
 mod_timer                    |
 (wait a time)                |
 fw_dnld_timeout              |  nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev
   fw_dnld_over               |   nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_abort
    release_firmware          |    fw_dnld_over
     kfree(fw) //(1)          |     release_firmware
     ...                      |      kfree(fw) //(2)

The third situation is shown below:

       (Thread 1)               |       (Thread 2)
nfcmrvl_nci_recv_frame          |
 if(..->fw_download_in_progress)|
  nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_recv_frame    |
   queue_work                   |
                                |
fw_dnld_rx_work                 | nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev
 fw_dnld_over                   |  nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_abort
  release_firmware              |   fw_dnld_over
   kfree(fw) //(1)              |    release_firmware
                                |     kfree(fw) //(2)

The firmware struct is deallocated in position (1) and deallocated
in position (2) again.

The crash trace triggered by POC is like below:

BUG: KASAN: double-free or invalid-free in fw_dnld_over
Call Trace:
  kfree
  fw_dnld_over
  nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev
  nci_uart_tty_close
  tty_ldisc_kill
  tty_ldisc_hangup
  __tty_hangup.part.0
  tty_release
  ...

What's more, there are also use-after-free and null-ptr-deref bugs
in nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start. If we deallocate firmware struct, gpio or
set null to the members of priv->fw_dnld in nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev,
then, we dereference firmware, gpio or the members of priv->fw_dnld in
nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start, the UAF or NPD bugs will happen.

This patch reorders destructive operations after nci_unregister_device
in order to synchronize between cleanup routine and firmware download
routine.

The nci_unregister_device is well synchronized. If the device is
detaching, the firmware download routine will goto error. If firmware
download routine is executing, nci_unregister_device will wait until
firmware download routine is finished.

Fixes: 3194c6870158 ("NFC: nfcmrvl: add firmware download support")
Signed-off-by: Duoming Zhou <duoming@zju.edu.cn>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: A flaw in Linux Kernel found in nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev() in drivers/nfc/nfcmrvl/main.c can lead to use after free both read or write when non synchronized between cleanup routine and firmware download routine.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Use-after-free: accessing nfc_dev after nci_unregister_device may have freed it
vulnerabilities.long_description: nci_unregister_device(ndev) is called first, which internally calls nfc_unregister_device() and can free the underlying nfc_dev structure. Immediately after, the code dereferences priv->ndev->nfc_dev->fw_download_in_progress. If nfc_dev has already been freed during unregistration, this is a use-after-free. An attacker who can trigger this code path (e.g., by removing an NFC device) could potentially exploit this to read or corrupt freed memory, leading to information disclosure, denial of service, or arbitrary code execution.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4563: "z=" in Visual mode may go beyond the end of the line

Problem:    "z=" in Visual mode may go beyond the end of the line.
Solution:   Adjust "badlen".
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow occurs in vim in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.4563.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Stack buffer overflow when appending remainder of bad word to suggestion in wcopy
vulnerabilities.long_description: The stack buffer `wcopy` is sized `MAXWLEN + 2`. The first `vim_strncpy` copies up to MAXWLEN bytes of `stp->st_word` into `wcopy`. Then, if `sug.su_badlen > stp->st_orglen`, additional bytes are appended at offset `stp->st_wordlen`. Since `stp->st_wordlen` can be up to MAXWLEN and `sug.su_badlen - stp->st_orglen` can be a significant additional length, the total write (`stp->st_wordlen + sug.su_badlen - stp->st_orglen`) can exceed `MAXWLEN + 2`, overflowing the stack buffer. An attacker who can craft a specially spelled document with specific word lengths could trigger this overflow, potentially achieving code execution.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4563: "z=" in Visual mode may go beyond the end of the line

Problem:    "z=" in Visual mode may go beyond the end of the line.
Solution:   Adjust "badlen".
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow occurs in vim in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.4563.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Stack buffer overflow when appending remainder of bad word to suggestion in wcopy
vulnerabilities.long_description: The stack buffer `wcopy` has size MAXWLEN + 2. The code first copies `stp->st_word` (up to MAXWLEN bytes) into `wcopy`, then if `sug.su_badlen > stp->st_orglen`, it appends additional bytes at offset `stp->st_wordlen`. If `stp->st_wordlen` is close to MAXWLEN and the remainder (`sug.su_badlen - stp->st_orglen`) is non-trivial, the total written data (`st_wordlen + su_badlen - st_orglen`) can exceed MAXWLEN + 2, causing a stack buffer overflow. An attacker who can craft a specially-formed spell file or document with specific word lengths could exploit this to overwrite stack data, potentially achieving code execution.

commit_message: Fix bug #79037 (global buffer-overflow in `mbfl_filt_conv_big5_wchar`)
cve_desc: When using certain mbstring functions to convert multibyte encodings, in PHP versions 7.2.x below 7.2.27, 7.3.x below 7.3.14 and 7.4.x below 7.4.2 it is possible to supply data that will cause function mbfl_filt_conv_big5_wchar to read past the allocated buffer. This may lead to information disclosure or crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read on cp950_pua_tbl when loop completes without finding a match
vulnerabilities.long_description: If the for-loop iterating over cp950_pua_tbl completes without breaking (i.e., no entry matches c2), k will equal sizeof(cp950_pua_tbl)/(sizeof(unsigned short)*4), which is one past the last valid index. The code then accesses cp950_pua_tbl[k][2], cp950_pua_tbl[k][0], and cp950_pua_tbl[k][3] without checking whether k is still in bounds. This results in an out-of-bounds read, which could lead to information disclosure or a crash. While is_in_cp950_pua() is intended to pre-filter values, if there is any inconsistency between is_in_cp950_pua() and the table contents, this out-of-bounds access will occur.

commit_message:
gsocketclient: Fix criticals

This ensures the parent GTask is kept alive as long as an enumeration
is running and trying to connect.

Closes #1646
Closes #1649
cve_desc: gio/gsocketclient.c in GNOME GLib 2.59.2 does not ensure that a parent GTask remains alive during the execution of a connection-attempting enumeration, which allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (g_socket_client_connected_callback mishandling and application crash) via a crafted web site, as demonstrated by GNOME Web (aka Epiphany).

vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference on `data` in the successful connection path
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `data` (which is `attempt->data`) can be NULL, as indicated by the comment 'data is NULL once the task is completed' and the explicit NULL check in the error-handling path (`if (data) { ... } else connection_attempt_unref(attempt);`). However, in the successful connection path (after `g_socket_connection_connect_finish` succeeds), `data` is dereferenced unconditionally (e.g., `data->socket`, `data->connection`, `data->connection_attempts`, etc.) without a NULL check. If the task completes and sets `data` to NULL between the initial cancellation check and the success path, dereferencing it will cause a crash. This could be triggered in a race condition where the task is finalized concurrently, leading to a denial of service.

commit_message:
cgroup: verify that source is a string

The following sequence can be used to trigger a UAF:

    int fscontext_fd = fsopen("cgroup");
    int fd_null = open("/dev/null, O_RDONLY);
    int fsconfig(fscontext_fd, FSCONFIG_SET_FD, "source", fd_null);
    close_range(3, ~0U, 0);

The cgroup v1 specific fs parser expects a string for the "source"
parameter.  However, it is perfectly legitimate to e.g.  specify a file
descriptor for the "source" parameter.  The fs parser doesn't know what
a filesystem allows there.  So it's a bug to assume that "source" is
always of type fs_value_is_string when it can reasonably also be
fs_value_is_file.

This assumption in the cgroup code causes a UAF because struct
fs_parameter uses a union for the actual value.  Access to that union is
guarded by the param->type member.  Since the cgroup paramter parser
didn't check param->type but unconditionally moved param->string into
fc->source a close on the fscontext_fd would trigger a UAF during
put_fs_context() which frees fc->source thereby freeing the file stashed
in param->file causing a UAF during a close of the fd_null.

Fix this by verifying that param->type is actually a string and report
an error if not.

In follow up patches I'll add a new generic helper that can be used here
and by other filesystems instead of this error-prone copy-pasta fix.
But fixing it in here first makes backporting a it to stable a lot
easier.

Fixes: 8d2451f4994f ("cgroup1: switch to option-by-option parsing")
Reported-by: syzbot+283ce5a46486d6acdbaf@syzkaller.appspotmail.com
Cc: Christoph Hellwig <hch@lst.de>
Cc: Alexander Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
Cc: Dmitry Vyukov <dvyukov@google.com>
Cc: <stable@kernel.org>
Cc: syzkaller-bugs <syzkaller-bugs@googlegroups.com>
Signed-off-by: Christian Brauner <christian.brauner@ubuntu.com>
Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
cve_desc: A use-after-free flaw was found in cgroup1_parse_param in kernel/cgroup/cgroup-v1.c in the Linux kernel's cgroup v1 parser. A local attacker with a user privilege could cause a privilege escalation by exploiting the fsconfig syscall parameter leading to a container breakout and a denial of service on the system.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow via signed left shift when subsystem index is large
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expression `(1 << i)` uses a signed integer literal `1` (type `int`, typically 32 bits). If the subsystem index `i` reaches 31, the shift `1 << 31` is undefined behavior in C because it shifts a 1-bit into the sign bit of a signed integer. For values of `i` >= 32 (if there were ever that many subsystems), the behavior is also undefined. This could lead to incorrect subsys_mask values, potentially enabling unintended controller attachments or other unexpected behavior. The fix is to use `1U << i` or `1UL << i` to ensure an unsigned shift operation.

commit_message:
ax25: Fix UAF bugs in ax25 timers

There are race conditions that may lead to UAF bugs in
ax25_heartbeat_expiry(), ax25_t1timer_expiry(), ax25_t2timer_expiry(),
ax25_t3timer_expiry() and ax25_idletimer_expiry(), when we call
ax25_release() to deallocate ax25_dev.

One of the UAF bugs caused by ax25_release() is shown below:

      (Thread 1)                    |      (Thread 2)
ax25_dev_device_up() //(1)          |
...                                 | ax25_kill_by_device()
ax25_bind()          //(2)          |
ax25_connect()                      | ...
 ax25_std_establish_data_link()     |
  ax25_start_t1timer()              | ax25_dev_device_down() //(3)
   mod_timer(&ax25->t1timer,..)     |
                                    | ax25_release()
   (wait a time)                    |  ...
                                    |  ax25_dev_put(ax25_dev) //(4)FREE
   ax25_t1timer_expiry()            |
    ax25->ax25_dev->values[..] //USE|  ...
     ...                            |

We increase the refcount of ax25_dev in position (1) and (2), and
decrease the refcount of ax25_dev in position (3) and (4).
The ax25_dev will be freed in position (4) and be used in
ax25_t1timer_expiry().

The fail log is shown below:
==============================================================

[  106.116942] BUG: KASAN: use-after-free in ax25_t1timer_expiry+0x1c/0x60
[  106.116942] Read of size 8 at addr ffff88800bda9028 by task swapper/0/0
[  106.116942] CPU: 0 PID: 0 Comm: swapper/0 Not tainted 5.17.0-06123-g0905eec574
[  106.116942] Hardware name: QEMU Standard PC (i440FX + PIIX, 1996), BIOS rel-14
[  106.116942] Call Trace:
...
[  106.116942]  ax25_t1timer_expiry+0x1c/0x60
[  106.116942]  call_timer_fn+0x122/0x3d0
[  106.116942]  __run_timers.part.0+0x3f6/0x520
[  106.116942]  run_timer_softirq+0x4f/0xb0
[  106.116942]  __do_softirq+0x1c2/0x651
...

This patch adds del_timer_sync() in ax25_release(), which could ensure
that all timers stop before we deallocate ax25_dev.

Signed-off-by: Duoming Zhou <duoming@zju.edu.cn>
Signed-off-by: Paolo Abeni <pabeni@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A NULL pointer dereference flaw was found in the Linux kernel’s Amateur Radio AX.25 protocol functionality in the way a user connects with the protocol. This flaw allows a local user to crash the system.

vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference when ax25_dev is NULL in STATE_3/STATE_4
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the AX25_STATE_3 and AX25_STATE_4 cases, the code dereferences ax25->ax25_dev->values[] without first checking whether ax25_dev is NULL. While ax25_dev is assigned from ax25->ax25_dev at the top of the function, there is no guarantee it is non-NULL. If ax25_dev is NULL, this dereference will cause a kernel NULL pointer dereference / oops. An unprivileged user who can create and manipulate an AX.25 socket could potentially trigger this to cause a denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Use-after-free: ax25 structure accessed after ax25_destroy_socket() frees it
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the AX25_STATE_0, AX25_STATE_1/2, and the else (datagram) branches, ax25_destroy_socket(ax25) is called which can free the ax25 control block. Execution then falls through to the code after the switch/if-else that accesses ax25->timer, ax25->t1timer, ax25->t2timer, ax25->t3timer, and ax25->idletimer via del_timer_sync(). If ax25_destroy_socket freed the ax25_cb structure, these accesses constitute a use-after-free. An attacker could potentially exploit this to achieve code execution or cause a kernel crash.

commit_message: LibVNCClient: really fix #250
cve_desc: LibVNC before commit 7b1ef0ffc4815cab9a96c7278394152bdc89dc4d contains heap out-of-bound write vulnerability inside structure in VNC client code that can result remote code execution

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in subrectangle count multiplication can bypass buffer size check
vulnerabilities.long_description: hdr.nSubrects is a uint32_t from the network. The multiplication `hdr.nSubrects * (4 + (BPP / 8))` can overflow, producing a small value that passes the `> RFB_BUFFER_SIZE` check. This causes ReadFromRFBServer to read a small (truncated) amount into client->buffer, but the subsequent loop iterates hdr.nSubrects times, reading far beyond the buffer bounds. This leads to an out-of-bounds read and potentially exploitable memory corruption when calling GotFillRect with attacker-controlled data.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read via ptr advancing beyond buffer when integer overflow bypasses size check
vulnerabilities.long_description: When the buffer size check is bypassed due to integer overflow, the loop iterates up to hdr.nSubrects times, advancing ptr by (4 + BPP/8) bytes each iteration. Since the actual data read into client->buffer is much smaller than expected, ptr will quickly go past the end of the buffer, reading arbitrary heap memory. The values read are then passed to GotFillRect, potentially causing further damage.

commit_message:
rgw: reject control characters in response-header actions

S3 GetObject permits overriding response header values, but those inputs
need to be validated to insure only characters that are valid in an HTTP
header value are present.

Credit: Initial vulnerability discovery by William Bowling (@wcbowling)
Credit: Further vulnerability discovery by Robin H. Johnson <rjohnson@digitalocean.com>
Signed-off-by: Robin H. Johnson <rjohnson@digitalocean.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in the Ceph Object Gateway, where it supports request sent by an anonymous user in Amazon S3. This flaw could lead to potential XSS attacks due to the lack of proper neutralization of untrusted input.
