[15840.675016] Code: 00 00 75 10 83 3d 88 2f ec c0 02 75 07 89 d0 e8 12 56 05 00 5d c3 55 ba 06 00 00 00 89 e5 53 89 c3 b8 3d eb 7e c0 e8 16 74 00 00 <8b> 03 c1 e8 1e 69 c0 d8 02 00 00 05 b8 69 8e c0 2b 80 c4 02 00
[15840.675016] EIP: [<c0116a4f>] kmap+0x15/0x56 SS:ESP 0068:cab0bc94
[15840.675016] ---[ end trace 4fea40dad6b70e5f ]---

This happens because the return value of read_mapping_page() is passed on
to kmap unchecked.  The bug is triggered after the first
read_mapping_page() in hfsplus_block_allocate(), this patch fixes all
three usages in this functions but leaves the ones further down in the
file unchanged.

Signed-off-by: Eric Sesterhenn <snakebyte@gmx.de>
Cc: Roman Zippel <zippel@linux-m68k.org>
Signed-off-by: Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org>
Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
cve_desc: The hfsplus_block_allocate function in fs/hfsplus/bitmap.c in the Linux kernel before 2.6.28-rc1 does not check a certain return value from the read_mapping_page function before calling kmap, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service (system crash) via a crafted hfsplus filesystem image.

code_snippets:
page = read_mapping_page(mapping, offset / PAGE_CACHE_BITS, NULL);
	pptr = kmap(page);
vulnerabilities.short_description: Return value of read_mapping_page() is never checked for errors, leading to potential NULL/invalid pointer dereference.
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function calls read_mapping_page() three times but never checks if the returned value is an error pointer (IS_ERR(page)). read_mapping_page() can fail and return an ERR_PTR value (e.g., -EIO, -ENOMEM). Passing such an error pointer directly to kmap() and subsequently dereferencing the mapped memory will cause a kernel oops/panic. An attacker who can trigger I/O errors on the underlying storage (e.g., via a crafted or corrupted HFS+ filesystem image) can exploit this to crash the kernel, resulting in a denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: When 'size' is a multiple of PAGE_CACHE_BITS, the end pointer calculation yields zero offset, causing end == pptr and potential out-of-bounds access.
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expression ((size + 31) & (PAGE_CACHE_BITS - 1)) computes to near-zero when 'size' is a multiple of PAGE_CACHE_BITS (or close to it), resulting in 'end' being set equal to or very close to 'pptr'. This means the scanning loops may incorrectly skip all entries on the last page or, in certain edge cases combined with 'curr' already being beyond 'end', lead to out-of-bounds memory reads and writes on the kmapped page. A crafted filesystem with specific size parameters could trigger this condition.

commit_message:
gzip -d: do not clobber stack for valid input on x86_64

* unlzw.c (unlzw): Avoid integer overflow.
Aki Helin reported the segfault along with an input to trigger the bug.
* NEWS (Bug fixes): Mention it.
cve_desc: Integer underflow in the unlzw function in unlzw.c in gzip before 1.4 on 64-bit platforms, as used in ncompress and probably others, allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (application crash) or possibly execute arbitrary code via a crafted archive that uses LZW compression, leading to an array index error.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Stack buffer underflow via unbounded prefix chain traversal
vulnerabilities.long_description: The while loop that walks the LZW prefix chain has no bounds check on `stackp`. A maliciously crafted compressed stream could create a very long chain (or a cycle) in the prefix table, causing `stackp` to be decremented past the beginning of `de_stack`. This leads to an out-of-bounds write before the buffer, potentially allowing arbitrary memory corruption and code execution. There is no check that `stackp` remains within the bounds of the `de_stack` array, nor is there cycle detection to prevent infinite traversal of a corrupt table.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds write to outbuf when oldcode is -1
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `oldcode == -1` (the first code), the function writes to `outbuf[outpos++]` without first checking whether `outpos` has reached `OUTBUFSIZ`. While this is the initial code case, repeated CLEAR codes followed by new initial codes (in block mode) could increment `outpos` past the buffer boundary without the overflow check that exists in the main output path, leading to a heap buffer overflow.

commit_message:
cadence_gem: switch to use qemu_receive_packet() for loopback

This patch switches to use qemu_receive_packet() which can detect
reentrancy and return early.

This is intended to address CVE-2021-3416.

Cc: Prasad J Pandit <ppandit@redhat.com>
Cc: qemu-stable@nongnu.org
Reviewed-by: Philippe Mathieu-Daudé <philmd@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: Alexander Bulekov <alxndr@bu.edu>
Signed-off-by: Jason Wang <jasowang@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A potential stack overflow via infinite loop issue was found in various NIC emulators of QEMU in versions up to and including 5.2.0. The issue occurs in loopback mode of a NIC wherein reentrant DMA checks get bypassed. A guest user/process may use this flaw to consume CPU cycles or crash the QEMU process on the host resulting in DoS scenario.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Inconsistent descriptor size used for desc_first read/write vs. other descriptor accesses, causing out-of-bounds DMA access.
vulnerabilities.long_description: When reading/writing the first descriptor of a completed packet (desc_first), the code uses sizeof(desc_first) which is DESC_MAX_NUM_WORDS * 4 bytes. All other descriptor reads in the function correctly use gem_get_desc_len(s, false) * 4 bytes. When gem_get_desc_len() returns a value smaller than DESC_MAX_NUM_WORDS, the desc_first operations read and write more DMA memory than the actual descriptor size, potentially corrupting adjacent descriptors or other DMA-accessible memory. A malicious guest could potentially leverage this to corrupt adjacent data structures in DMA memory.

commit_message:
Remove 'umask' calls from 'mkdir'.

Fixes <http://bugs.gnu.org/24659>.

* libguile/filesys.c (SCM_DEFINE): Remove calls to 'umask' when MODE is
unbound; instead, use 0777 as the mode.  Update docstring to clarify
this.
* doc/ref/posix.texi (File System): Adjust accordingly.
* NEWS: Mention it.
cve_desc: The mkdir procedure of GNU Guile temporarily changed the process' umask to zero. During that time window, in a multithreaded application, other threads could end up creating files with insecure permissions. For example, mkdir without the optional mode argument would create directories as 0777. This is fixed in Guile 2.0.13. Prior versions are affected.

commit_message:
sftp: Fix bug in sftp_mkdir not returning on error.

resolves: #84
(cherry picked from commit a92c97b2e17715c1b3cdd693d14af6c3311d8e44)
cve_desc: Multiple double free vulnerabilities in the (1) agent_sign_data function in agent.c, (2) channel_request function in channels.c, (3) ssh_userauth_pubkey function in auth.c, (4) sftp_parse_attr_3 function in sftp.c, and (5) try_publickey_from_file function in keyfiles.c in libssh before 0.5.3 allow remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) and possibly execute arbitrary code via unspecified vectors.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Double free of buffer and path when buffer construction fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: When any of the buffer_add_* or sftp_packet_write calls fail, the code frees both `buffer` and `path` inside the if-block, then falls through to free them again unconditionally. This is a double-free vulnerability that can lead to heap corruption, crashes, or potentially arbitrary code execution. Additionally, the code does not return after the error, so it proceeds to the while loop attempting to read a response for a packet that was never (fully) sent, which is a logic error.

commit_message:
Fix possible NULL dereference (savannah bug #55369)

* src/sparse.c (pax_decode_header): Check return from find_next_block.
cve_desc: pax_decode_header in sparse.c in GNU Tar before 1.32 had a NULL pointer dereference when parsing certain archives that have malformed extended headers.

vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference when find_next_block() returns NULL
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function calls find_next_block() in multiple places (both directly and inside the COPY_BUF macro) without checking if the return value is NULL. If the archive is truncated or malformed such that find_next_block() returns NULL, the subsequent dereferences (e.g., b->buffer, blk->buffer) will cause a NULL pointer dereference, leading to a crash. An attacker can craft a malicious sparse tar archive that triggers this condition, causing a denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Attacker-controlled allocation size can cause excessive memory consumption
vulnerabilities.long_description: The sparse_map_size is read directly from the archive data and validated only against TYPE_MAXIMUM(size_t). An attacker can craft an archive specifying a very large sparse_map_size value, causing xcalloc to attempt allocating a massive amount of memory. Depending on the xcalloc implementation, this could either exhaust memory (denial of service) or abort the program. There is no reasonable upper bound check on the map size before the allocation.

commit_message:
Remove 'umask' calls from 'mkdir'.

Fixes <http://bugs.gnu.org/24659>.

* libguile/filesys.c (SCM_DEFINE): Remove calls to 'umask' when MODE is
unbound; instead, use 0777 as the mode.  Update docstring to clarify
this.
* doc/ref/posix.texi (File System): Adjust accordingly.
* NEWS: Mention it.
cve_desc: The mkdir procedure of GNU Guile temporarily changed the process' umask to zero. During that time window, in a multithreaded application, other threads could end up creating files with insecure permissions. For example, mkdir without the optional mode argument would create directories as 0777. This is fixed in Guile 2.0.13. Prior versions are affected.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Race condition due to non-atomic umask read-restore pattern
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code reads the current umask by calling umask(0) (which sets it to 0 and returns the old value) and then immediately restores it with umask(mask). Between these two calls, the umask is momentarily set to 0. In a multi-threaded program, any other thread creating files or directories during this window will do so with no umask applied, potentially creating world-readable/writable files. The umask is a process-wide resource, so this race affects all threads.

commit_message:
Submitted by: Tomas Hoger <thoger@redhat.com>

Fix for CVE-2010-0433 where some kerberos enabled versions of OpenSSL
could be crashed if the relevant tables were not present (e.g. chrooted).
cve_desc: The kssl_keytab_is_available function in ssl/kssl.c in OpenSSL before 0.9.8n, when Kerberos is enabled but Kerberos configuration files cannot be opened, does not check a certain return value, which allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (NULL pointer dereference and daemon crash) via SSL cipher negotiation, as demonstrated by a chroot installation of Dovecot or stunnel without Kerberos configuration files inside the chroot.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Keytab reported as available when the key entry is actually not found
vulnerabilities.long_description: When krb5_kt_get_entry returns KRB5_KT_NOTFOUND (meaning the requested principal was not found in the keytab), the function sets rc = 1 and returns, indicating the keytab IS available. This is logically inverted — a 'not found' result should cause the function to return 0 (not available). This incorrect return value could cause callers to proceed with Kerberos authentication under the false assumption that a valid keytab entry exists, potentially leading to authentication failures, denial of service, or security policy bypass depending on how the return value is consumed.

commit_message:
Submitted by: Tomas Hoger <thoger@redhat.com>

Fix for CVE-2010-0433 where some kerberos enabled versions of OpenSSL
could be crashed if the relevant tables were not present (e.g. chrooted).
cve_desc: The kssl_keytab_is_available function in ssl/kssl.c in OpenSSL before 0.9.8n, when Kerberos is enabled but Kerberos configuration files cannot be opened, does not check a certain return value, which allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (NULL pointer dereference and daemon crash) via SSL cipher negotiation, as demonstrated by a chroot installation of Dovecot or stunnel without Kerberos configuration files inside the chroot.

code_snippets:
krb5rc = krb5_sname_to_principal(krb5context, NULL, 
                                     kssl_ctx->service_name ? kssl_ctx->service_name: KRB5SVC,
                                     KRB5_NT_SRV_HST, &princ);

    krb5rc = krb5_kt_get_entry(krb5context, krb5keytab, 
                                princ,
vulnerabilities.short_description: Return value of krb5_sname_to_principal is not checked before using princ
vulnerabilities.long_description: The return code from krb5_sname_to_principal() is assigned to krb5rc but never checked before princ is passed to krb5_kt_get_entry(). If krb5_sname_to_principal() fails, princ may be NULL or in an undefined state, leading to a NULL pointer dereference or use of an invalid pointer in the subsequent krb5_kt_get_entry() call. An attacker who can influence the service name or Kerberos configuration could trigger this failure path.

vulnerabilities.short_description: KRB5_KT_NOTFOUND incorrectly treated as keytab being available (rc=1)
vulnerabilities.long_description: When krb5_kt_get_entry() returns KRB5_KT_NOTFOUND (meaning the requested key was not found in the keytab), the function sets rc=1 indicating the keytab is available. This is a logic error — 'not found' should indicate the keytab entry is NOT available (rc=0). This could cause callers to proceed as if valid Kerberos keytab credentials exist when they do not, potentially leading to authentication bypass or unexpected behavior in the TLS/Kerberos handshake.

commit_message:
Prevent over long nonces in ChaCha20-Poly1305

ChaCha20-Poly1305 is an AEAD cipher, and requires a unique nonce input for
every encryption operation. RFC 7539 specifies that the nonce value (IV)
should be 96 bits (12 bytes). OpenSSL allows a variable nonce length and
front pads the nonce with 0 bytes if it is less than 12 bytes. However it
also incorrectly allows a nonce to be set of up to 16 bytes. In this case
only the last 12 bytes are significant and any additional leading bytes are
ignored.

It is a requirement of using this cipher that nonce values are unique.
Messages encrypted using a reused nonce value are susceptible to serious
confidentiality and integrity attacks. If an application changes the
default nonce length to be longer than 12 bytes and then makes a change to
the leading bytes of the nonce expecting the new value to be a new unique
nonce then such an application could inadvertently encrypt messages with a
reused nonce.

Additionally the ignored bytes in a long nonce are not covered by the
integrity guarantee of this cipher. Any application that relies on the
integrity of these ignored leading bytes of a long nonce may be further
affected.

Any OpenSSL internal use of this cipher, including in SSL/TLS, is safe
because no such use sets such a long nonce value. However user
applications that use this cipher directly and set a non-default nonce
length to be longer than 12 bytes may be vulnerable.

CVE-2019-1543

Fixes #8345

Reviewed-by: Paul Dale <paul.dale@oracle.com>
Reviewed-by: Richard Levitte <levitte@openssl.org>
(Merged from https://github.com/openssl/openssl/pull/8406)

(cherry picked from commit 2a3d0ee9d59156c48973592331404471aca886d6)
cve_desc: ChaCha20-Poly1305 is an AEAD cipher, and requires a unique nonce input for every encryption operation. RFC 7539 specifies that the nonce value (IV) should be 96 bits (12 bytes). OpenSSL allows a variable nonce length and front pads the nonce with 0 bytes if it is less than 12 bytes. However it also incorrectly allows a nonce to be set of up to 16 bytes. In this case only the last 12 bytes are significant and any additional leading bytes are ignored. It is a requirement of using this cipher that nonce values are unique. Messages encrypted using a reused nonce value are susceptible to serious confidentiality and integrity attacks. If an application changes the default nonce length to be longer than 12 bytes and then makes a change to the leading bytes of the nonce expecting the new value to be a new unique nonce then such an application could inadvertently encrypt messages with a reused nonce. Additionally the ignored bytes in a long nonce are not covered by the integrity guarantee of this cipher. Any application that relies on the integrity of these ignored leading bytes of a long nonce may be further affected. Any OpenSSL internal use of this cipher, including in SSL/TLS, is safe because no such use sets such a long nonce value. However user applications that use this cipher directly and set a non-default nonce length to be longer than 12 bytes may be vulnerable. OpenSSL versions 1.1.1 and 1.1.0 are affected by this issue. Due to the limited scope of affected deployments this has been assessed as low severity and therefore we are not creating new releases at this time. Fixed in OpenSSL 1.1.1c (Affected 1.1.1-1.1.1b). Fixed in OpenSSL 1.1.0k (Affected 1.1.0-1.1.0j).

commit_message:
grep -F: fix a heap buffer (read) overrun

grep's read buffer is often filled to its full size, except when
reading the final buffer of a file.  In that case, the number of
bytes read may be far less than the size of the buffer.  However, for
certain unusual pattern/text combinations, grep -F would mistakenly
examine bytes in that uninitialized region of memory when searching
for a match.  With carefully chosen inputs, one can cause grep -F to
read beyond the end of that buffer altogether.  This problem arose via
commit v2.18-90-g73893ff with the introduction of a more efficient
heuristic using what is now the memchr_kwset function. The use of
that function in bmexec_trans could leave TP much larger than EP,
and the subsequent call to bm_delta2_search would mistakenly access
beyond end of the main input read buffer.

* src/kwset.c (bmexec_trans): When TP reaches or exceeds EP,
do not call bm_delta2_search.
* tests/kwset-abuse: New file.
* tests/Makefile.am (TESTS): Add it.
* THANKS.in: Update.
* NEWS (Bug fixes): Mention it.

Prior to this patch, this command would trigger a UMR:

  printf %0360db 0 | valgrind src/grep -F $(printf %019dXb 0)

  Use of uninitialised value of size 8
     at 0x4142BE: bmexec_trans (kwset.c:657)
     by 0x4143CA: bmexec (kwset.c:678)
     by 0x414973: kwsexec (kwset.c:848)
     by 0x414DC4: Fexecute (kwsearch.c:128)
     by 0x404E2E: grepbuf (grep.c:1238)
     by 0x4054BF: grep (grep.c:1417)
     by 0x405CEB: grepdesc (grep.c:1645)
     by 0x405EC1: grep_command_line_arg (grep.c:1692)
     by 0x4077D4: main (grep.c:2570)

See the accompanying test for how to trigger the heap buffer overrun.

Thanks to Nima Aghdaii for testing and finding numerous
ways to break early iterations of this patch.
cve_desc: The bmexec_trans function in kwset.c in grep 2.19 through 2.21 allows local users to cause a denial of service (out-of-bounds heap read and crash) via crafted input when using the -F option.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer type mismatch: storing size_t value in int may cause truncation and bypass size checks
vulnerabilities.long_description: kwset->mind is likely a size_t or unsigned type, but it's stored in 'int len'. If kwset->mind is larger than INT_MAX, the value could wrap to a negative number. A negative 'len' would pass the 'len > size' check (since size is unsigned, the comparison promotes len to unsigned, but the initial assignment already lost information). This could lead to incorrect pointer arithmetic with 'tp = text + len' and subsequent out-of-bounds memory reads during the Boyer-Moore search, potentially causing information disclosure or crashes.

[15840.675016] Code: 00 00 75 10 83 3d 88 2f ec c0 02 75 07 89 d0 e8 12 56 05 00 5d c3 55 ba 06 00 00 00 89 e5 53 89 c3 b8 3d eb 7e c0 e8 16 74 00 00 <8b> 03 c1 e8 1e 69 c0 d8 02 00 00 05 b8 69 8e c0 2b 80 c4 02 00
[15840.675016] EIP: [<c0116a4f>] kmap+0x15/0x56 SS:ESP 0068:cab0bc94
[15840.675016] ---[ end trace 4fea40dad6b70e5f ]---

This happens because the return value of read_mapping_page() is passed on
to kmap unchecked.  The bug is triggered after the first
read_mapping_page() in hfsplus_block_allocate(), this patch fixes all
three usages in this functions but leaves the ones further down in the
file unchanged.

Signed-off-by: Eric Sesterhenn <snakebyte@gmx.de>
Cc: Roman Zippel <zippel@linux-m68k.org>
Signed-off-by: Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org>
Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
cve_desc: The hfsplus_block_allocate function in fs/hfsplus/bitmap.c in the Linux kernel before 2.6.28-rc1 does not check a certain return value from the read_mapping_page function before calling kmap, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service (system crash) via a crafted hfsplus filesystem image.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds write in allocation loop due to missing size boundary check for 'end'
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the block allocation phase (the second while(1) loop after the 'found' label), when a new page is mapped, 'end' is unconditionally set to 'pptr + PAGE_CACHE_BITS / 32' without checking if the remaining filesystem bitmap (bounded by 'size') ends before the full page. In contrast, the initial search loop correctly computes 'end' by checking '(size ^ offset) / PAGE_CACHE_BITS' and adjusting 'end' when on the last page. This omission means the allocation loop can read and write u32 entries beyond the valid bitmap region in the last page, corrupting adjacent data in the page cache. A specially crafted or corrupted HFS+ filesystem image with specific size/offset values could trigger this, leading to memory corruption in kernel space.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Incorrect 'end' calculation when size is a multiple of PAGE_CACHE_BITS results in zero-length scan range
vulnerabilities.long_description: When 'size' is an exact multiple of PAGE_CACHE_BITS, the expression '(size + 31) & (PAGE_CACHE_BITS - 1)' evaluates to 31, and 31 / 32 equals 0 in integer division, so 'end' becomes equal to 'pptr'. This means the scanning loop will skip the last page entirely when the bitmap size aligns to a page boundary, potentially causing the function to report the bitmap as full when free blocks exist, or more critically, causing 'curr' to exceed 'end' on a prior iteration leading to out-of-bounds reads from the mapped page.

commit_message:
Add a test for GENERAL_NAME_cmp

Based on a boringssl test contributed by David Benjamin

Reviewed-by: Tomas Mraz <tmraz@fedoraproject.org>
cve_desc: The X.509 GeneralName type is a generic type for representing different types of names. One of those name types is known as EDIPartyName. OpenSSL provides a function GENERAL_NAME_cmp which compares different instances of a GENERAL_NAME to see if they are equal or not. This function behaves incorrectly when both GENERAL_NAMEs contain an EDIPARTYNAME. A NULL pointer dereference and a crash may occur leading to a possible denial of service attack. OpenSSL itself uses the GENERAL_NAME_cmp function for two purposes: 1) Comparing CRL distribution point names between an available CRL and a CRL distribution point embedded in an X509 certificate 2) When verifying that a timestamp response token signer matches the timestamp authority name (exposed via the API functions TS_RESP_verify_response and TS_RESP_verify_token) If an attacker can control both items being compared then that attacker could trigger a crash. For example if the attacker can trick a client or server into checking a malicious certificate against a malicious CRL then this may occur. Note that some applications automatically download CRLs based on a URL embedded in a certificate. This checking happens prior to the signatures on the certificate and CRL being verified. OpenSSL's s_server, s_client and verify tools have support for the "-crl_download" option which implements automatic CRL downloading and this attack has been demonstrated to work against those tools. Note that an unrelated bug means that affected versions of OpenSSL cannot parse or construct correct encodings of EDIPARTYNAME. However it is possible to construct a malformed EDIPARTYNAME that OpenSSL's parser will accept and hence trigger this attack. All OpenSSL 1.1.1 and 1.0.2 versions are affected by this issue. Other OpenSSL releases are out of support and have not been checked. Fixed in OpenSSL 1.1.1i (Affected 1.1.1-1.1.1h). Fixed in OpenSSL 1.0.2x (Affected 1.0.2-1.0.2w).

commit_message:
lib-smtp: smtp-command-parser - Fix infinite loop occurring when command stream ends in UTF-8 character.

Broken by 8f08f1944be438a2422b604c08e5060b5c7bd72f.
cve_desc: lib-smtp in submission-login and lmtp in Dovecot 2.3.9 before 2.3.9.3 mishandles truncated UTF-8 data in command parameters, as demonstrated by the unauthenticated triggering of a submission-login infinite loop.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer underflow in size calculation passed to uni_utf8_get_char_n leads to potential out-of-bounds read
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expression `(size_t)(p - parser->end)` computes the remaining buffer length incorrectly. Since the loop condition guarantees `p < parser->end`, the subtraction `p - parser->end` yields a negative value. When cast to `size_t` (an unsigned type), this wraps around to an extremely large value. This tells `uni_utf8_get_char_n` that there is far more data available than actually exists, which could cause it to read well beyond the end of the allocated buffer. The correct expression should be `(size_t)(parser->end - p)`. An attacker sending crafted SMTP command parameters containing multi-byte UTF-8 sequences could trigger out-of-bounds memory reads, potentially leaking sensitive information or causing a crash.

commit_message:
sctp: correct bounds check in sctp_setsockopt_auth_key

The bonds check to prevent buffer overlflow was not exactly
right.  It still allowed overflow of up to 8 bytes which is
sizeof(struct sctp_authkey).

Since optlen is already checked against the size of that struct,
we are guaranteed not to cause interger overflow either.

Signed-off-by: Vlad Yasevich <vladislav.yasevich@hp.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: Integer overflow in the sctp_setsockopt_auth_key function in net/sctp/socket.c in the Stream Control Transmission Protocol (sctp) implementation in the Linux kernel 2.6.24-rc1 through 2.6.26.3 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (panic) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a crafted sca_keylength field associated with the SCTP_AUTH_KEY option.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Signed/unsigned comparison allows negative optlen to bypass validation check
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `optlen` is a signed `int`, but `sizeof(struct sctp_authkey)` is an unsigned `size_t`. When `optlen` is negative, it is implicitly converted to a large unsigned value for the comparison, which will be greater than `sizeof(struct sctp_authkey)`, causing the check to pass. This negative `optlen` value is then passed to `kmalloc()` where it is converted to a very large `size_t`, which will likely fail allocation. However, if allocation were to somehow succeed (e.g., on systems with different allocator behavior), `copy_from_user()` would receive the negative value cast to a huge `size_t` length, potentially causing a heap buffer overflow. The fix should use `optlen <= (int)sizeof(struct sctp_authkey)` or check `optlen < 0` separately.

commit_message:
* libtiff/tif_read.c: add protection against excessive memory
allocation attempts in TIFFReadDirEntryArray() on short files.
Effective for mmap'ed case. And non-mmap'ed case, but restricted
to 64bit builds.
Fixes http://bugzilla.maptools.org/show_bug.cgi?id=2675
cve_desc: The TIFFReadDirEntryArray function in tif_read.c in LibTIFF 4.0.8 mishandles memory allocation for short files, which allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (allocation failure and application crash) in the TIFFFetchStripThing function in tif_dirread.c during a tiff2pdf invocation.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential division by zero if desttypesize is 0
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function checks that `typesize` is not zero before proceeding, but performs no such check on the `desttypesize` parameter before using it as a divisor in the expression `2147483647/desttypesize`. If a caller passes `desttypesize` as 0, this results in a division by zero, causing a crash (denial of service). The `(void) desttypesize;` line earlier in the function misleadingly suggests the parameter is unused, but it is in fact used in this critical arithmetic check.

commit_message:
MDEV-25636: Bug report: abortion in sql/sql_parse.cc:6294

The asserion failure was caused by this query

  select /*id=1*/ from t1
  where
   col= ( select /*id=2*/ from ... where corr_cond1
          union
          select /*id=4*/ from ... where corr_cond2)

Here,
- select with id=2 was correlated due to corr_cond1.
- select with id=4 was initially correlated due to corr_cond2, but then
  the optimizer optimized away the correlation, making the select with id=4
  uncorrelated.

However, since select with id=2 remained correlated, the execution had to
re-compute the whole UNION. When it tried to execute select with id=4, it
hit an assertion  (join buffer already free'd).

This is because select with id=4 has freed its execution structures after
it has been executed once. The select is uncorrelated, so it did not expect
it would need to be executed for the second time.

Fixed this by adding this logic in
st_select_lex::optimize_unflattened_subqueries():

  If a member of a UNION is correlated, mark all its members as
  correlated, so that they are prepared to be executed multiple times.
cve_desc: MariaDB through 10.5.9 allows an application crash in sub_select_postjoin_aggr for a NULL value of aggr.

commit_message:
MDEV-25787 Bug report: crash on SELECT DISTINCT thousands_blob_fields

fix a debug assert to account for not opened temp tables
cve_desc: MariaDB through 10.5.9 allows an application crash via certain long SELECT DISTINCT statements that improperly interact with storage-engine resource limitations for temporary data structures.

commit_message: auth: mech-rpa - Fail on zero len buffer
cve_desc: In Dovecot before 2.3.11.3, sending a specially formatted RPA request will crash the auth service because a length of zero is mishandled.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Off-by-one read when p equals end, leading to out-of-bounds read
vulnerabilities.long_description: The bounds check `p > end` does not handle the case where `p == end`. When `p` equals `end`, the pointer is at the boundary (typically one past the last valid byte), yet the code proceeds to dereference it with `len = *p++`. This results in reading one byte past the valid buffer. An attacker supplying a crafted input where the data pointer exactly meets the end pointer can trigger an out-of-bounds read, potentially leaking sensitive memory contents or causing a crash.

commit_message:
lib-smtp: smtp-command-parser - Fix infinite loop occurring when command stream ends in UTF-8 character.

Broken by 8f08f1944be438a2422b604c08e5060b5c7bd72f.
cve_desc: lib-smtp in submission-login and lmtp in Dovecot 2.3.9 before 2.3.9.3 mishandles truncated UTF-8 data in command parameters, as demonstrated by the unauthenticated triggering of a submission-login infinite loop.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer underflow in size calculation passed to uni_utf8_get_char_n leads to potential out-of-bounds read
vulnerabilities.long_description: The length argument is computed as `(size_t)(p - parser->end)`. Since the loop condition guarantees `p < parser->end`, the expression `p - parser->end` is negative. When cast to `size_t` (an unsigned type), this wraps around to a very large value (close to SIZE_MAX). This tells `uni_utf8_get_char_n` that an enormous buffer is available, allowing it to read well beyond the actual end of the buffer. The correct expression should be `(size_t)(parser->end - p)`. An attacker sending a crafted multi-byte UTF-8 sequence at the boundary of a buffer could trigger an out-of-bounds read, potentially causing a crash or information disclosure.

commit_message: MDEV-24176 fixup: GCC -Wmaybe-uninitialized
cve_desc: MariaDB Server v10.6.5 and below was discovered to contain an use-after-free in the component Item_args::walk_arg, which is exploited via specially crafted SQL statements.

commit_message: auth: mech-rpa - Fail on zero len buffer
cve_desc: In Dovecot before 2.3.11.3, sending a specially formatted RPA request will crash the auth service because a length of zero is mishandled.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Off-by-one out-of-bounds read when p equals end
vulnerabilities.long_description: The bounds check uses `p > end` (strictly greater), so when `p == end`, the code falls through and dereferences `*p++` to read `len`. Since `end` marks the boundary of valid data, reading at `p == end` is a one-byte out-of-bounds read. An attacker supplying crafted input where the data pointer has been advanced to exactly the end boundary can trigger this read, potentially leaking sensitive memory contents or causing a crash. The fix is to change the check to `p >= end`.

commit_message:
Add a test for GENERAL_NAME_cmp

Based on a boringssl test contributed by David Benjamin

Reviewed-by: Tomas Mraz <tmraz@fedoraproject.org>
cve_desc: The X.509 GeneralName type is a generic type for representing different types of names. One of those name types is known as EDIPartyName. OpenSSL provides a function GENERAL_NAME_cmp which compares different instances of a GENERAL_NAME to see if they are equal or not. This function behaves incorrectly when both GENERAL_NAMEs contain an EDIPARTYNAME. A NULL pointer dereference and a crash may occur leading to a possible denial of service attack. OpenSSL itself uses the GENERAL_NAME_cmp function for two purposes: 1) Comparing CRL distribution point names between an available CRL and a CRL distribution point embedded in an X509 certificate 2) When verifying that a timestamp response token signer matches the timestamp authority name (exposed via the API functions TS_RESP_verify_response and TS_RESP_verify_token) If an attacker can control both items being compared then that attacker could trigger a crash. For example if the attacker can trick a client or server into checking a malicious certificate against a malicious CRL then this may occur. Note that some applications automatically download CRLs based on a URL embedded in a certificate. This checking happens prior to the signatures on the certificate and CRL being verified. OpenSSL's s_server, s_client and verify tools have support for the "-crl_download" option which implements automatic CRL downloading and this attack has been demonstrated to work against those tools. Note that an unrelated bug means that affected versions of OpenSSL cannot parse or construct correct encodings of EDIPARTYNAME. However it is possible to construct a malformed EDIPARTYNAME that OpenSSL's parser will accept and hence trigger this attack. All OpenSSL 1.1.1 and 1.0.2 versions are affected by this issue. Other OpenSSL releases are out of support and have not been checked. Fixed in OpenSSL 1.1.1i (Affected 1.1.1-1.1.1h). Fixed in OpenSSL 1.0.2x (Affected 1.0.2-1.0.2w).

commit_message:
MDEV-26402: A SEGV in Item_field::used_tables/update_depend_map_for_order...

When doing condition pushdown from HAVING into WHERE,
Item_equal::create_pushable_equalities() calls
item->set_extraction_flag(IMMUTABLE_FL) for constant items.
Then, Item::cleanup_excluding_immutables_processor() checks for this flag
to see if it should call item->cleanup() or leave the item as-is.

The failure happens when a constant item has a non-constant one inside it,
like:

   (tbl.col=0 AND impossible_cond)

item->walk(cleanup_excluding_immutables_processor) works in a bottom-up
way so it
1. will call Item_func_eq(tbl.col=0)->cleanup()
2. will not call Item_cond_and->cleanup (as the AND is constant)

This creates an item tree where a fixed Item has an un-fixed Item inside
it which eventually causes an assertion failure.

Fixed by introducing this rule: instead of just calling

  item->set_extraction_flag(IMMUTABLE_FL);

we call Item::walk() to set the flag for all sub-items of the item.
cve_desc: MariaDB Server v10.7 and below was discovered to contain a segmentation fault via the component Item_field::used_tables/update_depend_map_for_order.

commit_message: MDEV-26323 use-after-poison issue of MariaDB server
cve_desc: MariaDB Server v10.6 and below was discovered to contain an use-after-free in the component my_strcasecmp_8bit, which is exploited via specially crafted SQL statements.

code_snippets:
char *s= (char*)alloc_root(root, dl->length + so_ext_len + 1);
    memcpy(s, dl->str, dl->length);
vulnerabilities.short_description: Null pointer dereference if alloc_root returns NULL
vulnerabilities.long_description: If alloc_root fails and returns NULL, the subsequent memcpy and strcpy will dereference a null pointer, leading to a crash. An attacker who can influence memory pressure or the dl->length value could trigger this condition to cause a denial of service.

commit_message:
Fix EC_GROUP_new_from_ecparameters to check the base length

Check that there's at least one byte in params->base before trying to
read it.

CVE-2021-3712

Reviewed-by: Viktor Dukhovni <viktor@openssl.org>
Reviewed-by: Paul Dale <pauli@openssl.org>
cve_desc: ASN.1 strings are represented internally within OpenSSL as an ASN1_STRING structure which contains a buffer holding the string data and a field holding the buffer length. This contrasts with normal C strings which are repesented as a buffer for the string data which is terminated with a NUL (0) byte. Although not a strict requirement, ASN.1 strings that are parsed using OpenSSL's own "d2i" functions (and other similar parsing functions) as well as any string whose value has been set with the ASN1_STRING_set() function will additionally NUL terminate the byte array in the ASN1_STRING structure. However, it is possible for applications to directly construct valid ASN1_STRING structures which do not NUL terminate the byte array by directly setting the "data" and "length" fields in the ASN1_STRING array. This can also happen by using the ASN1_STRING_set0() function. Numerous OpenSSL functions that print ASN.1 data have been found to assume that the ASN1_STRING byte array will be NUL terminated, even though this is not guaranteed for strings that have been directly constructed. Where an application requests an ASN.1 structure to be printed, and where that ASN.1 structure contains ASN1_STRINGs that have been directly constructed by the application without NUL terminating the "data" field, then a read buffer overrun can occur. The same thing can also occur during name constraints processing of certificates (for example if a certificate has been directly constructed by the application instead of loading it via the OpenSSL parsing functions, and the certificate contains non NUL terminated ASN1_STRING structures). It can also occur in the X509_get1_email(), X509_REQ_get1_email() and X509_get1_ocsp() functions. If a malicious actor can cause an application to directly construct an ASN1_STRING and then process it through one of the affected OpenSSL functions then this issue could be hit. This might result in a crash (causing a Denial of Service attack). It could also result in the disclosure of private memory contents (such as private keys, or sensitive plaintext). Fixed in OpenSSL 1.1.1l (Affected 1.1.1-1.1.1k). Fixed in OpenSSL 1.0.2za (Affected 1.0.2-1.0.2y).

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read when params->base has zero length but non-NULL data pointer
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code checks that `params->base` and `params->base->data` are non-NULL, but does not verify that `params->base->length` is greater than zero before accessing `params->base->data[0]`. If an attacker provides a crafted ECPARAMETERS structure where the base octet string has a length of 0 but a non-NULL data pointer, the read of `data[0]` is an out-of-bounds read. This could lead to information disclosure or a crash. While `EC_POINT_oct2point` called subsequently would likely also fail with a zero-length input, the out-of-bounds access on `data[0]` occurs before that call.

commit_message: Fixed: HTML escape the log file content when viewed via Monit GUI.
cve_desc: Persistent cross-site scripting (XSS) in http/cervlet.c in Tildeslash Monit before 5.25.3 allows a remote unauthenticated attacker to introduce arbitrary JavaScript via manipulation of an unsanitized user field of the Authorization header for HTTP Basic Authentication, which is mishandled during an _viewlog operation.

fix a debug assert to account for not opened temp tables

commit_message: Fixed: HTML escape the log file content when viewed via Monit GUI.
cve_desc: Persistent cross-site scripting (XSS) in http/cervlet.c in Tildeslash Monit before 5.25.3 allows a remote unauthenticated attacker to introduce arbitrary JavaScript via manipulation of an unsanitized user field of the Authorization header for HTTP Basic Authentication, which is mishandled during an _viewlog operation.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) via unescaped log file content rendered in HTML
vulnerabilities.long_description: The contents of the log file are read and inserted directly into the HTML response (inside a <textarea>) without any HTML entity encoding. If an attacker can influence log file content (e.g., by sending crafted requests that get logged, or via any other log injection vector), they can inject a closing </textarea> tag followed by arbitrary HTML/JavaScript. This would execute in the browser of any authenticated user viewing the log page, potentially leading to session hijacking, CSRF, or other client-side attacks.

MDEV-26402: A SEGV in Item_field::used_tables/update_depend_map_for_order...

When doing condition pushdown from HAVING into WHERE,
Item_equal::create_pushable_equalities() calls
item->set_extraction_flag(IMMUTABLE_FL) for constant items.
Then, Item::cleanup_excluding_immutables_processor() checks for this flag
to see if it should call item->cleanup() or leave the item as-is.

The failure happens when a constant item has a non-constant one inside it,
like:

   (tbl.col=0 AND impossible_cond)

item->walk(cleanup_excluding_immutables_processor) works in a bottom-up
way so it
1. will call Item_func_eq(tbl.col=0)->cleanup()
2. will not call Item_cond_and->cleanup (as the AND is constant)

This creates an item tree where a fixed Item has an un-fixed Item inside
it which eventually causes an assertion failure.

  item->set_extraction_flag(IMMUTABLE_FL);

we call Item::walk() to set the flag for all sub-items of the item.
